div.p: About the Editors
Dr. Raghavendra Rao Chillarige

is Senior Professor (Retired) at School of Computer and Information Sciences (SCIS) at University of Hyderabad. He obtained his Bachelor of Science from Andhra University, and his master’s and doctorate degrees from Osmania University, Hyderabad. He has received Fellow of IETE and APAS and was elected as Senior Member of IRSS. His research areas include operations research, stochastic processes, computer graphic, modeling and simulation, artificial intelligence, and rough computing. He authored and co-authored more than 180 scientific papers and contributions in international journals, conferences, and wrote four books. He has supervised 15 Ph.D. scholars and many master’s students. He has organized many workshops and conferences. He was in the technical program committee members for many conferences including CIT2009, AMLTA2012, and MIWAI 2017. He is Professional Member, Society for Development of Statistics, Indian Society for Probability and Statistics, Indian Mathematical Society, IETE, and many more.
Dr. Salvatore Distefano

p.span: is Associate Professor at University of Messina, Italy; Fellow Professor at Kazan Federal University, Russia; and Head of the Social and Urban Computing Group and of the Cisco Innovation Center in Kazan. In 2001, he received the master’s degree in Computer Engineering from the University of Catania, and Ph.D. degree in Computer Science and Engineering from University of Messina in the year of 2006. He authored and co-authored more than 200 scientific papers and contributions in international journals, conferences, and books. He took part in several national and international projects, such as Reservoir, Vision (EU FP7), SMSCOM (EU FP7 ERC Advanced Grant), Beacon, and IoT-Open.EU (EU H2020). He is in the editorial boards of
p.em: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
International Journal of Engineering and Industries
International Journal of Computer Science and Information Technology Applications
p.span: , and
p.em: International Journal of Distributed Sensor Networks

div.p: Prof. Sandeep Singh Rawat

is Experienced Researcher and Consultant with proven leadership abilities in areas of knowledge extraction, information system and machine learning, database, information retrieval, data warehousing, big data, and groups decision support systems. He obtained his Bachelor of Engineering in Computer Science from National Institute of Technology, Surat (formerly REC, Surat), his master’s from Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee, and doctorate degree from University College of Engineering, Osmania University, Hyderabad. He was Research Scholar and Visiting Professor at Iowa State University, USA (2016–2018).

Dr. Sandeep actively participates in multiple committees at IGNOU, including those overseeing online course coordination, innovation promotion, and cybersecurity planning. He also contributes to the School Board at various IGNOU schools and serves as Board of Studies Member at UOU, Haldwani, Uttarakhand. He is Senior Member of IEEE and Life Members of Indian Society for Technical Education and Computer Society India and served as Co-editors of the prestigious Springer Advances in Intelligent Systems and Computing (AISC) and Lecture Notes in Networks and Systems (LNNS) series. He is Reviewer and Technical Committee Member for many national/international conferences. He has published/presented high-quality research papers in international, national journals, and proceedings of international conferences.

level2.h2: Optimizing Complex Challenges: Harnessing the Power of Genetic Algorithms and the Nelder-Mead Simplex Algorithm for Effective Problem Solving

p.span: Neha Majhi
and
Rajashree Mishra

div.p: Department of Mathematics, KIIT Deemed to be University, BBSR, Bhubaneswar, India

level3.p: Neha Majhi

level4.p: Email:

level3.p: Rajashree Mishra
(Corresponding author)
Email:

level2.p: This paper introduces a hybrid optimization framework that integrates the genetic algorithm (GA) and the Nelder-Mead simplex Algorithm (NMA) to address optimization challenges across benchmark functions and real-world problems. By synergistically combining the exploration capabilities of the GA and the local refinement prowess of the NMA, our approach aims to provide a robust and versatile solution for a wide range of optimization scenarios. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the hybrid approach through extensive experimentation on 20 benchmark functions and two real-world optimization challenges, demonstrating its adaptability, efficiency, and potential to outperform standalone methods.

level3.p: Keywords

In the field of optimization, finding optimal solutions to complex and multi-dimensional problems is a ubiquitous challenge across various domains, ranging from engineering and science to finance and artificial intelligence. Researchers and practitioners have explored a multitude of optimization techniques to tackle this challenge. Evolutionary computing techniques play a great role in handling those sorts of problems due to their simplicity and easy implementation. Hybrid algorithms are also gaining popularity as it is more efficient in finding the global optimal solution. Among these techniques, genetic algorithms (GAs) and the Nelder-Mead simplex algorithm (NMA) stand out as two distinct approaches, each offering unique advantages in addressing different aspects of optimization landscapes.

p.span: Genetic algorithms (GAs) [

] have emerged as powerful tools for solving complex optimization problems by mimicking the principles of natural evolution. GAs iteratively evolve a population of candidate solutions using selection, crossover, and mutation operations [
]. They offer the advantage of global exploration, enabling them to navigate vast solution spaces. However, GAs may suffer from slow convergence in high-dimensional or rugged landscapes.
On the other hand, the NMA [
] excels at fine-tuning solutions within well-behaved and smooth regions. The Nelder-Mead method attempts to minimize a scalar-valued nonlinear function of
real variables using only function values, without any derivative information [

]. Yet, the NMA may struggle when dealing with non-convex or complex landscapes that have multiple local optima or sharp discontinuities. The algorithm operates by shrinking, expanding, reflecting, and contracting the simplex based on the quality of function evaluations.

This paper presents a novel hybrid approach that combines the GA’s exploratory power with the NMA’s ability to converge quickly in well-behaved regions. By combining these two algorithms, we hope to demonstrate how their combined strengths can lead to higher convergence rates, higher-quality solutions, and increased robustness in the face of complex optimization landscapes. Various interesting works have been found in [

level3.p: The technique of our hybrid approach, including the integration of genetic operators with the NMA exploration and exploitation strategy, will be detailed in the next sections of this study.

We will provide experimental results from benchmark problems and talk about the advantages and disadvantages of our hybrid method. Finally, this study contributes to the ongoing investigation of hybrid optimization strategies and sheds light on how the synergy of multiple algorithms might provide more effective optimization methodologies.

p.span: Binary-coded genetic algorithms (GA) [

] utilize a binary string representation for individuals, with each bit representing a characteristic or gene. Genetic operations, including crossover and mutation, manipulate these binary strings to create offspring and introduce diversity. Crossover combines genetic information from parent individuals, while mutation randomly alters bits based on a specified probability. Fitness evaluation assesses each individual’s fitness using a defined function, determining their suitability for selection. Selection mechanisms choose individuals for reproduction based on fitness, and populations evolve over generations through genetic operations. Binary-coded GAs are valuable for optimization problems with discrete or binary decision spaces, finding applications in combinatorial optimization, machine learning, and feature selection. However, they may face challenges in problems requiring high precision or involving a vast solution space due to the discrete nature of the binary representation.
The Nelder-Mead simplex algorithm [

] is primarily designed for unconstrained optimization of nonlinear functions. It is not a general-purpose algorithm for nonlinear programming problems, especially those with constraints. If you have constraints in your problem, you may need to use more specialized nonlinear programming algorithms, such as the Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) method, interior-point methods, or genetic algorithms that are designed to handle constrained optimization. The Nelder-Mead algorithm may not guarantee global optimality, and its convergence properties can be problem-dependent. It can sometimes get stuck in local minima, and its convergence rate may not be the fastest, particularly for high-dimensional problems.

level4.p: The efficiency of the Nelder-Mead simplex algorithm in locating local optima is well recognized. It employs a geometric figure called a simplex, which consists of n+1 points in an n-dimensional space, to systematically navigate the search area. By employing several operations such as reflection, expansion, contraction, and shrinkage, the algorithm gradually progresses toward the optimal solution. This iterative process allows for swift and effective convergence. Moreover, the Nelder-Mead simplex algorithm possesses notable adaptability, enabling its application to a wide array of optimization problems encompassing continuous and discrete variables.

level3.h3: 3 Hybrid Genetic Algorithm with Nelder-Mead Simplex Algorithm (HGANM)

p.span: In a hybrid method, the capabilities of the genetic algorithm (GA) and the Nelder-Mead Simplex Algorithm (NMA) [

] can be combined to provide a potent optimization strategy that solves the drawbacks of each algorithm while maximizing their benefits. The type of the current optimization challenge also affects how well this combo works. While the hybrid technique might enhance convergence in some circumstances, it might not always be advantageous.

level3.p: The strengths of GAs and NMA are distinct. Due to their diversity-promoting methods, GA is good for exploring a large solution space and discovering global optima. On the other hand, NMA excels at local optimization and rapid convergence inside smooth regions since it is derivative-free. A hybrid strategy can successfully navigate through both global and local optima by combining these strengths.

p.span: Combining GA’s [
] exploratory power with NMA’s [

] speedy convergence can result in accelerated overall convergence in the optimization process. The exploration capabilities of GAs, which can give a wide set of beginning sites, can decrease NMA’s sensitivity to the initial simplex placement. GAs can act as a global search engine, discovering interesting parts of the solution space. NMA can then locally refine solutions in those promising locations, utilizing its efficient convergence in smooth regions. GAs frequently call for fine-tuning of performance affecting variables such as population size, mutation rate, and crossover frequency. The hybrid technique can lessen the susceptibility to parameter adjustment while still achieving efficient optimization by combining GAs with NMA.

level3.p: Many real-world optimization problems exhibit complex landscapes with both global and local optima. GAs alone might not guarantee finding the best solutions due to their inherent exploration-exploitation trade-off. By incorporating NMA, which specializes in local optimization, a hybrid approach can ensure that the best local solutions are efficiently uncovered. In essence, a hybrid approach that combines the global exploration capabilities of GAs with the fast convergence properties of NMA can lead to a more robust and efficient optimization process. By leveraging the strengths of both algorithms and mitigating their weaknesses, the hybrid approach stands to provide a comprehensive and effective solution to a wide range of optimization challenges.

p.span: Algorithm
shows the pseudo-code for hybridization of binary coded genetic algorithm and Nelder-Mead algorithm.

p.span: The performance of HGANM is tested on 20 benchmark functions [
] of 20 dimensions except Michalewicz (10D), Trid (6D), Powersum (4D), and Gramacy (1D). The parameter values of HGANM are crossover probability (
8), mutation probability (

005), bit length (l=10), gamma = 1.5, and beta = 0.5. Step size and termination criteria for NMA are taken to be 0.001 and 10e−12, respectively. The mean, std value less than 10e−6 will be considered zero. The results have been compared between GA, NMA, and HGANM, where the NMA is inserted in the GA cycle and also applied after every iteration to find better performance. The comparison data are shown in Table

div.p: Table 1
Comparison of objective-function value (GA, NMA, HGANM) for benchmark functions

p.span: 35 separate runs were performed on 200 population for each of the 20 benchmark functions. The best (minimum) values are shown in Table
. According to Table
, HGANM outperforms GA and NMA in all 20 functions (equal in four cases and superior in nine). Table

displays the mean and standard deviation (SD) values for all three methods, and it is clear that the SD for HGANM is better than that of GA and NMA. The results calculated by the HGANM algorithm are very close to the optimum in all functions (except Dixon-price, Schwefel, and Rastrigin) for their respective dimensions. In addition, the relatively small standard deviation means that HGANM is a stable algorithm.

The second experimental test is used to assess the general performance of the proposed method on the given problems by plotting the values of function values vs. the number of iterations for two situations, as shown in Fig.

. The solid line shows the traditional genetic algorithm, while the dotted line reflects the HGANM method’s performance after applying the NMA to the best-obtained solution from the standard genetic algorithm. Based on all five figures we may conclude that using the NMA technique as an intensification process in the last step of the proposed algorithm can speed up the search and result in an optimal or near-optimal solution.

div.p: 5 line graphs depict functional values versus generations. Each graph has a solid line for G A and an overlapping L-shaped dashed line for H G A N M, representing Sphere, Levy, Rosenbrock, Michalewicz, and Trid functions.

Convergence comparison graph for GA and HGANM for Benchmark functions

p.span: In this part, the Frequency Modulation Sounds Parameter Identification Problem (FMSPIP) and the system of linear equations (SLE) have been taken from the literature [

] as the two real-world problems that will be solved by GA, NMA, and HGANM. SLE and FMSPIP each have the optimum value of 0. SLE is a ten-parameter problem, whereas FMSPIP is a six-parameter problem, with each parameter ranging from − 6.4 to 6.35.
The outcomes are represented in Tables
and

for problems 1 and 2, respectively. These tables display the success rate, mean, and standard deviation of the function evaluations based on 100 separate runs. To ensure a fair comparison, the result obtained by Das and Mishra [

] has been reassessed. The lower standard deviation (SD) of HGANM compared to CGA, LX PM, and H-LXPM in problem 1 indicates its superior performance. Additionally, the HGANM algorithm demonstrates results that closely approach the optimal level when compared to GA, NMA, CGA, LX-PM, and H-LXPM in both problems.

Table 3
Performance comparison of HGANM, CGA, LX-PM, and H-LXPM to solve the system of linear equations

level3.p: The HGANM algorithm, which combines elements of the genetic algorithm and the Nelder-Mead simplex algorithm, has been successfully implemented on a total of twenty benchmark functions. Comparative analysis with the traditional genetic algorithm (GA) and the Nelder-Mead simplex algorithm (NMA) has demonstrated the superior performance of the HGANM algorithm. Furthermore, this algorithm has been utilized to effectively address and solve two real-life problems, which shows that the HGANM algorithm gives a better optimal solution than CGA, LX-PM, and H-LXPM. These findings emphasize the effectiveness and efficiency of the HGANM algorithm. Based on these findings, it can be concluded that the proposed hybrid HGANM algorithm is generally more effective, reliable, efficient, and resilient than existing algorithms in discovering optimal solutions for complex optimization problems.

level2.h2: State-of-the-Art Techniques and Evaluation Criteria for Mobile Application Offloading in Heterogeneous Networks: A Comprehensive Survey of Computation, Data, and Energy Offloading Approaches

level2.p: Mobile applications have become a part of our daily lives. As mobile devices have limited resources such as battery life and computing power, mobile application offloading has emerged as a technique to enhance the performance and efficiency of mobile devices. Mobile apps offloading defines the operation of resource-transferring tasks from a smart mobile device to distant servers. For reducing the hard task on the mobile device. In other words, mobile application offloading refers to the process of transferring computational tasks, data, or energy from mobile devices to other computing resources, such as cloud servers or distant servers, edge devices, or peer devices, to overcome the limitations of mobile devices. This review provides a comprehensive overview of mobile application offloading in terms of importance, limitation, state of the art with evolution, types of offloading techniques, comparing the different approaches in mobile application offloading including its benefits, challenges, and evaluate the performance, energy efficiency, and cost-effectiveness. Case studies of real-world applications of mobile application offloading is presented, along with their benefits and limitations. The impact of offloading on the user experience and application performance is also discussed. Also, discuss the current research trends and future directions in this field. Overall, this review highlights the importance of mobile application offloading in overcoming the limitations of mobile devices and improving the user experience.

level3.p: Keywords

Smart technology devices such as tablets and smartphones have become an important part of our daily lives. With the increase in the usage of mobile devices, the demand for mobile applications has also increased. Mobile applications have become an integral part of our daily activities, from entertainment to work. However, mobile devices have limited resources such as battery life, computing power, and memory, which can hinder the performance of mobile applications. The limited resources of mobile devices can result in slow application response times, reduced battery life, and increased heat generation. To overcome these limitations, mobile application offloading has emerged as a technique to enhance the performance and efficiency of mobile devices. The offloading called mobile application offloading is the process of transferring the hard task or data from a smart mobile device to the distant cloud server. This method is helpful for the smart mobile devices to minimize the usage of limited resources in mobile, and it helps to develop the power life and performance.

The major advantage of offloading is being able to have more access power in terms of computing resources and major size of dataset maintenance that is not available in today's enhanced smart mobile devices. These offloading methods are very useful for resource in depth tasks. Some of them are augmented reality, machine learning, video processing, image processing, and so on applications. These applications require more power and memory. Offloading the above kind of applications helps the smart mobile device user with better experience with limited resource usage in the mobile. Nowadays, the offloading techniques have enhanced mobile application services such as virtual reality, virtual game, cloud-based mobile games, and applications. But these all require the strong and continuous Internet connection. These methods also help to minimize the power usage of smart mobile devices and memory. This will be very useful when the smart mobile devices have lower or limited battery life.

By minimizing the power usage of smart mobile devices, the offloading helps to improve the lifespan of the smart mobile devices and it reduces the environmental and economic impact of mobile computing. The implementation of offloading will be done through different methods. The methods are data offloading, computation offloading, and hybrid offloading. Data offloading means offloading the controllable data from the mobile devices for the storage purpose to the remote server. The controllable data is nothing but the accessible data. For example, in building uncontrollable or non-accessible data such as camera or fingerprint are very sensitive which cannot be offloaded. Computation offloading means offloading the hard task from the smart mobile device to the distant cloud server while hybrid offloading is a combination of both data and computation offloading together to reform the performance and utilization of resources in the mobile applications. In recent times, the mobile application offloading is very important to make smart mobile devices more powerful and universal. It offers many advantages such as performance improvement, minimizing power usage, and access to high demand computing resources.

p.span: The system diagram for the mobile application offloading contains several elements such as smart mobile devices, distant cloud server, and Internet connection between them. Figure

shows the high-level system diagram for mobile application offloading. In this diagram, the smart mobile devices send the computation and data to the distant cloud through the Internet. The distant cloud will process the request given by the smart mobile device and react to the device through the Internet. The computation and tasks will be offloaded from the smart mobile devices to the distant cloud, which leads to the user better experience. The main motive of offloading the mobile applications consists of improvement in performance, powerful resource access, cost-effective, and opportunities to develop new mobile applications.

div.p: A flow diagram defines how the smart mobile devices access the distant cloud server through edge nodes. All the devices are connected to a common network through transmission towers, clouds, and modems.
Fig. 1
High-level system architecture for mobile application offloading

level3.p: The above architecture also supports edge computing. It will enhance the performance and efficiency by reducing the computation load from the cloud. The intermediate node called edge computing helps to transfer the computation and tasks from the distant cloud through the network, closer to the smart mobile devices. It will minimize the bandwidth and latency of distant cloud services and applications. So, it will improve the mobile user experience and minimize the network clogging. In edge computing, the smart mobile devices transfer the data or computation task to the nearby edge node instead of a distant cloud server, which processes and reacts to the result action to the smart mobile devices. Because of the closer devices, the network latency and bandwidth will be fulfilled. Edge node supports the real-time process and decision taker, which is very analytic in few areas such as telemedicine, industrial automation, and autonomous vehicles. The most important feature of mobile application offloading architecture is privacy and security.

During the data transfer or computation task or offloading stage, the data breaches or some vulnerability will happen by unauthorized access or interception. To fill this kind of gap, it is very important to implement the security protocols between every transfer in terms of data encryption and so on. Mainly while implementing these processes General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) are very important to follow. The GDPR and CCPA are the mechanisms where the customer data is handled properly or not. In final, this architecture plays a very important role in raising the efficiency, performance, and function of the enhanced mobile application. Smart mobile devices such as mobile phones and tablets are worldwide and are useful for a wide range of tasks in terms of entertainment, business, and communication. Anyhow these devices also contain certain limitations for the performance and resources. Some of the limitations are given below,

Limited power: Smart mobile devices are limited in power compared to the laptop and desktop computers. This is because of the smaller factor and limited battery life. This leads to complexity during application processing.
Limited storage: Mobile devices have limited memory power which leads to restriction in data and application storing and processing. Also, it leads to slower performance, crashes, and other issues.
Limited battery: All mobile devices have limited battery power. If any of the intensive applications runs or performs the task can easily drain the battery life of the devices.

Limited Internet: Now all the mobile devices use only the wireless networks such as Wi-Fi or cellular data to connect internet or other devices in terms of Bluetooth or screencast. Mobility is the major issue due to limitations in bandwidth or signal congestion.

Limited display: Mobile devices or tablets have smaller screen sizes than the laptop or desktop computer. This leads to viewing the complex data or performing the large task due to the limited size of the display.

Limited sensor: Mobile devices have sensors such as gyroscope and GPS. It helps to find the motion or location. All these sensors have limited resources which can affect the quality and reality of data.

Limited security and privacy: Mobile devices have rights to access secure or sensitive data such as transactions such as e-commerce, NET banking, and so on. If any unauthorized source from outside can take the rights of those operations which leads to loss of security and privacy.
Limited compatibility: All mobile devices have different operating systems or hardware platforms, which can affect few applications to support.
In final, offloading is the important task to overcome the limitation faced by smart mobile devices.

p.span: The evolution of smart mobile devices and the enhancement of different kinds of mobile applications are a closed twister. Mobile devices are developed from simple voice and text communication to modern multimedia and enumerate devices which can run hard applications. In the same while, the development of mobile applications has been charged by maximizing the potential of smart mobile devices. The first and foremost mobile devices are called basic cell phones. These cell phones are widely used only for voice calls and text messages. When mobile networks developed, these devices added features such as web browsing and email. The introduction of smart mobiles creates a major turning point in the history of mobile devices. These smart mobiles have voice, text, web browsing, and emails like existing mobile features along with that advanced level computing capacity including camera, sensor, and touch system. This may allow the creator to develop the games, social media apps, and some productive tools or platforms for the mobile user. Tablets represent next milestones in the evolution of mobile devices. Compared with the smart mobile, tablets have larger screens and more in power as shown in Fig.

. The enhancement of mobile applications nowadays has several factors by increasing the capacity of mobile devices, growth of mobile Internet, and boosting an app store. Most important one of the aspects of the evolution and the growth of applications will reflect in business and industries.

div.p: An illustration displays the evolution of mobile phones. From the basic cordless phones with keypad buttons which are larger to smartphones and tablets with touchscreen displays. Tablets have larger screens than smartphones.
Fig. 2
Models of mobile phone evaluation from basic cell phone to tablet

p.span: Offloading is the method of transferring requested hard tasks from the smart mobile device to the distant cloud. There are various offloading techniques that each have their own merits and demerits. Some of the most common offloading methods are given below,

: This method is where the offloading task will happen in between requested smart mobile devices and cloud. The distant cloud provides various resources for offloading such as storage, processing power, and bandwidth to complete the given tasks. This method is very useful for the mobile which has limited resources in terms of memory and battery. Any offloading techniques need constant network connection which is one among the problems in cloud offloading. The merits are that the cloud is accessible at any time also from any place via Internet connection, and it will be maintained by the authorized third party.

: This offloading method is where the hard task offloading will take place in the edge of the network near to the smart mobile devices. This method is helpful to reduce the latency and improve performance, especially as it is a time-sensitive application. Mainly edge offloading is used to reduce the network delay and improve the scalability. The demerits are limited processing power, security risk

: This is a method where the offloading tasks will happen between the mobile devices within the same network. This technique is mainly used to reduce the load on the cloud and edge servers. It improves scalability and performance. The peer-to-peer offloading can also cause network failures due to local network connection. This kind of method requires the cooperations. The demerits are increasing security risk. This technique contains offloading data, power, and bandwidth to nearby peers. This offloading is the decentralized infrastructure. Peer-to-peer offloading is very ideal for the real-time application for data processing and data transfer. The choice of the offloading is depending on the requirements of the application which includes processing power, memory, latency, and network connectivity.
Nowadays, the researchers explored different types of mobile application offloading along with that the researcher concentrated on security, scalability, and effectiveness. Li et al. [

] mentioned an overview of mobile edge computing (MEC) and its challenges. Offloading techniques such as local offloading and remote offloading are used to reduce latency, improved energy efficiency, and improved user experience. But the main challenges is security and privacy concerns, resource constraints, scalability, heterogeneity, and interoperability. The open research issues are energy-efficient MEC, dynamic resource management, security and privacy issues, standardization, service composition, and orchestration. Fan et al. [

] discussed the challenges and approaches in mobile cloud computing. The main task is to provide computation offloading, task scheduling, resource allocation enhanced processing power, extended battery life, reduced energy consumption. It helps to provide services to heterogeneous devices, security and privacy, limited network bandwidth, resource constraints. The main issue is to improve resource allocation and task scheduling algorithms, addressing security and privacy concerns, exploring novel offloading models and techniques. MR et al. [

] explained mobile cloud computing architecture, applications, and challenges. The data offloading, computation offloading, joint offloading, and resource pooling provide an overview of mobile cloud computing architecture. The advantages are heterogeneity of devices and networks, limited resources, data security and privacy concerns, connectivity, and energy consumption. The main concern is to improve security and privacy in mobile cloud computing, enhancing quality of service (QoS) in mobile cloud computing, developing new architectures for mobile cloud computing.
Mao [

] explores the emerging field of mobile edge computing with a focus on communication aspects. It serves as a valuable resource for understanding the communication-related challenges and opportunities associated with MEC, a field that has gained significant importance due to the proliferation of edge devices and the demand for low-latency, high-performance applications. Yahia et al. [

] explained the optimization scheduling using nature-inspired algorithms in cloud computing using task offloading. The main advantage of using this is to improve task allocation and scheduling, energy efficiency, and reduced delay. But there are open research issues such as scalability and complexity of algorithm design. Kottari et al. [

] explained about the concept, applications, and challenges of mobile cloud computing using task offloading and data offloading techniques. The main advantage is cost-effective, improved performance, and enhanced storage capacity. But demerits faced by the concept such as security, privacy, reliability, data management, and interoperability. The main open research issues such as quality of service, power efficiency, scalability, and fault tolerance. Chalaemwongawan et al. [

] said about the survey and solution framework for mobile cloud computing. The main techniques are to allocate resource, task scheduling, and load balancing. The challenges faced by the concept are network latency, limited battery life of mobile devices. Needs to improve effective task scheduling algorithms, energy-efficient offloading strategies, privacy-preserving data management, efficient use of edge computing resources. Noor et al. [

] explained about mobile cloud computing (MCC) using cloud offloading techniques. The main advantages are cost-effective, reduced energy consumption. Overviews of the latest challenges and future research are shown in Table

level4.h4: 2.3 Current Gaps in Research and Opportunities for Future Work

p.span: While there have been significant advancements in the field of mobile application offloading, there still exist some gaps in research that present opportunities for future work. Some of the current gaps and future opportunities in mobile offloading research are:

: The current research in mobile offloading has mainly focused on cloud and edge computing technologies. However, with the emergence of new technologies like Internet of Things (IoT), blockchain, and fog computing, there is a need to explore how these technologies can be integrated with mobile offloading to enhance its capabilities.

: Most of the existing offloading techniques are based on static policies that assume a fixed network condition. However, network conditions can be highly dynamic, which can affect the performance of offloading. There is a need for dynamic offloading strategies that can adapt to changing network conditions in real time.

: Most of the existing offloading techniques are designed to optimize system-level metrics such as energy consumption and response time. However, these metrics may not always align with the user's preferences and expectations. There is a need for user-centric offloading techniques that consider the user's preferences and constraints.

: Mobile offloading involves transferring sensitive data to external servers, which raises concerns about privacy and security. Future research should focus on developing secure offloading mechanisms that protect the privacy of user data.

: The current offloading techniques assume that there are unlimited computing and network resources available. However, resources are limited and need to be allocated efficiently to optimize the performance of offloading. There is a need for resource allocation and optimization techniques that can balance the trade-off between performance and resource utilization.

: Most of the existing research in mobile offloading uses performance metrics such as response time and energy consumption to evaluate the effectiveness of offloading techniques. However, there is a need for new evaluation metrics that can capture the overall user experience and consider the user's preferences and constraints.

level3.p: Mobile application offloading has become an essential technique to enhance the performance and efficiency of mobile devices. Offloading resource-intensive tasks to remote servers can significantly reduce the burden on mobile devices, resulting in improved performance, reduced energy consumption, and increased battery life. However, mobile application offloading also presents several challenges, such as selecting the right offloading technique, communication latency, and security concerns. Several techniques are used for mobile application offloading, including task offloading, data offloading, and computation offloading. Future research in this field is expected to focus on addressing these challenges and improving the offloading techniques to enhance the performance and efficiency of mobile devices further. Overall, mobile application offloading has the potential to revolutionize the mobile application industry and provide a better user experience.

level2.h2: IDSSA: An Intrusion Detection System with Self-adaptive Capabilities for Strengthening the IoT Network Security

p.span: E. Geo Francis
and
S. Sheeja

div.p: Department of Computer Science, Karpagam Academy of Higher Education, Coimbatore, India

level3.p: E. Geo Francis

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: This paper introduces a novel intrusion detection system (IDS) with self-adaptive capabilities, specifically designed to enhance the security of Internet of Things (IoT) networks. The rapid growth and interconnectivity of IoT devices expose them to a wide array of cyber threats, demanding intelligent and adaptable defense mechanisms. Our proposed IDS utilizes advanced machine learning algorithms, “Dynamic Neural Sentry (DNS)” and “Evolutionary Anomaly Tracker (EAT)” for real-time intrusion detection. DNS employs deep neural networks with recurrent feedback to learn from new attack patterns and adapt its detection strategies rapidly, enhancing accuracy in processing complex IoT network traffic. EAT uses genetic programming to evolve detection rules, identifying novel and sophisticated attacks beyond traditional signature-based systems. With an “IoT Contextual Analyzer” to reduce false positives, the self-adaptive IDS improves against emerging threats autonomously, outperforming static systems in detecting known and zero-day attacks, securing IoT networks efficiently.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: The Internet of Things (IoT), which is expanding quickly, has completely changed how humans engage with technology by creating a networked ecosystem of numerous intelligent objects. IoT networks have impacted a number of industries, including smart homes, health care, industrial automation, and transportation, and they are providing previously unheard-of ease and efficiency [

]. However, this widespread connectivity also opens up new avenues for cyber threats and security breaches, making the protection of IoT networks a critical challenge. To address the escalating cybersecurity concerns in IoT environments, we present IDSSA: an intrusion detection system with self-adaptive capabilities. IDSSA is a cutting-edge security solution designed to fortify the security of IoT networks by detecting and responding to potential intrusions in real time [

IoT's explosive growth has ushered in, a new era of connection, revolutionizing a number of sectors and changing how people interact with technology. IoT networks comprise a vast and diverse array of interconnected devices, from smart appliances and wearable gadgets to industrial sensors and autonomous vehicles [

]. This interconnected ecosystem has led to unprecedented convenience, efficiency, and automation, propelling us toward a more technologically integrated future. The sheer scale, complexity, and heterogeneity of IoT networks make them highly susceptible to a wide range of cyber threats and malicious activities. The interconnected nature of IoT devices opens up potential attack vectors for adversaries, compromising the integrity, confidentiality, and availability of critical data and services [

]. Intrusion detection systems (IDS) have long been an essential defense mechanism in traditional computing environments, aiding in identifying and responding to security breaches and anomalous behavior. The highly interconnected nature of IoT devices, along with their resource-constrained nature, presents unique challenges for network security [

]. Conventional intrusion detection systems are often ill-suited for IoT networks owing to their high resource overhead and limited adaptability to dynamic IoT environments. The system continuously learns from network traffic and adapts its detection strategies, enabling it to effectively identify both known and unknown threats.

We aim to design and implement an intrusion detection system tailored specifically for IoT networks, equipped with self-adaptive capabilities. The system will employ state-of-the-art machine learning algorithms to continuously learn from network traffic and adapt its detection strategies in real time. Our research will utilize recurrent feedback mechanisms to process sequential IoT network data efficiently. The evaluation will involve simulated IoT network scenarios and real-world attack traces to validate the system's ability to detect various types of threats accurately. Our article is organized as follows: Sect.
gives a summary of the research on IoT network security and intrusion detection that has already been done. Section
describes the system we suggest. Datasets, assessment criteria, simulation scenarios, and implementation are all covered in Sect.
’s Experimental Setup section. The Conclusion and next scope are covered in Sect.
Nguyen et al. [

] have presented an innovative deep neural network (DNN)-based network intrusion detection system (NIDS) specifically designed to operate on local gateways, providing robust protection for IoT devices within the network. SID-UAV is a revolutionary strategy that Fotohi et al. [

] presented to combat malicious UAVs that endanger UAV to UAV communications. A self-matching mechanism is used in the SID-UAV approach to determine the safest route between UAVs. By identifying prospective assaults, intrusion detection systems created by Shukla et al. [

] are essential for improving the security of computer networks. This paper offers an opposition self-adaptive grasshopper optimization algorithm based on mutation and perceptive idea to handle the problem of spotting new malicious or abnormal assaults. Maseer et al. [

] presented a novel approach, a hybrid deep learning for enhancing IoT network intrusion detection. The developed method combines the power of deep learning algorithms with the inherent strengths of traditional intrusion detection techniques to achieve a comprehensive and effective security solution for IoT networks. IoT devices have revolutionized several businesses by enabling unmatched connection and ease, according to research by Zer et al. [
]. However, owing to IoT devices’ frequent resource limitations, this widespread adoption has also created cybersecurity issues.

In this section, we present the architecture of our novel self-adaptive IDS designed specifically to strengthen the security of IoT networks. The proposed system leverages cutting-edge machine learning algorithms, including the DNS and EAT to enable real-time intrusion detection and response. The architecture of our self-adaptive IDS is designed to be scalable and adaptable to various IoT network environments. It consists of three core components: DNS, the EAT, and the IoT contextual analyser (Fig.

div.p: A diagram. The information source sends raw data to the data collection, followed by the detector, the response component, and the information source-monitored system. The knowledge base is connected to the detector. Configuration sends the system state to the detector and response component.
Fig. 1
Basic architecture of IDS

level4.p: A benchmark dataset frequently used in the field of network intrusion detection for assessing intrusion detection system performance is the NSL-KDD dataset. The first KDD Cup dataset, which previously was often used for intrusion detection research, has been improved and made more challenging. Other types of attacks, such as DoS, Probe, R2L, and U2R attacks, are also contained in the data.

p.span: The NSL-KDD dataset has undergone preprocessing to address issues such as duplicate and redundant records. This preprocessing ensures cleaner and more reliable data for evaluation. To address this imbalance, preprocessing techniques such as oversampling may be applied to balance the distribution of different classes, allowing for more accurate evaluation of the intrusion detection system's performance. As a result, the technique creates a single set
that contains all instances of the minority class. A new synthetic instance
is generated as follows for each of the instances

The dynamic neural sentry is a key component of our IDS, leveraging deep neural networks with recurrent feedback mechanisms. This enables the DNS to continually learn from new attack patterns and swiftly adapt its detection strategies. By processing sequential data effectively, the DNS enhances its understanding of complex IoT network traffic, leading to higher accuracy in identifying potential intrusion (Fig.

Architecture of DNS

. The feedback loop allows the DNS to learn from its own predictions and adapt its detection strategies based on the latest network traffic patterns. The EAT complements the DNS by utilizing genetic programming techniques. By simulating a process similar to natural selection, the EAT evolves its detection rules to identify novel and sophisticated attacks beyond traditional signature-based systems. This evolutionary approach allows the EAT to efficiently identify previously unseen threats, ensuring a robust defense mechanism for IoT networks.

level4.p: To minimize false positives and reduce detection latency, our IDS incorporates an IoT contextual analyzer. This component captures and analyzes contextual information from IoT devices, enabling the system to differentiate between legitimate device activities and potential malicious actions. The IoT contextual analyzer enhances the overall accuracy of the IDS and reduces unnecessary alarms, further improving the efficiency of threat detection.

level3.p: We outline our self-adaptive IDS's thorough assessment and performance analysis in this section. We have conducted the simulations using Python tool. We present the performance evaluation results of our self-adaptive IDS. The experiments demonstrate the superiority of our system in detecting both known and zero-day attacks compared to traditional static IDSs. The DNS and EAT algorithms showcased their adaptability and effectiveness in identifying evolving threats, providing higher detection accuracy than conventional signature-based systems.

level3.p: The suggested self-adaptive IDS using the DNS, EAT, and IoT contextual analyzer has proven to be successful in improving the security of IoT networks. The system's adaptive nature and machine learning algorithms allow it to constantly alter detection tactics, recognize new assaults, and reduce false positives. Through extensive simulations and evaluations, the self-adaptive IDS has showcased superior performance in detecting both known and zero-day attacks, outperforming static detection systems. The future scope of this work lies in several promising directions. Exploring the integration of threat intelligence feeds and real-time data from IoT devices can further improve detection accuracy and responsiveness.

level2.h2: Privacy and Security Aspects of Indian Population While Using Online Social Network: An Analysis

p.span: Mahavir Devmane
and
N. K. Rana

div.p: V.P.P.C.O.E & V.A., Mumbai, India
THEEM College of Engineering, Boisar, Mumbai, India

level3.p: Mahavir Devmane

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: The popularity of online social networking sites is growing day by day. Well-known online social networking (OSN) sites such as Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn, Google+, Myspace are having millions of users across the globe. The user base of Facebook alone is more than 3 Billion. The number of users of all such OSNs is increasing tremendously in India as well as around the world. This paper focuses on the points like whether the user has read and understood, terms of use of the OSN joined, privacy policies of the OSNs, who can see the user’s email ID, contact information, posting information about himself/herself, posting videos, photos on own profile, etc. 700+ Indians were contacted for this survey using a questionnaire consisting of 27 questions. The results of this survey indicate that significant difference is observed in the awareness regarding privacy and security between the male and female participants. The privacy and security concerns are more among the Indian females compared to the Indian males.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: The rise of online social networks (OSNs) has sparked interest in social correlation research and its potential for both analysis and exploitation. Privacy concerns loom as public disclosure of personal information becomes increasingly common on these platforms. Driven by their immense popularity, billions of users flock to OSNs like Facebook (boasting over 3 billion accounts) to spend countless hours connecting, sharing, and integrating these platforms into their daily lives [
]. Joining an OSN typically requires creating a profile with details like name, photo, date of birth, email, education, work, hometown, and interests [
]. While some fields are optional, others are mandatory, and requirements vary across different platforms.
The allure of OSNs lies in their ability to facilitate friend connections, idea exchange, new encounters, photo tagging and sharing, and fostering communities around shared values [
]. Surprisingly, users readily divulge private information on their profiles, granting access to a vast audience [
]. This study aims to analyze privacy and security awareness among users of OSNs in India and other countries, across diverse demographics like gender, age, and educational background.
Organization of this paper is as follows. Section
focuses on related work in this area, Sect.
focuses on the methodology used for collecting and processing the data of Indian Population. Section
describes the results and analysis, and finally Sect.
concludes the paper.
Zamzani et al. [

] tried to find out the awareness of online privacy in the university students belonging to IT as well as non-IT fields. For this purpose, a questionnaire consisting of three sections, viz. demographic, ethics, and privacy was prepared. A total of 100 students were selected for this survey. Data was collected by sending questionnaire by post to these participants. 90 students replied to this survey. The main intension of this work was to find out the relationship between the demography, level of awareness, and online privacy in the IT and non-IT students. The results of this work show that there is no significant difference based on gender as well as whether the respondent belongs to IT-related field or not. However, it is found that there is a significant difference between the level of awareness and expected attitude to online privacy.
In paper [

], P. venkateswari and T. purusothaman compared the usability and security aspect of US users and South Indian users. In this experimentation, two closed forums named (i) adult and (ii) children are studied to find out their usability and security aspect from an OSN named CHOKUT. The number of members in adult group is 68, and that of the children group is 24. The results obtained are then compared with the security aspect of US users from the work done by other researchers. This work tries to find out approach of the respondents toward unrestricted access of user data, threat of technology to the privacy of user. The authors conclude that the US respondents are more serious about privacy of personal information compared to south Indian respondents.
Authors of the paper [

] Kevin Lewisa, Jason Kaufmana gathered the dataset from Facebook and analyzed it. It was related to students of a college in southeast USA. The college was also agreed to share information of the students. The privacy of students was ensured by replacing student name with numeric values as well as encoding some of the data. The authors found that the subgroups based on race, gender, and socioeconomic status are characterized by the behaviors as well as social relationships with reference to location and cultural background of the users.

level3.p: This study seeks to draw insightful conclusions about Indian users’ privacy and security perceptions on online social networks by analyzing and interpreting their data.

The instrument used for this survey is a questionnaire having total of 27 questions, and each question is having two to five options based on the number of possible answers to the question. Literature review, discussions with the OSN users, and experts in the field of security are done thoroughly so as to cover maximum areas of concern. The questionnaire is prepared with the intention to investigate the usability trend of the user, privacy, and security awareness of the user while using the online social network. The questions related to usability trend, privacy awareness, and security awareness are grouped together in the questionnaire. This study delves into the motivations behind joining online social networks, user comprehension of privacy policies and terms of service, how criteria for accepting and sending friend requests are formed, and the factors influencing the sharing of private information, photos, and videos on these platforms.
Out of the 27 questions from the questionnaire, 18 questions which are related to the security and privacy awareness are taken into consideration for this paper.

Participants were invited to complete a voluntary survey accompanied by a consent form. The form clearly stated that participation was entirely optional and could be withdrawn at any time. It also assured participants that their responses would be solely used for quantitative analysis and their individual views would remain confidential.

The participants were divided into three categories of the users as teenager (upto 18 years of age), novice users (age 19 to 21 years and students), and professional. Considering the participants for the survey is using at least one of the online social networks.

This survey is conducted in engineering colleges in Mumbai and suburbs for the category of teenager and novice. For the category of professionals, this survey is conducted in various regions of Mumbai and suburbs.

This survey is also conducted online by sending the questionnaire as Google Docs to the people. Data received by this mechanism is added in the database of paper-based questionnaire. Out of 708 participants of this survey 10% participants participated online by submitting their questionnaire as Google Docs.

p.span: The demography of the participants is given in Table

div.p: Table 1
Demography of the participants

level3.p: To facilitate the survey, the author held open discussions with participants, both clarifying the study's intent and addressing any inquiries they had about the research or online social networks.

The questionnaire is given only to a person who is willing to be part of this survey. All these questionnaires and the response to the Google Docs questionnaires are further divided into three categories: teenager, novice, and professional. The data gathered from all these questionnaires is stored in a database. The fields related to identity of the participant are obscured and assigned a unique number to record of each of the participant.

This data is further used for analyzing. The analysis of the data is done with the focus of finding privacy and security aspects of the participants. It tried to find out the difference or similarity of privacy and security perspective across the genders, across the different categories. The MS-Excel as well as data analysis tool Origin pro is used for generating graphs and analysis purpose wherever required.

The study used quantitative analysis for evaluating the responses toward the sensitivity of privacy and security while using online social networks. The questions are grouped according to their relevance to either privacy or security. In this section, we first discuss privacy-related questions and then the security-related questions. Based on the responses given by the participants, we get very interesting results. Analysis of these results is discussed in this section.

When it came to birthday privacy, students were more open than professionals. While only 25.37% of students kept their birthdays public, a striking 66.61% opted for friends-only visibility. Professionals, on the other hand, prioritized privacy, with 77.31% choosing friends-only and a mere 17.53% keeping birthdays public. Interestingly, both groups favored the “friends-only” setting by a significant margin, highlighting a general concern for birthday privacy despite their differences in openness.

p.span: Whereas the survey conducted in Europe indicates that the majority of both female (98.5%) and male (100%) reveal their birthdate on Facebook [
]. Similarly, a survey conducted in Hong Kong shown that 69.5% of users reveal their birthdate on Facebook [

level3.p: So, there is a significant difference in revealing birthday by Indian, USA, and Hong Kong population. By disclosing the birthday, the user loses his privacy as well as it increases security threat to the user as so many websites use the birthday for password recovery, etc.
Among the total respondents, close to 25% of respondents kept the email ID visible to public, whereas 65% kept it visible to friends and remaining kept it limited to Only Me.

p.span: The survey done in Europe shows that 57.4% of females and 71.4% of males keep visibility of their email ID as public [
Whereas only 53% of users in USA [
] and 63.1% of users in Hong Kong keep their email ID visible [

level3.p: Clearly, more number of European users keep their email ID visible to public compared to the USA and Indian users. The Indian population is reluctant to expose their identity to others.

Analyzing responses to the previous questions reveals that less than 25% of users publicly display their birthday and email address. The majority opt for friends-only visibility, likely due to a sense of trust within their social circles. A small minority chooses maximum privacy by keeping this information visible only to themselves.

p.span: Figure
presents a comparative analysis of how teenagers, novices, and professionals choose to disclose their contact details on online platforms.

div.p: A grouped column chart of contact details disclosure to the public, friends, and only me for teenagers males is highest for friends, for teenager females is highest for only me, for professional males is highest for friends, and for professional females is same for friends and only me.
Fig. 1
Graph of you disclose your contact details to

level3.p: Gender discrepancies emerge when it comes to contact detail privacy among professionals. While only 25% of male professionals keep their contacts private, nearly half (48.98%) of female professionals do so. Interestingly, female teenagers also seem more privacy conscious, with 75.34% opting for the “Only Me” setting compared to 38.18% of male teenagers. Overall, a majority of participants, regardless of gender or age group, prioritize hiding their contact details on online social networks, highlighting their concern for contact information privacy.

p.span: In the survey conducted in Europe 54.2% of users kept visibility of their Hometown as public [
], whereas 61% of US teenagers publicly share their Hometown [

level3.p: Compared to European users and American teenagers on an average only 29.5% of Indian users keep the hometown public and on an average 57.27% of Indians disclose their Hometown to friends. This indicates that Indian users are careful while sharing their hometown information with strangers as compared to European and American users.

The study uncovers contrasting preferences regarding family photo and video sharing on online social networks. Teenagers, novices, and professionals exhibit similar rates of openness, with around 42% in each group expressing a fondness for sharing these glimpses of their family life. In contrast, the remaining participants value privacy and choose not to publicly share such personal content.

p.span: In the survey conducted in USA, it is found that 79% of participants have included photos of themselves and 66% of participants have included photos of their friends but they are reluctant in sharing photos of their family [
]. This indicates that the approach toward sharing family photos/videos is similar in Indian and US participants.

level3.p: When it comes to revealing their romantic lives on online platforms, discretion reigns supreme. A vast majority of participants across all age groups—over 90% of teenagers (93.99%), novices (91.36%), and professionals (92.78%)—choose to keep their significant others out of the public eye on social media.

This privacy preference is reflected in the low percentages of those who actively share partner information: only 6.01% of teenagers, 8.64% of novices, and 7.22% of professionals willingly post about their significant others on online social networks.

The reasons behind this reluctance to share could be multifaceted. Concerns about privacy and security are likely significant factors. Sharing personal details about loved ones online exposes them to a potentially vast audience, including strangers, which could raise concerns about unwanted attention or even potential harm.

Close to half the participants across age groups express a willingness to share photos and messages of others on online social networks. This trend emerges with 45.90% of teenagers, 50.00% of novices, and 43.30% of professionals indicating comfort with such sharing. However, the remaining half of the population chooses to refrain from sharing others’ content.

While sharing the information or photo the owner can apply settings so that only the relevant people are able to see it. But when the recipient of this information or photo shares it further it will reach to the people who should not get it according to owner. So, willingly or unwillingly the information or photos may reach in wrong hands. So whenever there is any data with limited audience reaches us it should not be shared with others.

The study reveals a divided sentiment regarding the influence of online social networks (OSNs) on spam and phishing attempts. Over 40% of participants across all age groups (41.53% of teenagers, 51.64% of novices, and 46.39% of professionals) reported an increase in spam/phishing messages, emails, and phone calls after joining OSNs. This suggests a potential correlation between disclosing personal information like email addresses and contact details on social platforms and becoming a target for unwanted communications.

However, it's important to note that nearly half of the participants (58.47% of teenagers, 48.36% of novices, and 53.61% of professionals) disagreed with this observation, suggesting that other factors might also be at play.

The study delves into the intriguing realm of online friendships, revealing interesting discrepancies in friend list size between students and professionals. While a whopping 50% of student participants boast friends’ lists exceeding 200, only 26.80% of professionals claim the same. This suggests a preference for tighter-knit circles among professionals, further reinforced by the observation that 30% of them keep their friend lists below 50.

p.span: The world of Facebook friendships in the US paints a picture of a population split down the middle. According to research [
], a stark 50/50 divide exists when it comes to friend list size: half of Facebook users in the USA have more than 200 friends, while the other half stick to a smaller circle of 200 or fewer.
Figure

dives into the intriguing realm of online friendships, revealing a fascinating comparison between the average number of friends users have on online social networks (OSNs) and the number of those friends they actually know in real life. This analysis sheds light on the complex interplay between our digital and offline social circles.

div.p: A grouped column chart for teenagers presents the highest number of friends in the friend list, for novices presents the highest number of friends in the friends list, and for professionals highest number of friends in the friend list.
Fig. 2
Average number of friends in friend list and out of that average number of friends in real life

level3.p: The survey reveals a divided landscape when it comes to user engagement with online social network (OSN) terms of service (TOS). Among participants, a minority actively delves into the full details: only 14.75% of teenagers, 11.45% of novices, and 12.37% of professionals report reading the TOS completely.

The majority, however, fall into a “skim and understand” category. A sizable 65.03% of teenagers, 65.65% of novices, and 48.45% of professionals claim to have read and understood the essential points of the TOS.

However, a significant portion remains entirely in the dark about the rules governing their online interactions. Notably, 19.67% of teenagers, 22.90% of novices, and a concerning 39.18% of professionals admit to never having read the TOS of the OSN they use.

p.span: A survey conducted among Facebook users in the US paints a concerning picture of user awareness and understanding of the platform's Terms of Service (ToS). A staggering 91% of participants admitted to never having read the ToS [
]. This lack of engagement with the very document outlining their rights and obligations highlights a potential disconnect between Facebook and its user base.

Adding to the complexity, the survey revealed a split opinion on Facebook's data-sharing practices. While 47% of users expressed confidence in Facebook's commitment to user privacy and believed their information wouldn't be shared with third parties, the remaining 53% held the opposite view, raising concerns about data security and potential misuse [

level3.p: A major concern overshadows the use of online social networks: the vast majority of users haven't fully grasped the ToS, the very document outlining their rights and obligations within the platform. This widespread ignorance poses a significant threat on several fronts.

Survey reveals a concerning disconnect between users and their understanding of online social network (OSN) privacy policies. Among participants: A mere 16.94% of teenagers, 18.69% of novices, and 12.37% of professionals reported reading the privacy policy completely. A larger group, 57.92% of teenagers, 55.37% of novices, and 49.48% of professionals, claim to have partially read and understood the policy. A worrying 24.59% of teenagers, 25.93% of novices, and a surprising 38.14% of professionals admitted to never having read the privacy policy of the OSN they use.

p.span: The results of survey conducted in four different universities in USA show that 23% of the participants are not concerned with Facebook privacy, 35.5% are barely concerned and 31.6% are somewhat concerned. Only 9.7% of the respondents are very concerned about the privacy policy of Facebook [

level3.p: As a whole, very less number of Indian as well as US respondents have studied and understood the privacy policy of Facebook. The privacy policy deals with the way in which the user-provided data will be handled by the OSN. So it is necessary to know what the privacy policy of the OSN is, and according to it, the OSN should be used.

p.span: The outcome of the survey conducted in USA shows that the 63.45% Facebook users tend to befriend the people they know, which means they never befriend strangers, 28.72% befriend strangers on occasion, and 7.83% says they become friend with strangers [
]. This indicates that a significant amount of users become friend with strangers which is a threat to the privacy and security of the user.

level3.p: The result of survey conducted in Hong Kong shows that 81.3% of the users are accepting friend request or adding someone as a friend only if he/she knows him/her.
Comparing the approach of Indian, USA, and Hong Kong population, the Indian and Hong Kong population is careful while accepting someone as a friend in the Online Social Network.
It is found that around 88.54% of the participants of professional category send friend request to only the people to whom the users know in real life.

p.span: Figure
is the graph regarding the criteria used by the respondents of different categories while sending friend request.

div.p: A grouped column chart presents the highest value of only those whom I know for teenage males, teenage females, novice males, and females, professional males and females, and the lowest value for sending requests to anybody for all of them.
Fig. 3
Graph of criteria for sending friend request

level3.p: The analysis reveals an interesting trend when it comes to sending friend requests to strangers on online social networks (OSNs): across all categories, females show a lower tendency to do so. Notably, teenage girls within the study exhibited the highest level of caution, with a zero percent rate of sending friend requests to unknown individuals. This trend suggests that teenagers, particularly females, are more mindful of the potential risks associated with connecting with strangers online. Sharing information and granting access to one's social network can lead to privacy breaches and even security threats. By exercising caution and being selective about who they connect with, teenage girls are taking positive steps to protect themselves in the digital world.

The male and female both show equal interest in joining the groups on OSN. 65% of the Novice and teenagers are member of at least 2 groups in any of the OSN. However, the professionals are less interested in joining such groups.

p.span: Figure
shows the graph of average number of groups joined by the participant in teenager, novice, and professional category.

div.p: A grouped column chart of several groups joined versus none, 1, 2, and more than 2 presents the highest number of teenagers and novices joining more than 2 groups.
Fig. 4
Graph of average number of groups joined by the participant

level3.p: The graph paints a revealing picture: teenagers exhibit the least concern about exposing their private data through apps compared to other age groups. A higher percentage of teenagers run any app, suggesting a more relaxed attitude toward potential privacy risks. On the other hand, professionals display the highest level of caution. They are more reluctant to run such apps, indicating a stronger awareness of the potential threats to their personal data. This difference may stem from the greater maturity and experience professionals have accumulated regarding privacy and security concerns.

Only a slim minority of users, around 9%, believe that sharing confidential information or photos on online social networks (OSNs) is entirely secure. This highlights a significant level of concern about online privacy across different age groups and experience levels. Leading the charge are teenagers and professionals, with a substantial 67.21% and 72.16% of users, respectively, expressing skepticism about the security of sensitive content on OSNs. Novice users follow closely behind, with 61.68% sharing similar concerns. Interestingly, the remaining participants hold a neutral stance on the matter, suggesting either a lack of awareness of the potential risks or a more nuanced understanding of the complexities of online privacy.

p.span: In this work, it is tried to analyze the privacy and security awareness of Indian population while using the online social network.

li.p: Facebook reigns supreme as the preferred online social network (OSN) in the Indian population, with over 98% of participants choosing it as their first choice. This highlights its widespread popularity and influence in the country's digital landscape.

However, when it comes to adding new friends, especially those unknown in real life, caution prevails. Analysis reveals a notable difference between genders across all age groups, with females generally less inclined to accept friend requests from strangers compared to males.

This trend suggests that Indian women, even within the digital realm of OSNs, demonstrate a preference for maintaining boundaries and prioritizing connections with individuals they know or have verified identities.
The results of survey conducted in four different universities in USA 23% of the participants using Facebook are not concerned with Facebook privacy.

While some participants (50%) actively share photos or messages of others, others prioritize privacy and exercise caution when disclosing personal information or credentials. This highlights a diverse range of comfort levels with online sharing among users.

A significant portion (50%) reported an increase in spam and phishing messages after joining OSNs. This emphasizes the potential risks associated with online activity and the need for awareness about digital security measures.

The observation about increased caution among both Indian and foreign females suggests a universal concern for privacy and security, regardless of nationality. This highlights the importance of accessible privacy settings and clear terms of service across platforms.

The potential for changes in privacy and security awareness due to platform popularity, updates, and new features emphasizes the dynamic nature of online behavior. It underlines the need for ongoing research and education to keep pace with evolving trends and ensure user safety.

level3.p: The valuable insights gleaned from this work will serve as critical building blocks for shaping the security framework of online social networks (OSNs). The findings, particularly those related to privacy and security awareness among the Indian population, can also be leveraged for comparative analysis across diverse global demographics. Such comparisons can offer valuable cross-cultural perspectives and inform the development of inclusive and adaptable security solutions that cater to the needs of users worldwide.

level2.h2: Digital HR Strategy in the Modern Business Environment of Ukraine in Conditions of Uncertainty

p.span: Liudmyla Shchetinina
Svetlana Rudakova
Nataliia Danylevych
and
Oksana Poplavska
