level2.h2: Leveraging Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) for Enhanced Sentiment Analysis

p.span: Aqsa Kazi
Gunjan Kumar
and
Renuka Agrawal

div.p: Department of Computer Science and Information Technology, Symbiosis Institute of Technology, Symbiosis International (Deemed University), Pune, India

level3.p: Renuka Agrawal

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: This study investigates how Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) can revolutionize sentiment analysis in a variety of fields. Sentiment analysis models can now capture complex emotions and context, thanks to BERT's contextual understanding and cutting-edge machine learning techniques like transfer learning. We group models according to domains and learning paradigms and then display their results. By bridging the gap between human expression and computer comprehension, BERT can transform the precision and nuance of sentiment analysis. This study clarifies the value of BERT in improving sentiment comprehension and provides information for wise decision-making. BERT is an open-source machine learning platform for Natural Language Processing (NLP). BERT aims to help computers understand the meaning of ambiguous words in the text by using the surrounding neighboring text to construct meaning.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Sentiment analysis acts as a link between the subtleties of human expression and the sophisticated computational capabilities of contemporary AI within the broad field of natural language processing (NLP). The complex effort of automatically identifying and extracting emotional sentiments and attitudinal context inherent within textual data is a part of this process [

]. Sentiment analysis offers a profound understanding of the various thoughts, feelings, and sentiments expressed by individuals or groups by unraveling the complex fabric of human language. Various industries have acknowledged the crucial part sentiment analysis plays in influencing public policy, marketing tactics, decision-making processes, and customer experiences. The insights gained through sentiment analysis enable organizations, decision-makers, and researchers to uncover the underlying themes in public sentiment, guiding their work in a direction that is consistent with the general sentiment pulse.

The introduction of pre-trained language models like Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) and the convergence of cutting-edge machine learning techniques have given sentiment analysis previously unheard-of capabilities beyond imagination [

]. With its cutting-edge architecture and contextual awareness, BERT has raised sentiment analysis to new heights by demonstrating outstanding performance across a wide range of fields. Sentiment analysis models can distinguish all different shades of emotion, levels of positivity or negativity, and even irony or sarcasm woven into the text because of their capacity to capture context, semantics, and the subtleties of language usage [

]. Understanding sentiments enables NLP practitioners and academics to access an endless reservoir of knowledge that extends beyond words to explore emotions, intentions, and interpersonal connections [

level3.p: This survey paper includes many aspects of sentiment analysis models constructed on top of BERT in the pages that follow. The parts that follow will explain the importance of domains, examine the research environment, and provide a comprehensive knowledge of how BERT has turned sentiment analysis into a dynamic and useful tool for understanding the nuances of human sentiment including e-commerce, social media, finance, healthcare, and more.

p.span: Due to the inherent difficulties presented by context, sarcasm, and cultural variations, standard sentiment analysis methods frequently fail to capture the richness and nuance of human language, despite its importance. At its core, sentiment analysis entails the application of computational techniques to assess the polarity of text, which can be categorized as positive, negative, or neutral. The ultimate objective is to discern the underlying emotional disposition of the writer or speaker. This process surpasses mere keyword identification, necessitating a profound grasp of contextual intricacies and linguistic subtleties. Sentiment analysis draws from a diverse spectrum of disciplines, encompassing linguistics, machine learning, and psychology [

div.p: Rule-Based Approaches: These methodologies rely on predefined rules and lexicons to attribute sentiment scores to words and phrases. The collective sentiment of a text segment is determined by aggregating these scores.

Machine Learning: Machine learning methodologies involve training models on labeled datasets to predict sentiment in new, unlabeled text. Notable algorithms encompass support vector machines, Naive Bayes, and neural networks.

Deep Learning: Deep learning, especially through neural networks, has gained prominence due to its aptitude for capturing intricate linguistic nuances. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and transformer models have achieved cutting-edge outcomes in sentiment analysis.
Hybrid Approaches: These strategies integrate rule-based techniques with machine learning methodologies to hitch the strengths of both approaches, achieving heightened accuracy and adaptability.

p.span: All these approaches of sentiment analysis have been summarized in Fig.

div.p: A diagram represents a conceptual framework for sentiment analysis. It includes the following stages, rule based approaches, machine learning, deep learning, and hybrid approaches.
Fig. 1
Discrete approaches of sentiment analysis

level3.p: Sentiment analysis makes use of language technology to extract emotional information from written content. This process improves text engagement, helps with decision-making, and understands public sentiment. The field has the potential to be more precise and insightful because of ongoing technological advancements that offer a deeper understanding of human emotions expressed in text.

p.span: By solving these issues, the development of sophisticated machine learning methods and pre-trained language models like Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) has created new opportunities for sentiment analysis. Although BERT demonstrates impressive contextual knowledge and the capacity to capture semantic nuances, its successful incorporation into sentiment analysis models comes with its own set of difficulties [

level4.h4: 2.1 Exploring BERT-Driven Models Across Various Domains

p.span: Diverse machine learning models have arisen across a range of sentiment analysis areas, with BERT playing a crucial part in their development. With the help of BERT's capabilities, these models are customized for certain situations. A BERT-based classifier in charge of the analysis of product reviews makes use of neural networks and transfer learning inside a supervised framework. For a comprehensive understanding of the constantly evolving social discourse, social media sentiment analysis uses a BERT-based LSTM model that blends semi-supervised learning, recurrent neural networks (RNNs), and word embeddings (Fig.

div.p: A flow diagram. It includes text input, B E R T, happy, sad, anxious, and depressed.
Fig. 2
Sentiment classification using BERT

p.span: BERT fine-tuning analyses subtle emotions in movie and entertainment reviews by combining deep learning and transfer learning. Using domain-specific BERT models, it integrates with sentiment analysis pipelines in finance to provide deeper insights. BERT-equipped models with attention mechanisms and natural language understanding are used in healthcare applications to extract valuable information from patient comments. Convolutional neural networks and CNN models based on BERT are used in political sentiment analysis to comprehend voter attitudes. BERT's expertise is applied to a variety of domains through cross-domain sentiment analysis, which investigates aspect-based and multilingual sentiment analysis [

]. BERT-enabled emotion recognition deciphers sentiments and pinpoints underlying emotions. With explainable AI, bias-aware sentiment analysis improves sentiment forecasts while addressing fairness and transparency [
]. These models demonstrate how BERT-enabled advanced machine learning is combined with domain-specific applications.

In the realm of natural language processing (NLP), Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) has emerged as a new architecture, altering how language models comprehend and process textual data [

]. The transformer architecture, which uses a self-attention mechanism to capture intricate links between words in a phrase, is the foundation upon which BERT is created. By using this special method, BERT can consider both the words that come before and after them, creating rich, contextualized representations. Each layer of the BERT encoder stack, which consists of several layers, is outfitted with feedforward neural networks and multi-head self-attention mechanisms. A key component of BERT's architecture, the self-attention mechanism, allows each word in a sequence to pay attention to every other word, collecting dependencies and patterns that add to the context [

]. BERT's bidirectional approach takes into account all words at once, in contrast to conventional models that read text from left to right or right to left. BERT is ideally suited for a variety of language understanding tasks since these embeddings are enhanced with a comprehensive awareness of both local and distant contexts as shown in Fig.

div.p: A chart illustrates computer architecture components such as encoder, add and norm, feedforward, and multi head attention, arranged in n layers, culminating in a concatenation of two sentences.
Fig. 3

p.span: BERT model [

level3.p: The core of BERT's capabilities is pre-training. BERT learns contextualized embedding during pre-training by anticipating masked words in a sizable, unlabeled corpus. BERT is given an innate understanding of language structures and semantics thanks to this unsupervised learning. Then, during the fine-tuning stage, the pre-trained weights of the BERT are tailored to tasks using supervised learning on labeled data. The model's task performance is improved by carefully adjusting the model's parameters to recognize task-specific patterns.

p.span: The influence of BERT on sentiment analysis is significant. Its contextual embeddings make it possible to comprehend sentiment cues scattered across a sentence in greater detail, which improves the accuracy of sentiment categorization. Beyond sentiment analysis, BERT has revolutionized a number of NLP tasks, including text synthesis, question answering, and language translation [

]. The architecture's flexibility in accommodating many domains and languages highlights this. BERT is being further optimized for certain applications, its functionality is being expanded to low-resource languages, and different approaches that take into account domain-specific nuances are being investigated.

div.p: Sentiment Analysis: The sentiment represented in a text, such as whether it is favorable, negative, or neutral, may be identified using BERT. It aids in deciphering and classifying the tone of online comments, reviews, and consumer feedback.

Named Entity Recognition (NER): BERT can correctly recognize and categorize named entities in a text, including names of persons, businesses, locations, expressions of times, amounts, monetary values, percentages, etc. This is essential for applications like information extraction.

Question Answering: BERT may be used to build systems that can answer questions by extracting the answer from the context after being provided a query and some other information. Customer support software, information retrieval systems, and chatbots all make advantage of this.
Text Classification: Using predetermined classes or categories, BERT can classify and categorize text. Applications like topic classification, sentiment analysis, and spam detection leverage this.
Language Translation: BERT is a language translation tool that aids in translating text from one language to another by comprehending the context and semantics of the source material.

Summarization: BERT can help in text summary by reducing lengthy stretches of text into more manageable, logical summaries while retaining the key points. This is helpful for summarizing news, documents, etc.

Text Generation: In applications like tale generation, poetry production, or content generation for multiple platforms, BERT may be utilized as a basis model for producing imaginative and cohesive text.
Semantic Similarity: BERT can calculate how semantically related two texts are to one another. This is used for several things, including question paraphrasing and duplication detection.

level3.h3: 5 Significance of BERT for Sentiment Analysis

p.span: BERT, or Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, has transformed sentiment analysis, bringing a seismic shift in how we analyze emotions in textual data. BERT's bidirectional architecture considers the words before and after, fostering a comprehensive understanding of context. This ability uncovers sarcasm, emotional subtleties, and intensity levels while decoding sentiments and layers of meaning. It bridges the gap between machine comprehension and human expression, recognizing that emotions often depend on linguistic context. BERT elevates sentiment analysis to view sentiments as intricate layers of human emotions, moving beyond simplistic positive or negative assessments [
]. Its contextual knowledge now extends to various fields, making it a transformational tool with precision, depth, and value.

level3.p: BERT's significance extends beyond its architecture to its vast pre-trained knowledge. It comprehends language across domains due to exposure to extensive text data. This adaptability allows BERT to excel in various sentiment analysis applications, from dissecting complex financial reports to assessing healthcare responses. BERT's integration of advanced machine learning methods, including neural networks, transfer learning, attention mechanisms, and deep learning, enhances its capacity to categorize emotions, comprehend complex contexts, and predict sentiments in multilingual environments.

level3.h3: 6 Methodology for BERT in Sentiment Analysis

p.span: The approach chosen for applying the Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) architecture in sentiment analysis entails a structured procedure that integrates both the pre-trained BERT features and the model's fine-tuning to achieve the best sentiment classification accuracy. A pictorial representation of the six steps described is done in Fig.
. The steps constitute part of proposed methodology for analysis of sentiments from COVID-19 tweets.

div.p: A flow diagram. It includes data repositories, data collection, fine tuning, testing the model, network classifier, classification, and analysis.
Fig. 4
Steps in sentiment analysis

p.span: : The first step is to gather an appropriate dataset for sentiment analysis, which may include labeled text samples annotated with sentiments. Preprocessing is done on the data to get rid of extraneous information, special characters, and irrelevant stuff. To ensure compatibility with BERT's input requirements, tokenization is used to divide the text into smaller chunks.

: As a core component, BERT's contextualized word embeddings, a byproduct of its massive unsupervised pre-training, are used. Based on a masked language model objective, these pre-trained embeddings anticipate masked words based on the context in which they occur. The model's comprehension of linguistic nuance is much improved by utilizing BERT's pre-trained weights.

: During the fine-tuning stage, BERT is modified to perform the unique purpose of sentiment analysis. The sentiment-labeled dataset is used to further train the pre-trained BERT model. To account for the number of sentiment classes in the dataset, the model's architecture is altered, particularly in the top layers. The model gains the ability to identify sentiment-specific patterns and correlations during fine-tuning.

: Tokenized words and segment embeddings are used to discriminate between distinct phrases in a single input and are translated from text samples into a BERT-compatible input format. To denote the start and end of phrases, specific tokens like [CLS] and [SEP] are used. By running the tokenized input through the model's encoder layers, BERT embeddings are created.

: The performance of the improved BERT model is assessed using a different validation dataset. Accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and area under the ROC curve are examples of common performance measurements. To further improve the model's performance, hyperparameter adjustment can be done.

: The final step entails deciphering the model's forecasts and assessing its advantages and disadvantages. Error analysis offers insights into situations that were incorrectly categorized and possible areas for improvement. You may use visualization tools to see how the model interprets the text's emotional cues.

level3.p: BERT has revolutionized sentiment analysis, fundamentally altering our approach to understanding sentiments in text. It equips models to grasp the intricacies of human expression, including context, subtleties, and nuances like sarcasm and irony. BERT's bidirectional modeling fosters contextual awareness, enabling a comprehensive exploration of text's nuanced layers. Its vast pre-trained knowledge expedites model development across domains, conserving time and resources. BERT's architecture, coupled with cutting-edge machine learning techniques like transfer learning and attention mechanisms, broadens sentiment analysis horizons. Models can identify sentiments, unravel emotions, capture nuances, and handle multilingual environments. BERT transcends binary sentiment classification, spurring precision, adaptability, and context-driven sentiment analysis. This transformation bridges computer comprehension with human language intricacies, empowering decision-makers, businesses, and researchers with deeper emotional insights for informed choices. In the era of sentiment analysis, BERT's precision in revealing human emotions has the potential to revolutionize industries, enhance strategies, and enrich experiences.

level2.h2: Securing the Future of Connected Cars: Investigating Vulnerabilities and Preventing Cyberattacks

p.span: Joy Winston James
and
Abdul Khadhar Jilani

div.p: University of Technology Bahrain, Salmabad, Bahrain

level3.p: Joy Winston James
(Corresponding author)
Email:
Abdul Khadhar Jilani

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: As the automotive industry continues to embrace the Internet of Things (IoT) technology, connected cars have become increasingly popular. However, this popularity has come with a significant downside: the growing threat of cyberattacks. This research provides a comprehensive analysis of the various types of attacks that connected cars are vulnerable to, such as remote access, denial-of-service, and malware attacks. The study also examines the methods of detecting and preventing these attacks, including intrusion detection systems, blockchain technology, and cloud computing. Furthermore, the research analyzes the future directions of connected car security, including the role of artificial intelligence and machine learning in securing connected cars, and the need for new security standards and regulations. Ultimately, the paper concludes that to ensure the security of connected cars, automakers and cybersecurity experts must collaborate to develop and implement stronger security protocols, increase public awareness of connected car security risks, and develop new security standards and regulations.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Connected cars, also known as smart cars, have become increasingly prevalent in recent years. These vehicles are equipped with a range of technologies that enable them to connect to the internet and other devices, providing drivers with real-time information and advanced features [

]. However, as with any technology, there are potential security vulnerabilities associated with connected cars that can leave them susceptible to hacking. The purpose of this survey article is to provide an overview of the current state of research on hacking connected cars. This article will discuss the various types of attacks that can be launched against connected cars, the potential impact of these attacks, and the methods currently in place for detecting and preventing them. Additionally, this article will provide examples of notable connected car hacks that have occurred in recent years, as well as future directions for improving connected car security. Overall, the goal of this article is to highlight the importance of continued research and development in the field of connected car security, in order to protect consumers and ensure the safe and secure operation of these vehicles [

Connected cars are vehicles that are equipped with advanced connectivity features, such as internet connectivity, ADAS, and infotainment systems, which allow for a more seamless driving experience. However, these features also introduce potential vulnerabilities and security risks [
]. Hackers can exploit these vulnerabilities to take control of a vehicle remotely, steal sensitive data, or cause accidents.

Research on hacking connected cars has identified several techniques used by attackers to exploit these vulnerabilities. These include attacks on wireless communication systems, such as Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, which can allow an attacker to intercept data or gain control of the vehicle [
]. Other techniques include attacks on the vehicle’s on-board diagnostic (OBD) system, which can allow an attacker to access sensitive data or take control of the vehicle’s functions [

Several types of attacks on connected cars have been reported in the literature. These include attacks on the vehicle’s engine control unit (ECU), which can allow an attacker to take control of the vehicle’s acceleration, braking, and steering systems. Other types of attacks include attacks on the infotainment system, which can allow an attacker to access personal data or even disable the vehicle’s brakes [

The existing regulatory framework for connected car security has been criticized for being inadequate. While some regulations exist, they are often focused on specific aspects of connected car security and may not be comprehensive enough to address all potential vulnerabilities [
]. As a result, stakeholders such as government agencies, automakers, and industry organizations have a critical role to play in ensuring the security of connected cars.

Finally, emerging trends and technologies related to connected cars, such as autonomous driving and machine learning, have the potential to further complicate the security landscape. For example, attacks on autonomous vehicles could have even more severe consequences than attacks on traditional connected cars, as they could put the lives of passengers at risk [

level3.p: Since then, there have been several other incidents of connected car hacking, including attacks on Tesla vehicles and vulnerabilities in the BMW Connected Drive systems. These incidents have further emphasized the need for improved security measures and more comprehensive regulations to protect against cyberattacks.

One proposed solution for improving connected car security is the use of blockchain technology. Blockchain can provide a decentralized, secure ledger that could be used to store and authenticate data related to connected cars. This could help prevent tampering with data and provide an audit trail of all transactions related to the vehicle.

An alternative approach involves the application of machine learning algorithms for the real-time detection and response to cyberattacks. Machine learning serves the purpose of recognizing unusual behavioral patterns within the network, thereby notifying security personnel of potential threats. Additionally, it can be harnessed to create predictive models of cyberattacks, thus establishing a proactive defense mechanism. The prevailing body of literature addressing the vulnerability of connected cars to hacking underscores the imperative need for ongoing research and the formulation of robust security protocols against cyberattacks. This research endeavor, by expanding upon the existing knowledge base, seeks to make a meaningful contribution toward the development of effective security measures for connected cars, with the ultimate goal of averting potential cyberattacks on these vehicles.

p.span: Remote exploits: Hackers can target the software and firmware in a connected car’s electronic control units (ECUs) through vulnerabilities in the car’s software, enabling them to take control of various functions and systems within the car. This type of attack was demonstrated in the 2015 Jeep Cherokee hack, in which hackers were able to remotely control the car’s steering, brakes, and transmission through its entertainment system [

Man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacks: MITM attacks involve intercepting and manipulating data transmitted between a car and other devices, such as a driver’s smartphone. This can allow an attacker to access sensitive data, such as the car’s location or the driver’s personal information. MITM attacks have been demonstrated in multiple research studies, including one that used a Raspberry Pi to intercept and alter the data transmitted between a car’s remote app and its server [

Denial-of-service (DoS) attacks: DoS attacks can be used to overwhelm a car’s network or communication systems, causing them to become unresponsive or crash. This type of attack has been demonstrated in research studies, including one in which researchers were able to disrupt a car’s cellular network connection and cause it to lose GPS signal [

Physical attacks: Physical attacks involve gaining physical access to a connected car’s components or systems, such as by tampering with its sensors or wiring. These attacks can be more difficult to execute than remote attacks, but they can also be more dangerous and have a greater potential impact. Physical attacks have been demonstrated in research studies, including one in which researchers were able to manipulate a car’s braking system by tampering with its sensors [

level3.p: Phishing attacks: Phishing attacks are not typically associated with connected cars, as they are more commonly used to target personal information such as login credentials or financial data. However, it is possible for attackers to use phishing tactics to gain access to a connected car system by tricking the user into clicking a malicious link or providing sensitive information. For example, an attacker could send an email or text message posing as a legitimate car manufacturer or dealership and ask the user to click on a link to update their car’s software or firmware. However, the link may actually lead to a fake website or malware download that compromises the car’s security.

level4.p: Malware attacks can cause serious safety risks for drivers and passengers, as well as the potential for theft or unauthorized access to personal information.

p.span: One type of malware attack is ransomware, where the attacker encrypts important files and demands payment to unlock them. In a connected car, this could include the control systems, rendering the car inoperable until the ransom is paid. Another type is Trojan malware, where the attacker gains access to the car’s systems and steals personal information or manipulates the car’s behavior [

level4.p: Wireless attacks on connected cars involve exploiting vulnerabilities in the wireless communication channels used by the car’s systems to communicate with external devices. These attacks can range from intercepting wireless communications to injecting malicious code into the car’s systems.

Another type of wireless attack is known as a replay attack. In a replay attack, an attacker intercepts wireless communications between the car and an external device, such as a key fob, and then replays those communications at a later time to gain unauthorized access to the car. This type of attack can be used to steal a car or to gain access to its systems.

p.span: Finally, attackers can also use jamming attacks to disrupt wireless communications between the car and external devices. Jamming attacks involve transmitting signals that interfere with the wireless communications used by the car’s systems, making it difficult or impossible for the car to communicate with external devices [
Table
illustrates the real-world impact of different types of attacks on connected cars, highlighting the need for comprehensive security measures to protect against them.

div.p: Summary of attacks

td.p: Exploiting a vulnerability in a car’s software to execute unauthorized code
In 2015, security researchers remotely took control of a Jeep Cherokee through its infotainment system, demonstrating the potential for physical harm to the driver and passengers (Greenberg, 2015)
Overwhelming a car’s systems with requests to disrupt or disable them
In 2020, a security researcher was able to crash the Tesla Model X’s infotainment system by sending it a malformed Bluetooth message (Heath, 2020)
Gaining access to a car’s systems through physical means, such as by connecting to the car’s OBD-II port or by stealing a key fob
In 2019, a group of thieves used a relay attack to steal a Tesla Model S without needing the key fob
Trick drivers into revealing sensitive information, such as login credentials or personal data
In 2018, researchers at Kaspersky Lab discovered a phishing campaign that targeted car owners with fake emails from a popular auto manufacturer, attempting to steal their login credentials
Infecting a car’s software or firmware to gain control of the car’s systems or steal sensitive data
In 2016, researchers discovered a piece of malware called “autopilot” that was capable of infecting cars’ infotainment systems, potentially allowing attackers to take control of the car
Exploiting vulnerabilities in a car’s wireless communication systems, such as its Bluetooth or Wi-Fi connections
In 2018, researchers demonstrated that they could remotely hack into a Tesla Model S’s autopilot system by spoofing a Bluetooth signal

level3.h3: 4 Methods of Detecting and Preventing Attacks
level3.p: Regular software updates: Car manufacturers and software vendors should provide regular updates to address known vulnerabilities and patch any security holes. These updates can be automatically delivered to the car over the air (OTA) or through physical updates to the car’s software. According to a report by the Ponemon Institute, 53% of car manufacturers and 35% of suppliers currently provide OTA updates to their vehicles.

Network security: The car’s network should be protected by firewalls, intrusion detection and prevention systems (IDS/IPS), and other network security measures. This can include isolating critical systems from non-critical systems, segmenting the network to limit the scope of any potential breach, and monitoring network traffic for unusual activity. According to a report by Intel Security, 32% of car manufacturers and 47% of suppliers have implemented network segmentation to secure their vehicles.

Encryption: Encryption should be used to protect data in transit between the car’s various systems, including the infotainment system, the engine control unit, and other critical systems. This can include using strong encryption algorithms and key management practices to prevent unauthorized access to sensitive data. According to a report by Frost & Sullivan, 80% of car manufacturers are planning to increase their use of encryption to protect connected car data.

Secure boot: Secure boot is a process that verifies the integrity of the car’s software before it is loaded, helping to prevent unauthorized code from running. This can involve verifying the digital signature of the software and checking that it has not been tampered with. According to a report by SBD Automotive, 78% of new cars sold in 2021 will have secure boot technology.

Access controls: Access to the car’s systems should be tightly controlled to prevent unauthorized access. This includes physical access controls, such as limiting access to the car’s diagnostic port, as well as access controls for wireless and wired connections. This can include implementing strong authentication mechanisms and limiting the scope of access to specific users or systems. According to a report by the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity, access control is one of the most important security measures for connected cars.

Intrusion detection: Intrusion detection systems can monitor the car’s network for suspicious activity and alert security personnel to potential attacks. This can include using behavioral analytics to detect anomalous activity or signatures of known attacks, as well as identifying known malicious IP addresses or other indicators of compromise. According to a report by the Ponemon Institute, 55% of car manufacturers and 45% of suppliers have implemented intrusion detection systems in their vehicles.

Behavioral analytics: Behavioral analytics can be used to identify anomalous behavior on the car’s network, helping to detect attacks in real-time. This can include monitoring the car’s network traffic and user behavior to identify patterns that may indicate a compromise, as well as identifying suspicious behavior by drivers or passengers. According to a report by ABI Research, behavioral analytics is one of the most promising security technologies for connected cars.

p.span: User education: Drivers should be educated about the risks of connected cars and how to protect themselves from attacks, including the importance of keeping their car’s software up-to-date and avoiding suspicious emails or websites. This can include providing training materials and guidance on how to secure the car’s network, as well as raising awareness about common attack vectors and how to avoid them [

level3.h3: 5 Future Directions in Connected Car Security
level3.p: There are several future directions for connected car security research. Firstly, developing and implementing stronger security protocols is essential to protect against potential threats. Multi-factor authentication, advanced encryption techniques, and up-to-date software and firmware are some examples of effective security protocols that can be implemented in connected cars. These protocols can prevent unauthorized access to vehicle systems and data, reducing the risk of malicious activities.

Secondly, collaboration between automakers and cybersecurity experts is necessary to develop and implement effective security measures. Automakers can partner with cybersecurity firms to perform regular security assessments and testing, as well as sharing information and best practices to improve overall security. This collaboration can ensure that automakers are aware of emerging threats and take the necessary steps to address them proactively.

Thirdly, increasing public awareness of connected car security risks can help consumers make informed decisions about how they use their connected car and take appropriate precautions to protect their personal information and safety. Awareness campaigns can inform the public about the potential security risks associated with using a connected car and provide guidance on how to protect themselves.

Fourthly, incorporating artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) into connected car security systems can help to detect and prevent potential threats in real-time. These technologies can identify patterns and anomalies in system behavior, alerting the system to potential threats and taking appropriate action to mitigate them.

Finally, developing new security standards and regulations can ensure that all vehicles meet minimum security requirements. Working with regulatory bodies to establish guidelines and requirements for connected car security, as well as developing industry-wide standards for security testing and certification can help to maintain the security of connected cars.

Hacking connected cars is a serious and growing threat that requires constant attention and proactive measures to ensure the security of these vehicles. As the automotive industry continues to integrate IoT technology into their cars, the potential attack surfaces for cybercriminals continue to expand. To address these vulnerabilities, automakers and cybersecurity experts must work together to develop and implement stronger security protocols and collaborate on security assessments and testing. Increasing public awareness of connected car security risks, incorporating AI and ML technologies into security systems, and developing new security standards and regulations are all crucial steps toward securing connected cars. By taking a proactive approach to connected car security, we can ensure that these vehicles remain safe and secure for everyone.

level2.p: These days, security and safety are top priorities. Whether at home or at business, people and organizations can utilize security measures to safeguard their property. Many different sensors are used in video surveillance cameras in modern security systems. This initiative seeks to provide you with suggestions on how to make sure that your property is protected and secure. When this technology recognizes an unfamiliar face from a photograph of an unknown face, it is doing facial recognition as an authentication method. In this paper, we suggest using the structural similarity index measure (SSIM), Tkinter, local binary pattern histogram (LBPH), and Haar cascade classifier algorithms to provide intrusion detection for rational closed-circuit TV (CCTV). CCTV systems that use intrusion detection do more than merely capture and edit real-time footage and this can be used in the goal of this project to create open-source code. Intelligent tools that help individuals or organizations create by themselves; it is an inexpensive and safe system. This will give them take full control of your device to protect your settings and customize it according to your needs.

level3.p: Keywords

While image processing uses digital technology, the development of the technology connected with processing using a computer is already advancing quickly. As technology develops, visual images are extensively used to promote human welfare. Digital image processing is inextricably linked to the act of creating the picture. The majority of the CCTV footage is used for buffering. CCTV footage is utilized to identify the offender in case there are any problems or events. CCTVs appear to be unable to identify a criminal; they can only record events or situations. Nowadays, CCTVs are deployed practically everywhere. They are exclusively employed to record live video occurring within its boundaries.

p.span: An intelligent surveillance system solves all the problems unique to CCTV. It uses computer vision and sensors to detect what is happening and what needs to be done about it. All this happens in real-time to save money. Detect, prevent, and report intruders—when intruders are about to occur. Monitor CCTV when something interesting happens. Reduce Internet bandwidth by 1/100 compared to continuous monitoring [

]. Keep backups of important records safe in the cloud at all locations and connect and control assets remotely. Collect data from across the enterprise, advanced reporting, cost part of traditional monitoring solutions, and we can include different kinds of features such as monitoring for a particular object, in and out detection, and motion detection.

level3.p: The term “surveillance” was coined by combining two words. Sur means “from above,” and veiler means “to look.” In order to increase the security of events and various activities, video surveillance started using analog CCTV systems to monitor people, but the task of threat detection was left only to human operators. The smart surveillance recognizes situations in the video automatically and sets off alarms. Smart surveillance systems are being increasingly impacted by smart cameras. Smart refrigerators, smart clocks, smart monitoring systems, and intelligent systems are already transforming technology.

p.span: Smart surveillance systems include an Internet connection, enabling users to view real-time video through a mobile app, get alerts when alarms go off, and operate the system from a distance. There was a time when only malls and retail centers used surveillance methods. Electric circuit televisions [
] are now present in practically every location, from modest houses and places of worship to tiny cheap businesses. They, therefore, offer more security at a reduced cost.

level3.p: Examining the background of the current project is the main goal of performing a literature study since it enables us to spot flaws in the system in place and highlight unresolved issues. There are problems that need to be solved. Because of this, the following themes reveal more than simply the project's history; they also highlight the problems and shortfalls that led to the creation of this project.

level4.h4: 2.1 A Hybrid Algorithm for Tracking and Counting Many Persons in Smart Surveillance

p.span: In order to increase accuracy and decrease error, we have used a hybrid approach for people verification that combines skin detection and body part detection. The original image’s mask has been constructed during the skin detection process and applied to the detected objects to turn them into RGB images. This RGB image is subsequently transformed to the YCbCr domain, and each object’s skin pixels are identified using their pixel values. Each silhouette’s head, hands [
], and feet are removed for part detection, and the distances between them are measured and used to determine whether the object is actually a human.

level4.h4: 2.2 Performance Evaluation of the Intelligent Traffic Surveillance System’s Object Detection Algorithm
level4.p: The regions’ objects are found using deep convolutional neural networks (DNNs). In the proposed implementation, class labels are predicted by the CNN algorithm, and objects are detected. The implementation methodology includes data collection. A vehicle detection dataset for city roadways was selected.

level4.h4: 2.3 Crowd Counting and Detection of Normal/Abnormal Events Using Multi-Person Tracking in a Smart Surveillance System

p.span: In this study, we created a modern, reliable smart surveillance to track situations of normal/abnormal event detection and multiple people crowding counts using a monocular camera [
]. Using normalized cross-correlation and the Jaccard similarity metric, template matching is used to keep track of and count the participants.

level4.h4: 2.4 Raspberry Pi-Based IoT-Based Smart Surveillance and Security System
level4.p: The most recent and efficient tools for preventing theft and robbery are video surveillance and a monitoring system. But for other people, the cost of installation and upkeep for these systems becomes prohibitive. When its Arducam mini-module is connected, the Arduino Mega 2560 microcontroller board might be used as a camera system.

level4.h4: 2.5 Intelligent Video Security Surveillance with Mobile Remote Control
level4.p: The paper’s objective is to define a low-processing-power safeguarding alarm system for the Internet of Things. The image processing, as well as the strategies used for attention and display of the captured images, are controlled by a Raspberry Pi the size of a MasterCard that makes use of Open-Source Computer Vision (OpenCV) software.

p.span: The LBPH uses five parameters:
: The radius is the distance encircling the center and is used to construct circular local binary patterns.
: The normal setting is 1.

: The quantity of sample points required to form a circle with a local binary pattern. Keep in mind that the computational cost increases as you insert more sample points. Usually, it is set to eight.

: The quantity of cells along the axis’s horizontal direction. The grid is tighter and the dimensionality of the resulting feature vector is higher with a rise in cell count. The default setting is eight.

: The number of cells along the vertical axis. The grid is tighter and the dimensionality of the resulting feature vector is higher as the number of cells increases. Eight is the initial setting. The radius and neighbors of the parameter support the idea of a window, which is used by the method to do this.

level2.h2: Hyperspectral Band Selection Considering Spectral and Spatial Information

p.span: Thogarcheti Hitendra Sarma
Haseeba Yaseen
Nikhita Balagoni
and
Maram Tanmayee

div.p: Vasavi College of Engineering, Hyderabad, India

level3.p: Thogarcheti Hitendra Sarma
(Corresponding author)
Email:
Haseeba Yaseen

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: Feature extraction or selection (also known as band selection (BS) for hyperspectral images) can be used to create dimension reduction approaches for hyperspectral images. A hyperspectral image’s extracted features have minimal physical relevance; however, the chosen features (or bands) can maintain the original spectral information. As a result, band selection is increasingly frequently utilized for hyperspectral images. In this article, we offer three band selection approaches: Spectral angle mapper with spatial coherence (SAMSC), morphological profiles with spatial similarity (MPSS), and multi-feature similarity measure (MFSM). For band selection, our suggested approaches incorporate spectral and spatial information from hyperspectral images.

level3.p: Keywords

level2.p: Band selection (BS)Spectral angle mapper with spatial coherence (SAMSC)Morphological profiles with spatial similarity (MPSS)Multi-feature similarity measure (MFSM)Hyperspectral images (HIS)

level3.p: Hyperspectral sensors capture the spectral responses of objects to a variety of small electromagnetic bands with varying wavelengths, resulting in a hyperspectral image. HSI data constitutes a 3D data cube that may be thought of as a collection of 2D pictures and 1D spectral response curves. They have found widespread application in biological analysis, medical imaging, and environmental monitoring. However, processing and analyzing hyperspectral data can be computationally expensive and challenging due to the high dimensionality of the data. Each pixel in a hyperspectral image contains a spectrum with hundreds or thousands of bands, and this can result in a large amount of data to be processed. The high dimensionality of the data also makes it difficult to analyze and interpret, especially if there are many irrelevant or redundant bands. Another factor that can reduce classification accuracy is the presence of noise and atmospheric effects in the data. Hyperspectral sensors may be sensitive to environmental factors such as clouds, haze, or aerosols, which can affect the spectral signature of the observed objects. This can make it difficult to accurately classify objects or identify subtle differences between different classes.

One approach to selecting relevant bands is based on ranking them according to their importance. This can be done by measuring the similarity between each band and the target class. The more similar a band is to the target class, the more important it is. However, existing methods for band ranking often suffer from limitations such as the lack of spatial information. Therefore, a new approach for choosing hyperspectral bands is needed. The paper aims to propose a new band selection method that addresses the challenges of processing hyperspectral data. The proposed methods use a similarity measure that takes into account both spectral and spatial information to select bands that capture relevant characteristics of the target class. The methods have an advantage of being simple and they do not require prior knowledge or assumptions about the data, and they can handle high-dimensional data easily. Additionally, the proposed methods have been demonstrated to outperform other approaches in terms of both classification accuracy and computational efficiency.

Hyperspectral imaging is a powerful technology that captures images in a variety of small and continuous spectral bands. Because the data is highly dimensional, the vast number of spectral bands in hyperspectral data makes data processing and interpretation difficult. The technique of hyperspectral band selection is used to minimize the dimensionality of hyperspectral data by selecting a subset of the most informative bands that contain the most important information for a certain application. However, because of the high dimensionality of the data and the enormous number of potential band combinations, selecting the appropriate subset of bands can be a difficult process. Various band selection strategies, including correlation-based feature selection, mutual information-based feature selection, and sequential forward selection, have been proposed to overcome this difficulty. These approaches, however, do not consider the interactions between the spectral bands and may result in inferior band selections. To address this limitation, we proposed three methods: the spectral angle mapper with spatial coherence (SAMSC), the morphological profiles with spatial similarity (MPSS), and the multi-feature similarity measure (MFSM), which take into account the pairwise similarity between spectral bands based on their spectral similarity and spatial coherence.

The proposed study aims to develop a band selection algorithm that can effectively identify the most relevant bands for hyperspectral image classification tasks. The proposed approaches aim to capture both spectral and spatial interactions between bands to select a more optimal subset of bands that can provide a comprehensive representation of the data. The method’s performance is evaluated on several hyperspectral datasets and compared with other band selection methods using various metrics such as overall accuracy, macro-precision, and macro-recall. The study provides a method that can effectively select informative bands in hyperspectral data analysis and demonstrates its efficacy on publicly available datasets like Indian Pines and Salinas.
In this section, we will go through different BS approaches. The BS approaches are classified as spectral-feature-based and spatial-spectral-feature-based.

level5.p: Spectra-Feature-Based BS Methods

p.span: Certain techniques for selecting the most informative bands from hyperspectral data for classification involve calculating various spectral features, such as mean, variance, and skewness of each band. A correlation-based feature selection (CFS) [

] method is then employed to identify the most informative features. Subsequently, a genetic algorithm (GA) is used to choose the optimal subset of bands based on the selected features. The proposed method has been assessed on various benchmark datasets and compared with other band selection methods. The results indicate that the proposed technique offers better classification accuracy while utilizing a smaller number of bands. Additionally, the authors have illustrated the effectiveness of the proposed method in practical scenarios like land-cover classification and urban materials mapping.
Minimum redundancy maximum relevance (MRMR) [

] is a technique for feature selection method that seeks to identify a subset of features that are highly correlated with the class label but have low redundancy among themselves. The method consists of three criteria: Max-dependency, max-relevance, and min-redundancy. The max-dependency criterion selects features with the highest mutual information with the class label. The max-relevance criterion chooses features that have the highest dependency with the class label while having the lowest dependency with the other selected features. The min-redundancy criterion removes redundant features by selecting a feature subset that maximizes the average mutual information between features and minimizes the average mutual information between features and the class label.
Chen et al. [

] suggest a technique for selecting bands from hyperspectral data that is based on principal component analysis (PCA). This approach involves utilizing PCA-based dimensionality reduction to extract the most informative features from the hyperspectral data. The authors employ a correlation-based feature selection (CFS) method to identify the most informative features. The selected bands are then classified using support vector machine (SVM).
Mutual information-based feature selection (MIFS) [

] is a feature selection method that is used to identify the most informative features from hyperspectral data. Zhang, Haibo, Tong, Lei, and Li, Jun apply a random forest classifier to evaluate the selected features and compare their performance with other feature selection techniques.
Recursive feature elimination (RFE) [

] involves recursively eliminating the least informative bands from the hyperspectral data until a desired number of informative bands is obtained. The authors use SVM classifier to evaluate the selected bands and use genetic algorithm to optimize the SVM parameters.
Sequential forward selection (SFS) using random forests and particle swarm optimization (PSO) [

] involves iteratively adding the most informative bands to the feature subset until a desired number of informative bands is obtained. Tong, L., Zhang, H., Li, J. use random forest classifier to evaluate the selected bands and use PSO to optimize the selection process.

level5.p: Spatial-Spectra-Feature-Based-BS Methods

p.span: Xu et al. [

] present a spectral-spatial feature extraction approach that integrates active learning and iterative band selection for hyperspectral image classification. The method has two main stages: iterative band selection and active learning. The iterative band selection stage involves using a new feature ranking method based on the Fisher discriminant ratio to rank the importance of each spectral band, with the most informative bands chosen iteratively until a specified number of bands is achieved. In the active learning stage, a support vector machine (SVM) is trained on the selected features and used to query the most informative samples for the next iteration.
Hybrid manifold learning algorithm [

] comprises two main steps: a hybrid manifold learning algorithm and a band selection algorithm. The first step involves using a combination of the Isomap algorithm and the Laplacian eigenmaps algorithm to learn the intrinsic structure of the hyperspectral data in both the spectral and spatial domains. In the second step, a modified version of the mRMR algorithm is used to select the most informative bands based on the learned manifolds. The proposed approach aims to select the most relevant bands by incorporating both spectral and spatial information.
The graph-regularized low-rank representation [

] method for band selection involves two steps: graph construction and band selection. Firstly, the method constructs a graph using both spectral and spatial information to capture the intrinsic structure of the hyperspectral data, by using a graph-regularized low-rank representation algorithm. This algorithm aims to learn a low-dimensional subspace representation of the data. Then, in the second step, a band selection algorithm is employed to choose the most informative bands based on the learned subspace representation. Specifically, Li, Wei, Li, Jun, and Guo, Changbao propose a sparse representation-based method for selecting the most informative bands.

level5.p: Jupyter Notebook, datasets, Internet connectivity.
Processor: Intel Pentium, RAM: 4 GB or more, GPU: 2 GB or more, hard disk.

p.span: Data preprocessing: The raw hyperspectral data is first pre-processed to reduce the number of pixels due to the complexity of the proposed work. Similarity computation: Pairwise similarity between the bands is then computed using a similarity metric, SAMSC, MPSS, and MFSM. This generates a similarity matrix that represents the similarity between each pair of bands. Selecting bands—Top
bands are selected based on similarity matrix. Scores are calculated using the similarity matrix and the
-highest bands are selected. Testing—To assess the classification results, we used RF, KNN, and DT classifiers and we used three indices, OA, macro-precision, and macro-recall.

Firstly, to reduce the pixel number in hyperspectral image data, the paper applies max pooling using a 2 × 2 filter, resulting in half the height and width but the same number of spectral bands. This is done by reshaping the data into a 2D matrix, applying the filter to each spectral band, and subsampling the resulting pooled data. The paper then uses three different band selection methods, SAMSC, MPSS, and MFSM, with varying values of
from 1 to 30. For each value of
, the paper selects the top

bands using the three methods and trains random forest, decision tree, and KNN classifiers on the selected bands. Finally, the paper computes and stores accuracy, precision, and recall metrics for each classifier in separate lists.
Dataset of 3D array and number of top

bands to be selected are passed to the function. Reshape the data to a 2D array (number of pixels × number of bands) to facilitate pairwise similarity calculation. Compute pairwise cosine similarity between spectral bands. Compute the minimum spanning tree (MST) of the similarity graph, which represents the most efficient way to connect all nodes (bands) with the minimum total edge weight (dissimilarity). Compute the spatial coherence matrix by iterating over each pixel and accumulating the dot product of each pair of adjacent bands (i.e., connected by an edge in the MST). Compute the SAM scores with spatial coherence by iterating over each pair of adjacent bands and calculating the dot product of their spatial coherence values and spectral vectors, divided by their Euclidean norms. Select the top
bands with the highest SAM scores.

Define the structuring element for the morphological operations. In this implementation, a disk-shaped structuring element with a radius of 3 is used. Compute the morphological profiles by subtracting the result of opening and closing operations from each band in the pooled data. Opening is used to preserve bright and thin features, while closing is used to preserve dark and thin features. Compute the spatial similarity matrix, which represents the Euclidean distance between the spatial coordinates of each pair of pixels. Cluster the pixels using
-means based on the concatenated feature vector of morphological profiles and spatial distances. Compute the variance of each band within each cluster. Select the top
bands with the highest variance within each cluster.

The function first reshapes the data into a 2D array of shape (number of pixels × number of bands) so that it can be used for feature selection using mutual information. Then, it uses the SelectKBest function from scikit-learns feature selection module with mutual info classif as the scoring function to select the top
bands. The mutual info classif function estimates the dependency between each band and the class labels to determine their relevance. Finally, it returns to the top
bands.

div.p: A block diagram has the following flow, original H S I layer, H S I after reducing number of pixels, similarity matrix via S A M S C, M P S S, and M F S M, and H S I after B S via top k bands.
Block diagram

p.span: In 1992, an Airborne Visible/Infrared Imaging Spectrometer (AVIRIS) sensor in the agricultural landscape of the northwestern Indiana’s Indian pine test site collected the Indian Pines dataset. The most often used image has 145 × 145 pixels and 200 spectral bands. The spatial resolution is approximately 20 m/pixel, and the spectral resolution is around 10 nm, with a spectral wavelength range of 400–2500 nm. The Indian Pines dataset utilized in this investigation included 185 bands after eliminating four zero bands, 20 poor bands disrupted by water absorption, and 15 severe noisy bands with low signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs). Because of the complexity of the proposed work, the pixel size is reduced from 145 × 145 to 72 × 72. AVIRIS collected the Salinas scene data collection in 1998. The Salinas dataset, like the Indian Pines dataset, has 224 bands with 10-nm widths and wavelengths ranging from 400 to 2500 nm. 204 bands were preserved after removing 20 bands of water absorption and poor SNR. The data was 512 × 217 pixels in size but had a spatial resolution of 3.7 m/pixel. 16 types of ground objects were developed for categorization purposes. Because of the complexity of the proposed work, the pixel size is reduced from 512 × 217 to 128 × 54.

div.p: Datasets

level4.p: Indian Pines dataset—For random classifiers, SAMSC and MFSM are superior to MPSS based on the OA. MFSM outperformed all the other methods based on macro-precision. MPSS outperformed all the other methods based on macro-recall. For KNN classifier, MPSS outperformed all the other methods based on the OA. MPSS outperformed all the other methods based on macro-precision. MPSS outperformed all the other methods on macro-recall. For decision tree classifier, SAMSC outperformed all the other methods based on the OA. SAMSC outperformed all the other methods based on macro-precision. MFSM outperformed all the other methods based on macro-recall.

p.span: Salinas Dataset—For random forest classifier, MFSM outperformed all the other methods based on the OA, macro-precision, and macro-recall. For KNN classifier, SAMSC outperformed all the other methods based on the OA. MPSS outperformed all the other methods based on macro-precision. MFSM outperformed all the other methods based on macro-recall. For DT classifier, MFSM outperformed all the other methods based on the OA. SAMSC outperformed all the other methods performed based on macro-precision. MFSM outperformed all the other methods for higher bands based on macro-recall. The values for OA, macro-precision, and macro-recall based on the random forest, KNN, and decision tree classifiers for both the datasets are low due to the reduction in the number of pixels (Figs.
and

div.p: 3 sets of 3 multi-line graphs of overall accuracy, macro precision, and macro recall versus the number of selected bands for R F, K N N, and D T classifiers, respectively. All 9 graphs follow an increasing and fluctuating trend with sharp peaks and dips.
OA, macro-precision, and macro-recall curves of Indian Pines dataset using the RF, KNN, and DT classifiers with different BS methods

3 sets of 3 multi-line graphs of overall accuracy, macro precision, and macro recall versus the number of selected bands for R F, K N N, and D T classifiers, respectively. They plot S A M S C, M P S S, and M F S M. All 9 graphs follow a fluctuating, increasing, and linear trend with sharp peaks and dips.
OA, macro-precision, and macro-recall curves of Salinas dataset using the RF, KNN, and DT classifiers with different BS methods

level3.p: The proposed work employed SAMSC, MPSS, and MFSM methods for selecting bands. In contrast to other methods that solely utilize spectral data, our approach integrates both spatial and spectral data. The results demonstrate that SAMSC outperforms the other two methods in terms of evaluation metrics.

In the proposed work, we used three methods SAMSC, MPSS, and MFSM for band selection. By applying these methods, we have observed that the accuracy of all these methods is low. As part of future scope, we can explore other feature selection methods to improve the accuracy of classification and reduce computational time. The proposed method can be further optimized to achieve better results.

level2.p: Irrigation system (IS) is considered as a crucial component in the human society. It plays a crucial role for the supply of water to the cultivation fields. So, it is very much essential to predict the water pumping requirement in IS. In this work, an Internet of Things (IoT)-assisted machine intelligence (MI)-based approach is proposed for the prediction of water pumping requirement in IS. This work is focused on the machine learning (ML)-based models such as random forest (RF), CatBoost (CB), K-nearest neighbors (KNN), and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) to perform the prediction. In this work, the water pumping requirement is monitored using IoT-assisted sensors. Here, the water pumping requirement and non-requirement cases are represented using 1 and 0, respectively. This work is carried out using cross-validation (CRV) by taking the number of folds (NFL) as 3, 5, and 10. These models are evaluated using classification accuracy (CA). This work is implemented using Python-based Orange 3.32.0.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: IS [

] is a major concern in the current scenario throughout the globe. It helps in supplying water to several cultivation fields. It has the crucial role to monitor the supply of proper amount of water to the field during several internals of time. So, it is an important task to track the water pumping requirement and not requirement status in IS for smooth monitoring of water supply to the cultivation fields.
Different techniques such as ML, deep learning (DL), IoT can be used to monitor water pumping requirement in IS in the current scenario. IoT [
] and ML [

] take a crucial role in monitoring water pumping requirement. IoT-assisted sensors can be used to collect the information regarding water pumping requirement. ML techniques can be categorized as supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement. In this work, IoT-assisted ML-based approach is considered as a solution to carry out the work related to water pumping requirement. This approach can help in predicting the water pumping requirement in IS. The water pumping requirement and non-requirement cases are represented using 1 and 0, respectively.

level3.p: The different MI-based models are RF, CB, KNN, SGD, support vector machine, decision tree, AdaBoost, XGBoost, etc. In this work, the MI-based models such as RF, CB, KNN, and SGD are used for classification mechanism. In this work, the CRV method is used. The results are analyzed by considering the scenario where the NFL is considered as 3, 5, and 10.

p.span: The key focus of this work is mentioned as follows:

li.p: This work is focused on IoT-assisted MI-based approach for the prediction of water pumping requirement in IS.
The water pumping requirement is monitored using IoT-assisted sensors.
In this work, different ML-based models such as RF, CB, KNN, and SGD are used to perform the prediction.
The water pumping requirement and non-requirement cases are represented using 1 and 0, respectively.
This work is carried out using CRV method by taking the NFL as 3, 5, and 10.
The ML-based models are compared using CA.
This work is implemented Python-based Orange 3.26.0.

p.span: Several works have been performed related to irrigation, agriculture and farming [
]. Some of the works are elaborated as follows. Munir et al. [
] focused on edge computing and IoT for intelligent as well as smart IS. Blessy et al. [
] focused on IoT and transfer learning process for sustainable irrigation requirement prediction. Aravinda et al. [
] focused on intelligent IS for agriculture by considering IoT and ML-based approaches. Saini et al. [
] emphasized on IoT-based approach for automatic irrigation control system. Mathi et al. [
] focused on IoT-based approach for smart farming. Vianny et al. [
] emphasized on IoT and ML for precision irrigation system using water optimization mechanism. Kasiselvanathan et al. [
] focused on IoT and ML-based approaches for agricultural management. Venkatesh et al. [

] emphasized on a smart framework by focusing on IoT and ML for precision agriculture. From the above analysis, it is observed that it is a challenging task to predict the water pumping requirement in IS accurately. So, further analysis is required by focusing on enhanced methodologies.

Different approaches can be used to classify the water pumping requirement and non-requirement in the IS. In this work, an IoT-assisted MI-based approach is used for the classification of water pumping requirement and non-requirement in the IS. Here, MI-based models such as RF, CB, KNN, and SGD are used to carry out such classification mechanism. Figure

describes the workflow diagram of the proposed work. Initially, the IoT-assisted sensor data is given as input to the Orange 3.32.0. Then, the MI-based models such as RF, CB, KNN, and SGD are used to carry out the classification of water pumping requirement and non-requirement in the IS by considering the CRV mechanism. In this work, for cross-validation, the NFL is considered as 3, 5, and 10. Test and score are used to analyze the performance of these models in terms of CA (in %). Here, the confusion matrix (CFM) is used to track the actual and predicted data for different models.

] which mainly focuses on Arduino board, soil moisture sensor, DHT11 sensor, and node micro-controller unit for collecting the data. This work is implemented using Python-based Orange 3.32.0 [
] by focusing on CRV principle. These data are processed using the MI-based models such as RF, CB, KNN, and SGD. These models are evaluated using CA performance metric.
This work focuses on the water pumping requirement prediction in IS in a better way. Here, Table
and Fig.
represent the CA for several models by considering the NFL value as 3, 5, and 10. The CFM for several models by considering the NFL as 3, 5, and 10 is mentioned in Figs.

, it is found that the CA (in %) of RF, CB, KNN, and SGD models at NFD = 3 is 98.5, 97.5, 97.5, and 97.0, respectively. The CA (in %) of RF, CB, KNN, and SGD models at NFD = 5 is 90.0, 98.0, 97.5, and 97.5 respectively. The CA (in %) of RF, CB, KNN, and SGD models at NFD = 10 is 99.5, 99.0, 97.5, and 97.0, respectively. In this scenario, the RF is able to perform better than other models by considering the NFL value as 3, 5, and 10. However, SGD is not able to perform better than RF, CB, and KNN by considering the NFL as 3 and 10. KNN and SGD attempt to provide similar classification results when NFL = 5.

level3.p: This work is focused on IoT-assisted MI-based approaches for the prediction of water pumping requirement in IS. In this work, several MI-based models such as RF, CB, KNN, and SGD are used for prediction. This work is carried out using CRV mechanism using the NFL as 3, 5, and 10. These MI models are evaluated using the CA performance metrics. From the results, it is observed that RF provides better classification results as compared to CB, KNN, and SGD and its CA value is 98.5, 99.0, and 99.5% when NFL is considered as 3, 5, and 10, respectively. However, SGD is not able to perform better as compared to other models when the NFL is taken as 3 and 10. KNN and SGD are able to provide similar performance when the NFL is considered as 5. This work can be used to develop improved methods to enhance the CA value by considering several scenarios. This work can also be extended to perform further analysis by considering other MI-based models.

level2.p: DDoS assaults use a number of prearranged computers to attack servers and network resources, flooding the system with messages, malformed packets, and connection requests that prevent the genuine user from accessing the system. To train a model for the purpose of identifying and categorizing the various kinds of DDoS attacks, this paper aims to design a hybrid algorithm that combines several machine learning techniques. Comparing the hybrid model to each of the individual machine learning techniques utilized, the hybrid method will be faster and more accurate. The current systems employ a variety of techniques to thwart DDoS attacks, such as CAPTCHA, which provides a straightforward strategy for attack mitigation. However, the system fails as a result of the DoS attack control inefficiencies revealed by recent studies. Other assaults, such as digital signature for network flow inquiry, which fails under standard DDoS attacks, and SEVEN, a different approach based on adaptive and selective verification, also fail below post-flooding attacks of HTTP since the massive number of indicators used to convey payloads. Consequently, in order to develop the algorithm more accurately, we are utilizing various machine learning algorithms.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Therefore, we are using multiple machine learning methods to construct the algorithm with improved accuracy. Cybercriminals can use denial-of-service (DoS) attacks to stop authorized apps from connecting to network infrastructure [

]. The goal of a denial-of-service (DoS) attack is to prevent authorized users from accessing the compromised machine network and information resources. DDoS attacks remain the hardest security threats to detect, stop, and monitor because to the limitations of popular networking tools, the diversity of attack techniques, and the host sites’ programming capabilities. Consequently, DDoS attacks fall into multiple categories, albeit they are typically divided into three groups. As shown in Fig.

, they are (i) traffic/fragmentation attack, (ii) bandwidth/volume attack, and (iii) application attack. With traffic-based assaults, the target system is inundated with a huge number of UDP or TCP packets, which slows down the system's operation [

]. The second type of attack, known as a bandwidth or volumetric attack, entails the attacker attempting to overload the target system with a significant number of anonymous data while simultaneously consuming more bandwidth than is required for legal purposes [
]. The last attack types, which exclusively target a particular system or network, are also specialized attacks. These attacks are more challenging to recognize and less easy to prevent [

div.p: A classification chart of the D D o S attacks. The types are traffic or fragmentation based attacks, bandwidth based attacks, and application attacks.
Fig. 1
Classification of DDOS attacks

p.span: A DDOS attack, often known as a DoS attack, is a deliberate effort made to prevent genuine users from accessing a website or application. Typically, this is accomplished by saturating the website or application with network traffic. Attackers directly target the target system with denial-of-service (DoS) attacks, also referred to as non-distributed directed attacks [

]. Theoretically, denial-of-service (DDoS) assaults are similar to DoS attacks, but they are distinct in that DDoS attacks implicitly involve several attack sources. These sources may consist of malware-infected PCs, routers, and Internet of things devices in a distributed setting [
] (Fig.

div.p: A flow diagram depicts how the bot master attacks the server via bot 1, bot 2, bot 3, bot 4, and bot 5.
Fig. 2
DDoS attack scenarios

p.span: The key benefit of the cloud is its ability to adapt to changing demands and provide an environment that scales up and down quickly; therefore, it requires exceptional security against DDoS attacks. These attacks have become more sophisticated and are still evolving quickly. The task of identifying and defending against these assaults has so grown challenging [

To create a hybrid algorithm that combines a number of machine learning approaches in order to train a model that is more accurate than every discrete machine learning technique employed in the crossbreed model in detecting and classifying the type of DDoS assault. The key benefit of the cloud is its ability to adapt to changing demands and provide an environment that scales up and down quickly; therefore, it requires exceptional security against DDoS attacks. These attacks have become more sophisticated and are still evolving quickly. The task of identifying and defending against these assaults has so grown challenging [

level3.p: To examine data and its parameters for any data redundancy that might have an impact on prediction outcomes. To eliminate all values that are empty or ambiguous. To compare the model's success in detecting DDoS attacks and use several classifier classes. Construct a hybrid model with the intention of offering superior model accuracy in comparison to existing models.

p.span: As a result, we are utilizing KNN, SVM, and Naive Bayes as well as other machine learning approaches to construct the algorithm and random forest to evaluate it. We used a single data set with twenty-three features in the suggested system, including source and destination addresses, packet ids, from node to node, packet types, packet sizes, and packet rates. Machine learning techniques such as SVM, KNN, random forest, GBT, neural network, and decision tree are used to classify values based on variables like source and destination addresses in order to detect DDoS attacks and determine the correctness of the model. The hybrid model will next be created by training the recently acquired data—also known as the validation input—using the random forest technique. The test input is transformed into final output predictions using this model. The accuracy of these forecasts is then ascertained by contrasting them with the actual test data (Fig.
and
