level3.p: As a result, the accuracy is determined by using the hybrid model to analyze the DDoS assault on the cloud computing environment.

p.span: First, the pertinent data is split into two categories: train data (70%) and test data (30%). After that, the train data is split again into the training set, which makes up 70% of the train data, and the validation set, which makes up 30% of the train data (Fig.

div.p: An architecture defines how the train data is split into training and validation sets by blending ensembling. They undergo S V M, K N N, and naive bayes algorithms to give validation and test inputs. They are combined under random forest to give a hybrid model.
Fig. 5
System architecture using hybrid approach

li.p: One sort of supervised machine learning called a decision tree divides the data continuously based on a specific parameter (you specify what the input is and what the related output is in the training data).

K-NN is, often known as KNN, is an abbreviation for “K-nearest neighbor.” It is an algorithm for supervised machine learning. The algorithm can handle problem statements with regression and classification. The number of nearest neighbors to a recently introduced unknown variable that needs to be predicted or categorized is represented by the letter “K.”

Random forests also known as random choice forests, are an ensemble learning method for classification, regression, and other problems. During the training phase, a large number of decision trees are constructed. For classification problems, the class chosen by the great majority of trees in a random forest yields the result.
Support vector machines, also referred to as SVMs, are supervised learning models that analyze data for regression and classification using learning methods.

Neural networks, also known as artificial neural networks (ANNs) or simulated neural networks (SNNs), are a branch of machine learning that forms the foundation of deep learning methods. They are named and structured after the human brain, and they are modeled after real neurons.

p.span: See Fig.

div.p: A worksheet of the dataset. It lists the i p source and destination, t c p source and destination, frame length, t c p flags, frame time, packets, bytes, transmitted packets, transmitted bytes, received packets, and received bytes.
Fig. 6
Data set

level4.p: This is the first stage in developing a machine learning model. The process at hand is to make the raw data match the machine learning model. The data set was imported into the model using the read_csv () function, which is used to read CSV files. The iloc[] method from the pandas package was used to extract both independent and dependent variables from the data set. The process of turning unprocessed data into something that a machine learning model can use is known as data preparation. It is the most important and initial step in building a machine learning model. It is not always the case that we are provided with clean, prepared data when working on a machine learning project. All the models in the hybrid approach are having the same accuracy for the given data set. Hence, we go for the calculation of the time complexities of the each model to find out an efficient algorithm.
By comparing the time complexities of each algorithm, a graph has been plotted to know about the efficient algorithm among all the algorithms.

p.span: Comparing the time complexities of each algorithm, SVM, neural network, and decision tree are the best and efficient algorithms to work out for the great accuracy and take less time for predicting and efficient detection of DDoS attacks. Among all algorithms, decision tree algorithm is faster and has higher accuracy (Fig.

div.p: A bar graph compares the time taken by the K neighbors random forest, support vector machine, neural network, gradient boosting, and decision tree algorithms. K neighbors algorithm has the highest value of 60, whereas the decision tree has the lowest value of 1. Data are approximate.
Fig. 7
Plotted model time complexities

level3.p: Cybercriminals frequently use DDoS assaults to their advantage by simply sending out ransom messages to a huge number of addresses. As a result, several methods and models must be utilized to stop the attacks. This hybrid model will increase the level of accuracy in DDoS attack prediction. Additionally, compared to the other models, it is more efficient. Using machine learning techniques, this notion is utilized to forecast DDoS in advance. Denial-of-service impact was identified, and by employing this hybrid approach, the flaw can be managed by taking the appropriate security measures. Series 1 denotes the initial testing of each model and calculation of its corresponding accuracies, as illustrated in the figure below. We can detect the accuracy of the hybrid model is upper than that of the individual models. A model's second and third series tests are denoted as Series 2 and Series 3, respectively. When compared to other models, the hybrid model has better accuracy for every test series.

level2.h2: A Novel Chaotic Map-Based Noise-Resistant Image Encryption Scheme for Medical Images

p.span: Shaik Kashif Hussain
and
S. Saheb Basha

div.p: Department of ECE, Jawaharlal Nehru Technological University, Ananthapuramu, Andhra Pradesh, India
Department of ECE, G. Pulla Reddy Engineering College, Jawaharlal Nehru Technological University, Ananthapuramu, Andhra Pradesh, India

level3.p: Shaik Kashif Hussain

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: In this work, the proposed method offers an image encryption technique that is robust against the impact of noise. When it comes to making a diagnosis, medical images are crucial. The images comprise sensitive diagnostic procedures such as X-rays, ultrasounds, brain scans, and MRIs of patients. Due to insecurities in the transmission and storage of these images in hospitals, unauthorized parties may get access to them. When it comes to safeguarding sensitive medical images from unauthorized parties, encryption is the effective approach. Bit-level encryption, as opposed to pixel-level encryption, was used, and it was performed by means of chaotic map-based encryption. The proposed work is divided into three parts: the first and third of which include spatial domain image encryption. The suggested approach utilizes frequency domain encryption using discrete wavelet transforms (DWT) as its core mechanism. This kind of encryption is known as a “sandwich encryption” because it employs two layers of spatial domain encryption and a third layer of frequency domain encryption. In addition, the proposed approach has analyzed the entropy statistics to determine how well the proposed system works. The cipher images generated by the proposed approach have much higher entropy values. Additionally, with the suggested encryption technique, both the unified average change intensity (UACI) and the number of pixels changed per second (NPCR) parameters are higher.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Rapid improvements in medical equipment technology have led to the widespread adoption of the use of medical images for the diagnosis of a variety of disorders. Digital image transmission must be safe, reliable, and genuine. Users’ right to privacy may be at risk if these images are used without their permission. As an added risk, a mistake might put patients’ lives in jeopardy if these images can be altered in any way. Digital pictures may be protected in a number of different ways, including via the use of cryptography [
], watermarking [
], and encryption [

]. When it comes to safeguarding image integrity, encryption is both the fastest and most reliable option. This uses a hidden key to convert a simple image into one that is more challenging to decipher. The original image cannot be retrieved without the hidden password. Diffusion and confusion are the two primary methods used in image encryption. Research into methods of protecting digital images, such as encryption and authentication, is therefore gaining prominence. There are many image encryption methods [
] that aim to decrease correlation between pixels. Image encryption employing a hybrid of chaos, hyper-chaos, and DNA sequence operation is performed in three stages in [

]. Some examples include the use of a key image to guide the shuffling of DNA bases and the use of a hyper-chaotic sequence to determine which bases will be moved about during the selection process. Key space expansion, pixel randomization, key sensitivity, noise resistance, and lossless decipherment and decryption are only some of the advantages of this method. When it comes to protecting the privacy of your image, image encryption is a powerful tool. However, it has been shown that a number of image cryptosystems using this form are susceptible to attacks. High-frequency exact parts and low-frequency approximate parts make up the basic picture in [

]. Pseudorandom sequences formed by chaos are successively disruptive to the two groups of components. Then, a lifting technique is used to encrypt the image. In comparison to the standard permutation-diffusion encryption method, the proposed cryptography requires fewer pseudorandom numbers, encrypts data more rapidly, and offers higher levels of security. The remaining sections of this paper are organized as follows. The suggested technique is analyzed in detail in Sect.
. In Sect.
, the findings and analyses from the simulations were given. The last section provides paper’s conclusion.

level3.p: In this part, the fundamentals of the suggested method for encrypting images were discussed. After adequate encryption, the original image is transformed into an unreadable one. The decryption procedure is used to get the plain image.

p.span: There are three distinct phases to the encryption algorithm. Both at the start and the end, the image is spatially encrypted. Frequency domain encryption using DWT takes up the bulk of the algorithm’s middle part. A logistic map is used to depict the process of key creation. Image encryption is shown in Fig.

div.p: A flow diagram of the image encryption. It starts with K 1, K 2, K 3, K 4, and K 5, chaotic sequences, K n u m 1, K n u m 2, input image, bit plane extraction, first, second, and third cipher images, confusion, D W T, I D W T, and diffusion.
Fig. 1
Block diagram of image encryption

p.span: The focus is on spatial domain encryption, where the bit-planes are extracted from the original picture and the pixel values are manipulated directly. Additionally, the starting values of cubic logistic map (C-LM) are used as keys in a chaotic structure to produce a 1D random signal. The values produced by repeating the chaotic map MN times will be amplified by an additional key, which will also be provided as shown in Fig.
. This key stream is used to permute just the MSB bits-planes and is created randomly across a single dimension.

Discrete wavelet transforms (DWTs) will be applied to each bit-plane in order to encrypt the data further at a certain frequency. Chaotic maps aid in the encrypting process confusion and diffusion steps. Each input is subjected to many rounds of confusion to maximize the degree of randomization. Confusion causes a shift in the original order of the image's pixels. However, because the frequencies of the LL sub-band contain the most information, frequency manipulation to a two-dimensional signal created by cubic logistic map (C-LM) with varying parameters was carried out. The frequency components will be converted into actual pixel values after manipulation of LL sub-band frequencies using inverse discrete wavelet transform (IDWT) as shown in Fig.

level5.p: Encryption in the spatial domain is the focus of this section. The frequency domain encryption process produces eight distinct images, which are referred to as pre-cipher images. These images are broken down into eight bit-planes. It will provide us with 64 bit-planes, 32 of which will be used for the most significant bit (MSB), and the remaining 32 for the least significant bit (LSB). The first 48 bit-planes are split into two groups, 1 and 2, with 24 bit-planes in each. To generate the third RGB cipher image, the process used to create the first two cipher images was repeated.

p.span: Bit-plane extractions involve slicing the original image into eight horizontal strips, each of which may take on one of two values—0 or 1. Bit-planes contain varying amounts of data. The most significant bit (MSB) of the eighth bit-plane (the most significant bit) carried more than fifty percent of the data of the original plain image. The LSB contains less than one percent of the information contained in the first bit-plane. Bit-plane extraction may be thought of as a solution to Eq.

Use DWT to turn the bit-planes into frequency components, and then use the produced 2D random signal and the proper keys to conduct XOR operations only on the low-frequency bands.
Using IDWT, permuted bit-planes are produced from frequency sub-bands.

level3.p: The proposed method is an effective method of image encryption that generates three RGB cipher images from a single gray-scale input. Since it is possible to recover every bit during decryption, the proposed method may be used for both text and image encryption.In order to protect the plain image, the proposed technique employs chaotic-based encryption. The proposed encryption is split into three parts, each of which significantly improves the system's overall security. The center of the other two sections has been encrypted in the frequency domain, while the initial and last parts have encryption in the spatial domain. The suggested encryption is anticipated to be both more secure and faster by combining time and frequency domain encryption into one method. The suggested algorithm's ability to encrypt was analyzed with the use of the NPCR, UACI, and entropy metrics. The results show that the suggested approach can successfully encrypt digital images.

level2.h2: Survey of Attacks, Detection Techniques, and Evaluation Metrics in Wireless Sensor Networks

level2.p: Secure WSNs are becoming increasingly important due to their rapid growth. Different assaults targeting different layers of remote organizations have been identified, and evaluation measurements and conventions have been developed to neutralize them. In this review study, attacks on various levels, evaluation measurements, and norms in remote organizations will be detailed. First, the article examines attacks on the real, information interface, network, transport, and application levels. Standard attacks and their potential impact on network security are introduced at each layer. The review includes specific actions like DoS, listening in, parodying, and changing. Evaluation of safety components and conventions relies on assessment metrics. This article lists several remote organization security assessments. These measures include FPR, FNR, discovery precision, throughput, latency, energy use, and adaptability. The overview discusses how these measurements can evaluate security conventions’ presentation and sufficiency.

level3.p: Keywords

WSNs have proven useful in nature observation, medical care, modern computerization, and shrewd urban communities. These small, asset-obligated sensor hubs collaborate to collect and transmit data remotely. The open and accessible nature of WSNs makes them subject to security threats and attacks. WSN security is crucial since it handles sensitive and basic data. WSN attacks can cause unauthorized access, information control, administration disruption, and protection gaps. Specialists and professionals have worked hard to develop strong security measures and conventions to protect WSNs. WSNs can be attacked at multiple organizational layers, attacking faults and problems. Signal sticking, snooping, and modifying may compromise the real layer, which transmits and gathers signals. Caricaturing targeted sending, and impact-based attacks can damage the information connections layer, which links hubs.

Blackhole, wormhole, and sinkhole attacks at the organization layer can disrupt the steering system and network availability. Attacks that utilize transport convention flaws to disrupt or impact information transfer can target the vehicle layer, which handles start-to-finish communication. The application layer, where explicit application-related capabilities are performed, can be vulnerable to DoS and information injection attacks. Various assessment measurements have been offered to evaluate safety components and conventions. These measurements assess WSN presentation and security, including location precision, energy productivity, throughput, postponement, variety, and assault flexibility. These measures provide valuable insights into safety arrangements and assist in establishing and improving WSN security conventions.

Due to WSN security issues, scientists have developed many safety norms and systems. These arrangements mitigate diverse attacks using cryptographic operations, key management, verification components, interruption recognition frameworks, and directing conventions. Understanding the quality and acceptability of these conventions in different setups is crucial for WSN safety. In this case, this review paper will explain attacks against remote sensor organization layers, evaluation measurements, and standards. It also provides a comprehensive understanding of WSN security issues and solutions by examining attacks, measurements, and protocols. The information provided can help scientists, experts, and organization administrators make informed decisions about secure remote sensor organization planning, execution, and executives.

level4.p: Remote sensor organizations have sinks, foundations, and sensor hubs. Temperature, humidity, light, movement, and natural boundaries are measured by sensor hubs. Small, inexpensive, and energy-intensive hubs limit registration power, capacity, and communication range. The base station passage, or sink, collects data and links to external organizations or apps. Sensor data is sent to a data storage server or control center. Sensor hubs connect, direct, and coordinate using correspondence protocols and calculations. Indoor and outdoor WSNs work. They can be sent static with hubs fixed or forcefully with hubs moving or adaptive. WSNs range in size from small networks with a few hubs to big networks with thousands or a few thousand hubs covering large geological regions.

p.span: WSNs have distinct characteristics and challenges compared to traditional wireless networks:
: Sensor nodes have limited computational, memory, energy, and communication capabilities.
: WSN nodes self-organize to establish an ad hoc network without central control. Their network construction, routing, and data aggregation techniques are distributed.
: These methods combine node data for accurate and concise information.
: Wireless communication makes WSNs deployable and adaptable. Wireless communication is sensitive to interference, signal attenuation, and range limitations.
: WSNs risk unauthorized access, data tampering, and privacy breaches. To protect sensitive data and maintain network stability, WSNs must be secured (Fig.

div.p: A chart on layer based attacks in W S N includes physical, data link, network, transport, and application. Physical attacks are eavesdrop, radio interference, jam, and tampering. Application attacks are data corruption and malicious nodes. Transport attacks are flooding, and resource exhaustion.
Fig. 1
Attacks on different layers in WSN

level3.h3: 3 The Importance of Security in WSN

p.span: : WSNs gather and transmit sensitive physical data like health, industrial processes, and classified data. Data confidentiality is essential to prevent data breaches, illegal access, and privacy violations.

: To keep data correct, unaltered, and trustworthy throughout transmission and storage, data integrity must be ensured. An adversary can manipulate sensor readings, introduce fake information, or tamper with crucial data without data integrity, causing wrong judgments and actions.

: Security mechanisms in WSNs assure reliability and availability. Security measures prevent malicious nodes from impacting network performance, data transfer, and routing protocols by protecting network integrity or communication attacks.
: Efficient cryptographic algorithms, optimized key management protocols, and lightweight security mechanisms save energy and extend network life.

: WSNs require node trust and communication partner authenticity. Strong authentication and trust models allow nodes to safely identify and communicate with valid peers while prohibiting malicious nodes from entering the network.

: WSNs are subject to data injection, DoS, routing, and eavesdropping attacks. Effective security mechanisms detect, mitigate, and recover from these threats, assuring network resilience and critical operations.
: Many WSN applications require compliance with healthcare privacy and industrial security regulations.
: WSNs in critical infrastructure, military operations, and disaster response enhance public safety and national security.
Protocols help sensor nodes communicate and coordinate in WSNs. These protocols solve WSN challenges in different ways that are listed here [
: A low-power, low-data-rate wireless protocol for short-range, low-cost sensor networks.
: A wireless communication protocol is used for short-range device connections and data transmission in WSNs.
: Several WSN protocols use this protocol to define the physical and media access control layers for low-rate wireless personal area networks.
: A low-power, long-range wireless communication protocol for wide-area networks enables WSN connectivity.
: For low-resource devices and networks, a web transfer protocol simplifies WSN node Internet connectivity.
: A web transfer protocol for low-resource devices and networks helps WSN nodes connect to the Internet.
: Low-power consumption and lossy network routing protocol for resource-constrained WSNs that maximizes energy efficiency.
: A protocol that lets WSNs to connect to the Internet using IPv6 addresses over low-power wireless networks.
: A process automation wireless communication standard for industrial WSNs that is reliable and secure.
: A low-power, low-latency, and secure wireless communication protocol for WSNs in homes and buildings, largely used for home automation systems.

level3.h3: 5 Literature Review of Layerwise Attacks in WSN

p.span: : The basic flood attack consumes bandwidth and node processing power by flooding a network with packets. This might cause network congestion and communication disruptions [
: Attacker Sybil nodes construct bogus identities in the network to impersonate other nodes [
: Homing attacks modify the network routing mechanism to steer traffic to a node. This may disrupt routing, delay data, or undermine security [
: The attacker can collect sensitive data, modify data, or launch further assaults by gaining control of the node, compromising network integrity and security.

: Unauthorized network monitoring is eavesdropping. An attacker can passively listen to network traffic to gain sensor readings, routing data, and cryptographic keys. Privacy breaches and network resource access can result.
: The attacker can record, change, or replay traffic by bypassing routing. Wormhole attacks can disrupt network connectivity, affect data integrity, and cause routing errors [

: The attacker claims that it has the shortest path to the recipient to get all traffic through it. However, the attacker drops or selectively alters received packets, losing or corrupting them. This can harm network communication and data delivery [

: A sinkhole attacker advertises as a desired sink node to gather network traffic. Network integrity and security are compromised when legitimate nodes route their data through the attacker, allowing the attacker to eavesdrop or modify it [
: A hostile node drops or forwards packets based on criteria in a selective forwarding attack.
: In a Byzantine attack, malicious nodes send incorrect, inconsistent, or conflicting information. This can disrupt network operations, modify data integrity, and cause routing errors.
: It seeks to eliminate legitimate users from network resources.

: The goal is to reduce sensor node battery power, memory, and processing. Attackers can damage network performance, cause nodes to fail, or disrupt operations by causing nodes to consume more resources than necessary.

: Attackers spoof nodes by changing their identities. Source addresses, MAC addresses, and other identifying information can be forged or tampered with. Spoofing attacks can compromise data, network operations, or access.
: Data injection attacks introduce malicious or unauthorized data into the network. This may compromise data integrity, interrupt network operations, or lead to bad data-driven decisions.
: A hostile node drops packets randomly or based on criteria in packet-dropping attacks. Data transmission, packet loss, and network performance can suffer.

: MitM attackers intercept and transmit communication between two legal nodes, impersonating one. The attacker can eavesdrop or modify the communication, compromising data confidentiality, integrity, or authenticity.
: Network authentication techniques are targeted in authentication attacks to bypass or compromise the process. Unauthorized access, data tampering, and node impersonation can result (Fig.

div.p: A schematic of 4 types of attacks are sybil, wormhole, blackhole, and sinkhole. The Sybil attacks have malicious entities, Sybil nodes, and ordinary nodes. In a wormhole attack, there is a node, a virtual tunnel link, and a wormhole node. The blackhole attack has a source, sink, and black nodes.
Fig. 2
Attacks in WSN

p.span: : This metric examines how often a detection system misidentifies benign behavior as harmful. False positives should be limited to reduce disturbances and alerts.
: It measures how often a detection system misses attacks. A low false negative rate means the system can detect and stop threats.
: This metric indicates how well the detection system detects attacks and distinguishes them from regular behavior. It considers false positives and negatives.
: It means detection and attack time. Attack mitigation and response are faster with shorter detection times, reducing network impact.
: After an assault is discovered, response time is measured. Shorter response times reduce attack damage and restore network operation.
: Data packet transmission success in the network is measured by it. Monitoring throughput can identify assaults since packet loss or delays can lower throughput.
: It shows the percentage of sent packets that were delivered. Attacks reduce packet delivery ratios by causing packet loss.
: Security systems and detection methods add signaling, communication, and computing complexity to the network. Utilizing network resources efficiently requires minimizing network overhead.
: It measures network energy use for security operations and detection. Network lifespan can be reduced by attacks that raise communication or computational overhead and energy consumption.
: Robustness assesses network and security mechanisms’ resilience to attacks. A strong system can detect, mitigate, and recover from threats, maintaining network continuity.
: Coverage is the network region the detection system monitors.
: Scalability measures the detection system’s capacity to add nodes or network size without affecting performance or resource usage.
: This metric measures network resilience to data injection attacks. To maintain data integrity and reliability, it measures the system’s ability to detect and filter such data.
: Node compromise resilience is the network’s capacity to withstand adversarial attacks on one or more nodes. It measures how well the system can identify and isolate infected nodes to stop attacks.
: Packets go via the network with latency. Attacks that increase latency or congestion may negatively impact network performance.

p.strong: Overhead in memory and processing power
p.span: : This metric measures security and detection mechanisms’ memory and processing needs. Overhead must not exceed network node resources.
: Collusion attack resilience measures the network’s ability to detect and mitigate attacks from multiple malicious nodes. It measures how well the system detects and stops collusion.
: Privacy preservation measures how well sensitive data and user privacy are protected from attacks. In privacy-sensitive applications, it is crucial.

level3.p: WSNs may be compromised by network stack attacks on integrity, confidentiality, availability, and functionality. This review summarizes assaults and literature-based defenses. No defense can stop all WSN attacks, so be aware. Use cryptography, IDSs, and authentication protocols to secure networks. To keep WSNs strong, research and development of cutting-edge countermeasures are needed. Interdisciplinary collaboration between researchers, network designers, and industry practitioners is needed to address WSN security issues. Understanding attacks and implementing effective solutions is crucial to protecting WSNs in environmental monitoring, healthcare, and industrial automation applications.

level2.h2: Big Data’s Ethical Frontier: Addressing Concerns in Data Acquisition and Analysis

p.span: Kanhaiya Sharma
Deepak Parashar
Vipin Tiwari
Siddhanth Shah
and
Sandeep Singh Rawat

div.p: Symbiosis International (Deemed University), Symbiosis Institute of Technology, Pune, India
School of Computer and Information Sciences, IGNOU, Maidan Garhi, New Delhi, India

level4.p: School of Technology, GSFC University, Vadodara, India

level3.p: Kanhaiya Sharma

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: In the digital era, big data's influence has surged, revolutionizing industries and fostering unparalleled opportunities for advancement. Yet, this advancement is accompanied by a surge in ethical complexities related to data collection and interpretation. This comprehensive research delves into multifaceted ethical issues in big data, highlighting concerns about privacy violations, inherent biases in datasets and algorithms, and opaque data handling processes. Real-world case studies from various sectors underscore the unintended consequences of big data practices. Offering practical recommendations, the study advocates ethical best practices to leverage big data's potential responsibly. It stresses the urgency of establishing a robust ethical framework that balances innovation and individual rights, emphasizing the need for ongoing dialogue and policy considerations. Ultimately, this paper aims to foster a harmonious coexistence between ethics and innovation in the expanding domain of big data.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Big data has become a pervasive and transformative force, revolutionizing various aspects of society and industry with its vast and multifaceted datasets. Defined by its immense volume, rapid generation, and diverse forms of data, including structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data [
], big data presents an array of challenges in storage, processing, analysis, and interpretation [

]. While its applications have yielded profound insights across domains like business, healthcare, and scientific research, they have also raised ethical concerns regarding privacy, consent, ownership, and security [
]. The ethical implications of big data are starkly illustrated through a series of real-life case studies, including the notorious Cambridge Analytica scandal [
], Target's pregnancy campaign [
], and the Aadhaar data leak [

]. These incidents highlight critical issues in data collection and analysis, emphasizing the need for a comprehensive understanding of ethical challenges and their implications. The growing interconnectivity facilitated by the Internet of Things (IoT) and smart devices has raised significant privacy concerns. Individuals often remain unaware of the extensive digital footprints they leave behind, enabling the collection of deeply personal information, which, when used for commercial purposes, can lead to tailored marketing strategies and personalized product recommendations. However, this also raises questions about user consent and the potential misuse of personal information. Furthermore, the rise of big data has ushered in an era of information-driven decision-making, prompting reliance on algorithms for data interpretation and action. However, this reliance introduces a new layer of ethical considerations, including the design of unbiased algorithms and ensuring stakeholders’ awareness of the potential consequences of algorithmic decisions. Instances of algorithmic biases reinforcing societal inequalities underscore the critical importance of ethical considerations in big data [

]. The sheer volume and complexity of big data often lead to a lack of transparency, leaving many stakeholders, including the public, unaware of how their data is being utilized. Maintaining transparency in data practices is crucial for building and sustaining public trust, especially as corporations and governments leverage data for various applications.

level3.p: This paper aims to delve into the intricate ethical landscape surrounding the big data revolution. By exploring the ethical challenges inherent in data acquisition and analysis, it seeks to foster a comprehensive understanding of the responsibilities shared by all stakeholders within this ecosystem. Through this exploration, the research aims to encourage the development of robust ethical frameworks that ensure the responsible and beneficial utilization of big data without compromising individual rights or societal well-being.

p.span: The collection and usage of big data present several ethical challenges due to the scale, complexity, and potential impacts on people and society. Some of these challenges include:

Privacy concerns: Big data many times contain personal information which raises privacy concerns as if malicious users can use this information. Techniques for anonymization might not always be successful in concealing people's identities [

Informed consent: Getting informed consent from people before collecting their data can be challenging, particularly when the data is passively or covertly gathered. People could feel pressured into giving consent or they might not completely comprehend the ramifications of the usage of their data [

Data ownership: It is not easy to identify who is entitled to use the data and who is its owner. The collection of data from many sources, such as people, businesses, and public databases, makes it hard to define ownership rights [

Algorithmic bias and discrimination: Given data may contain bias which can get perpetuated or even amplified by big data analytics. Biased algorithms can thus lead to unjust discrimination as well as influencing choices in important sectors like financing and employment [

Data accuracy and quality: Inaccurate or poor-quality data collected can result in erroneous judgments and conclusions. This makes it difficult to ensure the accuracy and quality of large data, especially when information is gathered from several sources that have variable degrees of dependability [

Security and data breaches: Large data collections and storage make for appealing targets for hackers. Sensitive information may be exposed in data breaches if security measures are not taken to prevent it, which might have negative effects such as monetary loss, identity theft, or other issues [

Data transparency: The lack of transparency can result from the opaqueness of the techniques used to acquire and analyze large data. People's faith in data-driven technology and organizations may decline because of their lack of understanding of how their data is utilized [

Social implications: Big data utilization has a variety of social effects, including the ability to affect political outcomes, public opinion, and even behavior. When data-driven actions have unforeseen repercussions or affect society outcomes without sufficient scrutiny, ethical quandaries occur [

In the year 2012, Target released an innovative marketing campaign which involved the use of their pregnancy prediction algorithm. It predicted the likelihood of a person being pregnant or not using the purchase history of the person for promotions depicted in Fig.

. This led to public backlash on the fact that Target used sensitive data without the consent of the customers and used it for publicity. Following this, Target removed the campaign, came out with a public apology, and implemented many data security measures. The ethical challenges this campaign produced were:

div.p: Customers complained that their privacy was being violated since the business was making predictions about their individual medical situations without getting their permission.

Handling of sensitive data: The use of such private and sensitive information without clear consent may result in unexpected repercussions or misuse, raising concerns about the need for businesses to safeguard and responsibly manage consumer information.
Manipulation and consent: According to some detractors, this type of personalized advertising could be manipulative since it might persuade consumers to make purchases they otherwise would not have.
Stigmatization: In certain situations, Target’s marketing may unintentionally inform family members or other people of a pregnancy before the person was prepared to share the news.

A conceptual framework depicts a marketing campaign that involves the collection of medical and purchase data. This approach is identified as ethically wrong, it leads to backlash and a call for more transparency.
Fig. 1
Target prediction algorithm

level3.p: The acquisition of big data is a multifaceted process, shaped by the diversity of data sources. Data sources can be broadly categorized into three main types: human-generated data, machine-generated data, and environmental data. Human-generated data encompasses the vast amount of information produced by individuals as they engage with digital platforms. This includes social media posts, emails, online searches, and even digital footprints like clickstreams and mouse movements. While users are often aware that their interactions generate data, they may not fully comprehend the extent to which this data is collected, analyzed, and utilized. Machine-generated data, on the other hand, originates from automated systems and devices. This includes sensor data from IoT devices, logs generated by computer systems, and telemetry data from vehicles and machinery. Machine-generated data often flows continuously and at high velocities, making it challenging to manage and analyze in real-time. Environmental data encompasses information collected from the physical world, such as weather data, satellite imagery, and geological data. These sources are crucial for scientific research, climate monitoring, and disaster prediction.

p.span: The Cambridge Analytica affair is seen as a turning point that revealed the moral dilemmas associated with big data, privacy violations, and political influence. The issue centered on Cambridge Analytica’s actions, a British data analytics company that used the personal information of millions of Facebook users to sway political outcomes shown in Fig.

. The method by which Cambridge Analytica acquired the data was at the central of the affair. The company made use of a third-party programmed created by Aleksandr Kogan, a researcher. This_is_your_digital_life was an app that offered a personality test and acquired data from users’ Facebook friends in addition to data from those who completed the test; as a result, data from almost eighty-seven million users was taken without their consent. Although the app’s initial goal was justified, the volume of data collection for secret purposes raised ethical questions. With access to this enormous dataset, Cambridge Analytica was able to create psychologically relevant political adverts, which brought about a disturbing reality. The company asserted that it had created a system for classifying people according to their personality traits, interests, and behaviors. This system would enable them to craft political messaging that would take advantage of people’s emotional triggers and prejudices. The suggestion that elections might be swayed by carefully focused psychological manipulation raised significant questions about the integrity of democratic processes. The issue also revealed a lack of accountability and openness in data-sharing practices. The amount to which their data was being gathered and shared with outside parties was often unknown to Facebook users. This incident demonstrated the necessity for better, easier-to-understand privacy policies and user-friendly data access control systems. Global indignation and numerous inquiries were sparked by the Cambridge Analytica scandal’s revelation. Facebook's CEO, Mark Zuckerberg, testified about the incident in front of the US Congress. The controversy also sparked a larger public debate about the function of the Internet corporations, the protection of personal information, and the need for strict legislation to stop such violations in the future. Facebook made several platform modifications in reaction to the scandal, including tighter data access regulations and improved user controls over data sharing. As a result of the incident, tighter data protection laws were created, including the general data protection regulation (GDPR) in the European Union, which is meant to give users more control over their personal data and how it is used. The ethical issues that arise in the field of big data collecting and analysis are starkly brought to light by the Cambridge Analytica controversy. It emphasized how crucial it is in the digital age to protect user privacy, enforce moral data practices, and guarantee openness. The incident's far-reaching impact reverberated across industries, prompting individuals, organizations, and policymakers to reevaluate their roles and responsibilities in the era of data-driven decision-making.

div.p: A conceptual framework depicts a British firm that influenced many voters during the 2016 U S election through a personality quiz for Facebook users. The firm collected data from 87 million Facebook users. They developed methods to categorize users. The result was a change in privacy policy.
Fig. 2
Cambridge Analytica scandal

level3.p: The Cambridge Analytica incident brought to light the unethical issues associated with big data analysis used for political influence. Situation leading to the problem: The issue started when it emerged that Cambridge Analytica, a political consulting firm, had illegally accessed the personal information of about eighty-seven million Facebook users. This information was gathered using a third-party software that took advantage of the rights users and their friends had provided it. When it became clear that this information was utilized to produce customized political adverts during the 2016 US presidential election and the Brexit campaign, the problem worsened. Solution used: Facebook, the platform used to collect the data, responded to the incident by introducing a number of measures to solve the problem. An internal inquiry was conducted, Cambridge Analytica and researcher Aleksandr Kogan were placed on administrative leave, and the app's access to user data was revoked. To avoid repeat mishaps, Facebook also examined its data access standards and tightened controls on third-party apps. What did we learn: The Cambridge Analytica scandal served as a stark lesson on several fronts? Data privacy awareness: The episode served as a reminder of the significance of being aware of how, in the digital era, personal data is gathered, used, and shared. The possible dangers of granting app permissions and disclosing confidential information online have come to the attention of users. Consent and transparency: The controversy made clear the importance of gaining users’ informed consent before accessing and utilizing their data. It demonstrated the drawbacks of ambiguous privacy policies and the requirement for plainly worded justifications of data usage procedures. Legislative gaps: The event exposed the weaknesses in the privacy and data protection regulatory frameworks. It sparked debates on the necessity of thorough data protection laws and stricter rules to hold businesses accountable for improper data handling. Algorithmic manipulation: The ability to manipulate data through analysis was demonstrated using gathered data to produce tailored political adverts. This sparked questions regarding the moral ramifications of algorithmic decision-making as well as the significance of justice and accountability in such procedures.

The February 2019 data breach of the Indian Aadhaar card database, potentially compromising 1.1 billion records, highlighted significant ethical and practical challenges in big data. The unauthorized leak sparked concerns about privacy violations, data security breaches, misuse, discrimination, and public trust erosion. Negligence on the part of government officials contributed to the breach, stemming from inadequate security measures, lack of employee training, absence of encryption, and reliance on outdated legacy systems.

To address these challenges, proactive strategies must be implemented, including comprehensive employee training on data security, modernization of legacy systems, and the adoption of robust data encryption measures. These efforts can help prevent further breaches, foster a secure data ecosystem, and restore public confidence in the government's integrity and data management practices.

Lessons from the Aadhaar card data breach underscore the significance of prioritizing data security, privacy preservation, and ethical data management. It underscores the critical need for stringent security measures, compliance with regulations, and transparent communication to safeguard sensitive information. The incident emphasizes the pivotal role of cybersecurity investments, employee training, and meticulous oversight of third-party vendors. Incorporating ethical principles, preparing for potential breach scenarios, and embracing technological advancements are essential in preventing such breaches and maintaining citizen welfare in the digital age.

level4.h4: 4.1 Ethical Concerns in Data Analysis and Regulatory Framework
level4.p: Ethical concerns in data analysis encompass algorithmic bias, fairness, discrimination, and transparency. Algorithmic bias arises from biased training data or algorithmic predispositions, resulting in unequal outcomes for different groups. Ensuring algorithmic fairness involves defining criteria, measuring fairness, and adjusting algorithms to mitigate biases. Discrimination in data analysis can lead to social and legal repercussions, emphasizing the need for transparent and accountable algorithms. Transparency involves making algorithms’ decision-making processes interpretable, while accountability mechanisms help identify and rectify biases and discriminatory outcomes. Globally, the GDPR in Europe and the CCPA in the USA enforce data protection and privacy rights, emphasizing data transparency and accountability. These regulations mandate explicit consent for data collection, access to personal data, and data security measures, with non-compliance leading to significant penalties. International bodies like the United Nations also advocate for ethical data practices, as highlighted in the sustainable development goals, emphasizing the need for responsible data-driven technologies.

level3.p: In this paper, the ethical dimension of big data is a dynamic and vital domain that demands continuous attention and robust ethical foundations. As we navigate the expanding landscape of data, responsible practices are essential for preserving individual rights, societal values, and digital trust. Big data's transformative potential is undeniable, capable of revolutionizing healthcare, business, education, and addressing global issues. Yet, ethical considerations must guide every step. Informed consent, privacy protection, transparency in algorithms, fairness, and accountability are pivotal principles. Upholding these values requires a collective commitment from governments, organizations, data experts, and society, adapting to evolving challenges. This ethical journey ensures big data's promises are fulfilled while respecting all individuals’ rights and dignity.

level2.h2: Evaluating Security Risks and Protecting Conversational AI: An in-Depth Vulnerability Assessment and Security Testing of the ChatGPT Android Application

p.span: Srinadh Swamy Majeti
T. Shyam Prasad
and
Sunil Kumar Thota

div.p: Department of Artificial Intelligence, Anurag University, Hyderabad, India
Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Anurag University, Hyderabad, India
Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Keshav Memorial Institute of Technology, Hyderabad, India

level3.p: Srinadh Swamy Majeti

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: Recent years have seen a surge in the popularity of conversational AI applications like ChatGPT, revolutionizing human–machine interactions. However, as these AI chat systems become more integrated into daily life, ensuring their security is paramount. This research paper conducts a thorough security assessment of ChatGPT, using static and dynamic testing. The study uncovers significant vulnerabilities, including potential data leaks and susceptibility to adversarial attacks, raising concerns about user privacy and potential exploitation. Recommendations for mitigation are provided to enhance ChatGPT's security. This research contributes to the growing body of knowledge on conversational AI security, stressing the importance of proactive measures to protect user data and maintain a secure conversational experience. It also highlights the necessity for ongoing security assessments as AI technology advances.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Conversational AI, a transformative technology that facilitates natural language interactions between humans and machines, has witnessed a remarkable surge in popularity and utility in recent years. At the forefront of this technological revolution stands ChatGPT [

], a powerful and versatile language model designed to engage users in seamless text-based conversations. While the capabilities of ChatGPT are undeniably impressive, the pervasive integration of such conversational AI systems into various aspects of our lives demands a heightened focus on security and privacy.

level3.p: This research paper embarks on a critical examination of the security landscape surrounding ChatGPT, aiming to uncover potential vulnerabilities and risks inherent in the system. As the utilization of AI-driven conversational agents grows, so too does the urgency to address security concerns. The overarching objective of this research is to shed light on the intricate web of security challenges that may compromise the integrity and privacy of users engaging with ChatGPT.

To achieve this objective, we adopt a multifaceted approach, combining both static and dynamic testing procedures to comprehensively evaluate the security posture of ChatGPT. Our research endeavors to unearth security vulnerabilities lurking within the system, going beyond the superficial examination to delve into the deeper layers of potential threats. By doing so, we aim to equip developers, users, and stakeholders with a heightened awareness of the security landscape, facilitating informed decision-making and proactive measures to safeguard sensitive data and preserve privacy.

The findings of our study reveal a series of significant security vulnerabilities, underscoring the potential risks associated with ChatGPT. These vulnerabilities span a spectrum of concerns, encompassing the possibility of data leaks and susceptibility to adversarial attacks, among others. This paper discusses the implications of these vulnerabilities and their repercussions on user privacy, emphasizing the need for robust security measures and proactive mitigation strategies.

Through this research, we aspire to contribute valuable insights to the evolving field of conversational AI security, reinforcing the importance of diligent security assessments as AI technologies continue to advance. It is our firm belief that by shining a light on the security vulnerabilities inherent in ChatGPT, we can collectively work toward enhancing the security posture of these AI-driven conversational systems, ensuring a safer and more secure conversational experience for all users.

p.span: To assess the security vulnerabilities in ChatGPT app (.apk), we have to dissect and analyze the ChatGPT software or hardware to unveil its inner workings and functionality. Reverse engineering is the process of dissecting a piece of software, hardware, or a system to understand its inner workings, design, and functionality [

]. Reverse engineering involves decompiling the executable code or binary back into a higher-level representation, typically the source code or something close to it. Reverse engineering of an APK to identify security vulnerabilities arises from several key factors—security assessment, understanding behavior, third-party libraries, malware analysis, and code audit. By reverse engineering an APK and analyzing its class files, resources, manifest, and any associated JAR files, security experts and developers can gain a deeper understanding of the application's security posture, identify vulnerabilities, and take steps to improve its security. Numerous tools are available for performing reverse engineering tasks.

Reverse engineering of ChatGPT app done using dex2jar and JD-GUI involves a systematic process for gaining insights into the inner workings of an Android application. Initially, dex2jar is utilized to convert the APK's Dalvik Executable (DEX) files into a Java Archive (JAR) file, effectively translating the bytecode into a more comprehensible Java format. Subsequently, this JAR file is opened in JD-GUI, which provides a user-friendly interface for decompiling Java source code from the APK. JD-GUI allows analysts and developers to navigate through the application's structure, explore its logic, and understand its functionality. This method assists in identifying security vulnerabilities, comprehending the code's architecture, and supporting debugging and analysis. In addition to dex2jar and JD-GUI, we leveraged various other reverse engineering utilities, including Androbugs and apktool, as well as automated assessment tools such as MobSF [
], immuniweb [
], and Quixxi [

level3.p: During our reverse engineering analysis, we successfully extracted the Java Archive (JAR) files from the ChatGPT APK. Notably, we observed a lack of code obfuscation, which is a common security practice to hinder reverse engineering attempts. This absence of code obfuscation represents a vulnerability in the application, as it makes it easier for malicious actors to access and potentially exploit the source code. The absence of code obfuscation in the ChatGPT APK is a significant security concern, as it facilitates reverse engineering and exposes the application's source code. Code obfuscation is a fundamental security practice that should be implemented to protect against unauthorized access to the source code.

p.span: Automated tool analysis revealed several security risks and vulnerabilities in the ChatGPT APK:

div.p: a.

p.span: : We identified the inclusion of the “android.permission.RECORD_AUDIO” permission, which appeared to be unrelated to the core functionality of the application. The presence of such permissions can pose privacy concerns, as they grant the app access to sensitive user data without a legitimate reason. “android.permission.RECORD_AUDIO” is a privacy concern that can erode user trust. App developers should carefully consider the permissions they request and ensure they are directly related to the app's functionality to protect user privacy.

div.p: b.

p.span: : Our analysis uncovered a vulnerability in the application related to the Janus vulnerability [

], which could potentially allow an attacker to manipulate the APK's resources or execute malicious code. This vulnerability highlights the importance of staying vigilant against emerging threats in the mobile app landscape.

div.p: c.

p.span: : An activity (com.auth0.android.provider.RedirectActivity) is found to be shared with other apps on the device therefore leaving it accessible to any other application on the device.

level3.p: In this comprehensive security assessment and vulnerability analysis of the ChatGPT APK for mobile applications, we uncovered critical security risks and vulnerabilities using a combination of reverse engineering techniques and automated tools. Our findings underscore the imperative need for rigorous security measures in mobile app development to protect user data and privacy.

Our reverse engineering analysis yielded a noteworthy vulnerability—the absence of code obfuscation in the ChatGPT APK. This omission allows malicious actors easier access to the application's source code, increasing the potential for unauthorized use, manipulation, or exploitation of the software. Code obfuscation is a fundamental security practice, and its absence represents a clear and present danger to the integrity of the application.

Automated tools further revealed two significant security risks. First, the inclusion of the “android.permission.RECORD_AUDIO” permission without a clear justification poses a privacy concern. Such permissions can grant the application access to sensitive user data without a valid functional reason, undermining user trust and privacy.

Secondly, the vulnerability to the Janus attack emerged as a substantial risk. The Janus vulnerability could potentially allow attackers to tamper with the APK's resources or execute malicious code, making it imperative for developers to address this issue promptly.

In conclusion, our findings emphasize the importance of robust security practices throughout the mobile app development process. Code obfuscation should be implemented to protect against reverse engineering attempts, safeguarding the application's source code. App developers must carefully evaluate and justify permissions to ensure user privacy and security, avoiding the inclusion of unnecessary permissions like “android.permission.RECORD_AUDIO.” Additionally, addressing the Janus vulnerability is crucial to maintaining the application's security and integrity.

This research underscores the significance of proactive security assessments and continuous monitoring in the evolving landscape of mobile app security. To build and maintain user trust, it is essential for developers to address identified vulnerabilities promptly, fortify security measures, and prioritize the protection of user data and privacy in mobile applications.

div.p: References

level2.h2: Neural Networks and GPU-Based Weed Detection Under Varying Light Illumination

p.span: K. Balakrishna
Zameer Gulzar
and
K. Sai Chandu

div.p: SR University, Warangal, Telangana, India

level4.p: MIE-SPPU Institute of Higher Education, Doha, Qatar

level3.p: K. Balakrishna

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: Destructive plants that spread and compete with agricultural crops for water and nutrients are sometimes referred to as weeds. Earlier, traditional techniques were used in agriculture to remove unwanted plants or weeds. Since weeds are a major cause of crop yield loss, they compete with productive crops for soil, nutrients, and sunshine. As a result, safer and more effective herbicide products are always being developed. Today, uniform herbicide application is a major contributor to crop poisoning, environmental contamination, and high herbicide consumption costs. The issues with uniform spraying in fields can possibly be resolved by site-specific spraying. Hence, reliable weeds detection or identification is done precisely under varying light illumination. Scale invariance feature transform (SIFT) is employed for feature extraction, and a neural network is used to distinguish weeds from plants utilizing geometric features in the existing model. The primary objective is to improve the accuracy rate and to reduce the computational time. The proposed system is closed to be more accurate if convolution neural network and region-based convolutional neural network are used. One of the strengths of convolutional networks is their inherent translation invariance, and by using CNN, depth of the volume is increasing at each level so that can keep the computation time at reasonable level. Labor and pesticide costs will be lowered in a greater proportion, and manual weed removal will need less labor. In the case of smart weed control system, i.e., automatic system, it is expected to improve the accuracy rate. The performance rate can be improved by parallel processing that can be done in GPU will boost performance. The fast RCNN and the CNN classifier are the two most important factors affecting the performance of the proposed system.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: One of the key areas in agriculture where automation is most needed is weed control. Since weeds compete directly with crops for resources like soil nutrients and water, making them one of agriculture's largest concerns as crop product performance is influenced [

]. The conventional methods for controlling weeds consist of spraying some kind of herbicides all over the farm areas. So, these not only affect the crops but also the soil land and food quality of the crop yielding. Using herbicides all over the area consists of increase in cost of the chemicals used, as only some areas are covered with weeds farmers [
]. In addition, the large extent of plantings frequently poses a computational time problem by [

] so, there must be some smart weed controlling system with improved efficiency. The efficiency can be increased using image processing techniques. There are huge varieties of weed species, and due to lack of general features, this task is still a wide and open for the classification algorithms like supervised, unsupervised, and deep algorithms. The first portion is in charge of detecting plants and deriving characteristics from them, while the second part is in charge of classifying them [

]. Various lighting circumstances, leaf overlap, weed patch density variability, scale differences across photos, and the fact that leaves of various species can look quite similar to one another are the key obstacles to the building of successful models [

]. Deep learning provides better classification and improves the performance of the system. There are many deep learning techniques mainly CNN that deal with the feature extraction and classification in image processing. The CNN architecture to classify the type of plants proposed by [

] took different image sequence collected from smart agro-station. CNN consists of multi-hidden layer perceptron (MLPS) which involves multiple convolutions, pooling, Rectified Linear unit (ReLU) and fully connected layers. The paper's contributions made by [

] are pixel-by-pixel labeled sugar beet and weed datasets from a controlled field experiment. A study uses dense semantic segmentation with variable multi-spectral input channels to classify crops and weeds. Additionally, it was suggested by [

] that a color model be used (hue, saturation, and value color) in order to reliably identify weeds under various lighting conditions in place of RGB. To get the minimum error rate, Bayesian classifier is used under normal distribution. Based on this rate, decision is made whether to spray herbicides or not. Reference [

] did a survey consisting of multi-wavelength laser profile (MWLP), a sensor-based approach for obtaining a 3D image by scanning the plants using a single camera with multi-wavelength resolution. Reference [

] provided weed segmentation and classification using watershed segmentation. They are image pre-processing, binarization using Otsu’s method, watershed segmentation, marker control methods, and classification.

level3.p: Both conventional and deep learning-based weed detection techniques offer benefits. Traditional weed identification techniques can be employed in agricultural machinery and equipment at a reasonable cost The goal of CNN technique is robust feature extraction and scale invariant, and it is mainly used for pattern reorganization and to improve the performance rate parallel processing can be done in GPU. For GPU implementation, the infrastructure such as NVIDIA GeForce GTX(GPU), 8 GB+ RAM, Platform (such as Ubuntu/Windows OS),and MATLAB tool is required for implementation. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are used to segment the objects and localize them, if labeled training data is scarce, and then to yield a significant performance, we can use regions-based convolution neural network. This study will address the accurate identification of weeds under different illumination on images. To find weeds in the middle line of crop rows, the current model uses line detection and perspective approaches. To evaluate the weeds more correctly and to boost efficiency, the suggested method uses the SIFT technique and geometric distortion. The main aim is to improve the accuracy rate and to reduce the computational time by doing parallel processing on a GPU.

p.span: The suggested approach is based on convolution neural network classifiers, color segmentation, morphological operations, well-known shape descriptors, and others. The classifier and shape descriptors are the two key elements impacting how well the suggested approach performs. Since the Hu moments are invariant under translation, rotation, and scaling, it has been demonstrated that they are important in the processes of shape identification. (An additional moment could be employed.) The seven Hu moments are obtained for each region in a picture in order to characterize the region and distinguish between crops and weeds. Six geometric shape descriptors (
p.em: perimeter, diameter, minor axis length, major axis length, eccentricity, and area
p.span: ) were employed to describe the shapes. Rest of the features are auto-extracted using CNN.
The digital images were captured under various light illuminations and over paddy form with DSLR camera as shown in Fig.
. One more dataset used here is carrot plant with weed dataset taken from github.com. Figure

shows the architecture diagram for weed detection. The weed image database consists of a collection of images of weeds and the dataset is the source of all images. First the input images are fed into the image pre-processing stage. The image pre-processing stage removes noise and other artifacts from the images. Next, the pre-processed images are then fed into the CNN classifier and the CNN classifier outputs a predicted label for each image. The predicted labels are then evaluated on the testing dataset. The parallel processing by GPU allows the weed image database to process images quickly and efficiently

Fig. 2
Architecture diagram

p.span: The process flow and stages for employing a convolutional neural network (CNN) to process weed images are shown in Fig.

. Images of processed weeds are used as the input images. To get rid of noise and other artifacts, the input photos are first processed. The photos may then be pre-processed through methods including cropping, scaling, and gray scale conversion before being examined for geometric shape descriptors. Weeds can be identified by their forms and separated from other objects in an image using these descriptors. The scale space extrema in the photos are then found using the geometric shape descriptors. Weed locations in the photos can be determined using these extrema. Following that, texture and color descriptors are examined in the scale space extrema. With the use of these adjectives, weeds can be further identified and set out from other things in an image. The CNN classifier is then fed the texture and color descriptors. Each image receives a predicted label from the CNN classifier that indicates whether or not it contains weeds. The weed image database can process photos rapidly and effectively because to GPU parallel processing. This is crucial for programs that detect weeds in real time. The process flow diagram demonstrates how weeds can be recognized in photos using a CNN. A dataset of photos of weeds and other objects can be used to train the CNN. The CNN may be used to classify fresh data once it has been trained.

div.p: A process diagram depicts how the input images from the weed image dataset undergo image preprocessing and parallel processing by G P U to examine crops by scale space extrema detection. It comprises geometric shaped descriptors, texture and color descriptors, and a C N N classifier.
Fig. 3
Overall process

p.span: Features are generally defined as shown in Fig.

as the distinctive attribute or unique properties to describe an image. It helps to differentiate between the images. Feature extraction is mainly used in all machine vision algorithms and train few algorithms using the extracted feature from the image and is widely preferred in machine learning. The process of transforming the input dataset into a set of features is termed as feature extraction. Features are often containing some information related to texture content or shape, gray shade. In other words, feature extraction is termed as a dimensional reduction that efficiently represents the most interesting parts of an image as a very compact feature vector. It is highly useful when size of the images is too large and reduced feature representation is highly required to quickly complete task such as image retrieval and matching. Feature extraction helps to reduce the amount of resource required to describe a large dataset. The major problem on analysis of computer data is number of variables involved. Increase in number of variables may cause large number of problems such as large memory, computation power, and amount of overfit issue in classification.

div.p: A workflow of convolution neural network layers. It defines how a block from the input image undergoes convolution, detection of non linearity, and pooling to give the output feature. It involves feature maps, f maps, and fully connected layers.
Fig. 4
Steps of CNN

p.span: Each hidden neuron in CNN has a local receptive field for the local connection pattern and is connected to local patches of the preceding layer. Semantically comparable features are combined into one by pooling layers. When the input fluctuates in position and appearance, the first layer of pooling produces output that varies very little, and when the network has multiple layers, the second layer of pooling can dramatically lower computing cost. The sigmoid and tanh nonlinear activation functions have upper limits, or low and high values, on their curves. However, it is possible to determine whether there is a weed or not by using the rectified linear functional unit (ReLU).
) (ReLU). It is made up of positive values.
The final output of each neuron is determined by its activation function,
). SoftMax activation function is employed for multiclass classification.
) = Sum of all exponential values divided by the input values’ exponential. The sum of all the probabilities in the output vector of
values, which ranges from 0 to 1, is equal to 1. Overall potential target classes, this SoftMax function generates probability for each target class.

p.span: : This stage consists of blurring the images of different octaves, i.e., different levels of scales called octaves, so that smoothening is performed. Blurring of images at different scales can able to find the key region or key points. Hence, using Gaussian operator noise is removed and image is smoothened. By multiplying the image with Gaussian, we get Laplacian of Gaussian (LOG). To find many key points, by calculating the difference of two blurred images, we get the difference of Gaussian (DOG). This can be achieved using a “scale space” function.

div.p: (ii)

should be in center so that it can be compared with each other points by plotting histogram, and bad points can also be eliminated as in Fig.

This data is then used to plot histograms and key points should get information about the neighbor key point so that can eliminate bad key point. After finding the key points using SIFT algorithm, key points matching is done between two images, and after that training and testing phase is done.

level3.p: Controlling harmful weeds in agriculture is essential to both agricultural productivity and environmental sustainability. Because of the shortcomings of conventional weed management techniques, a neural network-based technique is suggested for weed identification based on geometric data and the scale invariance feature transform (SIFT) for feature extraction. This study tackles the problem of accurate weed recognition while changing lighting circumstances. The convolution neural network (CNN) extracts feature and classifies the images and is used to improve the accuracy rate. The goal of RCNN is to take in an input image, and correctly identify where the main object (via a bounding box) in the image. In CNN, training progress is been calculated for the 10 epochs with 23 iterations as one graph and top1 error rate with top5 error rate as another graph. The top1 error rate, which indicates that the target class was not assigned the highest score by the classifier. Therefore, the expected outcome will have the maximum likelihood. The output may be predicted using top-1 accuracy, and the probability value that should match the expected value makes up the top-5 error rate. Here, the top5 error rate should be among the classifier's top estimates, or top5, as it shouldn’t be projected to be higher than these two classes. Accurate detection helps the farmers to remove the weeds at early stages efficiently. This feature selection approach efficiently removes redundant and irrelevant features and classifies the weeds from crop effectively. As a part of future work, the proposed method “GPU-Based Weed Detection Under Varying Light Illumination” can be extended for IoT-based applications with user interface. Other parameters such as more geometric and shape descriptors are required if field has higher growth crop and accuracy rate must be still improved. Hence, accurate detection helps the farmers to remove the weeds at early stages efficiently.
