level2.h2: Intelligent ECG-Based COVID-19 Diagnose Device Using CNN Deep Learning Approaches

p.span: Kumar Keshamoni
Koteswara Rao
and
D. Subba Rao

div.p: Koneru Lakshmaiah Education Foundation, Hyderabad, India
Siddhartha Institute of Engineering and Technology, Hyderabad, India

level3.p: Kumar Keshamoni

level4.p: Email:

level3.p: Koteswara Rao
(Corresponding author)
Email:
D. Subba Rao

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: The COVID-19 virus has caused millions of fatalities worldwide. As a component of our ongoing initiatives to curb the transmission of COVID-19, we are implementing the following measures; a quicker and safer diagnosis is highly desired. The analysis of electrocardiogram (ECG) data has shown usefulness for detecting cardiac disease, stroke, and COVID-19. We developed a fully automated procedure to detect COVID-19 infection in ECGs. Electrocardiographic (ECG) signal-based technologies and computerized models enable the precise identification of COVID-19 following substantial research into the process that the illness is going through, the symptoms that the patient is experiencing, and the laboratory diagnosis. The measures above (accuracy, precision, recall, FSCORE, and false prediction rate) showed excellent results. We discovered that the suggested models for COVID-19 detection with this cutting-edge technology worked well. Logistic regression, InceptionV3, KNN, decision tree, LSTM, SVM, ResNet, Naive Bayes and MobileNet were all equally efficient and precise for binary classification. Our results indicate the idea that it is possible to recognize COVID-19 in ECG images by utilizing deep learning has potential. This is a possibility. Our findings imply that COVID-19 can be recognized by a system that has been trained on digital ECG signals using deep learning.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: COVID-19 is a contagious ailment capable of transmission between individuals, presenting a significant risk [
]. The most vulnerable groups are those without vaccinations and those with compromised immune systems [
]. COVID-19 has been connected to more than 6.2 million fatalities and 507.9 million illnesses. The COVID-19 survivors experienced exhaustion, dyspnea, and respiratory problems [
]. This sickness has been reported in almost every country on the planet since it first appeared [
]. The respiratory system is heavily impacted by the disease’s causative agent, SARS-CoV-2 [

]. The main way that COVID-19 infections occur is through the inhalation of particles harboring SARS-CoV-2 in contaminated air. The proximity of infected persons is a crucial element in the transmission of the virus, even though it can also travel longer distances [
]. Of those diagnosed, at least one-third do not show symptoms [
]. Despite this, 14% of afflicted individuals require hospitalization, 5% require immediate medical intervention, and 81% have mild to moderate symptoms [
]. The exponential rise in COVID-19 cases puts pressure on the medical community to diagnose and treat many patients properly. Serious resource shortages have resulted from this.

“Real-time reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction testing may be used to detect the COVID-19 virus. Real-time reverse transcription is the term for this procedure (RT-PCR). The RT-PCR method of virus identification costs the researcher nothing and takes four hours of effort in total [

].” The findings of this study suggest that voltage measurements of the electrical impulses produced by the human heart should be used while doing electrocardiograms. This conclusion should be drawn based on the study’s findings (Lilly 2012). The kind of equipment used will affect how frequently data is collected. To perform an electrocardiogram (ECG), the patient will have electrodes applied to their skin. The electrical changes brought on by the repolarization and depolarization of the heart muscle during each cardiac cycle can be detected by sensors connected to the skin. Many disorders, including cardiovascular disease, can be diagnosed using these changes. It makes it possible to depict the patient’s heart rhythm more accurately. “The P-wave indicates the depolarization of the atria, the QRS complex indicates the depolarization of the ventricles, and the T-wave indicates the repolarization of the ventricles on an electrocardiogram (ECG) graph [
].” Several investigations have shown ECG plots to be capable of differentiating between COVID-19-positive persons, patients with various cardiac conditions, and healthy individuals [
]. The last portion of the material that was just given mentions a number of these academic books. An ECG is shown in Fig.

. The COVID-19 diagnostic test needs to be as accurate as possible economically and generate as few false positives and negatives as feasible. Put differently, it needs to be extremely precise. If the deep learning technique is used to analyze patient electrocardiograms (ECGs), it may be able to detect the COVID-19 virus.

div.p: An illustration depicts an electrocardiogram signal, the T wave, which is consistently upright in lead and inverted. There are seven points, P, Q, R, S, J, T, and U and these points divide the E C G wave into 3 intervals P R, Q R S, and Q T, and a segment S T.
Fig. 1
Graph depicting the normal ECG pattern and its individual components

p.span: Below is a summary of the significant contributions:

li.p: For the purposes of this study, ECG images are classified into multiple classes using the OWAE model. For binary classification, we employ deep learning models that have been pre-trained.
The outcomes produced by the use of several categories were arrived at by employing a method known as the weighted average ensemble technique.

The proportions of the weights that were first given to deep models have been made better. Through the use of grid search, the findings indicate that the grid search strategy was effective in producing better results.

p.span: A new coronavirus known as COVID-19 posed a global danger in December 2019 [
]. “As of July 31, 2021, the WHO reported 190 million COVID-19 cases and 4 million deaths globally [
].” Concern was raised globally by the disease’s rapid spread and a sharp increase in new infections [

]. Curfews, travel restrictions, and nationwide lockdowns were problematic. “In addition, the excess of COVID-19 infections that require beds and the shortage of essential medical supplies and equipment would lead to the demise of numerous healthcare organizations. Therefore, identifying COVID-19 quickly and accurately is crucial to reducing mortality and freeing up medical personnel. The Chinese government uses the real-time reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) test as the COVID-19 gold standard [
].” Qualified professionals must conduct the RT-PCR testing, and misdiagnosis could result from delayed sample procurement and strict laboratory setup requirements [

]. Therefore, better procedures are needed for faster and more accurate diagnosis. Examples include antigen tests and imaging methods like CT and X-rays. RT-PCR assays are less expensive, take longer, and yield worse accuracy than COVID-19 antigen assays. The World Health Organization does not recommend antigen testing due to its high false-negative rate and low sensitivity [
]. Scohy et al. [
] RT-PCR is more sensitive than it is. “Diagnostic imaging methods for lung diseases include CT and X-rays. X-ray and CT modalities are more accurate than RT-PCR, as numerous studies [

]” have shown. A radiologist’s knowledge is necessary for the diagnosis of certain imaging technology. The similarities between the novel coronavirus and known illnesses complicate the identification of COVID-19 [
]. Because manual inquiry takes a lot of time, automatic diagnostic tools are required.
AI is used to construct automated diagnostic systems that effectively analyze biosignals and pictures. Their prognosis and diagnosis of diseases are improved [
]. The review [
] claims that AI can aid the transmission of the novel coronavirus. Automated radiograph-based diagnostic methods for COVID-19 diagnosis are developed using deep learning (DL) [

]. While there are several radiographic imaging techniques for diagnosing COVID-19, they have limitations. Some drawbacks include high cost, immobility, radiation exposure, and the need for seasoned photographers [
]. Multimodal diagnostic techniques are needed to diagnose COVID-19 during the pandemic. The respiratory and cardiovascular systems are primarily affected by COVID-19 [
]. Cardiovascular problems have been found in numerous COVID-19 trials. The variations include conduction anomalies [
], arrhythmias [
], ST segment divergence of the PR interval [
], and QRST alterations [
]. Many cardiac abnormalities are visible on COVID-19 patients’ electrocardiograms (ECGs). ECG data can be used to detect coronaviruses because of these changes in the circulatory system [

]. Because of its affordability, portability, ease of use, safety, and real-time monitoring, automated COVID-19 diagnostic procedures based on ECG data may be particularly beneficial in conjunction with imaging techniques and PCR testing. Therefore, more research is needed to determine the viability of ECG diagnosis.

The databases listed below were also searched for papers pertaining to the present topic, in addition to Google Scholar. Among our peers are “Elsevier, Wiley, Knowledge E, SBMU Journals, Jaypee Brothers Medical Publishing, MDPI, IEEE, Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory, Tech Science Press, and arXiv [

].” Jaypee Brothers Medical Publications is another one of our partners. Data from a minimum of two participant groups and an accuracy measure are required for papers to be considered for admission; at least one of the participant groups must be made up of COVID-19 participants. Articles also need to have an accuracy metric. It is essential to use the second group as a control. The months of April through November 2022 were dedicated to the task of finding documents. Among the items in the COVID-19 product family are “COVID-19 ECG Detection, COVID-19 Voice Detection, COVID-19 Artificial Intelligence Detection, and COVID-19 Accurate Voice Detection [

Due to COVID-19, 483,000 results were retrieved for our search. “Twelve thousand articles were found to have “COVID-19 detection.” We first conducted a keyword search for “COVID-19 detection ECG” (950 articles), “COVID-19 detection voice” (2100 papers), and “COVID-19 detection X-ray” (1000 papers). We then selected X-ray, voice, and ECG as biomedical signal or picture sources. Next, we identified the body parts most impacted by the illness and looked for the most prevalent biological cues and images (6300 papers). By adding “accuracy” to our previous search, we were able to find 855 articles with the phrase “COVID-19 detection ECG accuracy,” 1750 papers with the phrase “COVID-19 detection voice accuracy,” and 6020 papers with the phrase “COVID-19 detection X-ray accuracy.“ All these studies matched our inclusion criteria when using precision as a classification parameter. After applying the exclusion criterion for reviews, systematic reviews, duplicate publications, and irrelevant abstracts, we were left with 550 papers. Two hundred forty-nine research projects were turned down for need for more disclosure regarding the number of samples utilized for testing or training, the number of samples in the database, or public access to the database [

].” The retraction led to the rejection of one manuscript. Two hundred eighty papers were eliminated in the last stage of our search because they did not provide a code or the relevant technique needed to be sufficiently described for us to duplicate the recommended approach. Eventually, twenty publications were included in our analysis.

Our analysis reveals several problems with the reference-gathering processes. For instance, utilizing Google Scholar as a search engine can have serious disadvantages, even though “it can recommend papers from a variety of publishers or even analyze an entire page. The search engine may omit some original research publications if, for example, we configure it to reject reviews, systematic reviews, and cutting-edge articles. The lack of documentation or even sharing of algorithms constituted another obstacle to the proposed strategy’s replication, resulting in the rejection of publications that failed to give readers the tools necessary to replicate and fully understand the methodology. The code and classification model are the most pertinent resources to provide [
],” as the goal of this evaluation is the automated detection of COVID-19.
Figure
displays the number of publications that were retrieved from each publisher for this review.

level3.h3: 4 Deep Convolution Transfer Learning via Neural Networks

p.span: Deep learning was used on six well-known CNN models that had already been taught to tell COVID-19 from other things in ECG trace images. Throughout their first training phases, ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101, DenseNet201, InceptionV3, and MobileNetV3 all made use of ImageNet. ResNet [

], also called the Residual Network, was made to solve problems caused by degradation and the removal of gradients. There are numerous versions of ResNet, with ResNet18, ResNet50, ResNet101, and ResNet152 being the most frequent. These values represent the remaining network tiers. ResNets are frequently employed for transfer learning, which is particularly advantageous when classifying images of biological objects. ResNet, in contrast to the conventional behavior of deep neural network layers [
], which is to learn either low- or high-level features, learns residuals rather than features during the training phase. See the structure of a convolutional neural network [
] in Fig.

div.p: An architecture of a connection depicts layers includes image maps, input, output, fully connected, convolutions, and subsamples.
Fig. 4
Arrangement of the convolutional neural network’s structural layout

p.span: Compared to a CNN, a dense convolutional network, also known as a DenseNet, has a lower number of training parameters [

]. This is due to the fact that DenseNet does not need to perform redundant feature mappings. DenseNet’s thin layers limit feature map additions. DenseNet includes four variants: 264, 169, 121, and 20. DenseNet provides gradients and the original input image. DenseNet’s low processing cost makes it the most used picture categorization method. Comparing small networks to MobileNetv2 is difficult [

]. There are no additional convolutional layers after the first layer in a neural network. MobileNet only uses depth-wise separable convolutions, with the exception of the final fully connected layer, which uses fully connected convolutions instead. This layer is linear, and it supplies information to a Softmax layer that is in charge of categorization. The batch normalization of all of the layers is performed by the nonlinear ReLU technique. Before joining the layers together, a final average pooling is carried out, which results in the spatial resolution being reduced to only one. Convolutions, both point-wise and depth-wise, are spread out across MobileNet’s 28 layers to form the network. Inception networks make use of inception blocks to reduce dimensionality through the use of layered convolutions. This paves the way for deeper networks and improves the processing capabilities of the system.
The process that needs to be followed to carry out the research is shown in Fig.

. This study presents the findings from three distinct test types: “Three different systems of classification are available: (1) a binary system, which consists of two classes (normal and COVID-19); (2) a three-class method, which includes cardiac abnormalities, COVID-19, and normal; and (3) a five-class system. The first experiment was a two-class system (normal and COVID-19) or binary classification (normal, COVID-19, MI, AHB, and RMI). Six highly advanced CNN models were trained, examined, and assessed to determine whether anomalies were present in ECG trace photos. It was carried out for all possible classification schemes [

div.p: A flowchart depicts stages from E C G image input and data preprocessing to application models like C N N, H N N, naive bayes, L S T M, S U M, resnet, inception, decision tree, and MobileNet. Evaluation includes metrics like accuracy, recall, and P S C O R E, with outcomes categorized as normal.
Fig. 5
Overview of the methodology

p.span: Out of the five ECG traces obtained for this study, only one was considered to be normal. Three abnormalities were identified on the COVID-19 ECG trace photographs: an erratic heartbeat, a recovered MI, and a MI. Medical terminology states that if an electrocardiogram (ECG) is obtained from a healthy individual without any known heart issues, it is considered normal. Acute coronary syndrome can result in myocardial infarction, often known as a heart attack, which is a condition that seriously damages the heart. The best way to diagnose this issue is with an electrocardiogram monitor. Tightness or soreness in the chest is the most common sign of this illness. Furthermore, we have included pictures of the ECG traces of COVID-19 survivors who had breathing difficulties or other respiratory problems but were able to withstand the virus. There have been some survivors in spite of these symptoms. Viewing ECG trace images is restricted to myocardial infarction survivors. These little changes—such as a wave or a peak-to-peak interval—are typically utilized to recognize and diagnose cardiac abnormalities and their accompanying characteristics, like ST-segmentation shift,
-wave height, and
-wave abnormalities. A specific wave or peak-to-peak interval is used. The two rare species shown in Fig.

each have a unique collection of traits. Because deep CNN models are unable to gather distinctive features, their capacity to analyze complex visual data is restricted and makes it more difficult for them to arrange the data.

div.p: An E C G scan depicts S T segment elevation at the J point in anterior leads V 3 and V 4, as well as in septal and lateral leads.

Abnormal ECGs in patients with COVID-19 and b-type myocardial infarction. The crucial indicators for anomaly detection are highlighted in red and marked with arrows

COVID analysis using an ECG: This module allows the user to upload an ECG image, after which algorithms are applied to determine whether or not COVID-19 is present.

p.span: This discrepancy and the CNN model’s general efficacy indicate that COVID-19 could be identified by categorizing ECG pictures. 479 ECG images were used in the research in this publication. A total of 479 images were acquired; 239 belonged to COVID-19 patients, and 241 to healthy people or people suffering from different illnesses, such as pneumonia. Two COVID-19 classes totaling 238 samples are included in the basic ECG datasets. Because the sample was imbalanced, preprocessing was required. Only 54% of the original ECG datasets were accurate when CNN was created, making them unsuitable for the intended use. In this case, performance limits of up to 100% right predictions, 100% precision, 100% recall, 100% FSCORE, and 0% incorrect predictions were met [
]” (Table

p.span: ECG datasets are trained by “employing a wide variety of machine learning and deep learning algorithms, some of which include KNN, SVM, logistic regression, decision tree, Naive Bayes, CNN, LSTM, InceptionV3, ResNet, and MobileNet, among others [

level3.p: In conclusion, this paper presented a fully automated procedure for detecting COVID-19 infection in electrocardiogram (ECG) data. The study utilized various machine learning and deep learning algorithms, including KNN, SVM, logistic regression, decision tree, Naive Bayes, CNN, LSTM, InceptionV3, ResNet, and MobileNet, to train and test ECG datasets. The results showed excellent performance in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, FSCORE, and false prediction rate for all the algorithms tested. The proposed deep learning models, particularly CNN, exhibited high accuracy and effectiveness in handling binary classification for COVID-19 detection in ECG images.

Comparisons with other studies using the same ECG datasets demonstrated the superiority of the proposed approach, as it achieved 100% accuracy, precision, recall, FSCORE, and zero false prediction rate, outperforming other methods such as LSTM, InceptionV3, ResNet, and MobileNet. The findings suggest that COVID-19 can be reliably recognized by a system trained on digital ECG signals using deep learning techniques.

The implementation of the proposed system involved a homepage providing COVID-19 precautions, a preliminary analysis module to assess symptoms, and a COVID analysis module utilizing ECG images for COVID-19 detection. The experiments conducted using the ECG scans demonstrated the successful prediction of COVID-19 presence or absence. The CNN model showed promising results, indicating the potential for diagnosing COVID-19 by analyzing ECG images.

level2.h2: Analyzing Twitter Data Using Apache Hive—A Big Data Technology Exploration

p.span: Kanhaiya Sharma
Mansi Kapshe
Parth Bhargava
Prakhar Trivedi
Sanika Changde
and
Om Mishra

div.p: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Symbiosis Institute of Technology Pune, Symbiosis International (Deemed University), Pune, India

level3.p: Kanhaiya Sharma

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: In the daily lives of individuals, the impact of our personal beliefs may go unnoticed, but they have the potential to influence others significantly. Prominent figures and beloved celebrities, often through endorsements and interviews, possess substantial power to mold public perceptions and worldviews. The Internet has ushered in an era of rapid information dissemination, resulting in an explosive growth of textual data across the digital landscape. Modern individuals express their thoughts and opinions on a multitude of online platforms, leading to a continuous surge in data generation, growing in significance with each passing day. Notably, Twitter, a prominent social media platform, witnesses the daily posting of millions of tweets, serving as a valuable information source for numerous individuals. These tweets harbor great potential for application in business, small-scale industries, policy formulation, and various research endeavors. To harness valuable insights from this incessant data influx, organizations must adopt new tools and techniques to gain a competitive advantage, directly impacting profits in today’s knowledge-driven economy. This paper thoroughly examines emerging big data technologies, focusing on elucidating their key attributes and operational mechanisms while offering a comparative analysis from a business perspective.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: This modern age has taken to the origination of massive amounts of various data in many other formats, and there are numerous sources. Our everyday online activities, such as browsing the Internet, sending emails, making calls, and paying bills, all produce data that requires to be stored. Thousands of disks or hard drives have been needed to store these massive data volumes. Big data has reached production, i.e., 2.85 zettabytes in 2022, which is expected to be five times every two years, going nine zettabytes by the end of 2025 [
], and a detailed description is given in Fig.

. Social networking sites and the multimedia industries are the primary sources of big data creation. About 3.3 million pieces of material are shared on Facebook alone every minute, while 347.22 million tweets and 100 h of YouTube videos are shared on Twitter. Big data analytics is made more accessible with the help of Hadoop and related ecosystems, which are commonly utilized to extract commercial value from this massive volume of data [

]. The astonishing increase in zettabytes (ZB) of annual data demonstrates the remarkable leap in data output. The volume of data produced increased steadily from 2 ZB in 2010 to an astounding 181 ZB in 2025. A consistent trend can be seen in the growth over the preceding year, with significant increases in some years. For example, there was a 30% growth between 2011 and 2012, whereas there was a 16.13% increase in 2016. But there was the most significant uptick in 2020, with a staggering 56.59% increase over the year before. The exponential growth of digital information is reflected in this data explosion, highlighting the necessity for sophisticated technology and analytics to manage and extract knowledge from such massive databases. Factors such as the broad use of digital technology, the rise in Internet-connected gadgets, and the pervasive usage of social media platforms drive exponential development in data output. There are five sections in this research. The second section discusses the challenges of handling data from social media platforms using big data. The third section reviews the latest big data technologies. The fourth section presents the performance analysis of an Apache Hive query processing Twitter data. Finally, the fifth section emphasizes the significance of advanced big data technology for data management and analytics.

div.p: A bar graph of data generated versus year from 2010 to 2025. The graph plots exponential growth from 2010 to 2025. The maximum and minimum data generated is 170 in 2025 and 5 in 2010.
Fig. 1
Data generation trends

level3.h3: 2 Big Data and Twitter Data Analysis

p.span: The Hadoop ecosystem comprises an extensive array of open-source software tools and frameworks designed to handle the storing, processing collaboratively, and, finally, analysis of vast amounts of data. Among its essential components are the HDFS and MapReduce, which can be responsible for managing data storage and processing operations, as well as additional tools like Apache Hive, Apache Pig, and Apache; further tools like Apache Spark, Apache Kafka, and Apache ZooKeeper enhance Hadoop’s functionality and enable real-time data processing, streaming, and synchronization. Businesses and researchers may use big data to their advantage and make informed decisions thanks to the Hadoop ecosystem. Some emerging technologies build the entire Hadoop ecosystem [

]. Apache Pig can manage data and eat almost anything. Pig script authoring is nearly five times faster than MapReduce application development. In addition to that, it generates far less code. This reduces the time required for testing and development [

]. Hive is created on Hadoop; Apache Hive is used as an open-source tool that facilitates the querying and analyzing of extensive datasets. Hive allows users to perform read–write functions and manage large datasets using an SQL-like query language, and it enables users to formulate complex data queries with ease and efficiency without the need for extensive programming knowledge. In [
], Twitter tweets data analyzed using Hive query. In Fig.

, we visually represent the results of sentiment analysis conducted on Twitter data, providing a graphical representation of sentiments. Apache Zookeeper: Apache provides a dependable and scalable foundation for distributed messaging systems; Zookeeper and Apache Kafka are two crucial elements of the Apache software ecosystem. To maintain coordination, synchronization, and configuration of data among dispersed nodes, ZooKeeper serves as a central service. In contrast, the distributed streaming platform Kafka allows for the archiving and processing of real-time data streams. The efficient method is used, and fault-tolerant operation of disturbed messaging systems is ensured by the tool ZooKeeper and Kafka together, providing them the best option for appliances requiring real-time, high-throughput, low-latency data streaming and processing. Apache “is an open-source tool” that supplies a centralized architecture for synchronizing operations across the Hadoop cluster. It utilizes a hierarchical name space design, where every node is called a Z-node. In a conventional UNIX-like system, these Z-nodes are close to files and are likely to be altered by any node in the cluster. Whenever a node modifies a Z-node, it notifies it of its changes—overall architecture of Zookeeper for Kafka to store metadata about the Kafka cluster [
]. Zookeeper architecture is shown in Figs.
and

p.span: Twitter data collection [

Apache Flume: Flume is a Hadoop program that transfers large volumes of streaming data into HDFS. Stream data can come from sensors, logs, machines, and social media. Apache Flume has different components: Source, slink, channel, and delete. The main source component receives data into Slime. The sink sends the data to the requisitioned destination, and the channel component acts as the intermediary between mainstream and slink. The Flume runs on a physical Java virtual machine, which transfers streaming data into HDFS [
]; the working architecture of Flume with Twitter data is shown in Fig.

div.p: A block diagram of flume with Twitter data. The flume consists of twitter source, to memory channel, to H D F S sink. The Twitter source has an input from twitter streaming A P I and output from H D F S sink to H D F S.
Fig. 5

p.span: Architecture of Apache Flume [
This study analyzes user trends through big data collected from Twitter platforms. The following are some significant components of Twitter’s daily data generation:

li.p: Tweets: Users share their views, opinions, and updates by tweeting text, photographs, or videos. Millions of tweets are sent out daily, covering various subjects and trends.
Retweets are when individuals forward other people’s tweets to their followers. This activity helps information spread quickly on Twitter by expanding the original content’s audience.
Likes: Users can give a tweet a like to show their support or gratitude. This keeps track of a tweet’s popularity and makes it easier to spot trending topics.
Twitter users can participate in conversations by leaving comments or replying to tweet. This encounter produces additional information and insights into conversations and user interactions.
Hashtags and Trends: Twitter data provides information on trending hashtags, which show the most well-liked topics currently being discussed on the platform.
User profiles: The massive amount of data collected on Twitter is derived mainly from the information in each user’s profile, including their username, bio, location, and follower count.
Every activity on Twitter has a timestamp, which enables businesses and researchers to analyze trends over time and keep track of current events.

p.span: Twitter tweets stored in “JSON format on an Apache Hive cloud-based solution were retrieved and analyzed using Microsoft Azure cloud services.” HDInsight Hadoop solution was used to set up Apache Hive, while Microsoft Azure Blob Storage was used as the second Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) service. Twitter live data was retrieved through the Twitter Streaming API and saved in Blob Storage before being moved into a Hive table. Apache Hive queries were then run on a cluster of HDInsight nodes of varying sizes to analyze the tweets from Twitter. The process involved creating a cluster on Microsoft Azure’s cloud data center in East US and storing input and output data in Blob Storage. A Windows 7 host system and Windows Server 2012 guest machine were used to establish the cluster, and a Hive query was run after the Azure cluster was created to generate the necessary outcomes. Its status is shown in Table

div.p: Table 1
Microsoft Azure cloud service
A table with 3 columns and 3 rows. The column headers are name, type, and status with the first row highlighted reads h d storage sanika, storage account, and online.

p.span: The computation of tweet counts involved a Hive query on information kept in a Hive table. The performance of the HDInsight cluster used to run the query was evaluated based on the overall MapReduce CPU time and job run time. The cluster sizes tested were 1, 2, 4, and 6 nodes, each defined by the number of Master Nodes, Secondary Nodes, and Data Nodes running on separate machines. Detailed architecture of actual architecture is shown in Fig.

. In particular, a single-node point possesses the Master Node and the other node, which is the Data Node running on the same system; a two-node cluster has the Master Node and Data Node running on distinct machines, and a quadruple-node cluster has the Master Node, Secondary Node, and double Data Nodes running on various devices. One Master Node, one Secondary Node, and four Data Nodes operating on several machines comprise a six-node cluster. Table
presents a comparative analysis of previous research, showcasing its application within the realm of big data.

level3.p: The ever-increasing volume of data, often overwhelming traditional databases, underscores the significance of big data analysis for enterprises and researchers. This research paper highlights the pivotal role of contemporary tools such as HDFS, Pig, Hadoop, and others in efficiently managing vast datasets, ultimately accelerating development processes and enhancing overall performance. Notably, integrating Apache Hive for Twitter data analysis unveils invaluable insights into consumer behavior, sentiment analysis, and social media interactions. This empowers businesses to make informed decisions and enhance their competitive positioning in the market. The integration of Twitter data analysis augments the capabilities of big data processing. To advance this field, future endeavors should prioritize refining these technologies and integrating diverse data sources to derive comprehensive insights, fostering strategic decision-making and sustaining performance excellence in the competitive landscape. Leveraging state-of-the-art techniques reveals a treasure trove of insightful data for informed decision-making.

level2.h2: Detection of Phishing Websites Using Link Guard Algorithm

p.span: V. Laxmi Aishwarya
T. Srujan
S. Deepak
V. Bhavan
and
V. Pradeep Kumar

div.p: Department of Computer Science Engineering, B V Raju Institute of Technology, Narsapur, India

level3.p: V. Pradeep Kumar

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: Phishing is a brand-new form of network attack where the attacker creates a copy of an already-existing web page to deceive users into providing personal, financial, or password information to what they believe to be the website of their service provider (e.g., by using specially crafted emails or instant messaging). Using the common traits of hyperlinks used in phishing assaults, we suggested a novel end-host-based anti-phishing algorithm, which we term Link Guard, in this research article. Link Guard can recognize both known and unidentified phishing attacks because it is based on the general features of these attacks. In Windows 10, Link Guard has been implemented. Our tests showed that Link Guard can effectively identify and stop both known and unidentified phishing attacks with a low rate of false negatives. Eighty-five to ninety-five percent of phishing attempts are effectively detected by Link Guard. Our tests also demonstrated that Link Guard is lightweight and capable of quickly recognizing and preventing phishing assaults.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: The exponential internet growth has catalyzed a rise in phishing attacks, surpassing the capabilities of traditional defense methods. In response, the “Link Guard” Algorithm emerges as a solution, leveraging data analysis and machine learning for real-time identification of potential phishing websites. Gathering a diverse dataset containing genuine and phishing URLs, Link Guard extracts crucial features to discern between authentic and fraudulent sites. Employing decision trees, random forests, and neural networks, it trains models on this data, utilizing ensemble methods to heighten accuracy. Its real-time scoring system assesses URLs upon user access, assigning a phishing likelihood score, facilitating informed decisions. The primary project objective revolves around fortifying internet security and shielding users from phishing scams. Evaluation entails comparing Link Guard’s performance against existing methods, assessing metrics like precision, recall, accuracy, and
1-score, illuminating the algorithm’s efficacy in identifying phishing websites and its potential to significantly elevate online security standards.

The “Detection of Phishing Websites using Link Guard Algorithm” is a significant contribution in Cyber Security, standing out in the field of diverse phishing detection methodologies. Unlike papers like [
], it employs a hands-on, real-time approach for threat mitigation. This algorithm, akin to [

], leverages machine learning, emphasizing adaptive defense against evolving cyber threats. It excels in identifying subtle signs of phishing attempts within URLs, distinguishing itself from studies like [
]. Further, [
] highlights the importance of machine learning in enhancing detection accuracy—Link Guard harnesses this power for behavioral analysis. Reference [
] highlights specialized domain focus, a characteristic of the Link Guard Algorithm. Lastly, the integration of user feedback, akin to [

], allows continuous refinement, reinforcing its effectiveness. In essence, the Link Guard Algorithm, influenced by various research methodologies, offers a comprehensive and promising solution in the fight against phishing attacks in the realm of Cyber Security.

level3.p: Unwanted emails disrupt productivity and connectivity, while phishing sites pose severe threats, risking financial and reputational damage through identity theft. Identifying and blocking these sites are essential to prevent cybercriminals from exploiting compromised computers for illegal online activities like hacking, phishing, and malware dissemination. Necessary precautions are vital for self-protection against such harmful actions.

Develop a potent anti-phishing algorithm in order to accurately identify and detect phishing websites. To give consumers immediate protection, employ web browsers’ anti-phishing algorithms or security software. To take into consideration fresh and developing phishing techniques, the anti-phishing algorithm must to be updated and enhanced on a regular basis.

Effective phishing attack defense uses a variety of current strategies. First, human identification and prompt banning of phishing websites are essential. To stop consumers from visiting phony websites entails continually monitoring and recognizing suspicious URLs. Second, it is crucial to improve website security while it is still in the planning stages. Websites become more resistant to phishing efforts by adding strong security measures, such as encryption methods and regular software upgrades. Third, numerous spam filter software programs are used to stop phishing emails. These technologies analyze incoming emails using sophisticated algorithms and reroute or filter those that appear suspicious. Finally, consumer PCs can be protected further by installing online anti-phishing software. These programs analyze websites, emails, and other online information to look for signs of phishing and send notifications or automatically restrict access to dangerous domains. Users and organizations may greatly lower their chance of falling victim to phishing attempts by combining these strategies, which will also help to maintain a secure online environment.

The proposed work aims to develop and implement the Link Guard Algorithm, an advanced anti-phishing method, targeting the identification and prevention of phishing websites. This innovative algorithm employs modern techniques like URL structure analysis, domain reputation assessment, SSL certificate verification, and content evaluation to differentiate between trustworthy and malicious sites. Continuously learning and adapting using data mining and machine learning, it promises high accuracy in spotting new phishing tactics. Integrated into web browsers or security applications, it offers real-time website assessment, alerting users when potentially harmful sites are detected. The algorithm’s proactive approach empowers users by warning against interacting with phishing sites, enabling informed decisions and self-defense. By addressing identity theft, financial risks, and system compromise, this approach enhances internet security; ensuring users can confidently navigate online with peace of mind.

p.span: At the heart of a Windows 10 application lies the Link Guard Algorithm, part of a system encompassing Comm, Database, Analyzer, Alerter, and Logger components. The Analyzer, pivotal in processing, utilizes data from Comm and the Database, employing the Link Guard Algorithm to evaluate email credibility and relay findings to Alerter and Logger modules. This amalgamation efficiently detects and mitigates phishing risks, enhancing overall security and user awareness. Integrated into Windows 10, the Link Guard Algorithm comprises a Link Guard executive and the Whook.dll library. Whook.dll dynamically embeds into executing processes, gathering user-input URLs, visual links, and sender email addresses. Using event hooks, Browser Helper Object configuration, and Outlook access, Whook.dll filters and transmits refined data to the Link Guard executive for further analysis, as shown in the organizational structure (Fig.
). This system’s integration and functionality aid in proactively identifying and addressing phishing threats, contributing to a more secure computing environment within Windows 10.

div.p: A block diagram of the link guard algorithm connects blocks of logger, alerter, comm, and database to the algorithm analyzer. The algorithm also connects to layers of firefox with whook dot d l l and messenger, and outlook with whook dot d l l and i e.
Fig. 1
System architecture of Link Guard Algorithm

p.span: An investigation based on phishing email archives from 2003 to 2005, provided by APWG, highlights distinct traits of phishing URLs. Four primary categories emerge: URLs showing different destination DNS names but displaying DNS domain names in the anchor text (45%), substitution of dotted decimal IP addresses for DNS names (42%), deliberate encoding techniques to falsify hyperlinks (17.24%), and the use of DNS names in the URI to mimic reputable businesses or organizations. Additionally, a smaller percentage includes phishing attacks leveraging cross-site scripting (CSS) vulnerabilities or redirecting users to malicious sites (2%) (Table
; Fig.

Comparison of phishing email hyperlink attributes with bars in blue and red showing existing and current percentages, respectively, for Cname, Dot-decimal, Bytes, DNS URLs, and Attackers

level3.p: The project aimed to deploy the Link Guard Algorithm for enhanced internet security by detecting phishing websites, overcoming the limitations of traditional defense methods. Link Guard, utilizing advanced machine learning and URL analysis, demonstrated superior performance, reducing false positives and offering a reliable defense against online fraud, marking a significant stride in cybersecurity.

level2.h2: Forecasting Financial Commodities Using an Evolutionary Optimized Higher-Order Artificial Neural Network

p.span: Sudersan Behera
A. V. S. Pavan Kumar
and
Sarat Chandra Nayak

div.p: Department of CSE, SOET, GIET University, Gunupur, India
Department of CSE, SOT, GITAM University, Hyderabad, India

level3.p: Sudersan Behera
(Corresponding author)
Email:
A. V. S. Pavan Kumar

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: The dynamic nonlinearity approach and data series make financial time series prediction difficult. This research suggests the hybridization of an improved Firefly Algorithm (IFFA) and a higher-order artificial neural network termed as pi-sigma neural network (IFFA-PSNN) to forecast the future value of two financial time series (FTS). Two real-time financial commodities—crude oil and natural oil—are used to assess the suggested model. We evaluated the IFFA-PSNN using mean squared error (MSE) for one-day-ahead forecasting. The IFFA-PSNN is compared to several forecasting models, including the basic Firefly Algorithm (FFA)-based PSNN (FFA-PSNN), genetic algorithm (GA)-based PSNN (GA-PSNN), differential evolution (DE)-based PSNN (DE-PSNN), and gradient descent (GD)-based PSNN (GD-PSNN). The results show that the IFFA-PSNN model has the lowest MSE and very well integrates crude oil and natural gas data uncertainty.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: End-of-day financial market valuations are chaotic, nonlinear, and easily influenced by global economic and political happenings. FTS values are hard to predict. Market and macroeconomic factors cause huge FTS changes that appear random [
]. Even a relatively accurate stock market projection might provide uninformed investors with clues about the market's future. Articles [
] use statistical models for FTS forecasting. Soft and evolutionary-based methods have outperformed statistical ones in the last two decades [

ANNs and other forms of soft computing have been useful in many different contexts, including pattern reorganization, function approximation, prediction, recommendation systems, classification, and so on. Compared to modern ANN, traditional ANN, which often includes several hidden layers of neurons and provides ‘black box’ computation and visualization, is slower to converge and can become stuck in local minima. However, higher-order artificial neural networks, including those with either no hidden layers or just one, outperformed conventional ANNs. Sahu et al. [
] combined the power of Extreme Learning with metaheuristic optimization to enhance the precision of exchange rate forecasting. Dash et al. [
] tested and suggested that a PSNN is a suitable prediction model for exchange rates. Nayak [
] forecast crude oil prices using a PSNN. Nayak et al.’s PSNN index forecasting model performed well on the stock markets. Dash et al. showed that PSNN enhanced exchange rate predictions [
]. Nayak [
] found that PSNN model was consistent and accurate. Yilmaz et al. [
] used a PS-ANN to forecast other financial time series data. Pattanayak et al. [
] forecasted fuzzy time series data using PSNNs. Nayak and Naik studied a reliable, accurate PSNN classifier [
]. Kanungo et al. found PSNN as a best classifier [
]. Dash [
] used a PSNN to study currency exchange rates. Pattanayak et al. [
] engaged a PSNN and analyzed fuzzy time series. Sahoo et al. [
] found that a PSNN predicted BSE stock prices successfully. Nayak et al. [
] used a fast and accurate PSNN classifier. Nayak et al. [

] used an adaptive evolutionary PSNN (AE-PSNN) to predict stock market fluctuations. In this paper, we propose a PSNN model that utilizes an improved version of the firefly (IFFA) algorithm in order to establish a hybrid model for predicting the prices of financial commodities and then compare it to other hybrid models developed utilizing PSNN in conjunction with the FFA [
], GA [
], DE [
], and GD [
] optimization techniques.

The remaining part of this article is divided into four parts. We will discuss about firefly algorithm, Nelder–Mead Simplex Algorithm, improved Firefly Algorithm, PSNN, and IFFA-PSNN model under Sect.
. Section
discusses about the experimental setup and datasets used, and Sect.
examines and evaluates experimental outcomes of all the models. Section
offers some last thoughts, discusses the scope of the existing research, and outlines some potential future research works.
The Firefly Algorithm (FFA) is a metaheuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the flashing behavior of fireflies. It was first proposed by Xin-She Yang in 2008 [

]. FFA is based on the collective behavior of fireflies, where their flashing patterns are used for communication and mate attraction. Interested readers can find out the working principles of FFA and its pseudocode from article [

Nelder–Mead Simplex Algorithm (NMSA) is an optimization technique used to find the maximum and minimum of an objective function in a multidimensional space. It was introduced by Nelder and Mead in 1965 [
] and is particularly well-suited for problems where the objective function is continuous but may not be differentiable. The pseudo code and the working style of NMSA can be found out from article [

To enhance the exploration and exploitation abilities of the FFA, we incorporated NMSA parameters with existing FFA. By combining the FFA with the NMSA method, we introduced a more refined exploration and exploitation mechanism. The FFA provides the exploration capability by using the attractiveness function to guide fireflies toward brighter ones. The NMSA method, on the other hand, brings in the exploitation ability by refining the positions of the fireflies using the simplex operations. This modified approach allows for a more balanced exploration of the search space through the FFA’s movement rules and improved exploitation by leveraging the simplex operations from the Nelder–Mead method. The pseudocode for the improved FFA (IFFA) optimization is given in Algorithm

p.span: PSNN is a higher-order artificial neural network (HONN) [

] proposed by Shin and Ghosh. It consists of three layers i.e., input, summation, and product layers. The summation layer receives the input signals along with weights and performs addition of weighted input signals, while the product layer receives the output from the summation layer.

Notably, the weight between the input layer and the summation layer is adjustable, but the weight between the summation layer and the product layer remains fixed at 1. This fixed weight contributes to a significant drop in training time, as the network utilizes only one set of weights. The summation layer employs linear activation functions in its units, whereas the product layer employs nonlinear activation functions. The addition of a new summation unit enhances the network's organization, while the product units allow for the expansion of the input space into higher dimensions. This expansion facilitates stronger nonlinear mapping without requiring exponential increases in weight values. The architecture of a PSNN forecasting model is given in Fig.

div.p: An architecture of a neural network defines how the n number of input nodes from the input layer with trainable weights undergo a summation layer to give the combined output with fixed weights. The output layer depicts the product unit and activation and undergoes a learning algorithm.

p.span: All of the experiments were carried out on a MATLAB 9.0 platform running in a Windows 10 operating system environment. The same setting was used for developing, training, and testing each model. We have used natural gas as well as crude oil datasets to train and test all of our models. Crude oil and natural gas closing prices over the past decade were gathered from

. The date of May 22, 2013, is the beginning of the collection period, which will last through May 22, 2023.The primary results and statistical features of crude oil datasets and natural gas datasets are summarized in Table

div.p: Table1
Description of datasets

p.span: -value

In order to train all the models, initially we adopted a sigmoid normalized method to scale up all the data into normal, and then we split the entire datasets into training and testing sets using a sliding window [

] of size three. Overall, a 60%:40% ratio has been maintained between the training and testing datasets. After the input vectors have been normalized, they are sent to the networks. In order to facilitate comparisons between the model’s outputs and the target values, the results are denormalized at the end. All models were built according to the same specifications for a fair comparison. As the sliding window progresses, we discover that the results improve. Accordingly, the oldest data point is removed when a new one is added, which could mean that there is not much variation in nonlinear behavior between different time intervals. In this way, a new, randomly generated set of parameters is used for training rather than always maximizing the same ones (weight and bias). This type of training is called adaptive training. Iterations are reduced for future training sets after the network has been trained on the initial datasets. Because less iteration is needed, training could be accomplished in less time.

At first, the effectiveness of IFFA was examined and compared to that of other evolutionary algorithms like FFA, GA, and DE in the optimization of two widely used benchmark test functions: Ackley’s function [
], defined as in Eq. (
), and Rosenbrock’s function [
], defined as in Eq. (

A line graph of closing price versus years from 2013 to 2023. It plots 3 fluctuating trends for the training data, estimated value price, and actual value price. Each trend has multiple spikes and dips. Mid 2020 traces the lowest value for the estimated value price. Data are approximate.
Fig. 3
Close price forecast plot of natural gas

level3.p: By applying NMSA to the FFA, a modified version of the algorithm is created, and this improved FFA is known as IFFA. The PSNN model for forecasting the price of crude oil and natural gas uses IFFA and FFA for training. GA, DE, and GD algorithms are also used to train a PSNN model for the same purpose. After calculating the MSE for each model, it was found that the PSNN model based on IFFA (IFFA-PSNN) yielded the best results, with MSE = 0.041968 for crude oil and MSE = 0.042774 for natural gas.

Overall, the proposed IFFA-PSNN model outperformed and yielded superior results compared to the alternatives. We plan to combine the IFFA with additional ANNs in the future to develop new hybrid models, which we will then test on a variety of time series.

level2.h2: Plant Leaf Disease Classification Using GAN

p.span: D. R. L. Prasanna
Sai Kumar Reddy Barda
and
Swetha Vootkuri

div.p: Vasavi College of Engineering, Hyderabad, India

level3.p: D. R. L. Prasanna

level4.p: Email:

level3.p: Swetha Vootkuri
(Corresponding author)
Email:

level2.p: Plant diseases can be precisely detected using field leaves. However, collecting a sufficient number of unhealthy leaf images from various plants can be challenging, resulting in an unbalanced dataset. To address this issue, Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are employed. GANs consist of a generator and discriminator neural network. The generator creates synthetic data, while the discriminator distinguishes between real and generated samples. By adding GAN-generated images to an unbalanced dataset, the model’s performance improves, resulting in a more balanced dataset. This approach aids in accurate plant disease detection and benefits agriculture.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Plant leaves can be used to precisely identify plant diseases. The amount of ill leaf photos collected from various plants, however, is frequently excessive [

]. Disease identification is difficult because the damaged plant leaves are so unevenly distributed. Artificial intelligence approaches have significantly transformed plant disease detection and identification jobs from old methods, and these methods’ accuracy has also significantly increased.

Deep learning is currently the most effective method for using computers to detect plant diseases. To train models, deep learning needs a lot of photos, though. For model learning, a balanced and appropriate dataset for leaf disease detection must have enough critical information. Small datasets, particularly those with unequally distributed illness samples, which are prone to overfitting, cannot be used to train an efficient recognition algorithm [

]. The Plant Village database for the study of leaf disease detection noted the issue of uneven sample distribution and employed the methods of flipping and translating to augment the dataset. In comparison to regularization, different techniques can better enhance model performance and avoid overfitting.

level3.p: Plant diseases can be accurately detected using plant leaves, which makes it a reliable method for disease identification. However, the collection of an adequate number of images of unhealthy leaves from various plants is often challenging resulting in an unbalanced dataset. Using unbalanced plant diseases can significantly affect crop quality and output, resulting in financial losses for farmers and perhaps affecting food security. Utilizing machine learning techniques, automated plant disease detection can help to identify unhealthy plants early, enabling farmers to minimize their impact. There are typically more photos of healthy leaves than unhealthy ones in the datasets used to train machine learning algorithms for plant disease identification. Machine learning models may not perform well because of this imbalance. To increase the precision and dependability of automated plant disease detection systems, it is crucial to address the issue of imbalanced datasets in plant disease detection.

p.span: The implementation of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) is demonstrated in a recent paper that aims to explore the use of plant leaves to detect plant diseases and address the issue of unbalanced datasets in the field of plant disease detection. The primary objective of this problem statement is to develop a model or system that can accurately detect plant diseases using plant leaves as input while dealing with the issue of unbalanced datasets [

level3.p: Collecting an adequate number of unhealthy leaf images for different plants is often challenging, leading to an unbalanced dataset. Therefore, it is crucial to have a balanced and sufficient dataset that contains the necessary information for the models to learn and perform well. The implementation of GANs has been explored to supplement small datasets and to address the issue of unbalanced datasets. GANs are generative models that can generate synthetic data that can be used to supplement the small and unbalanced datasets. The generated data can help train models that are more accurate and robust, even with small datasets.

The proposed system or model aims to accurately detect plant diseases using plant leaves as input. The system will also address the issue of unbalanced datasets by using GANs to generate additional synthetic data that can help train the model more effectively. The implementation of GANs in the proposed system is expected to improve the accuracy and robustness of the model, even with small and unbalanced datasets.

p.span: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are a deep learning technique that generates new data by pitting two neural networks against each other: a generator and a discriminator. The generator creates data, while the discriminator distinguishes between real and generated data. Through adversarial training, the generator strives to produce more convincing data, while the discriminator becomes better at telling the difference [

GANs have diverse applications, from creating realistic visuals, music, and art to image style translation. While they have great potential, training GANs can be challenging, and they require ample data. Nevertheless, GANs are actively researched for their ability to produce innovative and unique data in various fields, like text, music, and images, benefiting computer vision, the arts, and data augmentation [

The VGG16 architecture is a convolutional neural network (CNN) developed by the Visual Geometry Group (VGG) at the University of Oxford. It is widely used in computer vision such as object recognition and image classification. VGG16 has 16 layers, including 13 convolutional layers and 3 full layers. The layers use a small 3 × 3 pixel filter with Step 1, while the maximum pooling layers are 2 × 2 pixels with Step 2. The total connected layer has 4096 neurons, and the output layer uses SoftMax processing to function to give the probability distribution. One of the main advantages of VGG16 is its simplicity and ease of use. Due to the use of small filters and maximum pooling layers, it can learn complex features in a hierarchical manner while increasing the number of parameters. This allows the network to train large datasets without the need for large budgets. VGG16 has demonstrated state-of-the-art performance on many image classification tasks, including the 2014 ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC), where it achieved the highest error of only 5% of 7.32%. Additionally, it has been used to transfer learning by showing that it can improve the performance of other computer vision programs even with limited training materials [

level4.p: In machine learning and computer vision, a technique called data augmentation is used to increase the size of data by creating new versions of existing data. This detailed information with many examples helps improve the performance of the model, and showing differences and disagreements helps reduce the risk of overfitting.

The use of different data management techniques depends on the type of data used. Various methods for image classification tasks include rotation, translation, scaling, and brightness and contrast adjustments. Language processing techniques may include operations such as word deletion, substitution, or collation.

With the use of these techniques, the database will gain diversity, including many changes and events. This increases the accuracy and robustness of the model. Adding randomness and unpredictability without data augmentation further reduces the risk of overfitting as it prevents the model from remembering the training data and encourages the acquisition of many features.

Data augmentation is especially important when training data is limited because it quickly and effectively improves the performance of machine learning models. This is a simple process that is incorporated into the training phase and helps increase the generality and accuracy of the model.

Deep learning is a subfield of machine learning that uses artificial neural networks to learn from large datasets and make predictions about new data. Deep learning in computer vision helps analyze and understand visual data such as images and videos.

Deep learning models for computer vision generally consist of multiple layers of neural networks designed to create hierarchical representations of input data. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) are a popular deep learning technique for computer vision that excels at learning spatial features in images. These models are typically trained on large domain datasets such as ImageNet or COCO.

p.span: A simple technology in computer vision deep learning is data augmentation, which not only expands the training data but also supports the processing work of the model. This involves using various image transformations such as flipping, rotating, or scaling to create new images from existing training data. Data augmentation increases the generalization and robustness of the model [

level4.p: Data augmentation is especially important when dealing with sparse information, which is common in computer vision problems. By creating different data patterns, the model becomes better at recognizing different types of images and adapting to new data. Deep learning and data augmentation are used in many fields, including object recognition and image classification.
Data preparation plays a vital role in the implementation of GANs. The subsequent steps outline the typical data preparation procedures for GANs.

level5.p: In the initial phase, it is imperative to collect the dataset that will serve as the foundation for training the GAN. This dataset must accurately represent the kind of data the GAN aims to generate. For instance, when generating images, it may involve amassing a substantial number of pictures depicting a specific object or location.
Once the dataset is assembled, data cleansing becomes essential. This process involves the removal of duplicates, elimination of flawed or damaged images, and ensuring uniformity in image size.

GANs often require images to be of a consistent size. Consequently, it is frequently necessary to resize the images within the dataset to meet these specific dimensions. Additionally, images may need to be normalized to ensure pixel values fall within a defined range, typically between 0 and 1.

Data augmentation techniques can be employed to diversify and expand the dataset, introducing variability into the data. These techniques may involve actions like flipping, rotation, or adding noise to the images.

The dataset should be partitioned into training, validation, and testing sets. The validation and testing sets are employed to evaluate the GAN’s performance with previously unseen data, while the training set is used for GAN training. In essence, data preprocessing represents a pivotal phase in the implementation of GANs.
Google Colabs, PyCharm, Datasets, Internet connectivity.
Processor: Intel Pentium, RAM: 4 GB or more, GPU: 2 GB or more, Hard Disk.

level6.p: An unbalanced dataset occurs when there is a significant difference in the number of samples for each class. When dealing with plant leaves, an imbalanced dataset with more healthy leaves than diseased ones can lead to biased model predictions. The model may excel in predicting the majority class but struggle with the minority class, potentially misidentifying plant diseases and reducing crop yields. To address this, techniques like oversampling, under sampling, and synthetic data can balance the dataset, enabling the model to learn from both classes for more accurate plant disease detection. This is crucial for sustainable agriculture and food security, as accurate models can aid in early disease detection, reduce agricultural losses, and ensure a steady food supply. Addressing imbalanced datasets is vital for robust and sustainable agriculture.

The VGG16 is a popular choice for deep learning image classification tasks, featuring 16 layers, including 13 convolutional and 3 fully connected layers. Although it is widely used in image classification, its performance can be limited when dealing with imbalanced datasets for tasks like plant disease identification. To address this, techniques such as oversampling, under sampling, and synthetic data generation can be applied to balance the dataset and improve disease diagnosis accuracy. While VGG16 can provide some accuracy in image classification, overcoming data imbalance is crucial to enhance its performance for plant disease detection in such datasets.

Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) can produce artificial data, which is useful when dealing with imbalanced datasets for plant disease diagnosis. GANs consist of a generator and a discriminator neural network that work together. The generator creates data like the training dataset, while the discriminator distinguishes between genuine and generated samples in a competitive process.

Addressing data imbalance involves augmenting the training dataset with new images generated by GANs. This addition can improve model accuracy by allowing it to learn from both majority and minority classes, making the dataset more balanced and diversified.

In practice, generated images are incorporated into the respective folders of the training dataset. The VGG16 model is then trained and tested using this updated dataset, leading to improved accuracy, particularly for the minority class. This process can be repeated multiple times to further enhance model performance.

After adding synthetic data, the training dataset can be utilized once again to train and evaluate the VGG16 model. The inclusion of generated data aids in dataset balancing and model accuracy improvement, leading to anticipated accuracy gains during retesting.

p.span: This iterative process of employing GANs to generate synthetic data and enhance the dataset can be repeated until the desired accuracy is achieved. Even with unbalanced datasets, this approach significantly boosts the model’s performance, enabling more accurate plant disease detection. Ultimately, this method facilitates the creation of an accurate and efficient model for plant disease detection, contributing to disease prevention and improved agricultural productivity.

p.span: This procedure of using GANs to generate synthetic data and improve the dataset can be repeated several times until the required accuracy is achieved. This can considerably improve the model’s performance and help it identify plant diseases with greater accuracy, even when working with unbalanced information. With the aid of this technique, it is possible to develop a model for plant disease identification that is accurate and effective, assisting in their prevention and boosting agricultural productivity.

level4.p: It is crucial to carry out an impartial review to judge the effectiveness and quality of images produced by a GAN. To do this, we classified the images produced by the GAN using the VGG16 architecture. To ascertain the usefulness of the generated photos, the dataset was first increased, and the plant species were categorized both before and after the expansion. The classification outcomes were then gathered into a table for additional examination. This indicates that while more study may be required to enhance the performance of the generated images, they were of sufficient quality to be helpful in practical applications.

div.p: Two sets of four images of leaves named, Generated images and Original images. The generated images lack clarity and sharpness.
Generated images versus original images

level3.p: In this research, a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) was developed to generate leaf images, with the primary goal of expanding the dataset for classification and assessing the quality of these generated images. GANs are commonly utilized for image generation, but most GAN models depend on having sufficient data for training.

Plant disease records often lack images of unhealthy leaves. This project aimed to create leaf images with limited data to address the issue of dataset imbalance. These generated images were incorporated to enhance the dataset quality and assessed accordingly.

Subsequently, the expanded dataset was utilized for classifying bacterial and plant species using the VGG16 model. The results reveal that the classification accuracy of the GAN-augmented dataset is slightly better than the original dataset but falls somewhat short of the dataset expanded through flipping and translation techniques. This indicates that the generated images effectively capture the essential features of real leaves.

p.span: The findings of this study provide support for the effectiveness of GANs in addressing dataset imbalance. Although the dataset in this study consists of only a small sample of leaves, the results demonstrate that the generated images are equivalent to real data. Future research should focus on generating higher-resolution images with a reduced number of samples, emphasizing the need for further exploration in this area.
