level2.p: In traffic management, surveillance, and intelligent transportation systems, vehicle recognition and counting are vital jobs with many applications. The development of practical and successful approaches to handle the challenges of vehicle recognition and counting has advanced significantly over time. This review article offers a thorough summary of the most recent methods, difficulties, and applications in the area of vehicle identification and counting. The review looks at studies that offer several methodologies for counting vehicles, including tried-and-true methods like background subtraction, optical flow analysis, and edge recognition. It also looks into papers that use cutting-edge methods like multi-camera systems, sensor fusion, and deep learning-based object detection to precisely estimate the number of vehicles in various situations. The review analysis of papers that present cutting-edge methods for recognizing and classifying automobiles according to their characteristics, such as type, make, and model, focuses on vehicle classification. It covers studies that use convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) as well as more conventional feature-based techniques, such as color, texture, and shape descriptors, for robust and accurate vehicle classification.

level3.p: Keywords

level2.p: Traffic monitoringVehicle detectionBackground subtractionVehicle countingVehicle classification

p.span: Vehicle counting and classification are essential tasks in the field of computer vision, with several applications in traffic management, monitoring, and intelligent transportation systems [

]. Understanding traffic patterns, maximizing traffic flow, boosting road safety, and enabling sophisticated transportation services all depend on accurate and effective vehicle count estimation and classification. Vehicle counting and categorization approaches have advanced significantly over time, thanks to developments in image processing, machine learning, and deep learning algorithms. Earlier methods frequently merged manually created features like color, texture, and form descriptors with well-known techniques like support vector machines (SVM) and random forest (RF) [

]. These techniques produced acceptable results, but they were constrained by their reliance on manually created features and their inability to deal with complicated scenarios involving various occlusions, lighting conditions, and vehicle types (Table

div.p: List of acronyms

p.span: Deep learning has advanced computer vision by enabling vehicle counts and categorization [

]. CNNs excel at object identification and categorization, including automobile classification. CNNs automatically train discriminative features from vast data sets to manage complicated visual patterns and vehicle appearances with high accuracy and resilience. Vehicle counting estimates the number of cars that pass through a region or road in a given time. Accurate counting helps infrastructure design and traffic flow. It helps traffic engineers and urban planners make smart decisions and implement effective traffic control systems. Vehicle counting methods include background subtraction, optical flow analysis, object detection, and tracking. Vehicle categorization groups vehicles by kind, manufacture, model, or even particular attributes like color or license plate information. Vehicle classification is crucial for law enforcement, toll-collecting, parking management, and targeted advertising. It allows intelligent systems to adapt to the different vehicle kinds detected or provide services tailored to each type. Traditionally, vehicle classification used manually derived attributes and rule-based algorithms. Occlusions, complicated backgrounds, and vehicle appearances were difficult to handle with these methods. Deep learning has improved vehicle classification. Deep learning techniques use CNN processing to automatically extract discriminative properties from images or video frames, enabling accurate and robust vehicle classification across many scenarios and types [

level3.p: We focus on modern approaches for recognizing, segmenting, and counting moving vehicles in our work, not tracking or camera adjustment. We looked at recent research articles’ approaches and techniques. This study is based on car identification accuracy. The article compares many researchers’ methods under different occlusion, illumination, and traffic density conditions. Also included is a brief explanation of car identification methods. In this survey paper, we will review recent vehicle counting and classification research. In this essay, we’ll evaluate the field’s successes, challenges, and emerging trends using traditional and deep learning methods. We examine state-of-the-art techniques to highlight relevant research objectives, assess the strengths and weaknesses of distinct methodologies, and provide ideas for future vehicle counting and categorization advances.

p.span: The study by Kamkar et al. [
] gives a thorough discussion of methods for locating, counting, and classifying vehicles under various circumstances. Kamkar et al. [

] discuss difficulties brought on by elements including varying weather, complex backgrounds, occlusions, and lighting situations. For applications like traffic management, surveillance systems, and autonomous driving, precise vehicle recognition, counting, and classification are essential. The study looks at various approaches, including deep learning-based methods as well as conventional computer vision techniques, used for vehicle recognition, counting, and categorization. They assess and contrast the advantages and disadvantages of each approach, providing information on how well it performs under various circumstances. This study also focuses on current developments in the field, such as the integration of several sensors (such as cameras and LiDAR) to improve resilience and accuracy. The authors highlight these multi-sensor techniques’ benefits in difficult situations while addressing problems and potential solutions related to them. The accuracy of the vehicle identification, counting, and classification methodologies presented in the research varies depending on the specific approaches and data sets employed. However, according to the approaches mentioned, normal accuracy rates fall between the range of 80 and 99% (Table

div.p: Results table

p.span: Arun et al. [

] various procedures used in vehicle counting and detection, including classic computer vision methods and more contemporary deep learning-based techniques, are probably covered in the study. To recognize and count automobiles, traditional computer vision approaches use feature extraction, object identification, and tracking algorithms. CNNs, in particular, have grown in prominence as a result of their capacity to automatically learn and extract features from enormous data sets, enabling more precise detection and counting. The proposed methodologies may be evaluated by the authors using benchmark datasets. Evaluation measures, including accuracy, precision, recall,

score, and mean average precision (mAP) are likely used to determine how effective the algorithms are. The study might also go over how various elements like lighting, occlusions, and complicated backgrounds affect the techniques’ accuracy. The background subtraction techniques are the foundation of the paper’s methodology. By modeling and removing the backdrop information, background subtraction seeks to distinguish moving items, such as vehicles, from the static background. Through this method, foreground items are recognized and isolated so that they can be processed further for vehicle detection and counting. A variety of background subtraction approaches, including the Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM), adaptive background modeling, and other variations, are covered by the authors. They tried to go through the background modeling processes, such as initial background estimation, background model updating, and thresholding methods for foreground extraction. Performance indicators like accuracy, precision, recall, and

score are anticipated to be used in the methodology’s evaluation. To show the benefits and drawbacks of the background subtraction-based approach for vehicle counting and detection, the authors run experiments using reference data sets or in-context examples. The proposed method’s accuracy in the study ranges from 90 to 97%.
The study by Arinaldi et al. [

] shows the importance of traffic video analytics for many applications, including traffic monitoring, congestion detection, and intelligent transportation systems, which are discussed by the authors in the opening paragraphs. They stress the importance of precise vehicle detection and categorization since they serve as the basis for drawing out valuable information from video footage. The suggested method’s two crucial phases are vehicle detection and vehicle classification. They successfully detect moving automobiles in the video frames by combining object tracking and background subtraction techniques for vehicle detection. The discovered vehicles are then extracted for additional analysis and segmented. They take a deep learning-based strategy in the second stage, which involves vehicle classification. To extract pertinent features from the segmented vehicle photos, they use a CNN architecture that has already been trained, such as VGG-16 or ResNet. These characteristics are then used to categorize the vehicles into various classes (e.g., car, bus, motorcycle) using a classifier, such as an SVM or an RF. On a sizable collection of traffic recordings, the authors ran comprehensive experiments to gauge the performance of the suggested strategy. They evaluate the improvement in vehicle classification and identification accuracy and contrast their approach with recently developed cutting-edge methods. The results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing techniques in terms of accuracy and computing effectiveness. Vehicle identification and classification systems based on the Faster R-CNN and the MoG background subtraction + SVM classifier were both developed and compared. The Faster R-CNN is better equipped to tackle the difficulty of recognizing and categorizing cars in a dynamic traffic situation with moving traffic, according to the findings of their studies. When it comes to identifying moving cars in traffic videos, faster R-CNN trumps MoG background subtraction.
The study by Lotliker et al. [

] provides a powerful and thorough vehicle detection, tracking, counting, and categorization system based on the video. In order to support applications like traffic management, surveillance, and intelligent transportation systems, the system seeks to deliver precise and effective analysis of vehicle movements in traffic scenarios. Vehicle detection, tracking, counting, and classification are some of its interrelated modules. The vehicle detection module correctly recognizes vehicles in video frames by using feature-based techniques and backdrop subtraction. Using techniques like the KLT tracker, the tracking module retains vehicle trajectories over multiple frames. Using optical flow and trajectory analysis, a counting module precisely counts the number of cars passing through particular areas of interest. The classification module, which is the final component, groups automobiles into predetermined classes using extracted data and machine learning techniques like SVM or RF. The system also has a vehicle categorization module that categorizes the discovered automobiles into predetermined groups. CNNs are used to extract pertinent information from the vehicles, such as color histograms, texture descriptors, or deep features. These features identify distinguishing qualities of the vehicles, which are then used to accurately categorize the vehicles into groups like cars, trucks, motorcycles, etc. using classifiers like the SVM or RF. This makes it possible for the system to offer thorough details regarding the makeup of the detected automobiles in the traffic video. The paper presents the accuracy rate performed on five sets of videos (Table

div.p: Results table

th.p: Counted number of vehicles by the method

p.span: Moutakki et al. [

] suggested the system’s methodology is divided into two basic stages: vehicle detection and vehicle categorization, both of which rely on feature extraction methods. It uses a variety of strategies to locate automobiles in video frames during the vehicle detection phase. Beginning with background subtraction, moving objects are isolated from the current frame by contrasting it with a background model. This assists in separating the areas of the structure that may house cars. Morphological techniques are used to enhance the size and shape of the vehicle candidates while removing noise from the identified regions. Edge detection algorithms are used in addition to background subtraction to improve the vehicle boundaries even more. The authors highlight the vehicle edges using the Canny edge recognition technique, which helps with precise localization. The regions corresponding to cars are then identified by processing the detected edges using methods like contour analysis and connected component analysis. The system moves on to the vehicle classification stage after detecting vehicles. They concentrate on removing pertinent features from the regions of vehicles that have been detected at this stage. To identify the distinctive qualities of various vehicle kinds, various feature extraction approaches are used. Color histograms, which depict the color distribution of the vehicle region; texture descriptors like Local Binary Patterns (LBP), which record textural information; and shape-based features, which encode the shape characteristics of the cars, are some examples of these features. The extracted features are subsequently sent into the vehicle categorization machine learning algorithms. The authors test out several classifiers, such as random forests, support vector machines, and

-nearest neighbors. These classifiers are trained using a labeled data set made up of examples of several classes of vehicles (such as cars, trucks, and motorcycles). The classifiers can correctly identify the detected automobiles by observing patterns and connections between the retrieved features and related vehicle classes.
Kim et al. [

] suggested approach makes use of a deep neural network architecture created specifically for on-road object detection to fully use the potential of deep learning. Multiple convolutional layers are incorporated into the network design to extract significant characteristics from the input images, and fully connected layers are then used for classification and bounding box regression. The authors present a number of strategies, such as data augmentation, transfer learning, and multi-scale training, to improve the network’s performance. Data augmentation entails increasing the training data set artificially through the use of transformations like rotation, scaling, and mirroring to increase the network’s adaptability to various conditions. With the aid of transfer learning, the network may use pre-trained models on sizable data sets, gaining access to learned features and speeding up training convergence. In order to capture objects of varied sizes, the authors also suggest a multi-scale training technique in which photos are scaled to multiple sizes during training. This strategy increases the network’s capacity to recognize objects of different sizes and raises detection precision. The effectiveness of the suggested method is assessed using a sizable data set made up of various road scenes, and its results are contrasted with those of cutting-edge object detection techniques. The studies’ findings demonstrate that the suggested deep neural network achieves better real-time performance and accuracy, which makes it perfect for applications requiring on-road object recognition. In terms of recognizing numerous objects, such as cars, people walking, and traffic signs—all essential for guaranteeing road safety in autonomous driving systems—the system exhibits encouraging results.
Wei et al. [

] suggested approach includes a thorough pipeline with numerous phases. First, to find and localize things of interest in street pictures, a reliable object detection technique based on deep learning is used. This approach extracts useful information and divides objects into various classes using a deep neural network architecture, such as a CNN. The authors offer a tracking system that enables the continuous tracking of detected objects across many frames in order to accommodate the dynamic nature of traffic scenes. This process links objects in successive frames depending on their spatial and temporal properties. Examples of these techniques include Kalman filters and Hungarian algorithms. The tracking system allows for the investigation of object behavior since it keeps track of object IDs across time. The authors cover both single-frame detection and the temporal aspect of vehicle tracking in addition to single-frame detection. They offer a tracking system that establishes the continuity of observed cars by using data from earlier frames. This technique helps keep precise vehicle tracking over a number of frames while reducing false positives. They divided the annotations into an 80/20 split using two sets of data: a training set and a testing set. On the test set, their vehicle detection classifier performed with an accuracy of 85%.

level3.p: We examined several OpenCV techniques and algorithms employed by numerous researchers over the previous years as part of our survey. Our in-depth analysis of the methodologies presented in each of the publications surveyed led us to the conclusion that they needed work, particularly in terms of run time complexity. The research provides a comprehensive examination of the different approaches used in traffic video monitoring. It focuses on different techniques for vehicle recognition, classification, and tracking utilized in numerous articles to develop an effective traffic control system through the use of video surveillance. The literature has examined smart visual surveillance in dynamic scenarios of varied environmental circumstances, with outdoor environments more difficult than inside environments because of changes in sunshine and illumination, human behavior, pedestrian crossings, and anomalies. However, we discovered that the currently used approaches are mainly based on the architectural concepts of a small number of these researchers. Several deep learning-based methods can work better while consuming less computing power.

level2.p: The improper disposal of household waste poses significant challenges to hygiene and environmental sustainability. This project introduces an innovative solution, An IoT-based smart dustbin for households has been designed to address waste management challenges by providing real-time monitoring and notifications for optimized waste collection. The system incorporates IR sensors to monitor garbage levels in bins and categorizes them as low, half, or full, improving waste segregation. In addition to real-time monitoring, the system automates the dustbin lid's opening and closing based on the proximity of a person's hand, enhancing user convenience and minimizing contamination risks. Wireless communication through Bluetooth technology enables the transmission of data to an Android mobile application, providing users with notifications about the dustbin's status. This system not only promotes household hygiene but also offers opportunities for efficient waste collection and smart home integration. By optimizing waste management and reducing overflow risks, it contributes to a cleaner and healthier living environment while also supporting environmental sustainability.

level3.p: Keywords

In an increasingly urbanized and interconnected world, the effective management of household waste has emerged as a critical concern for public health and environmental sustainability. Challenges such as overflowing bins and improper waste disposal can result in unsanitary conditions, the spread of diseases, and environmental pollution. Addressing these challenges, we present the IoT-based Smart Dustbin for Household project. This project represents an innovative convergence of technology and waste management, leveraging the Internet of Things. IoT encompasses the network of interconnected objects equipped with sensors and internet connectivity, facilitating data exchange and real-time monitoring. At its core, this initiative involves the deployment of infrared (IR) sensors within smart dustbins. These sensors fulfill a dual role: continuous monitoring of waste levels and communication of this data to users via Bluetooth technology. Once a dustbin reaches its capacity, the system promptly alerts users, prompting timely waste disposal. This approach ensures that bins are emptied before they overflow, effectively mitigating issues such as foul odors and unhygienic conditions associated with disease transmission.

The significance of this project becomes evident when considering the unrelenting global population growth. In this context, proper waste management transcends convenience; it is an imperative for ensuring clean and hygienic living conditions. As waste generation continues its daily upsurge, the preservation of cleanliness presents an escalating challenge, necessitating innovative, interconnected solutions.

This project is not solely about efficient waste management but also centers on the amalgamation of two progressive technologies: Android and Embedded Systems. Android, an open-source, Linux-based operating system primarily designed for touch screen mobile devices, hosts a wide spectrum of applications tailored to diverse user requirements. In this project, Android serves as the communication conduit between users and the IoT system. Through a dedicated application, users receive real-time updates and can promptly attend to their waste management needs.

Bluetooth technology, which operates within the universally available 2.4 GHz frequency, facilitates seamless communication between users’ Android devices and the smart dustbins, fostering an interconnected network in which appliances communicate cohesively. By synergizing IoT, Android, and Bluetooth technologies, this project forms the foundation for a more interconnected, efficient, and sustainable living environment.

In essence, this project responds to the mounting challenges of waste management in our rapidly urbanizing world. It not only advocates for cleanliness and hygiene but also opens avenues for optimized resource allocation and reduced environmental impact. It constitutes a significant stride toward a healthier, more sustainable, and technologically empowered way of living.

p.span: Robotic dustbins with sensors aim to improve waste management. These dustbins follow paths, alert users to dispose of trash, and notify when they are full. The system aims to enhance cleanliness and automate waste collection [

The literature survey for the robot's structure and microcontroller focuses on the use of aluminum for the structure, the dimensions of the robot, and the features of the PIC18F4550 microcontroller. It also includes information on the wireless communication module, specifically the Bluetooth device class-2 USB dongle and the Bluetooth Serial Port Profile device called Bluestrick. This dongle has a range of 10 m and allows the microcontroller to establish a connection with a computer. Additionally, the robot incorporates a Bluetooth Serial Port Profile device called Bluestrick for wireless serial data communications [

Rama Prabha et al. introduced the “Robodumpster,” an autonomous waste collection robot featuring a 7 Degree of Freedom (DoF) arm controllable via wireless means. Nevertheless, it is essential to note that the robot's ability to classify waste is limited, as it relies solely on object width for differentiation. Additionally, the manual construction of the 7DoF arm raises concerns regarding the cost-effectiveness of this technology [

]. Another paper introduces a 7-DoF autonomous robotic arm with IR sensors for tasks such as garbage collection. It highlights the arm's real-time PC control and custom fabrication using CNC milling [

The smart dustbin system using ultrasonic sensors and NodeMCU is like having a personal assistant for waste management. It keeps an eye on the dustbin's fill level and sends notifications when it's time to empty it. With loT integration, you can even manage and monitor the dustbins remotely. It is a smart solution that promotes cleanliness and contributes to a healthier environment. Say goodbye to overflowing bins and hello to efficient waste management [

Apoorva et al.—A group of innovators created an independent waste-collecting robot that utilizes a shaft and rotating blades to scoop up waste efficiently. This functional system gathers any visible debris within a specific range and deposits it into an attached bin. The robot maintains a constant check on the bin's trash level [
A Wi-Fi-controlled robot with an ultrasonic sensor for automated garbage collection, powered by solar energy, was validated through simulation tests [

]. Smart bins with sensors are being used to coordinate garbage collection in communities. These bins detect the amount of garbage and send data through GSM or Wi-Fi. This helps authorities efficiently manage waste disposal. Smart bins with sensors are proposed to address issues like affordability, maintenance, and durability. This coordination of garbage collection in communities can be done through a graphical user interface (GUI) supported by simulation. The GUI allows access to data from trash bins in different places, and the data can be transmitted through GSM [

Parashar et al. developed a waste management robot. This smart garbage-collecting robot, along with a monitoring system, detects waste using sensors and an RF module. A mobile software application is designed to monitor the amount of waste. The robot operates on a single platform [

level3.p: The proposed model represents a holistic approach to household waste management, leveraging advanced technology to streamline the process while prioritizing hygiene and user-friendliness. At its core, this system consists of a traditional household dustbin, retrofitted with four infrared (IR) sensors. Three of these IR sensors are strategically placed to detect varying waste levels (low, half, and full), while the fourth sensor is dedicated to lid control. These sensors work in tandem with an Arduino Uno microcontroller, which serves as the central processing unit. The Arduino Uno collects and processes data from the IR sensors, making real-time decisions about the waste level inside the bin and controlling the automated lid.

This innovative system integrates Bluetooth technology through an Arduino Bluetooth Controller, enabling wireless communication with user devices, typically smartphone or tablets. Users interact with the system via an Android mobile application. This application acts as a user-friendly interface, allowing real-time monitoring of the waste level within the dustbin. When the waste level is near its capacity, the Android app sends alerts and notifications to the user's device, prompting them to empty the dustbin to prevent overflow.

One notable feature of this system is its ability to automate the lid of the dustbin. The fourth IR sensor, responsible for lid control, detects the presence of a user’s hand, triggering a Servo Motor to automatically open the lid for waste disposal. The inclusion of an LED screen on the dustbin displays real-time waste level information, making it easily accessible to users.

In operation, the system efficiently manages waste, ensuring that the dustbin is emptied before it reaches capacity, minimizing the risk of unhygienic conditions and unpleasant odors associated with overflowing waste. This proposed model redefines household waste management, offering a connected, efficient, and hygienic solution that promotes cleanliness and health while simplifying the waste disposal process for users

p.span: Figure

demonstrates the flow of information and control from the IR sensors to the Arduino Uno, the automated lid control, the LED screen, and communication with the user’s mobile device through Bluetooth and the Android application. This integration offers an innovative solution for waste management, optimizing cleanliness and hygiene

level3.p: Smart Dustbin Hardware

This component represents the physical dustbin itself, which is enhanced with technology. It is equipped with four infrared (IR) sensors, an Arduino Uno Microcontroller, a Servo Motor, and an LED screen. These components are integral to the system’s functioning.

p.span: An infrared (IR) sensor is an electronic device designed to detect infrared radiation, which is a type of electromagnetic radiation not visible to the human eye due to its longer wavelengths. IR sensors operate based on either passive or active detection principles. Passive infrared (PIR) sensors are commonly used for motion detection, responding to changes in heat signatures within their field of view, making them valuable in applications like security systems and automatic lighting. On the other hand, active IR sensors emit infrared radiation and measure its reflection or interruption to detect objects or obstacles, making them suitable for proximity sensing and object detection. IR sensors are renowned for their ability to function in low-light conditions, their sensitivity to temperature changes, and energy efficiency, making them integral to various industries and technologies, including home automation, robotics, and autonomous vehicles (Fig.

div.p: A photograph of an experimental setup that includes a tiny trash attached with sensors, I C chips, and circuitry.
Fig. 2
Smart dustbin

level3.p: This sensor is distinct from the waste level sensors and is dedicated to lid control. It senses the presence of a user’s hand and triggers the Servo Motor for automated lid control.

The Arduino Uno serves as the central processing unit of the system. It collects data from the IR sensors, processes this information, and makes decisions based on the data it receives. It also controls the Servo Motor for lid automation.

The Arduino Uno is a microcontroller board designed for DIY electronics projects and prototyping. It features an Atmel ATmega328P microcontroller at its core, which can be programmed using the Arduino software platform. The board offers a variety of digital and analog input/output pins, rendering it versatile for sensor connections, LEDs, motors, and other components. It can interface with a computer via a USB connection for programming and serial communication. Arduino Uno's open-source nature and extensive community support make it accessible for beginners and experienced electronics enthusiasts alike, enabling them to create a wide variety of interactive projects.

This component enables wireless communication between the Smart Dustbin system and user devices, such as Smartphone or tablets. It facilitates the exchange of data and instructions between the system and the user's device.

An Arduino Bluetooth controller is a system that utilizes an Arduino microcontroller board in conjunction with Bluetooth technology to establish a wireless control interface for a multitude of applications. The Arduino board, an open-source hardware platform, serves as the central processing unit for this setup. A Bluetooth module, typically an HC-05 or HC-06, is connected to the Arduino to facilitate wireless communication with other Bluetooth-enabled devices like smart phones or computers. The programming of the Arduino is accomplished using the Arduino IDE, which involves coding for Bluetooth module communication and defining the actions or devices to be controlled. This might include instructions for turning LEDs on and off, controlling motors, or gathering data from sensors. Once the Arduino is programmed and the Bluetooth module is configured, the system can be paired with a Bluetooth-enabled device, such as a smart phone or tablet. This enables bidirectional communication, allowing users to send commands from their device to the Arduino and receive data or feedback from the Arduino on their device. Arduino Bluetooth controllers find application in various domains, including home automation, robotics, remote control systems, and DIY projects, offering an accessible means to integrate wireless control into diverse applications and projects

level4.p: Algorithm: Garbage Monitoring System

p.span: Initialize Components:

li.p: Set up the LCD and servo motor.
Define pins for sensors (IR, LVL1, LVL2, LVL3).

p.span: Setup Function:

li.p: Initialize LCD and set the initial message.
Configure sensor pins as INPUT.
Start serial communication and attach the servo motor.
Set the servo motor to its initial position.
Wait for 500 ms.

p.span: Main Loop:
Continuously:

div.p: a.
Check the IR sensor:
If an object is detected, open the lid by rotating the servo.
Wait for 500 ms.
If no object is detected, close the lid.
b.
Monitor garbage levels:
Check the states of level sensors (LVL1, LVL2, LVL3).
Update the display based on garbage levels and print alerts.
Pause for 1 s.
Repeat Loop:
Return to the main loop to continue monitoring and updating.

p.span: In a smart dustbin, four IR sensors play distinct roles in ensuring efficient waste management. The first sensor serves as a trigger for the lid's servo motor, facilitating its controlled opening and closing, making it convenient for users to dispose of garbage. On the other hand, the remaining three IR sensors are responsible for the continuous monitoring of waste levels within the bin. When the first IR sensor detects an obstruction, it immediately communicates with the Arduino, prompting the servo motor to open the lid, enabling waste disposal. Conversely, if none of the three internal IR sensors register any interruptions, it indicates that the dustbin is empty, prompting the LCD display to read “NO GARBAGE,” streamlining the waste disposal process and ensuring the lid remains open only when necessary (Fig.

div.p: 6 photos of the experimental setup begin with data on the digital panel for interrupt sensing, no garbage is low, garbage is low, garbage is half, garbage is full, and arduino bluetooth controller conditions.
Fig. 3
Outputs of the research work

level3.p: When the bottom two IR sensors inside the dustbin are interrupted, while the remaining one IR sensor remains unobstructed, the LCD displays “GARBAGE IS HALF,” indicating the dustbin is approximately half full. When all three of the bottom IR sensors are interrupted, the display shows “GARBAGE IS FULL,” signaling that the dustbin has reached its maximum capacity. When the Arduino Bluetooth Controller is connected to the smart dustbin, it provides real-time monitoring values, including the current garbage level, enhancing the user's ability to track and manage waste efficiently.

This project presents an innovative system that merges IoT technology with Bluetooth modems and IR sensors to optimize the process of garbage collection, making it more efficient and cost-effective. The system includes a database that offers a more precise record of waste collection and the quantities of waste at different locations. Our examination encompassed existing solutions for the integration of modern technologies. Through the implementation of this project, we aim to significantly enhance waste management, ultimately contributing to a cleaner environment. The technologies incorporated into our proposed system are well-suited for practical and comprehensive monitoring and management of solid waste collection, supporting the cause of environmental sustainability.

level2.p: Large record analysis facilitates the reading of information transactions and established analytical procedures, including semi-structured and unstructured records. Internet browsing path information, mobile phone call details, and server logs are all examples of great facts. Datasets oriented to relational databases are not suitable for traditional statistical warehouses because large datasets are updated frequently and generate a large amount of data in real time. Many open-source answers are available to manage this information at scale. The Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) is one of the answers that allow large amounts of information to be stored, processed and studied. Hadoop has proven to be the first choice for distributed garages and computers in big data analysis applications. It has the strength to organize payment nodes for saving the task facts in a unique way. The Hadoop stage is also known for storing everything now and deciding what to do with it later. This article portrays the problems along with the challenges of installing and configuring a multi-node Hadoop cluster. Recognizing hub over-accessibility issues in uncommon disappointment conditions in Hadoop bunches is a trial with a dataset of a similar size. In experimental evaluation, this article works on the productive use of Hadoop clusters for research and investigation. It also provides rules for measuring Hadoop groups based on record length and design rate.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Big data analytics is the process of studying various and enormous units of data to recognize hidden patterns and use cutting-edge analytical tools for particular types of structured and unstructured data [

]. A speedier and more accurate decision-making process about the usage of previously inaccessible and unusable records is made possible by such an evaluation. To glean fascinating knowledge from previously undiscovered data, text analytics, device knowledge acquisition, and information mining techniques are employed. The big data time period frequently includes volume, velocity, and veracity usage. The Volume dimension refers to the massive amount of data measured in Peta or Zetta units, which is generated exponentially through typical internet or mobile structures via interactions between gadgets and people or devices, communication on social networks, and so on. There are several challenging situations for massive data analysis. The biggest challenge is storing a large amount of records that can be generated day by day or with unique packages. When it comes to security, the vulnerability of information is also a big problem that makes the facts much less secure. Handling different information sources at the same time can be very demanding in itself [

Large amounts of records provide a challenge that was not intended to be handled by conventional statistical analysis methods and tools. Apache has developed an open-source software solution called a Hadoop task for dependable, scalable, and dedicated computing to handle this sort of enormous amount of facts. It is the appropriate choice for both the business and research groups since a vibrant open-source network is readily available. The Google File System (GFS) and the MapReduce framework, both of which Google published in 2004, serve as the foundation for HDFS. A distributed file system called HDFS offers extremely high bandwidth across clusters and stores data on commercial servers [

A Hadoop cluster is made up of a number of nodes, which are pieces of cheap commodity hardware that are linked to each other. Your big data initiatives can use this technology since it is scalable and affordable. To create a statistical average, Hadoop nodes are networked together and distributed uniformly inside the rack. There are three models of the Apache Hadoop framework [
The top-level configuration of a typical Hadoop cluster is depicted in Fig.

. Client nodes, master nodes, and slave nodes are the three distinct types of nodes. Hadoop is deployed on client nodes as well, however, it is not required to use an in-cluster HDFS method. The Map Reduce job's setup is done by the client node. The job of the master node is to oversee cluster metadata and distribute load across the roles that are executed on certain cluster reality nodes. The master node role for maintaining and storing Hadoop cluster metadata has been modified, among other things. The actual users of the Hadoop cluster are the slave nodes. The Slave Node’s primary responsibility is to process, store, and oversee nodes for the Map and Reduce projects [

div.p: A schematic of a client with a master node. The resource manager of the master node is divided into 3 node managers which include the application manager, and containers. The node managers are named slave node 1, slave node 2, and slave node n.
Fig. 1
Hadoop high-level architecture

p.span: Hadoop cluster vision are Java method that you can run on your machine (general hardware) to perform all the storage, retrieval, control, processing, and other operations of running Hadoop on a cluster. These important visions of the Hadoop cluster are the Node, Secondary Name Node, Resource Manager, Data Node, and Node Manager. First, Hadoop1.X launched the Resource Manager and Node Manager vision with the names Job Tracker and Task Tracker, respectively. Hadoop 2.X versions renamed them Resource Manager and Node Manager. For multi-node Hadoop configurations, these strategies are configured one after another with specific machines depending on storage needs [

Nodenode: An HDFS cluster contains a Name Node. It is the server that manages the record widget namespace and uses clients to set access to documents. Roles or name nodes manage document system repositories when interacting with a Hadoop cluster. The Name Node also keeps a copy of the blocks in its data node available to the Hadoop system for fault tolerance. Hadoop cluster replication variability is primarily determined at cluster configuration time based on actual sensitivities and may change depending on application requirements. Node Name is responsible for managing the file directory structure, all blocks, copies, and data nodes in the metadata repository. The characteristics of a metadata repository include document localization, directory structure, names, and system information. The lifetime of a single Name Node in the cluster greatly simplifies the machine structure [

Data Nodes: There are several data nodes, usually corresponding to nodes in a cluster, that control the storage allocated to the node on which they run. Data nodes are the real workers in the Hadoop cluster where data is stored and processed based on client requests. Data nodes bill file system clients for processing probe and write requests. The DataNode also introduces, deletes, and copies blocks from the Name Node during training. Data nodes require a large amount of I/O to process and transmit statistics [

Secondary Name Nodes: Secondary Name Nodes are Java procedures that are like personal assistants to the Name Node. Below are the main tasks of a secondary name node running on Hadoop clusters for the purpose of keep the namespace repository up to date and in case of Name Node failure, secondary Name Node provides all cluster records [

The resource manager receives requests from consumers to run Map Reduce. Interact with name nodes to determine the realm of facts, extract good Node Manager nodes to form Hadoop clusters, mainly for statistical situations (close to data), and perform tasks on specific nodes based on available slots. The Resource Manager video display unit returns node managers to the buyer with an overall assessment of their activity. The resource management technique is important as an expression to implement Map Reduce in a Hadoop cluster. HDFS ensures continuous availability of facts even in case of resource manager failure. However, Map Reduce may fail to start and existing Map Reduce jobs may stop [

Node Manager Mapper and Reducer responsibilities are performed on data nodes managed by Node Manager. The Resource Manager selects a set of Node Managers and assigns tasks to them each time a new Map Reduce job is started. Node Manager maintains constant communication with resource managers informing them of the progress of running tasks. Node Manager failures are not considered critical. When a node manager becomes unresponsive, the resource manager assigns the tasks performed using that node to another node [

Similar to Hadoop configurations, large real documents are divided into one or more data blocks (the default block size is 128 MB) and exceptionally stored in unique data nodes arranged in racks. A complete hierarchical metadata repository of the file and its replication records can be maintained on the Name Node of the Hadoop cluster. Below are the steps of Hadoop read and write operations. The client connects to the Name Node to find the required statistics file block records. The Name Node provides regional information for all reality blocks needed to create a target reality report. The reproduction is closer to the block. The consumer contacts the name node for the data node sector. The calling node selects statistics nodes from unconventional shelves to shop block report files consistent document period and viable block department and presents the selected statistics node region to the consumer. The purchaser without delay writes the block file to the statistics node. Statistics nodes repeat the block on exception facts nodes. Repeat the iterations of steps 3 and four to shut the block [

For the output of “/person/gutenberg.” Understanding how the Map Reduce task is carried out is used as an example. HADOOP CLUSTER PROCESSING NODE FAILURES for the Word Count application. A set of stems are present in a record of textual material. It is divided into a row, then all of the traces in the Map portion are changed using the key, price template key, 1 > . Each key phrase in this case has a price of 1. The Shuffle and Sort stage of the Map Reduce programming level is crucial. Making a jar file that can be used with Hadoop (Fig.

level3.h3: 6 Failure of a Secondary Name Node
level3.p: However, the Hadoop cluster will be unavailable for some time if both the name node and the secondary node fail at the same time. Each time a time window for the backup system becomes available, this commit can be done through the newly elected node as the secondary name node in the event that the primary name node fails. However, if the name node and the secondary node die simultaneously, the Hadoop cluster would temporarily go down.

p.span: Since the metadata repository is situated on a unique data node of the cluster for a full reporting system, new nodes for Name Node and Secondary Name Node strategies are determined and produced during this time. A Hadoop cluster data node failure buys a copy of each block of information logs. The input fact document may be divided into blocks and stored in different data nodes. If one of the data nodes fails, copies of its reality blocks are available on other data nodes. A secondary name node’s primary role is to maintain at the storage of Hadoop file machine data, however, statistics can be acquired from another data node that can store a copy of that block of records [

Lack of a Resource Manager: The Map Reduce process is not run by the resource manager in a Hadoop cluster. In order to stabilize the load across data nodes, a position or support supervisor specifically assigns processes to data nodes. Running jobs won’t be impacted if the Resource Manager crashes, but all jobs will be put on hold until a replacement node is chosen and set up for the Resource Manager process. Failure of Node Manager Each record block is duplicated and stored in a separate statistics node. The allocated jobs in a Hadoop cluster are rolled back and assigned to another name node if any manager node in the cluster fails. This information block has a duplicate in the connected data node. This research project aims to assess the total CPU performance for extremely long textual information. Py is used to run the number expressions software [

level3.p: This paper gives you a thorough understanding of configuring and implementing a multi-node Hadoop cluster. High availability troubleshooting for various scenarios is covered in step-by-step implementation plans. A typical Hadoop cluster is built up and tested in a variety of situations using datasets of various sizes. It also outlines the actions. Hadoop clusters do so in the event of a node failure. A MapReduce framework called YARN is another resource negotiator offered by the Hadoop HDFS core installation Running Map Reduce programs on datasets of various sizes\is also covered. This paper’s empirical study contributes to a more productive usage of Hadoop clusters for research and analysis. We also offer recommendations for selecting the size of a Hadoop cluster based on the volume and pace of data generation. The future scope is suggested on big data analysis employing enormous datasets of various sizes and sorts.

level2.h2: Artificial Intelligence in Finance: A Systematic Literature Review

p.span: Satish Chandra
and
G. Bala Krishna

div.p: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, Anurag University, Hyderabad, India

level3.p: Satish Chandra
(Corresponding author)
Email:
G. Bala Krishna

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: The advancements in computer storage and processing capabilities have led to the adoption of various tools and techniques of artificial intelligence (AI) across all facets of life. The discipline of finance has been no exception and has been gradually adopting AI. Machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) are major subfields of AI. Historically, we seem to have reached an inflection point of adoption of AI in finance. In this paper, we endeavor to trace the transformational journey and understand the current state of the use of AI in Finance through a Systematic Literature Review. A SLR is a structured methodology that guides researchers to collect literature data, review the important content and analyze the data to provide actionable insights. We present the gaps that we have identified based on our data analysis. The identified gaps are potential areas for carrying out further investigation and detailed research.

level3.p: Keyword

Artificial intelligence tools and techniques are steadily being adopted in the field of finance. Artificial intelligence primarily comprises machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL). We are experiencing rapid growth in the advancement of tools, technologies and processes related to ML and DL. Such growth has led to their applications within various fields like finance, healthcare and agriculture. We have conducted a systematic literature review to study the use of AI in finance.

p.span: This paper is structured as follows. Section
provides a brief theoretical background by defining the various terms of AI used in the paper with an intent to set the context for the rest of the paper. Section
discusses the various aspects of the field of finance. Section
presents the adaption of the SLR methodology to our study. Section
concludes the paper and discusses the avenues for future research.

level3.p: This section provides a brief overview of the concepts of AI, ML, and DL to set the context for further discussions in this paper. There are related concepts like supervised learning, unsupervised learning and reinforcement learning and artificial general intelligence. However in this paper, we will discuss AI from ML and DL perspectives.

level4.p: AI is a term used to describe how a machine whose software mimics the behavior of a normal human. The exhibited behavior could include any or all of the sensory capabilities of a human. Hence, this has led to the development of various artificial intelligence sub-areas like machine learning, deep learning with application areas like computer vision and natural language processing.

Machine learning (ML) is the ability of the machine to learn and develop algorithms and programs on its own based on sufficient inputs provided by humans. Its learning is based on the patterns and behavior of the input.

Deep learning (DL) models are based on neural networks which result in the learning of a large number of parameters. Advancements in the DL technologies have led to networks like recurrent neural networks, GNNs, LSTMs, encoders, decoders, autoencoders, and transformers. With the advent of generative pretrained transformers (GPTs), AI is getting closer to human intelligence.

Finance is a broad area comprising various subfields. At a higher level of abstraction, finance can be viewed as that subject area which deals with persons at one level and corporates at another level, i.e., personal finance and corporate finance.

Personal finance deals with handling money at a personal level with the greater global and national-level economies. Corporate finance on the other hand deals with the operations and management of corporations.

level3.p: Finance is a vibrant field that affects each and every one of us in our daily lives and hence with the evolution of our environment it keeps adapting itself to changes in technology and other environmental factors.

Broad indicative areas of finance that are the recent focus areas for application of AI in finance are Portfolio construction and optimization, Fraud Detection and Prevention, Algo trading, Stock Price Prediction and Trading Strategies. Indicative AI algorithms applied in finance are Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, SVM, Neural Networks like RNNs and GANs. The focus of this paper is to study how the AI algorithms are applied in Finance.

p.span: A Systematic Literature Review (SLR) is a structured methodology that guides researchers to collect literature data (such as journal papers and conference papers), filter noise in the data, analyze the data based on certain hypotheses, and arrive at actionable insights [

SLRs enable academic researchers to gather existing knowledge, examine the state of a field and identify gaps that can be filled by carrying out further research. The methodological steps for conducting SLRs are replicable and scientific by design. Bibliographic mapping approaches enable visualization of bibliometric information and results of the systematic literature review [
]. The SLR deals with small datasets that are manageable enough for its content to be manually reviewed and the scope of review is specific [
]. As our focus is to conduct SLR of AI in Finance, we adapted the SLR methodology by considering steps relevant to our study.

level4.p: We considered the following steps in the planning phase:

div.p: Define keywords
Formulate research questions
Select a digital library
Define inclusion and exclusion criteria.

Definition of Keywords: As our objective is to conduct a systematic literature review of the use of AI in Finance, we arrived at the following keywords: “artificial intelligence”, “machine learning”, “deep learning”, “finance”, and “systematic literature review”.
Formulation of Research Questions: As our area of interest is to study the use of Artificial Intelligence in the Finance domain, we formulated the following research questions:

RQ1: In which scenarios is AI (ML and DL) used to improve upon existing traditional approaches, methodologies and processes for the Finance domain? RQ2: What are the current challenges of applying AI in Finance? RQ3: What type of research/interventions would help enrich the use of AI in Finance?

Selection of a Digital Library: As the focus of our research questions is on technology and applications or use of technology in finance, we decided that the most suitable digital library for our study would be the IEEE Xplore digital library.

Definition of Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria: As AI is a relatively new field and its use in the finance domain is growing rapidly, we decided to include year-wise data from 2014 to 2023 and club the data for the period 1980 to 2013 since the year-wise data would be very low for the later period.

p.span: We considered the following steps in the execution phase:

div.p: Build digital library search strings
Search the library
Download the results
Clean the results data
Examine refined results.
Building digital library search strings: As our objective and research questions focus on “machine learning”, “deep learning” and “finance” we decided to have them as search strings.
Searching the IEEE Xplore digital library: Search terms used for performing “Advanced Search” on the IEEE Xplore digital library’s database are as follows:
a.
“machine learning” and (boolean and) “finance”
b.
“deep learning” and (boolean and) “finance”.

The searches were carried out with the option of “All Metadata” for various publication periods. Searches on “All Metadata” result in search within the content related to abstracts, index terms and bibliography citation data of the digital database.

Downloading the Results: The year-wise results from the year 2014 to 2023 and for the period 1980 to 2013 have been downloaded from the IEEE Xplore digital library’s database in the form of.csv,.ris and.bib in whole or partially depending on the features we wanted to study and report results thereon.

Cleaning the Results Data: Search results had 30 columns viz. Document Title, Authors, Author Affiliations, Publication Title, Date Added To Xplore, Publication Year, Volume, Issue, Start Page, End Page, Abstract, ISSN, ISBNs, DOI, Funding Information, PDF Link, Author Keywords, IEEE Terms, INSPEC Controlled Terms, INSPEC Non-Controlled Terms, Mesh_Terms, Article Citation Count, Patent Citation Count, Reference Count, License, Online Date, Issue Date, Meeting Date, Publisher and Document Identifier. Based on manual inspection of the abstract column, we observed that 9 of the 30 columns were useful for our research.

p.span: Examining Refined Results: Based on the results, we generated Table
that shows the historical progress of publications related to AI in finance.

div.p: Historical progress of publications related to AI in finance

level4.p: As can be observed from the table, there has been a steady increase in the number of papers published year on year. There is a growing trend with regard to the research being carried out in the application of AI to Finance.

p.span: Based on a preliminary study, we have observed a regular pattern of cross pollination of ideas from various fields by way of bringing concepts from seemingly unrelated fields like Graph Neural Networks and Signal Processing [

]. The number of books, courses being taught at universities, number of papers in various international conferences and journals, complexity in the AI algorithms applied to Finance have been increasing rapidly.
A study of the papers of journals and conferences in the IEEE Xplore database [
], the results have been summarized in Table
, have reinforced the above observations.

level4.p: In relation to the research questions postulated by us, we find that with respect to RQ1 we find that the application of AI in finance is pervading into more areas of finance. For RQ2, we find that the challenges are due to sparse data in comparison to the data from other domains. For RQ3, ideas from other disciplines that deal with research and intervention related to AI algorithms like sparse data, high dimensional, and high frequency data need to be further investigated.

level3.p: In conclusion, to summarize with respect to our research questions. With respect to RQ1 we find that the application of AI in finance is pervading into more areas of finance. For RQ2, we find that the challenges are due to sparse data in comparison to the data from other domains. For RQ3, ideas from other disciplines that deal with research and intervention related to AI algorithms like sparse data, high dimensional, and high frequency data need to be further investigated.
There is scope for further research as discussed with related RQ3 discussed above.

level2.h2: UAV Data Collection and Analyzing Model Using Machine Learning-Based Precedence Ordered Data Gathering Technique

p.span: Ramya Krishna Dhulipalla
and
Sibi Chakkaravarthy Sethuraman

div.p: School Of Computer Science and Engineering, VIT-AP University, Inavolu, Andhra Pradesh, India

level3.p: Ramya Krishna Dhulipalla
(Corresponding author)
Email:
Sibi Chakkaravarthy Sethuraman

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: Climate change has created significant problems, including agriculture, industries, which could impact many fields. Agricultural technology is rapidly changing as the sector tackles the two issues of food safety and climate changes. To maximize farming outcomes and minimize losses, it is vital to collect credible and accurate data from crops. Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) provide a quick and ever more cost-effective way of data collection and provide a better, unconstrained view of each crop as compared to conventional satellite images. The autonomous sensors collect high- resolution images challenging for decision making with increasing speed and efficiency and cost savings. There are not yet standard workflow and processes for most UAV applications for precision agriculture. This paper proposed a Machine Learning-based Precedence ordered Data Gathering (MLbPODG) Model used for gathering the data from the UAV devices that is helpful in precision agriculture. The proposed model is compared with the traditional models, and the results show that the proposed model performance in data collection and analysis is better.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Agriculture productivity growth is considered imperative to meet the increased demands of the projected population to meet the food requirements. In general, precision agriculture means enhancing the control of farming inputs in the right place and at the appropriate time by applying the proper management practice. Due to their flexible and elastic operation, unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) have gained massive attention in recent decades. With dynamic topology and flexible deployment, the UAVs extend the Internet of Everything (IoE) technique. UAVs are used in various applications, including agricultural management, marine monitoring, and military transport by committed design and control. Remote sensing is used in the agricultural fields, and in the past 15 years, has effectively provided accurate agrarian solutions. In this sense, image data acquisition technology has now become an advantageous method of accurate agriculture. Small UAV sensing solutions have taken one more stage to provide many opportunities than satellite systems for quick, easy, and cost-effective field data acquisition. At a low altitude, UAVs function, which significantly enhances the system’s performance with ultra-high-resolution imaging. Further, UAVs can cover a large area without damage within a much shorter period and are much less expensive and more straightforward than aviation used by the public and efficient than manned aviation. The UAV devices are used in a variety of ways that are indicated in Fig.

div.p: A flowchart on U A V uses in agriculture includes data collection, application, and interpretation. Data collection includes a soil map, crop condition map, soil condition map, and yield map. The application is to protect, fertilize, and sow. Interpretation includes integration and crop modules.
Fig. 1
UAV uses in agriculture

p.span: Uncommon usage for crop field surveillance is now being performed using unmanned aerial systems (UAS). Farmers can use UAS to recognize which areas of the plants require changes or input and respond appropriately on time by carrying many different sensors. In a variety of Precision Agriculture applications, UAS can be used.

div.p: Weed detection and management are the most popular UAS applications.
Track vegetation growth and yield estimates.
Vegetation monitoring and disease detection, control of irrigation.
Soil conductivity assessment.

p.span: Since UAVs in precision agriculture are seen as the future of remote sensing, many studies examine their use in agricultural production. The critical difference between the static and mobile networks is that nodes covered by the UAV are differently transmitted. In the static case, the related speeds between nodes and the UAV are the same, and all protected nodes are fixed. When the UAV flies at a higher altitude, the relative distances almost have no difference. UAV’s are now robust sensing systems equipped with specialized sensors that complement the IoT technology. The small weight, low power consumption, and small size combined with high-resolution images are among the requirements that sensors must fulfill. The UAV devices gather the data and process it, which is used for precision agriculture. The process is depicted in Fig.

div.p: A schematic of an agricultural land with location pins. The data is collected by U A Vs from the location pins and is sent to a data center.
Fig. 2
UAV data processing

level3.p: In a particular transformational field, it is assumed that the data produced by the sensors is compressible, and it can be recovered at the sink with sparse projections. Each projection is collected from the sensor nodes to a selected cluster head (C.H.) node by creating a forwarding tree. The data collected at the C.H.s is subsequently collected by the UAV to be sent to the sink node. The sink node recuperates the original data by solving a convex problem of optimization after all predictions have been collected. The data are aggregated on the way to the C.H. rather than transmitting the readings of each sensor to the C.H. in one single packet through multiple hops. This involves developing a cluster tree to decrease the total transmission number and reduce the overall energy consumption of the sensors. Data analysis from RGB images was based on machine learning (ML). It usually produces efficient results. Another approach of ML techniques is to recognize and classify objects by Object-Based Image Analysis (OBIA) in agricultural UAV images by defining and detecting weaves or species. These techniques are used directly on the image data obtained by using RGB colors, intensity, or other functionalities and, in few cases, in each pixel neighborhood data. Furthermore, ML was also associated with the use of indexes as templates with high precision features.

p.span: Shen et al. [

] proposed a UAV model to gather data from ground sensor nodes with an age-effective trajectory strategy. Furthermore, the research sought to adopt the UAV as a mobile sink node. Still, only the implementation of the drones in the sensor network was investigated, and advanced operations of the UAVs, such as dynamic track design and the construction of a UAV network, were not considered. Li et al. [

] present computational solutions and algorithms to minimize the flight time of UAVs for the collection of sensor data. The critical challenge of optimizing UAV is to design pathways where certain constraints exist, such as a steady path, minimizing the duration, and controlling framework that guarantees simultaneous access of all UAVs. The preparation of routes should be calculated effectively, productive, and in “actual time,” allowing the UAV to schedule its flight again in case of an unanticipated danger. To solve these issues, practical optimization approaches should be used. Qin et al. [

] focused on a population of potentially updated pathways that follow up to the ideal route. This work is conducted in online and offline scenarios for multi-UAV route planning. But for typical route planning problems, this study appears to have slower convergence. Alessandro et al. [

] primary objective is to collect WSN data through the UAV. The sensor nodes in this research cannot transmit cloud data, and the UAV was used to transmit cooperative data. The platform includes a client- side with a collection of components hosting the UAV, which selectively download the recorded data packages to a cloud- based server. The server is hosted in the cloud infrastructure and can provide the control center and operator with real-time data processing and feedback services. Ebrahimi et al. [

] proposed a node storage model in which resources are restricted, each source loses data. These systems rely on the buffer overflow times restrictions at sensors or the latency caused by data collection. Zhan et al. [

] presented the collection of data from one or more UAVs in a clustered wireless sensor network to minimize mission cost for numbers of clusters and UAV travel distances while ensuring data from all collections. In the same scenario, the sensor nodes (S.N.), including the genetic algorithm and nearest-neighbor, were clustered using a “K”-means-based algorithm. Zong et al. [

] proposed a data collection system that includes multiple UAVs to collect data from clustered S.N.s to reduce the number and flight time, distances, and mission deadlines of deployed UAVs. Ali et al. [

] proposed a cooperative energy-efficient system for data collection from a remote sensor network using UAVs. The UAVs transmit the information to the base station. A formulated optimal programming problem is generated in the base station and ensures success rates and balanced energy consumption.
WSN core feature and its significant research theme is data collection from different sources. UAVs are shipped to collect data from

distributed two-dimensional devices and return the data to a data center. A UAV provides only one system at a time with a communication service. The channel atmosphere cannot always be suitable for each device if UAVs are just floating in the air because there can be a wide gap between the device and its corresponding UAV. level3.p: This paper proposed a more realistic form of data collection using the Machine Learning-based Precedence ordered Data Gathering (MLbPODG) Model, aimed at large-scale WSN. It highlights the regularized model that improves spatial consistency of the data collection for UAVs and given that node deployment is even-handed. This model designs and produces a simulation platform for data collection and analysis to test the relevant data collection parameters and carry out multi-group experiments as the airspeed and network scale change. From these tests, it can be found that NS2 simulation research based on a platform can evaluate the data collection time consumption, flight distance, and storage space parameters that can provide information for realistic data collection. The collected data is analyzed using the machine learning models for considering the relevant data used for precision agriculture and improving agriculture outcomes. The proposed model allocates precedence for the data to be gathered in a sequence to enhance the model’s accuracy. The proposed model accuracy levels are increased and accurate when compared to traditional models. UAV clusters need to be maintained for extensive area data collection in less time in future.

level2.h2: MAFD: Model Agnostic Forest Densenet Approach for Brain Tumor Detection

p.span: B. Thimma Reddy
and
V. V. S. S. S. Balaram

Anurag University, Hyderabad, Telangana, India

level3.p: B. Thimma Reddy

level3.p: V. V. S. S. S. Balaram
(Corresponding author)
