level2.p: Medical imaging is vital in diagnosis, treatment planning, and disease monitoring. DenseNet’s dense connections enable effective feature extraction from medical images, capturing local and global patterns with rich contextual information. This capability is crucial in identifying subtle abnormalities, detecting tumors, segmenting organs, and analyzing complex anatomical structures. By leveraging dense connectivity, DenseNet facilitates extracting relevant and discriminative features, enhancing the accuracy and reliability of medical image analysis. Acquiring large-scale annotated datasets can be challenging in the medical domain due to privacy concerns, cost, and limited availability. DenseNet addresses the problem of limited data by effectively leveraging the available samples. The dense connections promote feature reuse, allowing the network to learn from a wider range of data instances, even in scenarios with small or imbalanced datasets. This property enables better generalization and robustness, making DenseNet particularly valuable in medical applications where obtaining extensive labeled data are difficult. In the proposed model, using the concept of transfer learning, the model extracts the features based on the ranking mechanism and works on the extracted features to classify the MRI images as brain tumors. Medical images, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans, often consist of high-dimensional data with numerous voxels or pixels. Directly using the raw pixel intensities as input to a machine-learning model can be computationally expensive and may lead to over fitting due to the curse of dimensionality. Feature extraction techniques help reduce the dimensionality of the data by transforming it into a more compact and representative feature space, making the subsequent analysis more efficient and effective.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: In neural networks, automatic feature extraction allows the neural network to learn and extract relevant features from raw input data without explicitly specifying the features beforehand [

]. This contrasts with manual feature engineering, where domain knowledge is used to handcraft specific features. Neural networks excel at automatically learning complex representations from data, but the resulting features may need to be more interpretable. The learned features are often represented as high-dimensional vectors, making it challenging to understand individual features’ underlying meaning or semantics. This can be a limitation in domains where interpretability and explain ability are crucial, such as medical diagnostics or legal applications [
]. In this paper, the model extracts the features using the ranking mechanisms.

Feature extraction using ranking in neural networks refers to extracting relevant features from input data using ranking-based techniques within neural network architectures. Ranking-based methods can be effective in scenarios where data instances’ relative ordering or ranking is more informative than their absolute values [
]. Feature ranking in deep learning involves assessing the significance and relevance of input features, also known as variables or predictors, within a deep learning model. Figure
presents the different ranking techniques for feature extraction.

div.p: A chart classifies ranking mechanisms including gradient based, activation based, perturbation based, and model agnostic methods.
Fig. 1
Ranking mechanisms for feature extraction in neural networks
A.

p.span: Gradient-Based Methods: These approaches utilize the gradients of the model’s parameters concerning the input features to measure their importance [
]. By computing the gradients and analyzing their magnitudes, researchers can assess the impact of each feature on the model’s output.

div.p: B.

p.span: Activation-Based Methods: These techniques evaluate the activations of neurons in different layers of the deep learning model [
]. Examining the activations makes it possible to gauge the importance of different features in influencing the model’s internal representations.

div.p: C.

Perturbation-Based Methods: It involve perturbing or modifying individual features and observing the resulting impact on the model’s predictions. Researchers can rank the features based on their importance by measuring the changes in the model’s output due to feature perturbations.
D.

p.span: Model-Agnostic Methods: These approaches train an additional model, such as a decision tree to rank the features based on their predictive power. It uses the Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME), which generates local explanations for individual predictions by training an interpretable model, such as a linear regression model, in the neighborhood of the instance of interest. It assesses the importance of different features by measuring their impact on the predictions of the interpretable model. The computation is presented in Eq. (
Explanation(
) represents the local explanation for the instance
denotes the weight or importance assigned to feature
, which is determined through a process called sparse linear regression, where the interpretable model is trained to approximate the behavior of the complex model around the instance of interest.
′) represents the value of feature
in the perturbed instance
′ within the local neighborhood.

level3.p: An essential part is that featuring rank in DL is challenging due to the complex nature of deep neural networks and the high-dimensional input spaces they operate in. Different methods may yield varying results, and the interpretation of feature importance should be made cautiously, as it is only sometimes straightforward to establish causality or fully understand the learned representations within the model.

The extracted features are passed as input to the DenseNet in the proposed model. DenseNet introduces dense layer connections, where every layer receives direct information from all levels above it. This connectivity pattern facilitates maximum information flow throughout the network. Deep neural networks often encounter the vanishing gradient problem during training, where the gradients diminish as they propagate through multiple layers, leading to slow or ineffective learning. DenseNet mitigates this issue by providing short paths for the gradients to flow through the network.

p.span: In [

], Abdul Khan reported the four classes of the suggested innovative detecting model for brain tumor identification. The accuracy of the suggested model is 92.13%, while its miss rate is 7.87%. An intelligent method is developed for identifying and categorizing brain tumors using a convolutional neural network. To identify and categorize brain tumors, CNN is used to propose a hierarchical deep learning-based classification system. According to the suggested classification, there are four types of tumors: glioma, meningioma, pituitary, and no tumor. 826, 395, 822, and 827 of the 2870 input photos represent the glioma, no-tumor, meningioma, and pituitary classes, respectively. For instance, 826 samples in total are accurately predicted, but 44 are projected wrongly as meningiomas, and 2 are mistaken as gliomas.
In [

], Shtwai Alsubai et al. reported that a hybrid deep learning framework of CNN and long short-term memory is proposed in this research for classifying and predicting brain tumors using magnetic resonance images. CNN-LSTM is a hybrid deep learning model for diagnosing and forecasting brain tumors using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The results showed that the recommended model best recognizes MR brain images. 288 patients participated in the study. According to the researchers, the results showed that the suggested approach most successfully recognizes MR brain images. Future studies should evaluate the performance of the suggested method on images of various kinds of MR brain tumors.
In [

], Ayesha Younis et al. described methodologies for deep learning and VGG-16 combining learning in brain tumor analysis. The recommended approach for locating brain cancers is built on deep learning, and brain MRI is used to assess tumors using automated categorization methods—the suggested solution used convolutional artificial neural networks and a VGG-16 ensemble methodology. Using 253 MRI brain images, a convolutional neural network framework is proposed and evaluated on a dataset for identifying brain cancers. Feature extraction is unnecessary for the network model to apply a tiny amount. The suggested model combined deep learning and transferred learning techniques for a remarkable accuracy rate. High accuracy of 98.14%, 98.5% for the ensemble model and 96% for CNN. In the research, 155 individuals with tumors were enrolled. This study will employ the CNN model due to the current state of expertise and the need for better machine learning.
In [

], Shahzad Qureshi et al. compared to the previous state-of-the-art approach to identifying a tumor without any specialized hardware, the new method has a chance of utility in real-time surgery for brain tumors with a time reduction of 22.07%. To identify and remove brain tumors, they suggest an Ultra-Light Deep Learning framework for feature extraction that forms a Hybrid Feature Space with textural characteristics. A unique Ultra-Light Deep Learning Architecture (UL-DLA) for deep features, combined with extremely distinctive textural data retrieved via Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), forms the basis of an automated Ultra-Light Brain Tumor Detection system. The analysis included 233 patients. Some limitations of our study are recommended as areas for future investigation. This forecasting phase or decision-making is complicated and opaque in deep learning-based techniques. An open white box for modal and multi-center data agglomeration may be used to test the proposed deep learning structure, UL-DLA, from the viewpoint of XAI.
In [

], Hamza Almadhoun and Samy Abu Naser explored deep learning for brain tumor detection. In this thesis, a deep learning approach is described for analyzing magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) images to detect the presence of brain cancers. They obtained 99.28% validation accuracy and 100% training accuracy. In transfer VGG, the ImageNet dataset was used to train the neural network VGG to categorize real-world photographs. The investigation included 130 brain tumors. By analyzing the plot training, one may evaluate the possibility of over fitting. Data loss in the training and validation datasets is equivalent. This result may be improved by using bigger training datasets, other picture augmentation methods, and additional machine-learning algorithms with more ensembles.
In [

], Al-Akhir Nayan et al. reported using magnetic resonance imaging and deep learning to identify brain tumors. Brain tumors may be identified using MRI images using a model built on convolution neural networks. It has a modified hidden layer structure, an automated feature extractor, and an activation mechanism. The most popular and effective method for regularly diagnosing brain tumors is magnetic resonance imaging versatility imaging. In the technique described by Kumar et al., brain tumors were predicted from MRI scans using a fully convolutional neural network. An altered CNN has been used to receive an MRI scan of the human brain as input, find the tumor, and output the finding. The model has outperformed previous methods in MRI image-based brain tumor detection. The suggested design comprises five convolutional layers, five maximum pooling layers, two thick layers, and one input layer. The study’s participant population was 24,000. Table
discusses the analysis of different approaches.

p.span: DenseNet is a popular deep learning architecture successfully applied to various computer vision tasks, including medical image analysis. Brain tumor detection is one such application where DenseNet can be used effectively. Brain tumor detection requires the analysis of complex medical images, such as magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans. These images contain various structures, textures, and intensities vital for accurate tumor identification. DL models, particularly CNNs, excel at extracting hierarchical features from images. Transfer learning allows using pre-trained CNN models, which have learned rich representations of general visual features from large-scale datasets. These pre-trained models can capture intricate patterns and textures in medical images, aiding in accurately detecting brain tumors. The proposed model uses the model agnostic approach, i.e., a random forest ML approach to rank the features. The process is discussed in the below section and Fig.

div.p: A flow diagram for the classification of brain tumor detection using Image Net + B A R T S, featuring dataset, neural network with hidden layer, feature extraction using R F, DenseNet, and classification.
Fig. 2
Classification of brain tumor detection

level3.p: Identifying feature importance in a random forest model involves determining the relevance and impact of input features on the model’s predictions.

p.em: Step 1: Train the Random Forest Approach

p.span: First, an RF methodology needs to be trained on a dataset. This ensemble learning method consists of several DTs, each training on a random subset of the data and features. The process of random forest training is presented in Fig.

div.p: A flowchart illustrating feature importance using Random Forest includes a dataset split into subgroups, ranked feature subsets A to N, rank fusion to select common or highest ranked features, and culminating in the final feature set determination.
Fig. 3
Feature importance using Random Forest

level4.p: One common method to assess feature importance in a random forest is to compute each feature’s MDI. MDI measures the extent to which a feature decreases the impurity in decision tree nodes. Higher impurity reduction indicates greater importance. To calculate MDI for a feature:

div.p: a.
Randomly permute the values of that feature in the dataset while keeping the other features unchanged.
b.
Pass the permuted dataset through the trained random forest and record the resulting decrease in the impurity measure (such as Gini impurity or entropy) compared to the original dataset.
c.

p.span: Repeat this process for each tree in the random forest and average the impurity decreases across all trees. The average impurity decrease represents the feature importance. The computation is presented in Eq. (
denotes the number of samples,

div.p: Actual represents an original class label,
The target represents a predicted class label,

p.span: represents the pixel value of the corresponding instance.

div.p: a.

Once the MDI values are obtained for each feature, they can be normalized to obtain the feature importance scores. This normalization ensures that the important values are on a consistent scale and can be compared across features.
b.
Divide the MDI values by the sum of all MDI values to obtain the relative importance scores for each feature.
c.

p.span: Alternatively, the MDI values can be scaled to percentages by dividing each value by the sum of all MDI values and multiplying by 100. The computation is shown in Eq. (

. Finally, rank the features based on their importance scores. Features with higher importance scores are considered more relevant and impact the model’s predictions more. This entire process is presented in Fig.

div.p: A flowchart starts with loading the dataset, applying random forest to train it, and obtaining the features. Check features are not empty, if no then end, and if yes then select the features with processing, record entropy or average all the entropies for M D I, normalize the obtained feature scores, and rank the features.
Fig. 4
Computation of ranking for input features using Random Forest

p.span: After receiving the ranking features and promoting dense connectivity, DenseNet encourages feature propagation and information exchange across different network depths. This characteristic facilitates learning rich and diverse features, capturing low-level and high-level input data representations. As a result, DenseNet models have demonstrated enhanced feature learning capabilities, enabling them to capture intricate patterns and semantic information in images. DenseNet’s dense connections alleviate the need for individual layers to learn redundant features, lowering network parameters. This parameter efficiency is especially beneficial when computational resources and memory are limited. Moreover, DenseNet’s compact architecture enables efficient deployment on resource-constrained devices, such as mobile and embedded systems, without sacrificing performance. The architecture of DenseNet is shown in Fig.

div.p: A block diagram illustrates the Dense Net architecture, featuring dense blocks, convolution layers, pooling layers, and fully connected layers leading to output categories. Transition layers are depicted at the bottom.
Fig. 5
The DenseNet architecture

p.span: Feature extraction from pixels involves extracting meaningful and representative features from raw pixel values to facilitate machine-learning tasks. The most straightforward approach is to use the raw pixel intensity values as features. This can be useful in some instances where the intensity holds important information, such as in grayscale images. Figure
represents the pixel intensity.

level3.p: By combining the powerful feature extraction capabilities of the DenseNet architecture with feature ranking techniques, the model can leverage the learned representations to identify the most relevant features for brain tumor detection. This approach helps improve the tumor detection system’s efficiency, interpretability, and performance. Select the top-ranked features and employ them as inputs to a separate Random Forest classifier. Train the classifier using the selected features and corresponding tumor labels to distinguish between tumor and non-tumor cases. Utilize the DenseNet architecture as the base model for brain tumor detection. This deep convolutional neural network is known for its dense connections, which promote feature reuse and extraction. Refine the feature extraction and ranking process in future enhancements by experimenting with different feature selection techniques or adjusting the model architecture. Iteratively fine-tune the system to improve the detection performance.

level2.h2: A General Comparative Study of Various Video Compression Standards Based on Their Features, Working Mechanism and Usage Applications

p.span: K. K. Kavitha
and
Rekha B. Venkatapur

div.p: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, K S Institute of Technology, Bengaluru, Karnataka, 560109, India

level3.p: K. K. Kavitha

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: Digital video stand as one of the most prominent means of communication currently. It is due to their engaging capacity to convey a substantial amount of information in a compact format. Every year, there are continuous advancements in video capture, rendering, and display technologies, resulting in an exponential increase in the volume of videos being produced every day. Simultaneously, numerous video content providers encounter the challenge of meeting user expectations for higher-quality videos while maintaining costs at the same level or reducing them. High-quality videos tend to be quite large in terms of file size, resulting in extended upload times, impractical hosting expenses and a requirement for high-speed bandwidth from users and viewers. Consequently, digital videos must undergo processing before transmission, as they often contain significant redundancies. This preprocessing aims to optimize storage usage and fulfil bandwidth requirements more efficiently. Video compression is a method for reducing redundancies in visual data by identifying and representing these redundancies using far fewer binary digits or bits. It involves the elimination of various types of redundancies found in video frames by reducing the total number of bits required to represent each frame. Currently, there are numerous video compression standard techniques and codec that feature structured and logical mechanisms. This review work offers a comparative analysis of such established video compression standards and techniques based on various aspects.

level3.p: Keywords

Video is one of the most prominent mediums in the modern technology. The reason modern digital videos going so attractive is because of the absolute volume of information digital cameras can record with clear details and brilliant chromaticity. But the problem is that it takes loads of data to capture videos which lead to fast filling of computer hard drives because of the massive storage demands of videos and needs notably long wait time to transmit videos on online platforms. Video Compression provides the solution of converting huge amounts of video files generated by cameras into new files having fractional percentage of file size compared to that of original file making it easy to save, send, upload and stream full video contents by compressing it. The strategy is to compress the video streams in such a way that only the file size will be minimized as much as possible by removing elements like non-essential or non-functional data from video files with minimal amount of reduction in visual clarity.

p.span: is the process of transforming large, raw video streams into smaller video files by minimizing the total count of bits essential to display the video streams without disturbing the visual quality. Video streams can be examined as a collection of still images/frames which contains identical and repetitive frames. Video Compression methods retain only those frames which are essential and abolish the redundant frames shrinking the size of a video file. If there are two almost similar frames in the sequence, Compression will eliminate the data for one unnecessary frame and replace it with a reference for the other [
]. Thus, the encoded video result in benefits of consuming less space on hard disk drives and requires comparatively less time to upload or share it over network/Internet.
The structure of a general video-processing scheme is shown in Fig.

. Video compression is particularly carried out by the combination of image compression and motion compensation for spatial and temporal redundancies, respectively. It consists of the phases, motion estimation, motion compensation, transformation, quantization, and entropy coding. Most of the video compression algorithms use lossy compression schemes. Spatial redundancies can be removed through transform coding while temporal redundancies can be eliminated using block-based motion estimation and compensation techniques.

div.p: A block diagram exhibits rectangle on a plain background.
Fig. 1

p.span: Block diagram of a general video coding scheme [

Video compression is accomplished through a video codec that deals with one or more compression algorithms. Normally, video compression is carried out by eliminating repeated frames, audio sections and/or visual scenes from the video sequence. For example, a video may contain many frame sequences having similar background, images or audio that appears multiple times or it may also have some least significant data displayed/attached in it. Video compression will remove all such redundant data to minimize the size of video file [
Basically, redundancies are of four types, namely perceptual, temporal, spatial and statistical redundancies.

indicate the persistency of human eye. In a digital picture human eye cannot recognize few parts of the frame; such parts are selected and removed from the digital data. Due to human eye perception such parts go unrecognizable without affecting the quality of a video [

comes into picture when a video stream is divided into sequence of frames sampled at a particular rate. Two successive frames in a video will mostly have similar features. Temporal redundancy allows eliminating such identical frames in the sequence thus reducing the size of the file retaining all essential features of a video. Temporal redundancies offer good compression ratio during encoding [

explains that increase in the spatial frequency decreases the responsiveness of the eye. It tends to eliminate the higher frequencies existing in a frame without changing the quality of an image just by modifying the levels of frequencies that goes unnoticed by a human eye [

refers to the binary data generated during the final phase of video compression. The parameters such as transform coefficients, motion vectors and all associated data generated from the previous steps are transformed using binary representation. Binary coding can be achieved either by using 16 bits fixed length coding or by variable length coding as a better option instead of 16 bits information as it consumes lesser number of bits to increase the compression efficiency while eliminating statistical redundancies [

There have been extensive innovations occurred in the field of compression technology since the digital revolution of video, and it is destined to change progressively. Although compression technology is advancing consistently, here are some key factors that are to be observed to know on what exactly compression does to a video.

div.p: a.

Resolution: The pixel count existing in each frame is called video resolution. It decides the clarity/clear detail of the video. Resolution of a video can be measured as 360p, 480p, 720p, and 1080p and 4 k, where “p” stands for “progressive” by the way. The actual dimension (length and width) of a video in terms of pixels is provided by these measurements. This deals with the way how fields are scanned when watching video. First of all, a video with a high pixel count possesses sharper and more intricate images. Nonetheless, high-resolution videos tend to have a substantial file size. Hence, it is advisable to minimize the resolution and shrink its size to make it more compact. For instance, when uploading a high-resolution video on a social media platform, it is recommended to decrease the resolution to 720p. This is due to the fact that many viewers watch videos on small screens, particularly smartphones, where only skilled and focused eyes can notice any imperfections or discrepancies.
b.

p.span: Codec: A codec refers to a process, either hardware or software-based that compresses and decompresses extensive amounts of data

Codecs consist of a precise collection of algorithms utilized to achieve efficient video compression. Essentially, codecs comprise two main components: an encoder to compress the files, and a decoder to decompress them.

div.p: c.

Bitrate (Mbps): It is one of the significant aspects of a video file that contributes major portion of size of a file. If the bitrate count of a video is more, better is the overall quality of a video. The only recognizable difference between a low bitrate and a high bitrate video is the fast movement of moving objects. The maximum amount of data transmitted at any point of time is the bitrate and so a low bitrate video makes fast moving objects look uneven, fidgety and broken. Rather, high bit rates send adequate data at a time displaying high speed motion and complete movements evenly with greater detail.

level3.h3: 3 A General Comparison of Video Compression Standards

p.span: ITU-T and ISO/IEC are the two principal international organizations that concludes video compression standards. Two families of video compression standards came out from these organizations namely, H.26x and MPEG. H.26x series proposed by ITU-T focuses on video conferencing applications that include series, H.261, H.263, H.264, and H.265 [
]. The MPEG standards were developed by ISO/IEC concentrates on storage and transmission of motion pictures communications
which includes MPEG-1, MPEG-2, MPEG-4, MPEG-4 Part 10 (AVC), MPEG-7, MPEG-21, and M-JPEG [

]. The sequence of compression standards developed by H.26x and MPEG are meticulously connected to each other. The MPEG’s current compression standards follow a lot many techniques borrowed from latest advancements in H.26x’s current release and contra wise [
]. A comparison and analysis of all these video codecs have been discussed in the below section.

At first, video compression was carried out by applying JPEG techniques on successive video frames. JPEG was applied straightway right on video sequences and hence named as MJPEG. MJPEG mechanism works by encoding each independent frame one by one individually in sequence applying JPEG compression [

]. MJPEG found its application in video editing and for enhanced VCR functionality where irregular access to individual frames is mandatory. The data rates for MJPEG-compressed video typically range between 8 and 10 Mbps.

It was the first fundamental hands-on digital video coding standard and the most renowned international compression technique used for encoding videos. It was an initial pioneering effort, and the entire succeeding international video coding standards such as MPEG-1, H.264, H.265, MPEG-4 are most similarly dependent on its design. H.261 describes an encoder that compresses a video recording transmitted through Integrated Services Digital Network (ISDN) lines. The encoder concentrates on compressing only the video portion of an audio-visual assistance. The input images sent to the encoder must necessarily satisfy both color space and size (width and height) requirements. With respect to color space, the images are mandate to be YC
C
images with 4:2:0 chrominance sub sampling. With regard to size, the images must follow either the Common Interchange Format (CIF) or the Quarter-CIF (QCIF) format [
]. The Table
below defines the width and height dimensions of the format. The H.261 codec finds its primary application in video telephony and video conferencing. It supports data rates in multiples of 64 KBPS (
= 1, 2, 3…30), referred to as P × 64 [
]. Its operating data rates range from 40 Kbits/s to 2 Mbits/s. This codec works on non-interlaced frames that occurs nearly 29.97 times per second with tolerance of about ± 50 ppm.

div.p: Table 1

p.span: Height and width factors defined for QCIF and CIF formats [
In this standard, a hybrid motion compensation is used for inter-picture prediction. The essential components of this codec are
which is spatial to frequency domain translation
while decoder has
p.strong: prediction, motion compensation, loop filtering, etc.
p.span: Firstly, the encoder applies a spatial transform to the input video. Later scalar quantization is performed on it through which the quantized coefficients produced as resultants are scanned in zigzag fashion. Finally, entropy encoding is applied on it. Loop filtering is done to predict data so as to minimize flaws in inter frame coding phase. This working procedure is discussed step by step schematically below.
Initially, the video stream is divided into sequence of image frames of two types namely, Intra-frames called as I-frames and Inter-frames called as P-frames as shown in the Fig.
below.

div.p: A diagram of a set of 9 frames arranged in a sequence, labeled as I, P, P, P, I, P, P, P, I.
Fig. 2

p.span: Representation of I-frames and P-frames for a video sequence [

: I-frames are usually examined as individual images. Within each I-frame, a Transform encoding method is used same as JPEG. I-frame coding concentrates only on removal of spatial redundancy. Macroblocks size for the Y frame is 16 × 16 pixels, whereas for Cb and Cr frames it is 8 × 8 pixels, as 4:2:0 chroma Sub sampling is used. Each macroblock consists of four Y, one Cb, and one Cr 8 × 8 blocks, the representation of which is as shown in Fig.
below. To every individual 8 × 8 block, a DCT transform is applied. Later the resultant DCT coefficients undergo quantization process followed by a zigzag scan and finally entropy encoding.

div.p: A diagram of a computer frame with terms like C o, macroblock, block, D C T, quantization, 1-frame, entropy coding, and 1010010.
Fig. 3

p.span: Structure of intra-frame (I-frame) predictive coding [

: P-frames are non-independent frames which are coded using a forward predictive coding method almost same as motion compensation method. The required progressive macroblocks are anticipated from preceding identical macroblocks which can be either I-frame or P-frame to find the appropriate reference macroblock. Afterward, a difference macroblock is obtained to estimate the prediction error. Further, each and every 8 × 8 block undergoes DCT transformation followed by quantization, zigzag scan and entropy coding operations. The coding structure is depicted in Fig.
below. P-frame coding aims at eliminating temporal redundancy.

div.p: A diagram of a data process, features being processed, used as a comparison point, and a motion vector represents the movement or change from the reference frame to the target frame.
Fig. 4

p.span: P-frame coding based on motion compensation [

The gap between set of I-frames is irregular which will be estimated by the encoder. Normally, a typical digital video has one or two I-frames per second. In H.261 codec, motion vectors are always computed as portion of full pixels having finite range of ± 15 pixels,

In H.261, the process of quantization employs a uniform step size that remains constant for all Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) coefficients within a macroblock. In the case of DC coefficients in intra-mode, if we denote the DCT coefficients before and after quantization as DCT and QDCT, respectively, then,
For all other coefficients, excluding DC coefficients, then,

Scale is an integer ranging from 1 to 31. The H.261 encoder can execute either intraframe or interframe compression for each macroblock that it encodes. The compressor in intraframe approach encodes the existing YC
C
values present in the macroblock whereas the compressor in interframe approach observes the YC
C

values present in the macroblock being encoded and computes the variation/deviation between the anticipated values and the actual values of the macroblocks. The anticipated values are usually derived from the last compressed frame stored in a buffer. It further processes the deviated values if they are remarkable and further also encodes YCbCr values.

The primary objective of MPEG-1 was to achieve VCR NTSC video compression with a resolution of 352 × 240 and the video can be compressed to a level where it can be stored on a CD-ROM with a data rate of 1.2 Mbps. This allowed for instantaneous playback of the video through a standard 1 × CD-ROM drive. MPEG-1 supports frame rates of 25 fps (PAL) and 30 fps (NTSC),

In this technique, progressive frames are organized into a group of pictures (GoP) that contains four categories of pictures namely, I-picture (intra), P-picture (predictive), B-picture (bidirectional), and D-picture (DC). Intraframe pictures, known as I-pictures, are encoded using the JPEG format and are positioned at the beginning of a group of pictures (GoP). In contrast, P- and B-pictures are motion-compensated macroblock residual difference pictures encoded in JPEG, and they are distributed across the GoP [

Additionally, MPEG-1 was designed to exhibit efficient forward and backward searching, also to felicitate smooth integration and synchronization of both audio and video components. In progressive mode, the GoP in MPEG-1 is limited to sequences comprising 15 frames. During data loss and low computation times, a stable behavior was achieved in the encoding and decoding process, making it well-suited for harmonic applications like video telephony. This codec effectively removes both spatial and temporal redundancy.

This standard offers improved quality with a slightly increased bandwidth compared to its predecessor. MPEG-2 maintains compatibility with MPEG-1 and found expanded usage in High-Definition Television (HDTV) and DVD formats, rendering the MPEG-3 standard obsolete. The frame rate remains fixed at 25 fps (PAL) and 30 fps (NTSC), similar to MPEG-1. However, MPEG-2 offers enhanced scalability compared to MPEG-1, enabling playback of the same video in various resolutions and frame rates [

]. The standard's widespread adoption and efficient high-quality video compression led to its expansion to accommodate higher-resolution formats such as High-Definition Television (HDTV). While the encoding principles between MPEG-2 and MPEG-1 are similar, MPEG-2 provides significantly higher flexibility [

This codec was primarily created to serve as a compressed format with low bit rates. Its primary emphasis was on applications like video telephony and video conferencing. H.263 is structurally identical to H.261 and maintains backward compatibility. It offers superior picture quality compared to H.261 when operating within a bandwidth of less than 1000 kbps [

]. H.263 employs a block-based hybrid video coding method, utilizing 16 × 16 macroblocks consisting of YCbCr color sample arrays. It incorporates motion-compensated prediction, an 8 × 8 discrete cosine transform to handle prediction differences, followed by zig-zag scanning of transform coefficients. These coefficients undergo scalar quantization, run-length transformation into coefficient symbols, and variable-length coding. The variable-length coding is similar to Huffman coding but employs structured coding tables

This standard provided excellent picture quality at lower bit rates ranging from 10 Kb/s to 1 Mb/s. It represented a significant advancement from MPEG-2 and was primarily developed for utilization in interactive environments, including multimedia applications and video communication. It introduced new tools to efficiently reduce the bit rate, making it highly flexible and suitable for specific areas of video usage within the MPEG family. The frame content can be structured into objects, allowing for separate retrieval using the MPEG-4 Syntactic Description Language (MSDL) [

]. However, many of the tools in MPEG-4 require higher computational power for encoding and decoding, making them impractical for common, conventional, and real-time applications. The real-time tools in MPEG-4 were already present in MPEG-1 and MPEG-2. The MPEG-4 compression algorithm undergoes a 5-step data encoding process. Firstly, there is a resolution reduction step, followed by motion compensation to reduce temporal redundancy. The subsequent steps involve Discrete Cosine Transformation (DCT) and quantization, similar to JPEG compression, which helps minimize spatial redundancy based on human visual perception. The final step entails entropy coding through Run Length Encoding and the Huffman coding algorithm [

A video compression standard primarily designed for high-definition digital video. It excels in encoding high-quality video at lower bit rates compared to older compression standards. This standard operates by processing video frames using a block-oriented, motion-compensation-based video compression approach. One of the key advantages of H.264 is its superior compression rate, which is approximately 1.5 to 2 times more efficient than MPEG-4 encoding [

]. This high compression rate allows for storing more information on the same hard disk. Additionally, H.264 offers improved image quality and smoother playback compared to basic MPEG-4 compression. Another captivating feature is its ability to achieve lower bit-rates for network transmission [

H.266 is a video compression standard specifically tailored for the latest video generations with maximized visual quality and higher resolution. H.266, the successor to the widely-utilized H.265 codec, brings significant advancements in compression capabilities. It was developed with two key objectives in mind: enhanced compression performance and extensive application compatibility. This newer codec achieves approximately 50% higher compression compared to its predecessor, enabling a video of the same visual quality to occupy only half the storage space. Alternatively, a video of equal file size and bit rate would exhibit a notably improved visual appearance. H.266 can be regarded as the optimal codec for conserving bandwidth and storage space while maintaining a higher bitrate. This enhancement is partly attributed to the enlarged macroblock size. While H.265 permits 64 × 64 pixels macroblocks, H.266 supports 128 × 128 pixels macroblocks (referred to as coding tree units or CTUs), resulting in enhanced encoding efficiency across all resolutions [

VP9 is currently one of the highly versatile video compression formats utilized for streaming videos over the internet. Following the release of its predecessor VP8, Google developed an open-source video codec known as VP9. Although, both VP8 and VP9 formats were designed to be more efficient formats to achieve increased performance employing a block-based motion estimation and compensation technique, VP9 is more advanced compared to VP8. While VP8 has improved compression algorithms and better rate control, that affords better-quality video at lower bitrates and supports multiple resolutions, including HD and UHD. conversely, VP9 is even more upgraded that has substantially enhanced compression efficiency, increased rate control with more efficient coding tools, which allow for higher-quality video at lower bitrates and supports High Dynamic Range (HDR) and a color depth of 10 bits. Comparatively, VP9 is more advanced than VP8 that brings out better-quality video at lower bitrates than VP8 and has superior support for 4 K and 8 K resolutions, making it ideal for streaming high-resolution videos [

]. The VP9 codec operates similarly to the H.265 process and facilitates parallel processing. By employing an algorithm, it compresses raw video files to approximately half their original size, effectively reducing the bitrate without compromising quality making it suitable for smooth transmission over the internet. The VP9 codec tends to enhance the video streaming experience, particularly for low-end devices such as tablets or smartphones and hence due to its limited device and browser support, VP9 is not as extensively used as VP8. Furthermore, its higher decoding power requirement makes it inappropriate for low-power devices.
This section gives a systematic study of extensively used video codec standards. Each of the standards has their own working characteristic and is suitable for specific application. The Table

below gives the evaluation of different video codecs from the earliest to the latest standards over application of use along with their advantages and limitations. It is important to consider the limitations when choosing a compression standard for a specific application. Factors such as hardware support, computational requirements, available bandwidth and desired video quality should be taken into account

level3.p: As digital video delivery advances, so progresses its supporting technologies. Evaluating the effectiveness of a video compression algorithm is complex as each method comes with its own strengths and yields different outcomes depending on the datasets under consideration. Hence, the assessment of video compression technique’s efficiency relies on the datasets and applications being used. Consequently, in every scenario, experimental outcomes will illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed technique, providing well-defined statistics for comparing video compression methods. Numerous studies and developments are on-going in this field as a crucial subject aimed at enhancing compression rates while preserving the quality of compressed videos. Currently, “hybrid” compression schemes are employed to attain improved compression outcomes and the current trend is toward adopting deep learning-based compression methods to achieve higher compression rates. In conclusion, this paper underscores that video compression remains a dynamic field of research with numerous new approaches continuously evolving having several unresolved matters, necessitating further research efforts to investigate promising concepts for future improvements, particularly tailored to specific applications.

level2.h2: Enhancing Steganography Security: A Dual Layer Approach with LSB and AES Algorithm

p.span: K. Karthikeyan Reddy
N. Vinay Kumar
L. Sajan
M. Peddiraju Teja
and
V. Pradeep Kumar

div.p: Department of Computer Science Engineering, B V Raju Institute of Technology, Narsapur, India

level3.p: V. Pradeep Kumar

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: Confidential information such as words, pictures, audio and visual data can be concealed in the cover image. The primary objective is to hide words or images, within the images using the technique known as Significant Bit (LSB) encoding. Advanced Encryption Standard (AES) algorithm is used to protect secret words or images and provide security. AES is used for protecting hidden data.

level3.p: Keywords

Steganography, along with cryptography, is one of the oldest sciences. Its major objective is to convey information secretly in another communication. Steganography does not always provide total protection for a secret massage. As a result, the secret massage requires additional safeguards. For this aim, a steganographic technique that employs the AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) encryption process can be used.

p.span: Through the use of supplementary semantic data, the SSS-GAN model may directly produce stego pictures. The addition of category constraint information allows for speedy training of the model to converge on various image datasets and the production of an extractor that can successfully extract messages [

The proposed method involves using a combination of PSO (Particle Swarm Optimization) and IWT (Integer Wavelet Transformation) for hiding data in communication. By applying packing techniques, the capacity of the image to detect and recover from transmission errors is improved [

A system, for hiding images successfully. In order to transform the cover image efficiently we utilize DHT. The process of embedding employs a substitution based technique that takes into account the characteristics of DHT coefficients [

A cutting edge technique, for securing documents has been developed using deep learning steganography. By combining face recognition and steganography with an approach to measuring changes in structure, the performance of the CodeFace encoder and decoder has been significantly enhanced [

A new approach has been developed to locate the carrier and stego images by employing a method, for determining the suitable LSB matching. The identification of data obscurity bits involves techniques like shifting in both vertical and horizontal directions altering the pixel scanning direction transposing the secret image flipping secret bits and utilizing XOR operations. Given the number of search possibilities steganography is considered an optimization search problem [

level3.p: To provide a dual layer security for steganography method using the LSB technique and the AES algorithm.
It focusses a two-layered strategy that combines LSB (Least Significant Bit) steganography and the AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) algorithm on enhancing the security of steganography.

Ensuring that secret information is communicated in a cover media in a secure and private manner is the key objective. Only authorized users are permitted to examine the secret data because it is secured by AES encryption.
The fixed-size block-operating AES encryption method. Both the sender and the intended receiver are aware of the secret encryption key used.

AES-based LSB steganography works with a variety of media types, including pictures, audio files, and movies. Without requiring significant alterations or changes, it can be used with many types of cover material.

A technique known as bit matching (LSBM) is used to hide confidential information within the least significant bits of pixels, in an image. This method ensures that the secret data remains difficult to detect and uncover. To hide the information in a manner that’s difficult to detect LSBM modifies the least significant bits of the cover image.

Randomized embedding techniques, such as random pixel value changes or random bit flipping, add random modifications to the cover media to embed the hidden information. These methods could be less effective in terms of capacity and security. Random adjustments may cause additional visual distortion, lowering the quality of the cover media and thus increasing the risk that the hidden information will be discovered.

There are techniques used in steganography including Transform Domain Steganography. This method involves hiding information within the modified domain of a carrier signal, such, as an image or audio file. By applying transforms like the cosine transform (DCT) discrete Fourier transform (DFT) or wavelet transform to the original signal we obtain what is known as the “transformed domain,” which represents the original signal mathematically.

p.span: Any organization’s top priority is data security. “Enhancing Steganography Security: A Dual Layer Approach with LSB and AES Algorithm” is the project that we suggest (Figs.
and

p.span: The LSB encoder is essential in steganography because it embeds a secret message into a cover medium. The encoder begins by translating the secret message into binary form. It reads the cover media, obtaining each pixel or sample’s binary representation. The encoder sequentially changes the binary representation’s least significant bits to match the bits of the secret message. The LSB (Least Significant Bit) decoder is a critical component of steganography systems designed to retrieve a secret message from a stego medium. It works pixel by pixel or sample by sample on the stego medium, depending on the medium type. The decoder collects the LSBs of each pixel or sample to generate a binary representation (Table

level6.p: PSNR ratio and MSE ratio for 256 bit AES

p.span: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) encryption involves several main operations, which are repeated in multiple rounds.
AES Encryption

div.p: Sub-bytes Transformation: Each byte of the cyphertext is substituted from the 256-element s-box standard lookup table in a sub-byte. The row index is represented by the first hexadecimal value in a byte, and the column index is represented by the second hexadecimal value.

Shift Rows Transformation: The rows of the matrix are moved left-circularly in shift row transformation. Row0 remains intact, however Rows 1 and 2 are left-shifted by one byte, two bytes, and three bytes, respectively.
Mix Column Transformation: Each column in the freshly produced matrix is multiplied by each column in the previously established matrix.
Add Round Key: The resulting matrix is XORed using the extended key calculated from the initial key.

p.span: AES Decryption

div.p: Inverse Sub-byte Transformation: Each byte from the matrix is substituted with the inverse s-box table to produce the new matrix.
Inverse Shift Rows Transformation: The row of the matrix has been moved to the right in a circular motion.
Inverse Mix Columns Transformation: Function that operates in the opposite direction of the mix column transformation.
Inverse Add Round Key Transformation: In reverse order, the round keys should be XORed with the state matrix.

p.em: Integrated Approach by Using LSB and AES Algorithm

level3.p: The main objective is to ensure the security of information hidden within an image by utilizing the LSB technique and AES algorithm. The initial phase involves decrypting both the cover image and the secret data. After that we proceed to conceal the data within the image using an LSB encoder. To protect it from attackers we then encrypt the resulting stego image using AES, which essentially scrambles each pixel. When it comes time to retrieve the stego image we perform AES decryption. Feed the output into an LSB decoder, which allows us to extract the concealed data at last.
MSE: It quantifies the level of dissimilarity or similarity, between the concealed images. A lower MSE score indicates quality and minimal divergence, from the image.

PSNR Ratio: The peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) measures the relationship, between the power and the noise that affects the representation of an image. A higher value indicates better image quality.

LSB steganography takes advantage of the fact that changing the least important bits of pixel values has the least visible influence on an image. The AES algorithm is a well-known symmetric encryption technology noted for its security and efficiency. It employs a symmetric key, ensuring that the same key is used for both encryption and decryption.

p.span: GitHubRepository “

level3.p: In this study, we employ the LSB approach to hide the secret data in the cover image. As a result, we use the AES algorithm to secure our hidden information, which is one of the symmetric algorithms recommended by NIST and provides stronger security than other techniques.

level2.h2: Deep Features for Age-Invariant Face Recognition

p.span: Priyanka Ravidutt Jadli
Riya Huddar
Ankita Gothi
and
Rupali Kute

div.p: Maharashtra Institute of Technology, SavitribaiPhulePuneUniversity, Pune, Maharashtra, India
Dr. Vishwanath Karad MIT World Peace University, Pune, Maharashtra, India

level3.p: Rupali Kute

level4.p: Email:

p.span: The performance of a face recognition algorithm is affected as facial appearance undergoes a lot of changes with age. Thus, the development of a robust age invariant face recognition (AIFR) system is essential. Training a model from scratch requires a huge dataset and is also computationally expensive. The transfer learning approach has provided solutions to these problems. In this paper, the performance of three pre-trained models FaceNet, VGG-Face, and OpenFace are compared on the FG-NET and MORPH aging datasets. MTCNN and dlib libraries are used for face detection purposes during experimentation. The detected faces are then provided as input to the models which act as feature extractors. The feature vectors generated by the model are passed to Support Vector Machine (SVM) or
-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifier, and accuracy is calculated on the test data. This is used as the parameter for comparison among the models.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Age-Invariant Face Recognition (AIFR) involves recognizing a person accurately despite variation of their age in images. This task is sometimes difficult for humans to perform, but today, there are learning methods that have surpassed human performance at this task. It has become quite popular and has found applications in several fields such as criminal detection, security, and access related applications [

]. A major challenge in AIFR is detecting the person correctly despite the changes in a person’s face with time. Some of the physical changes seen as a person ages are wrinkles, sagging skin, etc. Some of these are natural processes while others are brought about by the behavior patterns in the subject’s lifestyle [

]. For carrying out the AIFR task, it is essential to use a comprehensive database that has specific features including—A large number of images (with multiple images per subject), a wide range of variation in the ages, race, along with the variation of other photographic conditions. There are two options for training a deep network to perform a task—One by training the model from scratch, and the other, using a pre-trained model which employs transfer learning. The former method would include beginning with a very high number of layers, parameters, and randomly initialized weights, which need to be trained over time [

]. The models used for transfer learning are already trained using large datasets, and the weights learned are retained from the original training, which can change as per the application. Some modifications that are made to the model are: Freezing, discarding, or adding some layers. In this paper, we have compared the performance of three pre-trained models FaceNet, VGG-Face and OpenFace on two datasets: FG-NET and MORPH owing to their advantages. We have used TensorFlow at the backend to implement the same. The techniques of MTCNN and dlib have been used for face detection. SVM and KNN are used as classifiers as they prove to be most promising for face recognition applications.

As an increasing number of datasets with high variation in subject ages become publicly available, experimentation in the field of AIFR has gained popularity. However, transfer learning has specifically caught the attention of numerous researchers owing to the high computational costs of the alternative, training from scratch. One such implementation involved the use of the pre-trained AlexNet model to implement face recognition on a custom dataset [

]. With the advent of the FG-NET database, research aimed at interpreting the changes in the appearance of face with respect to age were largely benefitted. As the time between training and testing images increases, face recognition algorithm performance degrades, necessitating the development of an AIFR system [

]. Today some of the widely used databases for AIFR tasks are as follows: YGA, Burt’s Caucasian Face, Gallagher’s Web-Collected, FG-NET, LHI Face, HOIP Face, Iranian Face, MORPH, AI&R Asian Face, WIT-DB, and PAL, etc. However, some of them have drawbacks. The images in YGA, LHI Face, AI&R Asian Face, WIT-DB databases only consist of subjects of Asian race, which limits their accuracy on images across different races. Gallagher’s Web-Collected Database doesn’t have labels. Additionally, most of them do not contain multiple images of the same subjects [

]. If a system uses a dataset with two images of each individual, it is called Shallow Age-Invariant Face Learning. For the existing state-of-the-art methods (SOTA), it is very difficult to give good results on such datasets. In [

], different SOTA methods are evaluated on such Shallow Age-Invariant Face data. Detecting faces is an essential step in the face recognition process. Comparison of different face detection methods Haar-cascade, HOG, LBP is studied in [
]. In [

], Multi-Task Cascaded Convolutional Neural Network (MTCNN) is proposed for detection and alignment of faces and it is seen that deep learning outperforms every other method for the same. The databases used to evaluate the models discussed in this paper are FG-NET [
] and MORPH [

]. The tensorFlow framework is used at the backend while using three pre-trained models: FaceNet, VGG-Face, and OpenFace. FaceNet was developed by researchers from Google for the recognition of faces in 2015. There are two architectures proposed for implementing FaceNet. Here, we are implementing a variant of the Inception layer in this paper as it has been able to aid in reduction of parameters [
]. In [
], a live surveillance model is proposed, using multiple cameras, and uses the FaceNet model for face recognition. In [

] a large dataset consisting of 2.6 M images of 2622 subjects is generated. A deep CNN architecture called VGG-Face is also proposed. The performance of VGG-Face architecture is evaluated on CMU PIE and FERET dataset for face recognition in [
]. Zhoa et al. [

] has put forward a deep architecture, Age-Invariant Model (AIM) and, a benchmark dataset Cross-Age Face Recognition (CAFR) with about 25,000 subjects and an average age of 28.23. A Multi-feature Fusion and Decomposition framework is introduced in [
] for cross-age face recognition. The implementation of the OpenFace model for face recognition is proposed in [
]. Face recognition systems based on OpenFace are trained and evaluated on publicly available datasets like MOBIO, FRGC in [
]. Here, we have performed a comparative study of three pre-trained models FaceNet, VGG- Face and OpenFace on two datasets: FG-NET and MORPH.

The nn4.small2.v1 model being trained with face images which are aligned, hence, face alignment is essential before feeding the images to the model. In this work, Dlib is used for detecting faces, and OpenCV to crop and transform the image generating aligned 96 × 96 × 3 face images as seen in Fig.

. The face detection part gives bounding boxes around the faces in an image. These bounding boxes cannot be fed directly to the model as the faces can be under different poses and illumination. The model can manage with such images if the training dataset is large, but for the smaller dataset, it is important to reduce the size and transform the input image. Dlib makes use of OpenCV’s 2D affine transformation for this purpose. In affine transformation, 68 facial landmarks are used for feature detection. The distances between those points are calculated and compared to the points located in a normal face image. Based on those points, the image is rotated and transformed and thus makes the location of the eyes, nose, and mouth in each face image consistent. In this way, affine transformation normalizes the face for comparison and finally crops the image to 96 × 96 pixels. This pre-processing step is crucial for the overall performance of the model. These images are then used to train and test the model performance.

div.p: A block diagram defines how the input portrait of a woman undergoes face detection and preprocessing to derive the output of class 117497. It involves model architecture and classifier S V M or K N N.
Fig. 1
Block diagram of the proposed system

level3.p: Our paper focuses on comparing the performance of three different pre-trained models on specifically chosen databases—MORPH and FG-NET.

p.span: FG-NET stands for Face and Gesture Recognition Research Network database and is one of the most widely used databases in CNN applications. It consists of 1002 images in total, of 82 different people. The ages of the subjects range from 0 to 69, with about 10–13 images per subject. Sample images of three different subjects, identified by their IDs have been shown in Fig.
. For this dataset, one image has been extracted per class for the validation set.

div.p: Six stacks of I D 013, 04019 E, 08, 327312, 02, and 321159. Each has four portraits of people from different genders, races, ages, and ethnicities with different facial features and expressions.
Fig. 2
Sample images from FG-NET and MORPH database for three subjects at different ages

p.span: MORPH is much larger than FG-NET. It consists of 55,134 images in total with 13,618 subjects with 4 images per subject. The ages of the subjects vary from 16 to 77. The metadata file comprised of details regarding the attributes for each image with a wide variation in races for instance. The sample images from this database can be seen in Fig.

. For this dataset, we extracted validation images only from a small subset of the total classes as number of images in some classes were less (1 or2). We only extracted one image from each class having greater than or equal to four images in its original class to ensure sufficient data for proper training of the model. This turned out to be 6002 classes out of the total 13,618 classes.
The performance of the pre-trained models on the FG-NET and MORPH databases can be seen in Table

. As shown, the accuracy of the FaceNet model is higher than that of the other two models. Also, it is observed that the SVM classifier gives more accurate results on VGG-Face model while the KNN classifier gives more accurate results on OpenFace model. It is also noticed that the models give a different value of accuracy when evaluated on two different databases. FaceNet and VGG-Face provide higher accuracy when evaluated on the MORPH database. All the experiments in this project have been conducted on Google Collaboratory without using GPU.

div.p: Table 1
Comparison of effect of databases on the accuracies of models using SVM classifier

level3.p: Face Recognition is an essential requirement in today’s world. Today, whether it’s a case of security or arranging photos of different people in separate folders, face recognition is the only tool to do this automatically without human involvement. The system should also be able to recognize a particular person at any age. Age-Invariant Face Recognition is the solution to this requirement. The performances of three specific models which are trained on face data: FaceNet, VGG-Face, and OpenFace have been evaluated on two databases: the entire FG-NET database and 1000 randomly selected identities of the MORPH database. This comparative study proved that the FaceNet model provides the highest accuracy on both datasets while VGG-Face is the second most accurate model. The effect on the performance when using two classifiers: KNN and SVM on the three models is also studied. The accuracy when using the SVM classifier is better on VGG-Face while the KNN classifier is more accurate on FaceNet and OpenFace model. The accuracy of models can be further improved by tuning the different parameters like number of convolution or dense layers in the model architecture. The future scope of this project is to enhance the performance of the models by using fine tuning.

level2.h2: Efficient Auditing Scheme for Secure Data Storage in Fog

p.span: P. Lavanya
K. Sandya Rani
and
B. Chaitanya

div.p: Department of CSE, Anurag University, Hyderabad, India
Department of IT, Kakatiya Institute of Technology and Science, Warangal, India
Department of CSE, SR University, Warangal, India

level3.p: B. Chaitanya

level4.p: Email:

p.span: As the Internet of Things (IoT) has in practically no time filled in ubiquity, haze to-distributed computing has arisen as a state-of-the-art new technique. Fog-to-cloud computing, in contrast to regular cloud computing, requires the involvement of additional entities outside the cloud service provider (CSP), such as mobile sinks and fog nodes. Because of these distinctions, fog-to-cloud storage's integrity auditing will be unlike that of conventional cloud storage. Tian et al. have recently taken the initiative to construct a public auditing system for cloud computing transition. Bilinear mapping, proof of knowledge, and other complex public key cryptography techniques are central to their strategy. It becomes utterly ineffective. In this research, we use the common private key cryptography methods of MAC and HMAC to create a more flexible and effective auditing system. Our auditing method is made solid via the use of MAC and HMAC. Finally, both theoretical research and experimental findings confirm that our suggested method is more cost-effective in terms of both data transmission and processing

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Since its introduction by Peterson et al., [
], fog computing has become a standard method for many different types of industrial domains that rely on IOT devices [

]. Mist registering hubs, which go about as a scaffold between IoT gadgets and mists, are furnished with the vital equipment to perform errands like information pretreatment and transmission, as well as essential calculation and capacity abilities. As a result, the fog-to-cloud computing engineering is arising as an engaging choice for information capacity in a few enormous scope modern applications with limited resources.

Some of the same classical difficulties plaguing conventional clouds also affect fog- to-cloud setups. The most effective method to safeguard the honesty of information kept with a cloud service provider (CSP) is a typical concern. This is for the following reason. Some CSPs may attempt to hide the fact that vital information stored on Internet of Things gadgets or Fog Node sensors has been lost or harmed because of different types of attack [
]. Therefore, very much like in conventional distributed computing, fog-to-cloud computing necessitates the creation of reliable auditing tools for secure data storage.
Numerous private and public auditing systems have been published for conventional cloud storage over the last several years [
]. However, there are two primary reasons why fog-to-cloud computing is not immediately applicable to these schemes [

]. The _rst is that it isn’t suggested for clients (or information proprietors) to initially remove this information and produce related authenticators prior to re-appropriating, since the information from IOT is created by a wide assortment of gadgets. The second, more huge issue is that current reviewing arrangements do exclude haze figuring hubs, which are fundamental parts of haze to distributed computing in light of the fact that to their capacity to really process and expeditiously send for enormous scope IOT data. For fog-to-cloud computing, new auditing methods that guarantee data integrity must be developed right away. The first move in this manner was taken by Doerr B et al. in their recent study [

], where they attempt to _fill this gap. Utilizing bilinear planning and the supposed tag-changing technique, they made a public auditing framework that safeguards clients’ namelessness. They used both theoretical research and extensive experiments to determine the scheme’s efficacy.
Proof of retrievability (PoR) is a method proposed by Yardi et.al., [

] that ensures the authenticity of data kept in the cloud. Data integrity in PoR may be guaranteed by combining error-correcting coding with random data block checks. However, the number of verification procedures that can be performed using this method is somewhat low. Provable information ownership (PDP) based RSA-homomorphic authenticators, which might deal with a perpetual number of difficulties and public examining, were additionally introduced simultaneously by Ateniese et al. Subsequently, several studies [
] aimed to enhance the efficacy of communication.
Other studies [

] have pondered the possibility of dynamic updates to PDP systems. Public auditing techniques have increasingly integrated various verified data formats to facilitate data dynamics. Merkle-hash-tree-based public inspecting for live information [
] was presented, for example, by Wang et al. in 2011. To achieve information elements, Zhu et al. later introduced a clever information structure they named a file hash table [
]. A two-layered information structure called a powerful hash table was proposed by Abu-Nimesh et al. in 2017 [
] to give both public examining and ongoing information refreshing. Around the same time, Shen et al. [

] fostered an interesting construction for accomplishing dynamic information that joins a doubly connected data table with a position exhibit. Though beneficial techniques have been offered for conventional cloud storage, only a select few may be immediately extended to accomplish effective and safe check for information capacity in mist to-cloud based IoT circumstances.

There are essentially two basic causes. To begin, instead of the data owners, it is often different IoT devices that create the data in the fog-to-cloud scenario. Second, in the fog-to-cloud scenario, certain new entities are created, such as fog nodes, which also play key roles in processing and transmission. However, they are ignored by the prevalent model of cloud storage. As a result, this void in the public auditing scenario was addressed by Chen y [
] and Doerr, B et al. [

] using methodologies that complement one another. Both articles overlook the most effective private key auditing systems. With regard to fog computing, we point out that Miller Z et al., in their recent work [

], proposed a haze registering empowered mental organization capabilities virtualization approach for a data driven future Web, and they likewise planned a correspondence plot between the mist hubs and the future Web Hubs for the sending system.
In Yardi et.al., [

], by doing random checks on data blocks in addition to using error-correcting code, data integrity may be guaranteed in PoR. However, the number of verification procedures that can be performed using this method is somewhat low. Provable information ownership (PDP) based RSA-homomorphic authenticators, which might permit an unending number of difficulties and public examining, were also presented at the same time by Atniese et al.

level3.p: Though beneficial techniques have been offered for conventional cloud storage, only a select few may be immediately extended to accomplish effective and safe confirmation for information capacity in haze to-cloud based IoT circumstances. There are essentially two basic causes. To begin, instead of the data owners, it is often different IoT devices that create the data in the fog-to-cloud scenario. Second, in the fog-to-cloud scenario, certain new entities are created, such as fog nodes, which also play key roles in processing and transmission. However, they are ignored by the prevalent model of cloud storage.

p.span: As a result, this void in the public auditing scenario was addressed by Kashif and Mohammed [
] and Tian et al. [
] using methodologies that complement one another. Both articles overlook the most effective private key auditing systems.

level3.p: The absence of Attribute Based Encryption (ABE) and data auditing techniques in the system has compromised its security. The system’s vulnerability is primarily attributed to the lack of Identity-Based Encryption (IBE). The omission of ABE leaves the data susceptible to unauthorized access, as it fails to enforce fine-grained access control based on attributes. Additionally, the absence of data auditing mechanisms undermines the system's ability to monitor and track changes, potentially exposing it to integrity and compliance issues. To enhance security, integrating IBE for personalized access control and implementing ABE with robust auditing features is imperative for safeguarding outsourced data.

p.span: The suggested system makes an attempt to go in this direction. Message verification code (Macintosh) [
] and homomorphic Macintosh (HMAC) [

] plans are two examples of private authentication techniques that we propose using in our innovative auditing system. The Macintosh procedure is used in the transmission cycle between versatile sinks and haze hubs, while the HMAC technique is utilized to check the respectability of information blocks put away in CSP. Since a common confidential key is required for the gatherings in Macintosh or HMAC for creating or confirming the labels, this technique isn’t reasonable for adding TPA. We exhibit the value of the framework by trying the hash-based Macintosh procedure portrayed in [
] and the effective HMAC technique described in [
] by Agrawal and Boneh.

System architecture is the conceptual model that describes the structure, behavior, and more views of a system. It is related to the organization of components, their relations and interactions, and guiding principles that govern the design and evolution of systems. It secures scalability, flexibility, and maintainability. As shown in Fig.

Accesses and examines all user requests for decryption permissions on files, as well as the transactions and information associated with those requests.

The private key is generated using this module’s private key generator. Keys like pkey1 and pkey2 are created by splitting them in half. Each user may access their own copy of the produced secret key and any associated transactions in their own private key file.

level3.p: When an end user uploads a file to the cloud server, the content undergoes encryption before being transmitted and stored in the cloud. The cloud system, upon receiving the uploaded data, reads the content and displays it along with a uniquely generated Message Authentication Code (MAC) for integrity verification. Subsequently, if the end user wishes to modify the content, both the data and its associated MAC are automatically updated in the cloud.

Access to files and their contents is governed by permissions granted by the cloud computing platform. Any end user seeking to retrieve or modify files must obtain explicit permission from the cloud server. This ensures a secure and controlled environment where the cloud not only encrypts and stores data but also regulates access through robust permission mechanisms. Users can request and obtain permission from the cloud computing platform to access specific files and their contents, thereby maintaining a secure and authenticated interaction with the cloud storage system.

The purpose of this research is to provide a thorough auditing methodology for organizations making the move from fog-to-cloud environments. Even though our method is not designed for public auditing, it is definitely better to that described by Tian et al. in terms of communication and computation efficiency. The computing efficiency is shown by the simulation results. We think our suggested solution would be a great option for fog-to-cloud computing safe data storage.

level2.h2: CNN Architectures in Lung Carcinoma Nodule Identification: Detailed Analysis with Performance Comparison

p.span: S. Athiramol
M. Sudheep Elayidom
Blossom Treesa Bastian
and
Sowmya K. Menon

div.p: School of Engineering, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi, India
Augsense Lab Pvt Ltd, Thiruvananthapuram, Kerala, India
School of Communication and Management Studies, Kochi, Kerala, India

level3.p: S. Athiramol
(Corresponding author)
Email:
