level4.p: Email:

level2.p: Lung cancer is considered one of the deadliest diseases that have affected many lives in recent years. Early diagnosis of the disease can help in a successful prognosis. Therefore, cancer detection in the early stage is a hot research topic nowadays. CT scans play a crucial role in detecting and diagnosing cancer. They are a valuable tool in the field of medical imaging. This paper uses various deep learning models: AlexNet, VGG-16, VGG-19, MobileNet, DenseNet, and ResNet50. This study aims to find a better classification model that suits the real-time data collected for nodule detection. Better classification is achieved through better feature extraction methods. Among the analyzed models, MobileNet is considered the lightest one. At the end of this paper, a hyperparameter tuning strategy is performed to improve the overall performance of the model such that it approaches the same accuracy as in VGG-16.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Recently, the application of deep learning models has brought transformative advancements to medical imaging analysis, particularly in the early detection of diseases like lung cancer using computed tomography (CT) scans [

]. Deep learning models have demonstrated their efficacy as powerful feature extractors, enabling the automatic identification of intricate patterns and anomalies within medical images. This capability has paved the way for enhanced accuracy, speed, and efficiency in diagnosing lung cancer from CT scans, thereby revolutionizing the field of medical diagnostics. According to the American Cancer Society, the estimates state that out of around 238,340 new lung cancer cases in the US, more than 50% are women. In this context, deep learning models serve as remarkable tools for feature extraction, capturing subtle variations and representations present within CT scans that might elude conventional methods [

]. The remarkable capacity of DL to learn from vast amounts of data has made them invaluable assets in the early detection of lung cancer, where early intervention is crucial for improving patient outcomes. This paper aims to find the best classification model [

] thereby finding the best feature extractors that can be used in various object detection models for the localization of the nodules. Different algorithms and techniques for discerning benign and malignant patients from the CT scan images exist [

]. The study mainly focuses on the performance of CNN models in the dataset collected from the LIDC-IRDI repository. The models are analyzed based on real-time datasets collected from various hospitals and scanning centers for checking the model’s generalization capability.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section
figures out the default values of hyperparameters of various CNN models which are widely used for classification. Section
presents a study of literature. Section
is the experimental setup. Section
depicts the results and discussions. Section
is the conclusion part that concludes the paper.

level4.h4: 1.1 CNN Models for Feature Extraction and Their Default Values of Hyperparameters

p.span: This exploration delves into the utilization of various deep learning architectures of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) [

], as feature extractors for detecting lung cancer from CT scans. The Convolutional neural network comprises of many layers which are the combinations of the Convolution layer, Activation layer, and Pooling layer, followed by a fully connected layer as shown in Fig.
. This section explains various CNN models and their default hyper parameters.

div.p: A schematic of a lung C T image followed by convolution layer plus Re L U, pooling layer, convolution layer plus Re L U, then again a pooling layer is formed. The data undergoes a flattening layer and then a fully connected layer detects a benign or a malignant tumor.
Fig. 1
Layers of CNN

Table 1
]. ResNet50 stands out for its remarkable ability to train extremely deep models effectively. To overcome the challenges of vanishing gradients during training, Residual blocks are used. Table

p.span: ]. MobileNet is designed for efficient deployment on mobile and embedded devices. It aims to achieve high accuracy with minimal computational resources. Table

p.span: ]. In a DenseNet, layers are organized into dense blocks, where each layer takes feature maps from all preceding layers in that block. Dense connectivity mitigates the vanishing gradient issue, making training deeper networks more effective. Table
shows default parameters of DenseNet.
Table 5
In this review, various kinds of literature that propose novel approach for lung cancer detection using deep learning is presented. Su et al. [

] proposed a Faster R-CNN for lung cancer nodule detection in CT images. Using data augmentation, a dataset of 7000 slices was prepared. Training involved multi-stage processes with RPN and Fast R-CNN, comparing ZF and VGG-16 models for feature extraction, with VGG-16 achieving a higher AP of 83.6%.
Zhang et al. [

] proposed multiscale Mask R-CNN framework for cancerous lung nodule identification in PET scan images, this work addresses increased false positives by combining three different scales of Mask R-CNN. Training datasets are formed using three scaled images (512 × 512, 768 × 768, and 1024 × 1024), enabling feature extraction at different scales. The ensemble method is applied to candidate regions suggested by the three models for false positive reduction [
]. Vinit Kumar et al. [

] proposed a novel lung carcinoma detection method from CT scans using Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), involving preprocessing with a median filter, feature extraction using Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), optimization with Gray Wolf Optimization (GWO), and final classification with an iterative RNN.
Pradhan et al. [

] proposed a 3D CNN-based approach for lung cancer detection, involving preprocessing, conversion of pixel arrays to Hounsfield Units (HU), extraction of nodule masks, and subsequent application for retaining nodules. The image is then subjected to cropping, rescaling, and resizing. The model consists of two convolution layers and two fully connected layers, utilizing the Adam optimizer with hyperparameter tuning for training.
Mohamed et al. [

] proposed a method for malignant lung nodule detection using VGG-16 architecture. The input image (224 × 224) passes through 16 layers with a 3 × 3 filter. Image augmentation, including flipping and rotating, enhances training efficiency. Features are extracted using a combination of VGG-16 and ResNet, with model optimization and loss minimization for improved performance. Shah and Parveen [

] explores how machine learning and deep learning techniques perform based on factors like data collection and feature choice. The conclusion touches on limitations and suggests future research directions in applying machine learning to lung cancer.
Asuntha et al. [

] introduced a lung cancer detection method involving offline training and online processing. It includes denoising CT scans, lung window segmentation using Artificial Bee Colony, and utilizing texture features selected by Fuzzy Particle Swarm Optimization for CNN-based classification. Bhatia et al. [
] proposed a lung cancer detection method using deep residual networks. Extracted features are fed into XGBoost and Random Forest classifiers for accurate image classification. Iftikhar et al. [
] proposed a model structured to classify nodules as benign or malignant, the model utilizes a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for binary classification. Barbouchi et al. [

] proposes a strategy that involves utilizing transformer-based technology by incorporating the DETR model as a tool for tumor detection, aiming to aid physicians in the staging of lung cancer patients.
This study uses the LIDC-IRDC [

] dataset which comprises 1018 cases and a dataset of around 4000 images collected from various hospitals and scanning centers. Annotations are done with the help of radiologists. The collected data is split into testing and training datasets in the ratio of 80:20. Diverse CNN models are analyzed via transfer learning. The evaluation matrix used is accuracy. Figure
shows a sample lung CT image.

div.p: An axial C T image of the chest with a hypodense appearance of the cavities of the heart.
Fig. 2
Sample CT image

p.span: This section compares different CNN models on LIDC-IRDI datasets, and the datasets are collected from various hospitals and scanning centers. The analysis is done on the raw images without doing any preprocessing steps other than cropping the image. The aim of this analysis is to identify the model that outperforms other models without doing any preprocessing steps. Figure
shows the performance comparison of different CNN models on the dataset collected. Table
shows the quantitative analysis.

div.p: Table 6
Performance analysis of CNN models

p.span: In the early iterations of our analysis, the VGG-16 model emerged as a clear frontrunner, delivering outstanding accuracy and robust performance. However, it’s important to note that VGG’s prowess comes at the cost of substantial computational demands, rendering it less suitable for resource-constrained environments. Figure

f indicates overfitting. Overfitting arises when a model not only grasps the patterns in the training data that are inherent, but also incorporates noise and random fluctuations unique to that dataset.

level3.p: As we explore practical applications and real-world deployment scenarios, we must consider the trade-off between model complexity and computational efficiency. Recognizing the need for models that strike a balance between accuracy and efficiency, we turned our attention to MobileNet. Notably, MobileNet’s architectural design is tailored to optimize computation cost and model weight, making it an attractive contender for scenarios where resources are limited. Given these considerations, we are currently engaged in fine-tuning MobileNet to further enhance its performance on our specific lung cancer detection task. Fine tuning of the MobileNet is explained in the next section.

p.span: The model used is MobileNetV2. This iterative refinement process aims to harness the strengths of MobileNet while customizing its capabilities to align seamlessly with our dataset and problem domain. Our pursuit of achieving optimal accuracy within the constraints of resource availability underscores the pragmatic nature of our research approach. As we proceed with our investigation, we are poised to uncover insights into the delicate balance between model sophistication, computation, and real-world viability. Through this exploration, we strive to contribute to the advancements in lung cancer detection. The hyperparameters selected for tuning are batch size, dense units, Activation function, and Learning rate. Table
illustrates hyper parameters of the best model that exhibited the highest score in the training phase.

div.p: Table 7
Hyper parameters of the best model

In conclusion, the adoption of the Selu activation function through rigorous hyperparameter tuning signifies a deliberate stride toward optimizing our cancer detection model. Figure
depicts the performance of MobileNet after Hyperparameter tuning. Its inherent self-normalization and stabilization properties have proven instrumental in elevating the model's performance.

div.p: A double line graph of the training and testing accuracy is plotted on accuracy versus the number of epochs. The curve for testing accuracy is parallel to the horizontal axis and the curve for training accuracy has an increasing trend.
Fig. 4
Hyperparameter tuned MobileNet

level3.p: The amalgamation of Selu as the activation function, dense units of 256, a batch size of 32, and a learning rate of 0.01 stands as a testament to our quest for excellence in cancer detection. This composite configuration not only bolsters our model's accuracy but also underscores the importance of hyperparameter optimization in yielding practical, tangible improvements. As we navigate the intricate landscape of medical diagnostics, these choices pave the way for a promising future where technology and healthcare seamlessly intertwine.

In the panorama of our investigation, VGG emerged as the preeminent feature extractor, illuminating a trajectory toward heightened performance and precision. However, the weightiness of the VGG architecture poses a challenge in resource-constrained scenarios, urging an exploration of alternatives that harmonize efficiency with excellence. In this exploration, MobileNet entered the arena as a formidable contender, boasting a lightweight design that navigates the complexities of limited resources. Initially, the model’s training accuracy lagged behind testing accuracy, indicating a potential underfitting issue. This suggests that the model struggled to capture the complexities within the training data. The rapid convergence to 100% training accuracy after hyperparameter tuning implies improved capacity to fit the training data, addressing the initial underfitting concern. The shift in training accuracy highlights the pivotal role of hyperparameter optimization in deep learning. The adjustments made during tuning likely facilitated a more efficient exploration of the parameter space, resulting in faster convergence and better model fitting.

level2.h2: Applying the Image Outline Creation Procedure for Extracting the Visual Content of Images

p.span: D. Saravanan

div.p: Faculty of Operations & IT, ICFAI Business School (IBS), Hyderabad, The ICFAI Foundation for Higher Education (IFHE) (Deemed to be University U/S 3 of the UGC Act 1956), Hyderabad, India

level3.p: D. Saravanan

level4.p: Email:

level2.p: Content-based image retrieval extracts the visual content of images, such as their color, text, and shape. This complexity arises due to the nature of image datasets. This study focuses on the creation and indexing of image files, demanding more attention, and concentration in this domain. The current system produces output based on text-based retrieval. Users must input information in textual form, and the output is provided either as text or in a format requested by the user. In text-based image search, keywords, and image attributes are used to extract the required content. In the proposed technique, image outlines are generated and stored in the image database through image preprocessing steps. This enhances user accuracy in locating desired information compared to text-based retrieval. This approach finds application in various image processing systems, such as Elite, which identifies suspicious images within the database based on user-provided image queries. The method yields more accurate results compared to text-based outputs. Our results demonstrate the convenience and efficiency of this approach.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Today, information retrieval is one of the hot topics in the data mining research domain. Technology improvements change all datasets into digital information. This technology allows users to create more information sets every second. From this large dataset, extracting useful or specific content is one of the hardest tasks for many researchers [
]. Many existing algorithms are available to bring this needed content more quickly, but these algorithms are effective only for specific inputs; they may not work for other datasets [

]. This brings more attention to these specific applications among the many researchers. There are different types of datasets that exist for this; no single procedure or method is suitable, or the existing source does not provide the needed contents effectively. For extracting the image datasets, the user needs to consider various image attributes such as image values, image frame count, time delay between the frames, attribute difference between the frames, etc., which need to be calculated and stored [

]. Extracting this type of information is not as easy as extracting other input datasets. For this type of information, highly trained datasets are required, which helps the user extract the information more accurately.
The User needs to train the image dataset before the actual process gets done. Based on the input, the training phase takes more time, and certain processes are unable to process the indexing [

]. The image datasets are growing very rapidly, which will add an additional burden to the user. For this, a new indexing and retrieval system is required. It will require more time, and the outputs are not efficient. Some of the advantages and drawbacks of the existing technique are as follows:

div.p: Images are identified through key words.
For training the image data, low-level features are used.
The creation of image indexing reduces image searching time.
Brings more relevant datasets.
Inputs are specified in the form of images.
The creation of indexing for image datasets is too complex.
Image datasets are too large.
The user needs to specify the image datasets by using key words or image attributes only.
Image interpretation is complex in nature.
It is time-consuming to retrieve the specific image and more expressive.

p.span: Extracting image data from the image repository poses a significant challenge for many users due to the complex nature of image datasets [

]. These datasets comprise various image attributes, and one or more of these attributes can be combined to extract relevant image sets. However, achieving this process in a single step is rarely feasible due to the intricate nature of the dataset. To address this challenge and avoid confusion, we propose an Image Significance Response mechanism in this paper. The Image Significance Response is a collaborative, iterative process wherein users identify needed or significant images based on their input [

]. This is achieved through the use of average pixel comparison techniques. In each iteration, the most similar images within each set are refined and subsequently stored in the database. Initially, the most significant datasets are constructed into a list. Users then need to identify the most significant image and refine the image query. This process is repeated until the user obtains the most relevant image based on their input [

div.p: Input in the form of images.
Image attributes are identified and used to construct an image query.
The initial training process yields accurate results.
Responses are quicker than text-based searches.
More relevant and accurate results are generated.
Efficiency and search time are improved compared to the existing technique.

p.span: See Fig.

Fig. 1
Recommended structural design

p.span: The investigational arrangement is depicted in Fig.

. This process holds significant importance for many researchers, as it is one of the most crucial operations. The successful implementation of this investigational process instills greater confidence in researchers and enhances the performance of the existing system [

]. A well-structured design leads to accurate outcomes, necessitating researchers to invest ample time in meticulously constructing this process. Most investigational arrangements function as improvements on the existing technique. This facilitates easier design and testing of the newer system. It also provides users with the flexibility to test each module using their previous knowledge of the existing system, enabling them to establish benchmarks for individual modules and thereby achieve more effective results [

]. The improved system encompasses a few additional operations compared to the existing one, allowing researchers to comprehend each operation comprehensively and swiftly. The entire process is divided into two separate functions. In each function, image features are extracted and stored separately for further operations. Currently, various image comparison techniques are available. Utilizing image average pixel comparison, each image is tested, and the most relevant sets are identified [

]. These image sets are categorized into various groups based on their nature, expediting the output generation process. The investigational arrangement serves as a conversion mechanism, transforming the existing system into an improved new method. This phase encompasses manipulator training and server training processes, resulting in more accurate outcomes.

Our proposed work is separated into two main processes: one is client- or user-side operation, and the other is server- or system-side operation. On both sides, input image sets are converted into shots, and each shot's image pixel is eliminated and stored. This process is done on both sides of our operations [

]. Initially, this process may be time-consuming, but it will help extract the needed information more quickly and accurately. Due to this, the efficiency of the proposed technique gets improved. An image histogram or color grid gives the appearance of image pixels in the given image shots [
]. Consider the below table, which contains the image intensity and appurtenance of pixels in the image as shown in the table. A histogram graph plotted for the given Tables

p.span: The image processing workflow is depicted in the preceding figure. Initially, the provided input image is segregated into shots or individual images. This is followed by employing image preprocessing techniques to eliminate unwanted or blurred images through the utilization of image histogram manipulation methods this process is shown in the Fig.

. Subsequently, the remaining frames are gathered and archived within the database to facilitate subsequent user extraction procedures. The average pixel values of each image within the shots are computed and subsequently stored in the database. These values are subsequently employed for both image comparison and image extraction tasks [
]. With each occurrence of user input, this procedure is iteratively executed, and the outcomes are stored within the image database. The output of the process is shown in the Figs.
and

Trained image search function
Step 1: Convert the input image into frames or shots.
Step 2: For each frame, using file handling techniques, extract image features.
Step 3: Each frame's values are extracted and stored.
Step 4: Using the image average pixel comparison technique, compare the segregated frames.
Step 5: Find the duplication and remove it.
Step 6: Refresh the dataset. The entire image dataset will be tested.
Step 7: Repeat steps 3–6 and store the calculated value.
Step 8: Store the extracted frame average pixel value for further processing.
Step 9: Stop if every value is extracted.

Step 9: Stop if every value is extracted.

level3.p: The proposed image outline-based technique operates in two primary phases: the client side and the system side. In both phases, image datasets are trained and refined through various image preprocessing techniques. This refinement process is facilitated with the assistance of a user opinion technique, wherein users provide feedback on the generated image sets. Each pixel manipulation technique is effectively employed to compare image frames and identify duplicate or necessary frames. The current application offers a solution for image retrieval through sketch-based methods. Images can be retrieved based on an image index search property. This property allows for image comparison and retrieval. By utilizing the index search property, various types of documents can be efficiently searched and accessed.

level2.p: Fast development of web-based solutions with different paradigms and multiple industries having platforms emerges as enablers or value creators. Financial companies also have enabled by trends, help develop new services, and enhance internal business procedures, to enhance the now-optional data sharing of realized value enhancements between financial institutions. At the same time, there were also concerns about the data leakage regarding information protection which affects both customers and financial organizations. In order to identify the proper data classifications, it is critical for stakeholders in the financial services industry to determine what information can be shared across financial services companies. In order to detect cyber-attacks that big data cybersecurity analytics (BDCA) systems impact those big data technologies (for example, Spark and Hadoop) for store, analyze, and collect large amounts of security incident data, this research focuses on this problem by describing a new approach that combines attributes extraction for large data cybersecurity analytics. For BDCA systems, reaction time and accuracy are the two most important factors. Frequent modifications to the BDCA system's operating environment, such as those affecting the quantity and quality of security data events, have a substantial impact. Lastly, the technique is evaluated in single-mode and multimode settings utilizing various adaption scenarios and a Hadoop-based BDCA system. The response time and accuracy of the average BDCA are enhanced, indicating evaluation.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: The new direction of technology for big data is government, science, business, and industry. Typical database software is capable of handling and managing data that goes beyond the size of data files for big data in terms of storing, analyzing, and recording information. There weren't many ideas, and big data storage was possible at the time due to current technology. In our society, data was always the most important resource. For instance, the information is collecting by government agencies that contains private data. In past few years, the security of big data has grown as quick as major customer concern. 88% of the clients expressed serious concerns about the privacy of their data [

]. This is taking place as a result of growing security concerns brought on by the increased use of big data through technological adaption. The big data was increased by variety, volume, and velocity. From different input resources, every second, more data is being collected. The massive amount of structured and unstructured data for big data, the normal databases technologies software tools are very difficult to handle. Gathering and processing data from huge implementations of highly connected devices is causing an exponential development in the volume of data inputs at a rapid rate such as that webcams, sensor networks, cars, smart phones, and RFID readers that are required ensuring the security of data, and this size of data is handled by big resources. These devices have continuously generated the data streams without human interference. It has different kinds of data from different sources, which is unstructured. A tremendous impact has the velocity, variation, and large amount of data for current security solutions. These big data security solutions are not designed and created to replace large volumes of data of this type. 90% of data was generated by the world's data in past two years [

]. The different storage media and different storage strategies are required, and the amount of data doubles every two years. The customers and service providers are having big challenges for big data privacy and security. In 2016, 80% of large organizations suffered from big data major security issues. Based on today available tools, analysis is made more challenging by the fact that the majority of these are not in standard forms [
]. In 2013, the new technology trends were introduced by large data, but there are significant challenges in the form of security and privacy concerns that threaten to stall the progress.

Big data has many challenges, not the least of which are security and privacy issues. Threats to the current security have been reported, and the growth of big data has increased data. This large data does not affect the performance of big data privacy and how it is enforced that was a management policy with one issue. Privacy policy is required for flexible, Integrated and customizable information. The user’s privacy was protected by proposed many privacy solutions. For privacy protection device, the integration is used only for one proposed solution. While the majority of research concentrate on a single mechanism, this study applies many mechanisms to ensure user security. The extensible access control markup language (XACML) was created with privacy protection in remember. It uses consistent control rules. A language standard based on XML is XACML, developed by the Organization for the advancement of structured information standards (OASIS) [

]. Due to its volume and size, most businesses have found it difficult to protect big data once it has been stored, especially when trying to improve data performance and efficiency. It has no purpose because the best way to keep data from falling into the wrong hands is to make sure. Big data security is classified into different levels that are processing, authentication, storage level, and communications. The big data security was not built by current technology. Hackers can easily stole the data in critical information that data is stored in plain text. Access to essential details is not permitted, making it impossible to identify data misuse. The majority of firms utilize technology that ensures security and privacy, which is crucial for gaining over customers. These organizations analyze or keep both their own and their customers’ data. Therefore, in order to safeguard large data, it is necessary to invest, understand, and analyze the problems along with coming up with improved solutions. From digital information, the useful data can be examined and eliminated for gathering from various sources, including government, credit card, banking, and medical organizations. Big data's primary security and privacy concerns are overcome by the information's usefulness in consumption. These days, privacy concerns are the main cause for concern due to the large amount of personal data available on the internet in digital form [
]. The big data was managing the security policy for this challenge that described model was handle.
In Takabi and Joshi [

], a framework for cloud-based policy management was created, as introduced by policy management as a service (PMAAS). Hence, their policies can be controlled by users and accessed to their storage of resources. A structure for the company that provides policy management services, which manages access policies, is comprised of cloud users. It also consists of a service provider-managed policy system that allows users to define, update, and manage policies. A cloud service provider, that follows the guidelines that users of the cloud specify, controls resources through a third module. Last but not least, the resource requested by the particular cloud user is an application that works with a cloud service provider to grant access to secured data.
Engelhardt et al. [

] stated that security scenarios used to begin the processing of big data could produce incorrect results, and that it can be extremely challenging to determine if the data is faulty or not. For storing non-relational data, where security is dependent on middleware, the second level of protection is used. The next security case and our ability to prevent unauthorized access to data are safeguarding the data storage. Next, the significance of verifying the input devices was explained. The field of big data security is changed in real time security monitoring. Another crucial application for big data security is the privacy of the customer during data mining. End-to-end data security is one of the large data security use cases. In Hamlen et al. [

], the three important items are considered that are computation, policy requirements, and data types. A proposed framework is presented for cloud data management policy enforcement. A policy-reasoning module, that links a policy to a specific set of duties and a framework for data processing that complies with policies, offered the following modules. Different rewrite strategies are considered from enforces policies by data processing task rewriting module. Preprocessing modules execute preprocessing operations on the underlying data. To enforce policies and manage the final results, the post-processing module is implemented.
Basescu et al. [

] describe the convenience of cloud data storage for the providers, to put security plans and policies into practice, and a security program has been made accessible with the help of the policy management module. This allows the evaluation and analysis of user activities for every system, ultimately classifying them as either “malicious” or “fair.” A set of guidelines and policy definitions have been put into place for security maintenance, and they ensure that the system will function in accordance with the specifications.
In Bhuyan et al. [

], accuracy and response speed were the two most crucial BDCA system quality factors. A BDCA system's capacity to identify harmful access is evaluated based on accuracy rather than its ability to block any legitimate access. Accuracy and response speed were the two most crucial BDCA system quality factors (for example, hardware failures, parameter configuration and algorithm efficiency) that the BDCA system, which is crucial in promptly identifying threats, impacts accuracy and response time in addition to elements that are frequently referred.

level3.h3: 3 Frame Work of Security-Aware Information Classification Using Attributes Extraction for Big Data Cybersecurity Analytics
level3.p: In this section, frame work of Security-Aware Information Classification Using Attributes Extraction is observed for big data cybersecurity analytics (BDCA). Initially, the data sources are taken like Net Flow data, data from email servers, application servers, firewall logs, operating system logs, packet data, and intrusion detection systems (IDS).

The security data collects data collection from data sources in the module (e.g., IDS firewall logs, log data, Net Flow data, email servers, packet data, application servers, and operating system logs). This way of BDCA system for external data sources was connected. Modules use different tools that are used by data collection module (e.g., Nmap and Wireshark) for gathering the data. The data storage manages all the issues related to the security analytics layer and the application of big data technology and the Big Data Support Layer's leverage. As a result, there are two modules in the layer.

The back-and-forth storage of the data security is maintained by the huge data storage support module, and information is handled by following the steps of each module and security analytics layer. Several data storage systems are used by this module (such as Cassandra, Mongo DB, or HDFS) to support the necessary data storage. Phase engineers, or preprocesses, are effectively used in the later stages that allow data security for data engineering.

The following components are the phase incorporates. Duplicate components are eliminated in order to remove unnecessary details from the security data. The existence of duplicate data is a significant factor that makes the machine learning (ML) model on the AD component target and avoid numerous records, for gaining knowledge about uncommon behaviors related to dangerous attacks. The security information that has been gathered is used to evaluate the quantity of records that handle the missing values component.

This component either deletes records or selects data by utilizing statistical estimate, and missing values are imputed to fill in the empty spaces. Incorrect data component filters are removed from the obtained data in order to eliminate the records with wrong values. For example, a network connection's number of bytes with a negative value (such as − 20) it is considered to be an inaccurate value. Valueless data that doesn't support in attack detection is removed by the data component. A network sniffer, for instance, is used during handshaking in Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol (TCP/IP) connections to gather zero-byte data. For security analytics of such data does not have any application; hence, it should be discarded.

p.span: For instance, the first connection's 15 KB of data is just retained to guarantee that amount of data of cutoff limit of components that can be imposed for every network connection. The stage of data engineering known as “feature engineering” is in charge of creating, selecting, and modifying features (Fig.

level3.p: By utilizing advanced algorithms like Info Gain, the feature selection component chooses the most important features. The goal of development is accuracy, whereby feature creation components generate new features from existing ones. The part of the feature transformation uses methods like feature normalization into a 0–1 range to turn the features into a different space and assist the ML model distinguish among different classes more clearly (e.g., malicious record and benign record). This phase consists of elements that are in charge of implementing different strategies to guarantee efficient data processing in the processes that follow process engineering.

In this architecture, the phase involves components: (1) The best machine learning algorithm is determined by validating and utilizing existing algorithms, which determine the ML algorithm selection component. (2) The phase of data processing is necessary, and the parameter is the tuning section manages the adjustment of many parameters (e.g., K-means value). Phase procedures prepare the security data in simultaneously, signaling potential attacks and providing insight into data extraction. As a result, the two parts of the BDCA system phase anomaly-based detection and signature-based detection can be categorized according to the processing method.

The detection component of the attack, which is signature-based detection, is based on previously identified patterns, the anomaly-based detection component, on the other hand, utilizes machine learning techniques based on deviation from previously understood typical behavior to identify risks. Use attributes extraction's privacy-focused search technique to get a list of all sensitive data and the information.

p.span: Data utilization is the basis for the history, both secure and insecure operations by extracting attribute entities. The data classification requires to follow with the regulations of protection for financial privacy such as normal data, confidential, official, or restricted information. Financial enterprises were defining which harmful sharing is considered by the level of the information leakage. The requirement of shorter computation time is generated by an optimized decision tree generation algorithm. The normal data exchanges take places, usually the data sharing problem without awareness. For example, privacy sharing does not affect users’ birthday information separately. Though, the sharing of user’s birthday information with their data was determined to be a violation of their privacy. As a result, in order to maintain privacy, data sharing should cover both the sharing of common datasets and the transmission of individual datasets. The problem is defined as a problem based on this cognition. The security data that is analyzed, collected and prepared by the Security Analytics Layer is responsible for detecting attacks (Table

level3.p: The large framework for big data security is part of the privacy policy. Several strategies made up the big data security framework, including the encryption approach, security manager, security policy manager, and fragmentation strategy. Because the operational environment is subject to frequent changes, the BDCA system affects the system's accuracy and response time, to guarantee that the BDCA system's design is optimized to take into consideration variations in the environment that affect accuracy and response time during runtime. In this approach, the evaluation demonstrates the effectiveness in terms of adaptation stability, time, and accuracy. Each process can calculate the delivered Quality of Service (QoS) during design and use it during runtime to minimize adaptation time. The goal is to examine BDCA in an online mode to determine whether it works in real-world operations. Therefore, utilizing several adaption scenarios and a Hadoop-based BDCA system approach, in both single-mode and multimode settings are used. According to the review, the accuracy and response time of the BDCA were enhanced by the average BDCA.

level2.h2: Study and Analysis of FIDO2 Passwordless Web Authentication

p.span: Harshavardhan Ravilla

level2.p: As cyberthreats evolve, traditional password-based authentication systems have proven inadequate. FIDO2 passwordless authentication offers a compelling solution, eliminating passwords while enhancing security and user experience. This survey examines FIDO2’s core components, integration with blockchain technology, accessibility for diverse devices, and comparative usability. Findings suggest FIDO2 delivers superior security and usability compared to passwords. However, challenges remain regarding standardization, privacy, and balancing security with convenience. Further research into multi-factor authentication, biometrics, cross-device functionality, and blockchain integration can advance FIDO2’s accessibility, security, and adoption. This analysis underscores FIDO2’s transformative potential to redefine authentication.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: As digital systems proliferate, secure user authentication is imperative. However, traditional password-based approaches face rising threats [
]. Phishing, social engineering, and data breaches enable credential theft and unauthorized account access [
]. Users struggle to create and recall complex passwords across expanding online accounts [
]. These vulnerabilities necessitate an evolution in authentication paradigms.
Fast Identity Online (FIDO2) enables passwordless web authentication using alternative methods like biometrics and hardware tokens [

]. FIDO2 offers enhanced security, eliminating many password risks while improving usability and convenience. Findings reveal FIDO2’s advantages but also highlight ongoing standardization, privacy, and usability concerns [
]. Additional research can refine FIDO2’s security, inclusiveness, and adoption. This analysis underscores FIDO2’s potential as a disruptive force in authentication.
: Users register a public/private key pair to verify identities. The private key is kept safe on the user's device, while the public key is registered with the server [
: Fingerprints, facial recognition, or other unique user attributes. Users’ inherent physical characteristics and traits allow passwordless biometric authentication [
]. Peripheral devices, such as USB keys. The token activates to authenticate the user’s session [

level4.h4: 2.1 FIDO2 and Its Two Core Protocols

p.span: Figure
shows FIDO2 architecture-based communication [

]. Client-to-authenticator protocol version two (CTAP2) is used to communicate between the cryptographic authenticator and the client via USB. To communicate with the server over the network, the client's browser uses the JavaScript-based Web Authentication API.

div.p: An architecture diagram of the F I D O 2 system. The client or browser communicates with an authenticator that has a secret key via a U S B and the client to authenticator protocol C T A P 2. The client connects to a server or relaying party via a network and the Web A u t h n A P I.

p.span: FIDO2 system architecture [
WebAuthn is an web API used for web/app logins using on-device authenticators [
]. WebAuthn is the web API using public key cryptography, and servers can register and authenticate users.
Client-to-Authenticator Protocol (CTAP) allows authenticators to communicate with client devices. CTAP enables interaction between authentication hardware/software and the client platform [
]. FIDO2 provides standards enabling passwordless methods to authenticate users’ identities via biometrics, devices, and cryptography [
]. This eliminates reliance on passwords that are hard to manage and vulnerable to guessing, phishing, and reuse [

level3.p: This study evaluates previous work and analysis on traditional based password-based authentication and FIDO2 passwordless authentication.

p.span: A comprehensive study provides a broad overview of passwordless authentication [

]. They outline traditional password problems including vulnerability to guessing, reuse across accounts, and phishing. Passwordless techniques like biometrics and WebAuthn are highlighted as more secure and user-friendly. FIDO2 is recognized as a prominent passwordless platform due to its cryptographic strength and usability.

level4.p: This study emphasizes how passwords remain susceptible to brute force attacks, especially as computing power increases. Password reuse also critically undermines security by linking multiple accounts and services.

FIDO2 provides standards and protocols enabling such methods to be deployed and adopted at scale across the web ecosystem. Its cryptographic foundations and alignment with natural user behaviours highlight its game-changing potential.

p.span: The possibility for encompassing FIDO2 with blockchain infrastructure [

]. Blockchain characteristics like decentralization, transparency, and immutability can enhance authentication. For example, private keys used in FIDO2 can be registered on blockchain ledgers as user identities. Verification via WebAuthn would then allow passwordless blockchain transactions.
In Fig.
, blockchain-based FIDO2 implementation shows about

div.p: A flow diagram defines how the user authenticates the authenticator that sends C TAP 2 to the client device, and the user receives an authentication success message. The client device includes a browser, blockchain network, FIDO 2 server, and credential database.
Blockchain-based FIDO2 implementation

li.p: User authenticates locally to authenticator via biometrics or security token.
Authenticator communicates with client device over CTAP.
Client device calls WebAuthn API on the browser.
Browser sends authentication request to FIDO2 server.
FIDO2 server writes credential record to blockchain.
Blockchain adds transaction to distributed ledger.
FIDO2 server checks credentials in database.
After verification, authentication is approved via WebAuthn.
User achieves passwordless authentication.

level4.p: The blockchain network provides a decentralized tamper-proof store for credential records, enhancing security and transparency of the FIDO2 infrastructure. This demonstrates conceptual synergies between blockchain decentralization and FIDO2 passwordless authentication.

p.span: Address FIDO2 accessibility for devices with limited interfaces like IoT systems. They propose “Remote WebAuthn” to enable passwordless authentication on these devices via alternate interfaces like voice control or proxy devices [

]. For example, users could invoke verbal credentials on a voice assistant device to remotely authenticate with an IoT system lacking its own user interface. This preserves FIDO2’s security advantages while accommodating diverse user abilities and device capabilities. Users can leverage interfaces attuned to their needs and limitations to interoperate with less accessible systems [
]. It expands the scope of passwordless authentication to novel contexts of use, such as within smart homes controlling appliances via voice commands.

level4.p: This study emphasizes architecting authentication flows around users’ needs and constraints. Individuals with disabilities may struggle with certain biometric techniques. Supporting flexible authentication channels tailored to their abilities promotes inclusiveness.

p.span: Conducted a comparative usability study of FIDO2 passwordless methods versus traditional passwords [

]. Metrics included task completion time, user errors, and user satisfaction. Results showed FIDO2’s superior performance, with faster logins, fewer errors, and higher user satisfaction. However, users still faced challenges understanding new authentication concepts. The study involved 48 participants performing representative password and FIDO2 authentication tasks [
]. Password tasks involved entering an assigned complex password. For FIDO2, users authenticated via fingerprint reader or physical NFC card tap. Metrics captured included.
Task Completion Time
Measured duration for users to complete each login.
User Errors
Instances where users failed to login successfully.
User Satisfaction
Self-reported ratings on ease of use.

level4.p: This quantitatively demonstrates FIDO2’s advantages in efficiency, errors, and user perception relative to incumbent password practices. But quality of experience metrics also reveals ongoing adoption and integration challenges requiring further enhancement for truly seamless authentication experiences.

p.span: Table

summarizes key differences between legacy password-based models and emerging FIDO2 passwordless paradigms for user authentication. Traditional approaches rely solely on knowledge factors, requiring users to memorize and accurately type usernames and passwords. In contrast, FIDO2 passwordless authentication utilizes modern cryptographic protocols and biometric factors intrinsic to individual users. This provides enhanced resilience against many common attack vectors including phishing and data breaches. Further, employing user-unique attributes and devices streamlines the authentication experience, reducing the friction of recalling and inputting traditional passwords.

div.p: Comparison of traditional password model and FIDO2 passwordless model

td.p: Vulnerable for guessing, phishing, data spill
Passwords must be memorized and typed
Authentication like Touch ID, face recognition, hardware key
Separate credentials needed for each service
Can be challenging for some users
Accommodates more abilities for disabled one
Hard to reset and manage password
User friendly and need not to store the password

level4.p: Additional advantages of passwordless models highlighted in the table include improved accessibility for users with disabilities, scalability across services without separate credentials, and leveraging advanced technology beyond sole reliance on fallible text passwords. While trade-offs exist, FIDO2's multi-factor approach aims to deliver superior security and usability compared to legacy single-factor password paradigms.

level3.p: Integration with blockchain provides decentralized verifiability, while accommodating diverse devices enhances accessibility. However, challenges remain around standards, privacy, and balancing security with usability.

p.span: Varying FIDO2 platforms complicate integration. Differences in implementations and client support for emerging standards like WebAuthn pose hurdles [

]. Users struggle managing credentials across different services and devices. These fragmentation issues inhibit adoption and realization of FIDO2’s full benefits. Cohesive universal standards, rather than fragmented proprietary variations, are key to securely unlocking FIDO2’s potential at global scale.
The fractured technical landscape poses interoperability barriers. Differences between platform implementations complicate FIDO2 integration and consistent user experiences [

]. Communications research on technology diffusion emphasizes the importance of standardization and universal protocols enable disparate systems to interconnect. Accordingly, expanded efforts at harmonizing various vendors’ FIDO2 implementations can improve technical interoperability and usability. International standards bodies like the FIDO Alliance play an important role in driving alignment.

level4.p: Additionally, open source FIDO2 codebases enable collective development and transparency. Shared references like Google’s FIDO2 Libraries facilitate consistency. Open collaboration allows inspecting code ambiguities and aligning approaches.

p.span: Users struggle understanding and using new FIDO2 concepts. Lacking familiarity with passwordless paradigms impedes adoption. Furthermore, suboptimal integration and lack of single sign-on capability reduces usability benefits compared to passwords. Rethinking system designs and user interactions can enhance understanding and ease of use [

Sharing biometric profiles raises concerns. Stoking fears of surveillance or misuse will deter adoption. Accordingly, engineering privacy into technical FIDO2 implementations is critical. Cryptographic solutions that preserve confidentiality without compromising security warrant research [
]. For example, zero knowledge proofs allow validating identities without revealing underlying biometric data.
Simplifying authentication risks reducing effectiveness. However, forcing complex security procedures undermines user experience. Reconciling security with convenience remains an open challenge [

]. Adaptive and context-aware models may help achieve equilibrium. For example, transparently elevating authentication factors for high-risk transactions or unfamiliar devices provides added security when needed without constantly overburdening users. Research into dynamic and risk-based authentication tailored to usage situations can balance security and experience.

level4.h4: 4.3 Advancing FIDO2 Through User Centric Design

p.span: While promising, FIDO2’s potential remains encumbered by lack of standards harmonization, suboptimal integration with existing systems, and poor user experience design [
]. However, insights from human-centred design research offer pathways for refining FIDO2’s capability and adoption.
Driving widespread FIDO2 adoption requires designing authentication interactions that map to existing user mental models [

]. Cryptography and public key protocols fundamentally differ from ingrained password behaviours. Mapping abstract security mechanisms onto tangible metaphors and experiences makes them more intuitive [

level5.p: Consider hardware security keys, a FIDO2 authenticator option. These resemble common physical keys—a mental model familiar to most people. Software interfaces can build on this metaphor, representing cryptographic challenge response as akin to using a physical key to unlock a door. This frames the interaction in a relatable way, smoothing adoption. Leveraging metaphors deeply embedded in users’ existing mental frameworks makes novel authentication methods feel more natural and integrated into ordinary routines.

p.span: Authentication interactions should align with user habits and ingrained behaviours. Attempting to change entrenched routines requires immense effort and faces resistance. Accordingly, FIDO2 adoption benefits from seamlessly overlaying passwordless authentication onto habitual behaviours [

level5.p: Contextual integration enables habit-driven use critical for passive adoption. Location-based contextual triggers offer one avenue, automatically applying FIDO2 when entering offices or buildings. Coupling authentication initiation with entrenched environmental or behavioural cues makes it reflexive instead of interruptive. Habit-focused design that taps into habitual patterns rather than confronting them can dramatically accelerate FIDO2’s assimilation into everyday routines and rituals.

level4.p: While promising, FIDO2 faces integration hurdles with legacy systems and fragmented standards while advancing through the user centric design.

level5.p: Integrating FIDO2 with extensive legacy systems using embedded passwords poses challenges. Both protocols must be supported concurrently during transition periods. This raises technical and user experience (UX) complexities. Strategically bridging old and new paradigms is vital for successful assimilation.

p.span: Backward compatibility allows simple adoption. Ensuring FIDO2 authenticates seamlessly with existing password-only sites provides this. One approach is securely integrating FIDO2 identity verification into common web single sign-on protocols like OAuth and OpenID Connect [
]. This allows adding FIDO2 alongside legacy password authentication within the same flows.

level5.p: Gateway architecture also enables compatibility. Standalone FIDO2 authenticators users already possess can securely log them into password-only systems via credential translation gateways. This intermediates between interfaces, avoiding disruptive system changes.

p.span: Biometric authentication, using physical or behavioural traits for identification, is a core enabler of FIDO2 passwordless models [

]. Assessing its strengths and limitations provides insights into improving real-world performance. To balance biometric strengths and weaknesses for optimized security by ingeniously integrating multifaceted security orchestra like Multi-factor Authentication (MFA), continuous risk monitoring, tokens in a hybrid authentication.

level4.h4: 5.1 Biometrics Offer Notable Advantages Over Passwords

p.span: : Users need not memorize credentials. Presenting inherent characteristics is convenient.
: Biometric traits are unique to individuals. They cannot be guessed or directly shared, enhancing security.
: Modern recognition algorithms have high accuracy when well-implemented, rarely misidentifying legitimate users.
: Users need to only enrol once. The same biometric authenticates across services, avoiding proliferation of credentials.
: Biometrics accommodates diverse needs and abilities lacking dexterity for passwords or tokens.
These advantages highlight biometrics appeal for usability, security and inclusion. When thoughtfully applied, biometrics can strengthen authentication experiences

level4.p: Biometric authentication also exhibits shortcomings:

p.span: : Biometric data reveals sensitive personal details warranting protection.
: Sensor or environmental variability can degrade signal quality, reducing reliability.
: Adversaries may attempt to fake fingerprints or other characteristics.
: Ailments or disabilities can impede using certain biometric modalities.
: Processing and validating biometrics adds potential delays compared to instant password checks.
: Biometric traits cannot be reset like passwords if compromised. New enrolments may be needed.

level4.p: These limitations highlight the need to judiciously apply biometrics based on context, augment with secondary factors, and provide alternatives accommodating user diversity. While offering advantages, biometric technologies warrant thoughtful implementation considering context, diversity, and combinations with other mechanisms. Careful engineering can maximize their potential while minimizing limitations.

Hybrid authentication combining multiple factors offers one approach balancing biometric strengths and weaknesses. For example, a fingerprint scan might unlock a mobile device, but high-risk transactions could require an additional PIN or hardware token. Redundancy compensates for limitations like biometric noise or spoofing. Contextual risk-based factors adjust security strength dynamically. Multimodal biometrics strengthens reliability via multiple traits. Hybrid models thus strategically overlay factors to optimize security, usability, and inclusion.

Advanced models using biometrics for continuous risk-based authentication. Unlike single-point login events, continuous authentication passively validates users throughout sessions based on background monitoring of their behaviours and movements. This enhances security, responding dynamically to potential risks.

level3.p: This study analyses research illuminating FIDO2’s principles, applications, and adoption challenges. Findings reveal FIDO2’s immense promise to transform authentication. Its passwordless approach offers security, usability, and accessibility benefits. However, ongoing standardization promoting interoperability remains imperative, as does inclusive UX design and education optimizing user experiences. FIDO2 demonstrates immense potential to reshape authentication technology and culture. But deliberate collaborative action is needed to develop frameworks enabling its capabilities to be compellingly and equitably harnessed worldwide.

Future research can focus on exploring postquantum cryptographic algorithms, continuous multi-factor authentication frameworks responsive to contextual risks and zero trust network authentication, enhanced accessibility and inclusivity, and improved interoperability through standardization efforts.

level2.h2: Identifying Objects at Construction Sites Using CNN

level2.p: The construction industry generates a massive number of images throughout a project’s lifecycle, including design, construction, and post-construction phases. However, managing and organizing these images can be a challenging task due to their large volume and the need to identify and label the various objects and materials captured in each image. This project aims to develop a system for identifying and labelling images captured at a construction site. The system utilizes object detection and deep neural network (CNN) to accurately identify different objects and materials present in the images, such as excavator, dump truck, concrete, and forklift. The identified objects are then labelled with descriptive tags to facilitate easy search and retrieval of the images later. By automating the identification and labelling of objects in images, the system can improve project management, enhance safety and security, and reduce errors and delays associated with manual image labelling and organization.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Object detection and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) are powerful techniques that can be used to improve safety, productivity, and efficiency in construction sites [

]. In recent years, there has been a growing interest in using these technologies to automate tasks such as monitoring worker safety, identifying equipment and materials, and tracking progress on construction projects [

]. To identify and locate objects, a computer vision technique such as object detection must be used. This technique can be used to identify safety hazards on construction sites, such as workers not wearing safety gear, or to identify equipment and materials on the site. With object detection, it is possible to automate the monitoring of safety and compliance, reducing the risk of accidents and injuries. Neural networks like CNNs have proven to be very effective in image processing and recognition [

]. CNNs are capable of learning and extracting elements from images, which makes them ideal for object detection and classification in construction site imagery. By training a CNN model on a large dataset of construction site images, it is possible to automatically identify and label different objects and materials on the site, making it easier to manage and organize images. In summary, object detection and CNNs are powerful tools that can be used to improve safety and efficiency in construction sites. With the ability to automatically identify and label objects and materials in construction site images, these technologies have the potential to revolutionize the way construction projects are managed and executed [

level3.p: The proposed problem is to develop an automated system that utilizes Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to identify and categorize different objects and materials present in construction site images. The system will be trained on a large dataset of construction site images, and the CNN model will be designed to learn and extract features from the images to identify different objects and materials accurately.

CNNs contribute to enhancing safety by enabling real-time detection of potential hazards. These networks can accurately identify objects such as unattended tools, equipment malfunctions, or misplaced materials, alerting workers and supervisors to potential risks. By promptly identifying hazards, construction companies can take preventive measures to avoid accidents and create a safer working environment. The use of CNNs improves efficiency by automating the process of object recognition. Construction sites involve the coordination of various resources, and CNNs enable the automatic identification and tracking of tools, equipment, and materials. This automation reduces manual effort, saves time, and streamlines workflow management. Workers can quickly locate and access the necessary resources, leading to improved efficiency and productivity on construction projects.

p.span: The proposed work aims to develop an automated system that utilizes Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to identify and categorize objects and materials in construction site images. This system aims to leverage the power of deep learning and computer vision techniques to automate the process of analysing construction site images and extract valuable information. The primary objective of identifying objects at a construction site using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) is to automate the process of detecting and classifying various objects present in construction site images. This includes recognizing construction equipment, building materials, safety gear, structural elements, and other relevant objects. The use of CNNs for object identification at construction sites provides several benefits. It reduces the need for manual inspection and human intervention, saving time and resources. It also enhances safety by identifying potential hazards or non-compliance with safety protocols through the detection of safety gear and equipment [

li.p: Introduction: This section provides background information on the project, defines the scope of the report, and states the objectives and research questions. It should also provide an overview of the structure of the report.

Literature Survey: This section summarizes and evaluates the existing research and literature related to the project. It should provide a critical analysis of the key theories, concepts, and empirical studies related to the research questions.
Proposed Work: This section describes the research methods and procedures used in the project. It should also discuss the basic flow of the project.
Experimental Study: This section describes each data set pre-processing. It should also focus on the result part.
Summary and Future Scope: This section summarizes the main findings and conclusions of the project and identifies any recommendations for future research or action.

p.span: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been widely used in the construction field for object detection tasks. CNNs can be employed to monitor construction sites by analysing images or video footage captured by cameras. They can detect and track objects such as vehicles, construction equipment, workers, and safety gear. This helps in ensuring safety compliance, tracking progress, and identifying potential risks or hazards. CNNs can aid in automating equipment and asset management by identifying and tracking specific objects or equipment on construction sites [

]. This enables efficient inventory management, preventing loss or theft and optimizing resource allocation. CNNs can be utilized for quality control purposes, identifying defects or anomalies in construction materials, components, or structures. They can detect cracks, deformations, or other irregularities that may impact the integrity or functionality of the construction elements. CNNs can classify and categorize different types of construction materials, such as bricks, concrete blocks, and steel beams. This facilitates inventory management, automates material tracking, and streamlines construction processes. CNNs can analyse construction site images or videos to identify safety violations or non-compliance with safety protocols. They can detect the absence of safety gear, improper use of equipment, or unsafe working conditions, helping to ensure a safer work environment [

]. CNNs can assist in site layout and planning by analysing construction site images and providing insights into the arrangement and placement of materials, equipment, and structures. This helps optimize workflows, improve efficiency, and minimize conflicts or bottlenecks [

]. By leveraging the capabilities of CNNs, the construction industry can benefit from enhanced automation, improved safety measures, better resource management, and increased productivity. These applications of CNNs in object detection contribute to streamlining construction processes, reducing costs, and delivering projects more efficiently. AWS: Amazon Web Services (AWS) provides a comprehensive suite of services and tools that can be leveraged for object detection applications. AWS offers a range of services that facilitate the development, deployment, and scalability of object detection systems. Here is a brief explanation of how AWS can be used for object detection. Amazon Rekognition is a fully managed deep learning-based service that provides object detection capabilities. It allows developers to easily integrate object detection into their applications without the need for extensive machine learning expertise. With Amazon Rekognition, users can analyse images or videos to detect and identify objects, scenes, and faces. It can recognize common objects and also perform custom object detection using custom training datasets. Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3) is a highly scalable and secure object storage service. It can be used to store and manage the image and video data used for object detection. AWS S3 provides reliable storage, and its integration with other AWS services allows seamless data transfer and processing [

Developers can run code without having to manage servers through the use of AWS Lambda, which is a serverless computing service. It can be used in object detection workflows to trigger and execute custom code or serverless functions for processing and analysing images. AWS Lambda can integrate with other AWS services, such as S3 and Rekognition, to create automated object detection pipelines. Amazon Sage Maker is a fully managed machine learning service that provides a platform for training and deploying machine learning models. It offers a range of tools and frameworks for developing custom object detection models using popular deep learning frameworks such as TensorFlow and PyTorch. Sage Maker simplifies the model training process by providing a scalable and managed infrastructure. Amazon Rekognition: Amazon Rekognition is a cloud-based computer vision service provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS) that offers powerful object detection capabilities [

]. It uses deep learning algorithms to analyse images and videos, allowing developers to easily incorporate object detection into their applications without the need for extensive machine learning expertise. Amazon Rekognition can identify and locate multiple objects within an image or video frame. It can detect a wide range of common objects such as vehicles, furniture, animals, and everyday items. Additionally, it can recognize scenes, providing contextual information about the environment. In addition to pre-trained models, Amazon Rekognition allows you to train custom models for object detection using your own datasets. This enables you to teach the service to identify specific objects or categories relevant to your application. Rekognition provides confidence scores for detected objects, indicating the level of certainty in the predictions. Developers can set thresholds for confidence scores to filter out less reliable detections based on their application’s requirements. For each detected object, Amazon Rekognition provides bounding box coordinates that specify the location and size of the object within the image or video frame. These coordinates can be used to overlay boxes around detected objects or to perform further analysis. Rekognition supports object detection in video streams by analysing individual frames and tracking objects across multiple frames. This enables applications such as surveillance systems, video content analysis, and real-time object tracking. Amazon Rekognition seamlessly integrates with other AWS services, allowing you to build end-to-end solutions. For example, you can use Amazon S3 for storing images and videos, AWS Lambda for triggering object detection processes, and Amazon DynamoDB for storing and retrieving metadata associated with detected objects. Rekognition is designed to handle large-scale object detection tasks. It can process images and videos in real time or batch mode, providing fast and accurate results even with large datasets. AWS ensures the security and privacy of your data by providing encryption at rest and in transit, as well as fine-grained access control through AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM). You have full control over your data and can choose the regions where your data is processed and stored [
