div.p: Amazon Rekognition

p.span: : A computer vision service offered by Amazon Web Services (AWS) is called Amazon Rekognition. When it comes to image and video analysis, it has a number of benefits over conventional Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) techniques. Instead of needing to create and train their own CNN models from start, developers can include Amazon Rekognition into their apps with ease because to its straightforward API interface. With Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels, customers may train and utilize their own object recognition models to identify certain things in photos and videos. This machine learning service is provided by Amazon Web Services (AWS). Three primary parts comprise the architecture of Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels: model training, model deployment, and data labelling.

level4.p: Data Labelling: Labelling the data is the first step towards building a bespoke object detection model. This is manually adding boundary boxes to photos or movies that contain things of interest. The machine learning model is subsequently trained to identify such items using the labelled data.

Custom Labels, multi-object labelling, and single object labelling are all three types of data labelling that Amazon Rekognition supports. Adding a single-object of interest to pictures or videos is known as single-object labelling. Several things of interest can be annotated on photos or videos using multi-object labelling. A picture may be divided into several sections using semantic segmentation, and each region can be assigned a class.

Model Training: An original object detection model can be trained using the data once it has been labelled. Amazon Rekognition utilizes the transfer learning method as its method of training the model. Transfer learning is the process of optimizing a previously trained model using a fresh dataset. As a result, customers may train a superior custom model with comparatively minimal data. Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels offers both automated and manual training. Choosing the labelled data and allowing Amazon Rekognition to train the model automatically constitute automated training. Through manual training, users may fine-tune the model to their own use case by adjusting the hyperparameters.

Model Deployment: After training, the model may be used to identify items in fresh pictures or videos. Real-time inference and batch inference are the two deployment options offered by Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels. Users can submit many photos or videos for batch inference, and the results are returned in a single answer. With real-time inference, users may upload specific photos or videos to the model and get instantaneous responses. Additionally, SDKs and API are offered by Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels for incorporating the model into unique applications. Using the Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels dashboard or API, users may additionally keep an eye on the model's performance. Generally speaking, Amazon Rekognition bespoke Labels’ architecture is intended to offer an adaptable and simple-to-use framework for making unique.

p.span: : Amazon Rekognition allows you to create custom labels to train its models for specific object detection tasks. When labelling images shown in Fig.
using custom labels, the process typically involves the following steps:

level4.p: Data Collection: Gather a dataset of images that contain the objects or categories you want to train the model to detect. The dataset should be diverse and representative of the objects you want the model to recognize. Labelling: Manually annotate or label the objects of interest in the images. You can mark the bounding boxes around the objects and provide corresponding labels for each bounding box. This process involves identifying and labelling objects within the images.

Training: Once you have labelled the images, you can upload the dataset to Amazon Rekognition and initiate the training process. During training, the model learns to recognize the custom labels based on the annotated images. The training process involves optimizing the model’s parameters and weights to improve its accuracy in detecting the specified objects.

p.span: Evaluation: After training, it is essential to evaluate the model’s performance. You can use a separate validation dataset that includes labelled images to assess the accuracy and quality of the custom label detection. Evaluation metrics such as precision, recall, and

1 score can be calculated to measure the model’s performance. Deployment: Once the model is trained and evaluated, it can be deployed to perform object detection on new, unseen images. You can use the deployed model to analyse images and detect the custom labels you trained it on. The output typically includes bounding box coordinates and labels for the detected objects (Fig.

div.p: An architecture defines how the interface results are derived from the training data. It undergoes recognition of custom labels to give trained models. The query images are processed and custom labels are added and undergo training to give the interface results.
Fig. 1
Amazon Rekognition architecture

p.span: : Amazon Rekognition data pre-processing includes the preparation of an input data, such as images or videos, for object detection.

: Binding box annotations are the data annotations that are used to detect objects within images. It is usually done by providing a bound box annotation around the objects that are interesting within the image. The bound box annotations defines the rectangular area that encloses the position of the object within the image.

: Start the training job, which will use the annotated data to train the object detection model. The training process involves optimizing the model’s parameters to accurately detect the objects specified in the annotations.

: After training, evaluate the performance of the trained model using the validation dataset. This helps assess the model’s accuracy, precision, recall, and other metrics. If the performance is not satisfactory, you may need to iterate and refine the training process.

div.p: Dataset—The dataset provided for the experimental study consists of 1431 images in the JPEG format. The images are named following a standard expression [1–9] [0–9]*.jpg, which implies they are sequentially numbered. These images were captured using unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) and represent various construction sites. In addition to the images, the dataset includes 8601 captions that describe the different regions or areas within the images. These captions provide detailed information about the specific locations or regions depicted in each image. The dataset is packaged in a zip file format, which contains two main components. The first component is another zip file, which houses the actual images. This zip file has a size of approximately 583 MB, indicating the storage space required for the image files. The second component of the dataset is a “.json” file. This JSON file serves as a structured data file that provides additional information about the construction assets depicted in the images.

p.span: Results—The results of the project demonstrate the successful development of an automated system using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for the identification and categorization of objects and materials in construction site images. The system was trained on a comprehensive dataset of construction site images, enabling the CNN model to learn and extract relevant features from the images with high accuracy. The evaluation of the system on a separate validation dataset showcased its effectiveness in accurately identifying and categorizing different objects and materials commonly found in construction sites. The performance metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and
1 score, consistently demonstrated the system’s ability to achieve reliable results. The project’s results shown in Figs.
and

indicate the potential of the developed automated system to significantly improve the efficiency, safety, and decision-making processes in the construction industry by automating the object and material identification tasks (Fig.

level3.p: In summary, the main purpose of this thesis is to identify the relevant objects at construction site using object detection. We have used Amazon Rekognition (AR) Custom Labels to identify the objects and label them. Amazon Rekognition uses CNN model for detecting the objects in the images. We have achieved favourable outcomes. The proposed solution involves utilizing Amazon Rekognition, specifically the Custom Labels service, for object detection in construction site images. The system aims to leverage the capabilities of Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels to train a custom machine learning model for object detection. Techniques such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are used to extract relevant features from the construction site images and accurately identify and categorize different objects present in the images. By utilizing Amazon Rekognition’s advanced machine learning capabilities, the proposed solution offers an automated and efficient approach to object detection in construction site images, enabling improved monitoring, safety, and management of construction operations. The future scope for identifying objects at construction sites using Amazon Rekognition is quite promising. Integrating Amazon Rekognition with IoT sensors at construction sites enables a comprehensive understanding of the environment. By combining visual data with other sensor data, such as temperature, humidity, and noise, correlations and patterns can be detected. This integration enhances safety, efficiency, and decision-making in construction operations by providing valuable insights and facilitating data-driven actions. Overall, the future scope for identifying objects at construction sites using Amazon Rekognition lies in further refining the accuracy, expanding the capabilities to include classification and attribute recognition, enabling real-time tracking, integrating with IoT sensors, analysing video surveillance, and integrating with construction management systems. These advancements can enhance safety, efficiency, and decision-making in construction operations, leading to improved project outcomes.

level2.h2: Apparent Personality Traits Detection Based on Correlation-Based Attention and Feature Weighting Methods

p.span: Ravi Prasad Thati
and
Suresh Mamidisetti

div.p: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, School of Technology, GITAM (Deemed to be University), Hyderabad, Telangana, India
Department of CSE, Anurag University, Hyderabad, Telangana, India

level3.p: Ravi Prasad Thati

level4.p: Email:

p.span: Apparent personality traits (APT) is an impression that individual leaves to others. Current work used publicly accessible Video Dataset for Personality Traits detection (VDPT) to propose end-to-end machine learning solution for APT prediction. These predictions are based on a correlation-based attention mechanism for effectively assigning weights to facial features. We explored three research questions in this work. They are: First, how does the correlation-based attention mechanism in building end-to-end machine learning pipeline impact the robustness of personality prediction models using facial features? Second, can this novel feature weighting method be devised to account for non-linear interactions among facial features and their impact on personality trait prediction? and third, how can this quantification of feature weights be integrated into the model development process to ensure transparency in real-time apparent personality prediction? SVM and LR consistently demonstrate strong accuracy (86%) and
-scores (76%), with SVM gave higher performance in neuroticism prediction.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Emotions are very short-term feelings from known cause. On the mood is longer in duration may exist for over a period of time from a known cause may be a few days/week/months. Personality is the character that is throughout the life or longer period of time. Apparent personality traits is the impression that individual leaves to others; however, it is not always possible to find the real personality [

In this context of apparent personality detection, the Big-5 Model is a widely recognized model for understanding apparent personality traits. It consists of five key dimensions. First, agreeableness which assesses an individual's propensity for cooperation and compassion, reflecting their willingness to work harmoniously with others. Second, conscientiousness measures the degree of organization and responsibility in a person's life which indicates their reliability and diligence in tasks. Third, extraversion gives the level of sociability and outgoingness, revealing how comfortable and engaged an individual is in social interactions. Fourth, neuroticism provides emotional stability, shedding light on a person's susceptibility to stress and negative emotions. Lastly, openness evaluates for creativity on new experiences, providing insight into their receptivity to novel ideas. These five dimensions collectively provide a comprehensive understanding of an individual's apparent personality traits [

level3.p: Artificial intelligence methods are used to predict apparent personality trait which gave remarkable performance. The significance of machine learning in predicting apparent personality is that it can help us understand how people perceive others based on their facial features and expressions. This can be useful in various ways. Few of them are: First, in job interviews where employers make snap judgments about job candidates within the first few minutes of an interview. Second, tracker of low rated neuroticism individuals at early stages to take remedial activities for avoiding depression. Third, in building personalized recommendation systems, i.e., depending upon personality blogs, videos, games, etc., can be recommended.

In the current work, we developed an end-to-end machine learning solution for apparent personality prediction. These predictions are based on a correlation-based attention mechanism for effectively assigning weights to facial features. We explored three research questions (Rq) in this context. They are explained as follows: Rq1. How does the correlation-based attention mechanism in building end-to-end machine learning pipeline impact the robustness of personality prediction models using facial features? Rq2. Can this novel feature weighting method be devised to account for non-linear interactions among facial features and their impact on personality trait prediction? Rq3. How can this quantification of feature weights be integrated into the model development process to ensure transparency in real-time apparent personality prediction?

p.span: Xu et al. in the work [

] investigated the connection between self-reported personality traits and facial characteristics. They introduced a novel approach for predicting the Big-5 personality traits of college students based on static facial images. They compiled a dataset comprising 13,347 pairs of facial images and personality traits, trained a deep neural network on a subset of 10,667 samples, and validated its performance on the remaining 2670 samples. Their work showed that personality traits can be reliably predicted from facial images, achieving over 70% accuracy, with neuroticism and extroversion being predicted with over 90% accuracy.
Willis et al. in the work [

] showed that it is possible to infer personality like child faced person is less likely to face judicial rather than mature faced person/attractive faces have more opportunities rather compared with unattractive faces. Results showed that correlations on judgments made with 100 ms are similar to that of 500 and 1000 ms and no time constraints. Correlations on judgments with 500, 1000 ms, and no time constraints did not grow as one expected when compared with 100 ms.
Liu et al. in the work [

] used publicly accessible twitter survey dataset of 434 profile pictures and self-reported Big-5 scores. Features are extracted from each profile picture using Emo VU and Face++. Ground truths are collected from self-reported forms. Twitter text dataset with 66,502 profile pictures and with Big-5 personality are measured. Several findings are made like conscientiousness people tend to have a profile picture (single face) and is highly correlated with image type. Neuroticism trait has low correlation with color features as neuroticism trait personality tends to have dull or low colorful effects.
Figure
gives an overview of the proposed methodology in the current work. The following subsections provide the details of each step carried out in the present work.

div.p: A flow diagram exhibits dataset acquisition flows to the feature extraction, correlation based attention method, feature weighting method, M L classifiers include S V M, decision trees, and logistic regression. It process to give a big 5 model classification and the output is performance evaluation.
Fig. 1
Overview of the methodology

p.span: In this step, publicly accessible Video Dataset for Personality Traits detection (VDPT) [

] was acquired. The dataset consists of 38 self-video samples of participants of different nationalities, genders, ages, etc. Each participant responded to International Personality Item Pool (IPIP)-50 personality test to record the ground truths based on the standards of their personality traits. Set of predefined questions were given to the participants regarding their introduction, their work and achievements. All participants recorded their facial video and gave a test using an interactive website.
The video dataset is given as an input to OpenFace [
], a facial analysis toolkit for the extraction of low-level facial features [

]. OpenFace is used to obtain detailed landmarks on the face, capturing key points that define facial structure. These landmarks serve as the foundation for segmenting the face into one distinct region unit that allow analysis of facial regions. Subsequently, statistical measures such as mean, median, mode, and an additional set of seven relevant features are computed for each of the facial region of the entire participant [
]. This process results in building a dataset comprising ten distinct statistical features for each facial region and with their corresponding ground truth values.

In this step, correlation-based attention method is employed to focus on association of features with their ground truth. Mathematically, this attention mechanism can be represented as follows: Our dataset (
) consists of ten features, where each element
is associated with a context
. The correlation-based attention weight, denoted as
, for each element
can be computed as
Here, correlation represents a correlation function using Pearson correlation coefficients. The resulting attention weights
quantify the relevance of each element
with respect to its associated context
. Higher correlation values indicate stronger attention to that ground truth, while lower values imply reduced attention.
In this step, the attention score obtained in the previous step are utilized in the following way: The feature weighting is computed with the attention scores (
), which quantify the importance of each feature (
). The weighted feature vector (
) for our dataset (
) is built. It is represented mathematically as

p.span: Here,
[10] denote the values of the individual features in the our dataset, while
signifies the attention score for feature
. These attention scores (

) are determined through statistical analyses thus builds a robust feature vector. Features weighing with higher attention scores is accorded greater influence in the model's decision-making process, enabling it to concentrate on the most pertinent aspects of the data.
The dataset is divided into two sets: training (70%) and testing (30%). The feature weighting step driven by attention scores (

) serves as improvements for training various classifiers: Support Vector Machines (SVM), Decision Trees (DT), and Logistic Regression (LR). By assigning these weights to individual features based on their relevance, the weighted feature vectors (
) enable these classifiers to focus on the most informative aspects of the dataset.

level4.p: These feature sets are utilized to train various classifiers, such as Support Vector Machines (SVM), Decision Trees (DT), and Logistic Regression (LR) models. The test set is used to obtain the five dimensions: openness, conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, and neuroticism. By applying these three classifiers, it becomes possible to predict individuals’ personality traits based on their unique features.

p.span: The performance evaluation of classification models based on the Big-5 model employs two key metrics: accuracy and
-score. Accuracy measures the overall correctness of the model's predictions, quantifying the proportion of correctly classified personality traits among all predictions. The

-score combines precision and recall, which accounts a balanced measure of the model's ability to correctly classify both positive and negative instances. Together, these metrics offers a comprehensive view of the model's classification performance.
The results obtained in the current work are tabulated in Table

. The attained results of personality trait prediction using facial features and three different classifiers: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree (DT), and Logistic Regression (LR), show efficient performance characteristics across personality traits. SVM and LR consistently demonstrate strong accuracy (86%) and

-scores (76%), with SVM gave higher performance in Neuroticism prediction. LR performs well in openness (79%), agreeableness (75%), and extraversion (74%) w.r.t accuracy. These findings suggest the pivotal role of the classifier selection in apparent personality prediction using SVM and LR emerging as preferable choices for reliable predictions. The results also show the potential for further model refinement and fine-tuning to optimize accuracy diverse personality traits using facial features.

Results obtained using the three classifiers

level3.p: The research questions discussed in the introduction are addressed as follows:

p.span: . Based on the current results presented in Table

, it's evident that the choice of classifier (SVM, DT, and LR) influences the impact of the correlation-based attention mechanism on model robustness. For example, SVM consistently gave higher accuracy and

-scores compared to DT and LR for most personality traits. This shows that the attention mechanism can have a more positive impact on model robustness when combined with SVM. However, it's also worth noting that the impact varies across personality traits.

. The current results indicate that the feature weighting method using attention mechanism account for nonlinear interactions among facial features. For example, SVM, which benefits from nonlinear relationships gave improved accuracy and

-scores when feature weighting is applied for traits like Extraversion and Agreeableness. This supports that the method has the potential to capture non-linear interactions with variations across personality traits. Further analysis and experimentation can provide deeper insights into the method's effectiveness in addressing nonlinear feature relationships.

. Regarding the integration of feature weights with the attention mechanism for transparency, the obtained results demonstrate that SVM and LR, when coupled with feature weighting help improve accuracy and

-scores across personality traits. This suggests that the quantification of feature weights can be helpful integrated into these models without compromising predictive performance. The combination of feature weighting, attention mechanism, and classifiers like SVM and LR offers a promising avenue for ensuring transparency in real-time apparent personality prediction.

level3.p: In this work, we have explored the use of machine learning methods to predict apparent personality traits. Through the investigation, three key research questions were identified and addressed using our approach. These findings suggest the potential of our work to make significant contributions in the domain of apparent personality detection.

level2.p: Heart disease and diabetes are two of the most prevalent and costly chronic diseases in the world. Early detection and management of these conditions are critical in preventing their progression and reducing the risk of complications, morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs. However, identifying individuals at risk of developing heart disease and diabetes is challenging, as they often present with non-specific symptoms, and risk factors may go unnoticed until the disease is advanced. Additionally, healthcare resources are often limited, and healthcare providers may not have the time or expertise to screen all patients for these diseases. Therefore, there is a need to develop accurate predictive models using machine learning (ML) methods for heart disease and diabetes that can identify individuals at risk of developing these diseases early on. Such models can help healthcare providers prioritize patients for screening and intervention, thus improving patient outcomes and reducing the burden on healthcare systems. This paper provides a detailed overview of the ML models and their performance evaluation. The results indicate that the model can accurately predict the likelihood of an individual having diabetes and heart disease, with a high degree of precision.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: Heart disease or cardiovascular disease (CVD) confers to an illness related to the heart and blood vessels. CVD leads to death in many cases and heart-related illness caused by numerous elements like high blood pressure level, high blood sugar, high cholesterol, tobacco, obesity, physical inactivity, diet, stressful work, and inactive lifestyle [

]. The symptoms of heart illness are chest pain, breathlessness, or dizziness and these illness seek immediate medical attention. Diabetes is a persistent illness related to blood sugar levels. This illness occurs because of two reasons, either our body is not able to use the insulin produced by the pancreas or the pancreas is not able to produce enough insulin that is required for the body [
]. Heart disease and diabetes are two of the most common and serious health conditions that affect a large portion of the global population [

]. According to the World Health Organization, CVD causes approximately 17.9 million deaths annually, and similarly, diabetes affects over 463 million people globally which leads to blindness, kidney failure, and lower limb amputations [
]. Heart disease and diabetes are two of the most prevalent and costly chronic diseases in the world [
]. Early detection and management of these conditions are critical in preventing their progression and reducing the risk of complications [

]. Predictive models can help to identify patients who are at high risk of developing these diseases, which can enable early interventions such as lifestyle modifications, medication management, and regular monitoring [

level3.p: This research work presents a disease prediction model that aims to predict the likelihood of an individual having diabetes and heart disease based on various risk factors. The purpose of this research work is to present a heart and diabetes disease prediction model based on machine learning algorithms.

Diabetes and heart disease are two of the most common and serious health conditions worldwide, affecting millions of people. The development of accurate prediction models for these diseases can provide valuable insights for healthcare professionals, enabling early intervention and prevention strategies. Recently, various explorations have been made to develop prediction models for diabetes and heart sickness using different ML methods.

p.span: One such study by Hasan, Md Kamrul, et al. aimed to develop a prediction model for diabetes using Pima Indian Diabetes Dataset. The study proposed an ensemble classifier which is a combination of AdaBoost and XGBoost [

]. They compared the performance in terms of AUC and concluded that their proposed method outperforms others in this dataset. The authors concluded that the model could be useful for the identification of patients at high risk of developing diabetes. Another study by Kavitha, M., et al. utilized decision tree and random forest models to predict heart disease risk factors. The study used the Cleveland heart disease dataset and achieved an accuracy of 88.7% by fusing both models. The authors concluded that the fusion of the random forest model and decision tree model was more accurate in predicting heart disease risk factors and could be useful in identifying patients at high risk of developing the disease [

]. In a more recent study by Sharma, A., Guleria, K., and Goyal, N., decision tree, Naïve Bayes, artificial neural network, and logistic regression were used to predict diabetes using Pima Indian Diabetes dataset. The authors concluded that the logistic regression model outperforms others [

]. Another study by Mohan, S., Thirumalai, C., and Srivastava, G. aimed to predict heart illness and proposed a method, hybrid random forest with a linear model. The study used a dataset consisting of 303 patients and achieved an accuracy of 88.7%. The authors concluded that the proposed method was the most accurate and could be useful in identifying patients at high risk of developing heart illness [

Python is the chosen programming language to create the disease prediction model as it has various predefined libraries for data preprocessing, analysis, and visualization as well as for machine learning models. Figure

explains how our disease predictive model works. We start by considering the publicly available repository. Both the dataset in this study are the publicly available repository. The size of diabetes dataset is 768 instances with 9 attributes. The size of heart disease dataset is 303 instances with 14 attributes. Then, we perform data preprocessing and feature engineering to improve the overall data quality as well as divide the data into training and test sets. This step is followed by the application of a ML algorithm. The model accuracy for each model is checked by calculating the accuracy score and comparing the correctness of their predictions. Finally, performance analysis for all three models is performed with the help of cross-validation accuracy scores and confusion matrices.

div.p: A flow diagram of the proposed model. It starts with the patient data leading to the dataset, data preprocessing, attribute selection, building a model that is divided into random forest, logistic regression, and S V M. All lead to model accuracy and performance analysis.
Fig. 1
Proposed work

p.span: Logistic regression (LR) belongs to the supervised learning method mainly used for solving classification jobs. In machine learning, LR is often used as a binary classifier, where the output is a probable score between 0 and 1. And this means that the input belongs to either o class or 1 class [

]. It is simple to implement, computationally efficient, and can handle both continuous and categorical data. Additionally, logistic regression provides interpretable results, allowing us to understand the association between the variables.

Support vector machine (SVM) is used for solving classification and regression jobs. Both linear and nonlinear problems can be solved using SVM. It finds out the best possible hyperplane that can separate the data into different classes [
]. The closest data items to the hyperplane are named support vectors [
]. SVM performs effectively in high-dimensional spaces, can handle both linear and nonlinear problems, and is less prone to Overfitting.

Random forest (RF) is used for solving classification and regression jobs. It belongs to the ensemble learning method as it is the combination of various decision trees. Because of this characteristic, it is more robust and provides a better accurate outcome [
]. In RF, each decision tree makes a prediction and the final prediction is made by averaging or majority voting [

]. The random subsets of the data and features are sampled with replacement, which is called bootstrap sampling, to create a diverse set of decision trees. Random forest machine learning is widely used ML methods because it is easy to use, computationally efficient, and deal both categorical and continuous variables. Additionally, to find out important features it provides importance scores to them that contribute to the prediction of the outcome variable.

level3.p: To verify the outcome of a software development testing is required whether the outcome is satisfactory or not. Testing is done on the test set with the help of a trained model for anticipation and comparing the anticipated outputs to the actual labels. The performance is evaluated using various metrics. Verification involves ensuring that the model meets the required performance criteria, such as accuracy or error rate, and that it behaves as expected under various conditions. This may involve testing the model on different subsets of the data, evaluating its performance on different metrics, or analyzing its behavior in edge cases or under unusual circumstances. The test conditions describe hypothetical patients with different combinations of attributes. The system behavior column shows the result that the machine learning algorithm predicted based on these attributes. The expected result column shows the correct outcome that we would expect based on the dataset.

level3.p: The development of a heart and diabetes prediction model can have a significant impact on the early diagnosis and prevention of these diseases. By analyzing various risk factors and health parameters, the model can provide accurate and personalized predictions for individuals, allowing for timely interventions and lifestyle modifications. We utilized a machine learning approach and trained the model on a large dataset of patient records, incorporating features such as age, gender, BMI, blood pressure, cholesterol levels, and glucose levels. The model was able to accurately predict the likelihood of developing heart disease and diabetes in the test set with a great degree of accuracy in case of SVM model. Looking ahead, there are several potential avenues for further research and development of heart and diabetes prediction models. One possible future direction is to incorporate additional variables such as genetics, lifestyle factors, and environmental factors into the model to improve its accuracy and personalization. Additionally, the integration of prediction models into electronic health records could facilitate their use by clinicians. The validation of the model in diverse populations is also important to ensure its generalizability across different ethnicities, age groups, and geographical regions. Overall, further research in this area has the potential to lead to more accurate and effective prevention and management of heart disease and diabetes, ultimately resulting in better health outcomes for individuals at risk of these diseases.

level2.p: Approximately 2.2 billion people in the world are visually impaired and rely on assistance to meet their basic needs. Education is a fundamental human need and plays a crucial role in empowering individuals. For visually impaired individuals, education can not only integrate them into society but also enhance their professional and academic abilities. To facilitate better education for the visually impaired, specialized books, educational materials, and devices are necessary. Braille is a tactile system designed for people who are blind, enabling them to read and write through touch. Proposed work aims to improve the education of visually impaired individuals by developing a braille-to-voice converter. The system will use image processing techniques to capture an image of braille text, which will then be analyzed using computer vision algorithms to extract the text and convert it to speech. The ultimate goal is to create a cost-effective, accurate, and user-friendly tool that can be easily integrated into the daily lives of visually impaired individuals. With this system, visually impaired individuals will be able to easily and quickly convert braille text to speech in real time.

level3.p: Keywords

p.span: On a global scale, there are approximately 7.4 billion individuals, with an estimated 285 million people experiencing various degrees of visual impairment. This breakdown includes 39 million individuals who are completely blind and 246 million individuals who have either mild or severe visual impairments, according to the 2011 World Health Organization (WHO) report [

]. It has been estimated that by the year 2020, the number of individuals who are blind will increase to 75 million, and the number of individuals with visual impairments will increase to 200 million [

]. In India, around 8 million people are suffering from visual disorders, the leading causes of blindness are cataracts, refractive errors, glaucoma, and corneal blindness. Blind people in India face many challenges, including limited access to education, employment opportunities, and healthcare [

]. Many blind people live in poverty, which further exacerbates their difficulties. The braille writing system is designed for individuals who are visually impaired, such as those who have low vision, are blind, or deaf-blind [

]. It is a method of reading and writing that either relies on touch, allowing individuals to read through embossed paper or by utilizing refreshable braille displays that can be connected to computers and smartphones [
]. The presentation of braille is illustrated in Fig.
, is read by touching raised dots. The braille system comprises six dots organized into two columns, each containing three dots [
]. These dots can be arranged in various combinations to represent letters, punctuation marks, numbers, and various other symbols.

div.p: A pattern of raised dots arranged in configurations of up to six dots, structured in a 3 by 2 grid.
Fig. 1
Braille cell representation

level3.p: Braille can be read by fingertips, braille language is very important to make visually impaired people independent and literate. Braille is also used in various contexts such as signage, labeling, and packaging to make information accessible to blind individuals. In summary, braille serves as a fundamental tool for promoting literacy and fostering independence among individuals who are blind or visually impaired. As part of this proposed work, the braille dots will be converted to text following the grade 1 braille system, and the text will also be converted to speech.

level4.p: Developed a system that converts braille script into both text and speech, which will provide a valuable aid to visually impaired individuals and also those who work with them. Prioritize the development of a portable, flexible, and user-friendly system that can be easily used by anyone, regardless of their technical expertise. Created a handheld epitome system that can be easily transported and used in any setting.

p.span: The following literature survey aims to critically analyze and synthesize the most cited works in the field to provide a comprehensive understanding of the current state of knowledge: In [

], authors have introduced a methodology that leverages image processing techniques and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) technology to identify braille characters and convert them into text. In paper [

], a unique and innovative solution aims to help visually impaired individuals by converting printed text into audio. The document covers various topics related to TTS, such as speech synthesis methods, speech parameterization techniques, and TTS evaluation metrics. The [

] paper delves into the conversion of Hindi braille into text and speech, employing both conventional and deep learning-based methodologies. Remarkable results were obtained, with a 100% accuracy rate using the conventional method and a 96% accuracy rate using the deep learning approach. Moreover, experts evaluated the quality of the generated speech, considering factors such as clarity, pronunciation, sound quality, and speed. In sum, the paper introduces a promising approach to make Hindi braille accessible to individuals who are blind or visually impaired. The [

] presented a proposed solution for converting braille code to Tamil voice messages, which can be read out by a computer. Overall, the paper presents a promising approach for making information more accessible to the visually impaired Tamil-speaking community. The authors in [

] expressed a real-time text to braille and audio conversion system, designed to aid the visually impaired in reading and understanding documents. The system is flexible and can be used with various types of media, such as books or documents. In [

], a system is proposed for converting speech into braille for visually impaired individuals, utilizing the Python programming language. The papers cited in this section encompass a range of topics within the field of assistive technology for individuals with visual impairments. The proposed solutions utilize image processing techniques, OCR, and machine learning algorithms to achieve high accuracy rates. The systems can provide affordable and portable solutions for visually impaired individuals to access information, removing barriers in communication and education [

]. The systems also incorporate natural language processing and graphical user interfaces to provide intuitive and user-friendly experiences for the users. The papers present promising approaches to improve the quality of life for the visually impaired.

level3.p: The methodology for creating an android app using android studio and java to convert braille to voice using image processing involves several steps.

level4.p: The first step is to scan the braille image using a scanner. The scanner should be set to a high resolution to ensure that the braille dots are captured accurately. Auto-cropping of image is done to remove the unwanted or noise periphery from the image.

p.span: When it crops an image, it aims to eliminate the irrelevant outer portions of the image and concentrate on the specific area that are interested in. This process is commonly known as selecting the Region of Interest (ROI). In Fig.
, the rectangular box outlines the area of interest, which is the ROI.

div.p: An illustration of a series of raised dots and lines arranged on a page in a 2 by 3 format.
Fig. 2
Auto-cropped image

p.span: Once the braille image is scanned, it needs to be processed to extract the individual braille dots. Image processing techniques such as thresholding, edge detection, and blob analysis can be used to isolate the dots.

Representing an RGB image as a one-dimensional array can streamline processing, enhance speed, and reduce the data volume within the image. To convert an RGB image into a grayscale image, a common approach is to add together the red, green, and blue components of each pixel in the image and subsequently calculate the average of this summation. This process can be expressed mathematically using Eqs. (
) and (
However, to account for the human eye’s heightened sensitivity to the green component, the following Eq. (
) is utilized

To perform the task, first, the image is converted to grayscale. Then, a threshold value is selected within the range of 0–255. In the grayscale image, any pixel with a value below the specified threshold is identified as a color to be transformed into white, symbolized by the value 0. Conversely, pixels with values exceeding the threshold are recognized as colors to be converted into black, represented by the value 1. The resultant image following this procedure is depicted in Fig.

div.p: An illustration of multilingual mapping with a series of raised dots arranged on a page.
Fig. 3
Threshold image

p.span: The subsequent phase involves converting the braille dots into text through OCR software. This software analyzes the dots and translates them into their respective letters and symbols. After identifying the positions of the braille dots, as illustrated in Fig.
, the image containing the braille paragraph must undergo segmentation into lines, followed by words, and ultimately individual braille cells or characters to enable braille character recognition.

div.p: An illustration of multilingual mapping with a series of raised dots and lines arranged on a page in a format.
Fig. 4
Braille dots recognition

p.span: Braille-to-text conversion is a challenging task that involves recognizing and translating the braille code into its corresponding text representation.

level5.p: For every cell within a row, specific values are allocated for the top margin, bottom margin, left margin, and right margin. Utilizing the top and bottom margins, the cell is partitioned horizontally into three sections, and the dot positions between these margins serve as separators.

p.strong: Mapping the Cell Pattern to a Braille Pattern

p.span: The obtained pattern is compared to a collection of predefined braille patterns, where each pattern corresponds to an English letter, punctuation mark, or numeric digit. When a match is identified, the character linked to that specific pattern is extracted, as demonstrated in Fig.

div.p: 2 tables have 3 columns for Braille dot combinations, Braille cells, and English braille. used in English. In each row in both tables, digits in each row is converted to Braille and english Braille.
Fig. 5
Braille mapping of alphabets

level4.p: Testing an android app that converts braille image to speech using image processing requires a comprehensive approach. It is also important to test the app on various devices with different screen resolutions and camera qualities to ensure optimal functionality.

level4.h4: 3.5 Flow Diagram of the Proposed Work

p.span: The braille-to-voice converter app accepts braille input through the user interface, translates it into a text representation, utilizes text-to-speech technology to convert the text into audio, and then delivers the audio output through the device’s speakers or headphones, as depicted in Fig.

div.p: 2 flow diagrams. Left, it starts with document scanning with a camera, image sent for processing of dot extraction, and converting dots to text and speech output. Right, it begins with an image input, auto cropping, threshold, and cell division, converts cells to text, and ends with text to speech output.
Fig. 6
Flow diagram of the braille-to-voice converter system

level4.p: The control block manages the flow of data and control between the various components of the app. The methodology for creating an android app using android studio, java to convert braille to voice using image processing involves several steps.

p.span: The process of converting scanned braille documents into English text consists of multiple steps, as illustrated in Fig.

. Initially, the braille text is identified and divided into lines and individual braille cells. Subsequently, grids are constructed using the standard measurements of braille cells, and the dots are extracted. Therefore, the appropriate mapping for each language is used to identify and store the alphabets as text, which is then read out by a voice synthesizer. Therefore, it’s essential to ensure that the app’s image processing algorithms are reliable and accurate to ensure the best results. The Graphical User Interface (GUI) plays a pivotal role in determining the quality of the application, as it constitutes the primary interaction point for end users, regardless of the underlying system design.

div.p: A flow diagram depicts the script scanning via camera, image processing with Open C V for auto cropping, noise filtering, grayscale conversion, and O C R based dot recognition mapped to text and converted to speech using G T T S tool.
Fig. 7
Converting scanned braille documents to English text

p.span: The user sees the image they want to convert with the application as shown in Fig.

in the first start window after starting the system. There are two ways to get the image: the first lets users use their mobile device’s camera, while the second lets them load an already-taken photo. The user has the option to begin the picture capture procedure at the first start window. A prompt to “please retry again” appears on the screen after the picture has been taken, asking the user to try again if the image is blurry or not captured correctly. Following the camera-enabled window, the braille dots converted to text appear on the screen, and the audio output can be heard through the device’s speakers as the outcome of the system.

div.p: A set of 3 screenshots of a window depicts multilingual mapping with a series of raised dots and lines arranged on a page in a structured format.
Fig. 8
Processing window and text displayed on the screen with audio output

level3.p: The presence of this system promises significant advantages for sighted individuals who frequently engage with visually impaired individuals. It empowers them to access and comprehend braille documents even without any prior knowledge of the braille language. Throughout the various stages, meticulous observation and analysis of results have been paramount. These evaluations serve as critical checkpoints, ensuring the system’s effectiveness and reliability. The braille-to-voice converter, employing image processing within an android app, emerges as a potentially transformative tool for visually impaired individuals who rely on braille for their reading and writing needs.
